Fine-grained temporal analysis not possible for scraped search_query Arshkon: absent. Asaniczka: search_position field. Scraped: structured query. Different origins but all repurposed as search metadata

docs.title: 6. Key Constraint Mapping for Downstream Tasks
Text analyses (embeddings, topic models, requirement extraction)
docs.text: Binding constraint: description_core_llm coverage at 20.7% for scraped SWE. Severity: HIGH. Only ~7,300 scraped SWE rows have LLM-cleaned text vs ~26,000 from the 2024 sources. This creates an asymmetric comparison base. Using description_core as fallback introduces boilerplate noise. Mitigation: Use description_core_llm where available; run sensitivity on description_core; report text_source distribution. For keyword-presence analyses, raw description is acceptable.

docs.title: Seniority analyses
docs.text: Binding constraint: seniority_llm coverage too thin (1.9-10.0% known) to serve as primary. seniority_native missing entry for asaniczka. Severity: HIGH for entry-level trend analysis. The arshkon-only entry-level baseline is 769 SWE rows (seniority_native) or 848 (seniority_final). Mitigation: Use seniority_final as primary with seniority_native ablation. Use arshkon-only for 2024 entry-level baseline. seniority_llm is available for ablation where labeled but cannot drive the analysis.

docs.title: Geography analyses
docs.text: Binding constraint: metro_area coverage varies 61-78% across sources. Severity: MODERATE. ~25-40% of SWE rows lack metro assignment. Mitigation: state_normalized at 89-98% is a viable fallback for geographic stratification.

docs.title: Company analyses
docs.text: Binding constraint: company_industry is 0% for asaniczka and has different label granularity between arshkon (374 values) and scraped (5,134 values). Severity: HIGH for industry stratification. Company_size limited to arshkon only for SWE. Mitigation: Industry analysis requires harmonization and can only cover arshkon (2024-04) vs scraped (2026). company_name_canonical is usable cross-period.

docs.title: Requirement extraction / ghost detection
docs.text: Binding constraint: ghost_assessment_llm thin (10-62% by source). yoe_extracted at 60-72% is the best cross-period requirement signal. Severity: MODERATE for ghost analysis (LLM ghost requires more budget). LOW for YOE analysis. Mitigation: Rule-based ghost_job_risk available at 100% but very conservative (519 non-low). YOE is adequate.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Scraped data now has two periods. The scraper has continued into April 2026, producing 69,323 rows in period=2026-04 in addition to 145,280 in 2026-03. This was not anticipated in the schema documentation. Downstream analyses should decide whether to pool or separate these.

  2. is_remote_inferred is 0% everywhere. Despite the description having remote-related text in many scraped postings (22.3% have is_remote=true from the source), the text-inference flag catches none. This suggests the inference logic is either broken or very conservative — it is not a viable replacement for the source flag.

  3. seniority_llm is much thinner than expected. At 1.9-10.0% known-seniority coverage for SWE, it cannot serve as the primary seniority variable despite being designed for that purpose. The LLM classification budget was insufficient to cover even the SWE sample. This contradicts the schema documentation which positions it as "primary analysis variable."

  4. description_core_llm asymmetry. Arshkon SWE has 99.2% coverage while scraped SWE has 20.7%. This means text-dependent 2024-vs-2026 comparisons will either use a small scraped subset or fall back to lower-quality text. The 89.7% coverage for asaniczka SWE is better than expected.

  5. Scraped row count is larger than documented. Schema says ~146K scraped linkedin rows; actual is 214,707 (with ~69K in the new 2026-04 period).

  6. 4 undocumented columns exist: analysis_group, analysis_in_scope, eligible_for_extraction, llm_text_skip_reason are present but not in the schema reference.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• Row counts for scraped source will grow with continued scraping. Numbers above are as of the 2026-04-08 pipeline run.
  • The 9 scraped rows with date_flag=date_posted_out_of_range are negligible.
  • Description quality is excellent: only 23 SWE rows flagged too_short across all sources.
  • The total column count (105) exceeds the documented 101, suggesting the schema needs updating.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Pipeline: Increase LLM budget for scraped SWE. The 20.7% description_core_llm coverage for scraped SWE is the single biggest data quality gap. Running additional LLM budget would close this.
  2. Pipeline: Investigate is_remote_inferred. The 0% rate everywhere suggests the inference logic needs repair.
  3. Schema: Document the 4 new columns and update row counts.
  4. Analysis: company_industry harmonization needed before any industry-stratified cross-period comparison (374 vs 5,134 distinct values).
  5. Downstream tasks should treat seniority_final as the primary seniority variable, not seniority_llm, given the coverage gap. seniority_llm is an ablation check only.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: Asaniczka associate is NOT a usable junior proxy. On every available signal — YOE distribution, title overlap, seniority_final conditional distribution, and rule-based imputation — asaniczka associate behaves like arshkon associate, not arshkon entry. Specifically: asaniczka associate has median YOE of 4.0 (vs 3.0 for arshkon entry), 72% are classified as associate by seniority_final (vs 98.4% of arshkon entry being classified as entry), and its rule-based imputation profile matches arshkon associate almost exactly. Treating asaniczka associate as entry-level would inflate the 2024 entry-level baseline and bias trend estimates.

docs.title: Label inventory (SWE rows, default filter)
docs.text:
Source Labels available Entry present? Key gap kaggle_arshkon entry (769), associate (275), mid-senior (2,344), director (25), intern (20), executive (11) Yes Small n for entry kaggle_asaniczka mid-senior (21,199), associate (2,014) No Zero entry-level labels scraped mid-senior (22,872), entry (3,972), associate (1,156), intern (585), director (448), executive (44) Yes Strong coverage

Critical: Asaniczka provides only two seniority levels for SWE: mid-senior (91.3%) and associate (8.7%). There are zero native entry, intern, director, or executive labels.

docs.title: Seniority label coverage rates (SWE)
docs.text: Metric Arshkon Asaniczka Scraped seniority_native non-null 68.6% 100.0% 82.9% seniority_final != unknown 80.4% 100.0% 93.6% seniority_llm != unknown (and non-null) 10.0% 2.6% 1.9%

docs.title: 2. Comparability Audit: Asaniczka Associate vs Arshkon Levels
2a. YOE Distribution
docs.text:
Group N Has YOE Mean Median P25 P75 arshkon entry 769 475 (61.8%) 4.12 3.0 2.0 5.0 arshkon associate 275 155 (56.4%) 3.85 3.0 2.0 5.0 asaniczka associate 2,014 1,311 (65.1%) 4.53 4.0 3.0 5.0 arshkon mid-senior 2,344 1,570 (67.0%) 5.56 5.0 4.0 7.0

Asaniczka associate has a median YOE of 4.0, sitting between arshkon associate (3.0) and arshkon mid-senior (5.0). Its mean (4.53) is closer to mid-senior than to entry. The IQR (3.0-5.0) also sits above arshkon entry's IQR (2.0-5.0).

YOE comparison with 2026 scraped data:

Group Median YOE Mean YOE scraped entry 2.0 2.33 scraped associate 4.0 4.31 scraped mid-senior 5.0 5.36

Scraped entry has markedly lower YOE (median 2.0) than asaniczka associate (4.0), further confirming asaniczka associate is not entry-like.

See exploration/figures/T02/yoe_comparison.png for the full distribution.

docs.title: 2b. Title Overlap
docs.text:
Asaniczka associate has 998 distinct titles. Overlap with arshkon seniority groups:

Arshkon group Distinct title overlap Row-weighted overlap arshkon entry 88 titles 38.1% of asaniczka associate rows arshkon associate 54 titles 32.4% of rows arshkon mid-senior 141 titles 43.5% of rows

The highest row-weighted overlap is with arshkon mid-senior (43.5%), not entry (38.1%). The top asaniczka associate title is "software engineer" (105 rows), which is the generic SWE title appearing at all levels.

docs.title: 2c. Title Cue Rates
docs.text:
Group N % Junior cues % Senior cues % No level cue arshkon entry 769 0.8% 0.3% 99.0% arshkon associate 275 0.4% 1.1% 98.5% asaniczka associate 2,014 0.7% 1.0% 98.3% arshkon mid-senior 2,344 0.2% 1.9% 97.9%

Junior title cues (junior, jr, entry-level, intern, new grad): asaniczka associate (0.7%) is very close to arshkon entry (0.8%), but this is a very weak signal since >98% of ALL groups lack explicit level cues. The senior cue rate (1.0%) is closer to arshkon associate (1.1%) than entry (0.3%).

Verdict on title cues: Inconclusive. Explicit level cues are too rare in titles to discriminate.

docs.title: 2d. Seniority_final Conditional Distribution
docs.text:
What does the rule-based seniority_final assign, conditional on the native label?

Native label group % entry % associate % mid-senior % director arshkon entry (n=769) 98.4% 0.0% 1.6% 0.0% arshkon associate (n=275) 3.6% 74.9% 21.5% 0.0% asaniczka associate (n=2,014) 2.0% 72.0% 25.9% 0.0% arshkon mid-senior (n=2,344) 0.4% 0.0% 99.4% 0.2%

See exploration/figures/T02/seniority_final_conditional.png.

Asaniczka associate maps to: - 72.0% associate (matches arshkon associate at 74.9%) - 25.9% mid-senior (matches arshkon associate at 21.5%) - 2.0% entry (matches arshkon associate at 3.6%)

This profile is almost identical to arshkon associate and completely unlike arshkon entry (98.4% entry).

docs.title: 2e. Rule-Based Imputation (seniority_imputed)
docs.text:
Group % unknown % mid-senior % associate % entry arshkon entry 81.4% 4.2% 8.1% 6.4% arshkon associate 67.6% 22.9% 4.7% 4.7% asaniczka associate 62.3% 27.6% 6.9% 3.3% arshkon mid-senior — — — —

The rule-based imputer (which ignores native labels) classifies asaniczka associate as 27.6% mid-senior, which closely tracks arshkon associate (22.9%), not arshkon entry (4.2% mid-senior).

docs.title: 2f. seniority_llm Cross-Check
docs.text:
Where seniority_llm is available (thin coverage):

Native group NULL unknown entry associate mid-senior director arshkon entry (n=769) 37 647 30 2 53 0 arshkon associate (n=275) 67 184 13 4 7 0 asaniczka associate (n=2,014) 1,556 340 15 67 34 2

Among rows with non-null, non-unknown seniority_llm: - Arshkon entry: 30 entry / 85 total non-unknown = 35.3% entry, 62.4% mid-senior (surprising) - Arshkon associate: 13 entry / 24 total = 54.2% entry - Asaniczka associate: 15 entry / 118 total = 12.7% entry, 56.8% associate, 28.8% mid-senior

Caution: seniority_llm coverage is extremely thin here (only 24-118 non-null non-unknown rows per group), so these numbers have wide confidence intervals. However, asaniczka associate's 12.7% entry rate is the lowest of the three groups, further arguing against it being a junior proxy.

docs.title: 3. Comparability Verdict
docs.text:
Asaniczka associate behaves like arshkon associate, NOT like arshkon entry. Evidence:

Signal Closer to entry? Closer to associate? Closer to mid-senior? Median YOE No (4.0 vs 3.0 for entry) Yes (3.0 for assoc) Partially (5.0 for mid) Mean YOE No (4.53 vs 4.12) Closest (3.85) Getting close (5.56) Title overlap (row-weighted) No (38.1%) Partial (32.4%) Highest (43.5%) seniority_final profile No (2.0% entry) Yes (72.0% assoc) Partial (25.9% mid) seniority_imputed profile No (3.3% entry) Yes (6.9% assoc) Closest to assoc Junior title cues Inconclusive — —

Decision: Asaniczka associate is classified as "lower-mid" — a mix of associate and lower mid-senior, NOT a junior proxy. It should NOT be pooled with entry-level for any analysis.

Practical consequence for entry-level trend analysis: - The only 2024 entry-level baseline is arshkon: 769 SWE rows (seniority_native=entry) or 848 (seniority_final=entry). - Asaniczka provides zero usable entry-level rows for the primary comparison. - seniority_llm would resolve this in principle (by applying uniform labels to asaniczka descriptions), but at 2.6% known coverage for asaniczka SWE it currently yields only 39 entry-level asaniczka rows — too few to materially expand the baseline.

docs.title: By seniority column and source
docs.text:
Source Seniority column Entry N Has YOE Has desc_llm Has metro Has industry Has ghost_llm arshkon seniority_native 769 475 764 526 769 732 arshkon seniority_final 848 488 843 583 843 755 arshkon seniority_llm 84 54 83 47 82 84 asaniczka seniority_native 0 — — — — — asaniczka seniority_final 129 55 121 98 0 6 asaniczka seniority_llm 39 29 39 28 0 39 scraped seniority_native 3,972 1,552 820 3,056 3,971 707 scraped seniority_final 4,656 1,595 966 3,554 4,655 760 scraped seniority_llm 180 47 179 112 180 180

Key bottlenecks for entry-level analysis: - 2024 baseline: 769 arshkon entry rows (seniority_native) is the binding constraint. This supports descriptive statistics but limits the power of any entry-level regression. - 2026 text quality: Only 820 of 3,972 scraped entry rows have description_core_llm (20.6%). Text-dependent entry-level comparisons are limited to this subset. - LLM seniority: seniority_llm yields only 84 + 39 + 180 = 303 entry rows total across all sources. Not viable as primary.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Asaniczka associate YOE is HIGHER than arshkon entry, not lower. Mean 4.53 vs 4.12, median 4.0 vs 3.0. If anything, asaniczka associate skews more experienced than arshkon entry. This is the opposite of what a "junior proxy" assumption would predict.

  2. Arshkon entry has surprisingly high YOE. Median 3.0 years for postings labeled "entry" on LinkedIn. This suggests either (a) LinkedIn's entry label is generous, or (b) YOE requirements in entry-level postings were already inflated in 2024. Compare with scraped entry at median 2.0 — this could be a genuine temporal shift or a source artifact.

  3. seniority_llm disagrees with seniority_native for arshkon entry. Among the 85 arshkon entry rows where seniority_llm is non-null and non-unknown, 53 are classified as mid-senior (62.4%). The LLM's explicit-signal-only approach apparently detects senior signals in many postings that LinkedIn labeled as entry. This means either LinkedIn's entry label is noisy, or the LLM is miscalibrated. This warrants investigation before relying on seniority_llm.

  4. The 129 asaniczka "entry" rows in seniority_final are imputation artifacts. Since asaniczka has zero native entry labels, these 129 entry rows come from rule-based title/description pattern matching. They have 0% industry coverage (asaniczka-wide gap) and only 4.7% ghost_llm coverage.

  5. seniority_final upclassifies ~26% of asaniczka associate to mid-senior. This is consistent with the YOE evidence that asaniczka associate includes a substantial chunk of what would be mid-senior elsewhere.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• YOE extraction is available for only 56-67% of rows in each seniority group, so YOE-based comparisons represent the subset with extractable mentions.
  • Title cue analysis relies on regex patterns and may miss unconventional phrasing.
  • seniority_llm comparisons use very small sample sizes (24-118 labeled rows per group) and should be treated as directional only.
  • The comparability audit uses SWE rows with default filters only. Results may differ for SWE-adjacent or control groups.

docs.title: Recommended Usage Rules
docs.text:
1. Entry-level 2024 baseline: Use arshkon seniority_native=entry (n=769) as primary. Do not pool asaniczka.
  2. Seniority primary variable: Use seniority_final (best coverage, 80-100% known). Report seniority_native and seniority_llm (where available) as ablation.
  3. Asaniczka associate: Treat as "associate/lower-mid" in seniority analyses. Do not reclassify as junior.
  4. Cross-period entry share: Report arshkon-only 2024 baseline alongside any pooled estimate. Flag directional discrepancy.
  5. seniority_llm caveat: Investigate the 62.4% mid-senior rate for arshkon native-entry rows before using seniority_llm for any entry-level analysis.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: The choice of seniority operationalization flips the direction of the RQ1 entry-level trend. Using arshkon-only as the 2024 baseline, all rule-based variants (seniority_final, seniority_native, seniority_final_highconf) show a decrease in entry-level share from 2024 to 2026 (~21% to ~14%). Pooling asaniczka into 2024 (where it has near-zero entry labels) reverses this to an increase (3.6% to 14.2%). The seniority_llm column -- which can label asaniczka rows -- shows entry share increasing from 6.4% (asaniczka) / 16.7% (arshkon) to 27.7% (scraped), but on very small sample sizes (504-649 labeled non-unknown rows per source). This directional instability is the single most important finding for RQ1 credibility.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Population: LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok, is_swe=true rows (~63K total; arshkon 5,019; asaniczka 23,213; scraped 35,062).
  • Cross-tabulations: seniority_native vs seniority_final, seniority_imputed vs seniority_final, seniority_native vs seniority_imputed (where both non-null/non-unknown). Also seniority_llm vs seniority_final and seniority_llm vs seniority_native where labeled.
  • Agreement metrics: Simple agreement rate and Cohen's kappa for each pair.
  • Per-class accuracy: Rule-based imputed vs native as pseudo-ground truth, separately for arshkon and scraped.
  • Junior share: Computed under 6 operationalizations: seniority_final, seniority_native, seniority_final_highconf, seniority_final_POOLED_2024, seniority_llm (arshkon 2024), seniority_llm (asaniczka 2024).

docs.title: 1. Cross-tabulation agreement
docs.text:
Pair Agreement Kappa N native vs final 0.956 0.841 55,734 imputed vs final 0.951 0.715 37,726 native vs imputed 0.876 0.354 33,584 llm vs final 0.744 0.468 1,766 llm vs native 0.794 0.507 1,503

Interpretation: Native and final agree very well (kappa 0.84, near-perfect), as expected since final backfills from native. Imputed vs final is also high because imputed feeds final. The real diagnostic is native vs imputed: kappa only 0.354 (fair agreement), indicating that title-keyword rules have substantial disagreement with platform labels. LLM vs native/final kappa is moderate (0.47-0.51) -- driven by the LLM's explicit-signal-only design classifying many rows as unknown that native labels as mid-senior.

docs.title: 2. Rule-based classifier accuracy (imputed vs native)
docs.text:
Arshkon SWE (1,706 rows with both):

Class Precision Recall F1 Support entry 0.49 0.34 0.40 143 associate 0.12 0.15 0.13 89 mid-senior 0.92 0.96 0.94 1,428 director 0.40 0.21 0.28 19 Overall accuracy 0.84 1,706

Scraped SWE (15,727 rows with both):

Class Precision Recall F1 Support entry 0.46 0.54 0.50 972 associate 0.07 0.17 0.09 490 mid-senior 0.93 0.92 0.93 13,382 director 0.40 0.22 0.28 410 Overall accuracy 0.82 15,727

Key finding: No temporal instability. Arshkon and scraped show nearly identical overall accuracy (84% vs 82%) and very similar per-class patterns. Both periods have the same weaknesses: poor associate classification (F1 0.09-0.13, many misclassified as mid-senior), entry recall around 34-54%, and reasonable mid-senior performance. The classifier is temporally stable but uniformly poor at distinguishing associate from mid-senior.

docs.title: 3. Asaniczka seniority profile
docs.text:
Column Entry Associate Mid-senior Unknown seniority_native 0 2,014 21,199 0 seniority_final 129 1,451 21,542 0 seniority_llm (labeled) 39 69 498 2,164

Asaniczka native has literally zero entry-level labels -- only associate and mid-senior. seniority_final recovers 129 entry-level labels via title keywords, but this is an extremely thin 0.56% rate. seniority_llm labels 39 asaniczka rows as entry out of 613 non-unknown, yielding a 6.4% entry rate -- substantially higher than rule-based but still below arshkon's rate. The LLM finds genuine entry-level signals in asaniczka that native labels miss entirely.

docs.title: 4. RQ1 junior share by operationalization
docs.text:
Primary comparison: arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026 (combined Mar + Apr):

Operationalization 2024 Entry Share 2026 Entry Share Direction seniority_final (arshkon only) 21.0% 14.2% DECREASE seniority_native (arshkon only) 22.3% 13.7% DECREASE seniority_final_highconf (arshkon only) 21.4% 14.4% DECREASE seniority_final POOLED 2024 3.6% 14.2% INCREASE seniority_llm (arshkon) 16.7% 27.7% INCREASE seniority_llm (asaniczka) 6.4% 27.7% INCREASE

Critical observations: 1. All three rule-based operationalizations agree when using arshkon-only 2024 baseline: entry share decreased from ~21% to ~14%, a drop of ~7 percentage points. 2. Pooling asaniczka into 2024 reverses the direction to an apparent increase. This confirms the documented artifact. 3. seniority_llm shows the opposite direction from rule-based: entry share increases from 16.7% to 27.7%. However, this is based on very small samples (504 non-unknown arshkon, 649 non-unknown scraped). The LLM's high unknown rate (~82%) means only explicit-signal rows survive, which may not be representative.

docs.title: 5. seniority_final_source composition by source
docs.text:
Source title_keyword native_backfill unknown Other arshkon 37.7% 39.7% 19.6% 3.1% asaniczka 62.5% 37.5% 0% 0% scraped 45.7% 45.3% 6.4% 2.6%

Asaniczka resolution is entirely split between title_keyword and native_backfill, with no unknown rows (since native is always available). Scraped has a much lower unknown rate (6.4%) than arshkon (19.6%), likely because scraped SWE titles are more keyword-rich.

docs.title: Surprises and Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. The LLM seniority column disagrees with all rule-based variants on the RQ1 direction. This is not a data artifact -- it reflects a genuine methodological difference. The LLM's explicit-signal-only design produces high unknown rates and small labeled samples, making its entry-share estimates unstable. But it also finds entry-level signals that native labels miss (especially in asaniczka).

  2. Associate classification is catastrophically bad. Across both periods, the rule-based classifier has F1 of 0.09-0.13 for associate-level roles. Most associates are misclassified as mid-senior. This means any analysis that distinguishes associate from mid-senior using rule-based labels is unreliable.

  3. Scraped data has a much higher entry-level share in seniority_final (13.3%) than arshkon (16.9%). But when measured by seniority_native, both have similar magnitudes (22.3% vs 14.0%). The 2026 native entry-level count is actually substantial: 3,972 scraped entry-level postings vs 769 arshkon entry-level postings. Entry-level SWE postings are NOT vanishing in absolute terms.

  4. Intern labels are present in native data but collapse into other categories in seniority_final. Native shows 20 interns (arshkon) and 585 interns (scraped), but seniority_final maps these to entry. The scraped data has ~14x more intern-labeled postings, suggesting either a genuine growth in intern postings or different LinkedIn labeling behavior in 2026.

  5. seniority_llm's unknown rate is ~82% even for labeled rows. This means the LLM applies an extremely conservative explicit-signal threshold. Only ~18% of SWE postings have unambiguous seniority signals in their text/title. The unlabeled rows may systematically differ from labeled ones, creating selection bias.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
1. Sample sizes for seniority_llm are dangerously small. Only 504 arshkon and 649 scraped SWE rows have non-unknown LLM labels. These are not large enough for reliable share estimates, especially for minority classes like director.

  2. Arshkon's entry-level baseline is the binding constraint for rule-based RQ1. All rule-based variants derive their 2024 entry rate primarily from arshkon's ~769-848 entry-level SWE postings. Arshkon is a single Kaggle snapshot from April 2024 with unknown selection methodology.

  3. Period 2026-04 has appeared in the scraped data (8,565 mid-senior, 1,350 entry, etc.), indicating the scraper has been running into April 2026. The 2026 estimates should clarify whether they pool March and April scraped data.

  4. Native seniority labels in scraped data include executive (44 rows) and intern (585 rows) that have no mapping path in rule-based columns. These collapse into other categories or unknown in seniority_final.

docs.title: Recommendation: Which seniority operationalization to use
docs.text:
Analysis Type Recommended Rationale RQ1 entry-level share trend seniority_native, arshkon-only 2024 as primary; seniority_final as sensitivity Avoids the asaniczka contamination problem. seniority_native is highest quality where available. Cross-sectional comparison (single period) seniority_final Best coverage within a period. Unknown rate is manageable for scraped (6.4%). Entry-level text analysis seniority_native = entry OR seniority_final = entry Maximizes the entry-level sample while maintaining label quality. Senior archetype analysis seniority_final (mid-senior) Abundant and reliable; mid-senior classification F1 ~0.94. Ablation / robustness Report all 4 variants Agreement strengthens findings; disagreement is a critical flag. seniority_llm Cross-check only until larger budget run Sample too small for primary estimates. Valuable for validating rule-based trends on the subset where both exist.

Bottom line for RQ1: The entry-level share trend is sensitive to operationalization. Using rule-based labels with arshkon-only 2024 baseline: entry share decreased from ~21% to ~14%. Using seniority_llm: entry share increased from ~17% to ~28%. These directional disagreements must be prominently reported. The most defensible claim is that entry-level share is in the 10-20% range across periods, with the direction of change uncertain pending a larger LLM classification budget.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Run a larger LLM classification budget to increase seniority_llm coverage beyond 9,659 labeled SWE rows. The current labeled count provides only ~1,766 non-unknown rows, insufficient for reliable trend estimation.
  2. Report all 4 seniority variants for any entry-level metric in downstream analyses. Flag directional disagreements prominently.
  3. Do not pool asaniczka with arshkon for entry-level metrics using rule-based labels. This is confirmed to flip the trend direction.
  4. Investigate the intern/executive native labels in scraped data -- are these a genuine LinkedIn labeling change or a scraper artifact?
  5. Consider a hybrid approach: use seniority_llm where labeled, fall back to seniority_native for remaining rows. This maximizes both quality and coverage.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: The SWE classifier is reliable for its core regex tier (91.4% of SWE rows, mostly high-confidence pattern matches) but has meaningful quality issues at the margins. The LLM cross-check estimates a 13.5% false-positive rate (rule says SWE, LLM says NOT_SWE) and an 8.5% false-negative rate (rule says not-SWE, LLM says SWE). The title_lookup_llm tier is the weakest link, with an elevated FP rate. QA/test roles are the primary source of contamination in the SWE sample: 23 of 50 borderline SWE titles (46%) contain QA, test, automation, or firmware keywords. Zero dual-flag violations exist. Boundary roles (ML Engineer, Data Engineer, DevOps, AI Engineer) are classified consistently across sources/periods, indicating no temporal classification drift.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Population: LinkedIn, English, date_flag=ok rows (1,344,441 total; 63,294 SWE; 23,366 SWE-adjacent; 152,211 control).
  • Tier breakdown: Counted SWE and non-SWE rows by swe_classification_tier.
  • Borderline SWE sample: 50 rows with swe_confidence 0.3-0.7 or tier=title_lookup_llm, reviewed for quality.
  • Borderline non-SWE sample: 50 rows with "engineer", "developer", or "software" in title but is_swe=False.
  • FP/FN estimation: Cross-tabulated rule-based is_swe vs swe_classification_llm where available (36,057 rows).
  • Dual-flag check: Verified (is_swe + is_swe_adjacent + is_control) never exceeds 1.
  • Boundary-case analysis: Tracked SWE classification rates for key straddler roles across sources.

docs.title: 1. SWE classification tier breakdown
docs.text:
SWE rows (is_swe=True): 63,294 total

Tier Count % regex 57,879 91.4% title_lookup_llm 4,459 7.0% embedding_high 956 1.5%

Non-SWE rows with a classification tier:

Tier Count % of non-SWE unresolved 1,143,062 89.2% regex (excluded patterns) 125,281 9.8% embedding_adjacent 7,493 0.6% title_lookup_llm 5,311 0.4%

91.4% of SWE classifications come from high-confidence regex matching. The remaining 8.6% (title_lookup_llm + embedding_high) are lower-confidence and account for most of the false-positive risk.

docs.title: 2. Borderline SWE quality assessment
docs.text:
Of 50 sampled borderline SWE postings (all title_lookup_llm tier, confidence 0.90):

  • 23/50 (46%) have suspicious titles: QA Engineer (6), Automation Engineer (5), Test Engineer (6), Firmware Engineer (2), Data Science Engineer (1), MLOps Engineer (2), Azure Lead Engineer (1).
  • Many QA/test roles write test code but do not write production software. These are borderline by any reasonable definition.
  • The title_lookup_llm tier assigns uniform 0.90 confidence, providing no gradation for borderline cases.

Assessment: The QA/test engineer contamination is the largest source of classification noise. Depending on the research definition of SWE (does writing test code count?), somewhere between 20-46% of borderline SWE labels could be considered false positives. For a labor market study focused on software development roles, QA roles with no software development responsibilities should arguably be SWE-adjacent rather than SWE.

docs.title: 3. Borderline non-SWE quality assessment
docs.text:
Of 50 sampled non-SWE rows with "engineer"/"developer"/"software" in the title:

  • Only 3/50 (6%) appeared to be genuine SWE false negatives (e.g., roles that involve writing production software but were missed).
  • Most non-SWE titles with these keywords are correctly excluded: Structural Engineer, Civil Engineer, Mechanical Engineer, Sales Engineer, Electrical Engineer, Project Engineer, Instrumentation Engineer, etc.
  • Several borderline cases are correctly classified as SWE-adjacent rather than SWE: "Software Engineering MTS", "Continuous Integration Development Engineer", "Sr. Network Engineer".

Assessment: The false-negative rate is low for clear SWE roles. The classifier correctly excludes non-software engineering disciplines.

docs.title: 4. SWE-adjacent and control profiles
docs.text:
Top SWE-adjacent titles: Network Engineer (1,359), Quality Engineer (1,228), Data Scientist (1,199), Data Analyst (1,011), Architect (538), Security Engineer (373), Solutions Architect (351), Windows Engineer (333), Systems Administrator (256). These are clearly distinct from core SWE roles -- they involve technical work but not primarily writing/maintaining software.

Top control titles: Accountant (7,014), Registered Nurse (3,730), Electrical Engineer (2,997), Mechanical Engineer (2,243), Financial Analyst (2,226), Accounting Manager (2,160). The control group spans both technical (EE, ME) and non-technical (accounting, nursing) occupations for comparison.

SWE-adjacent tier distribution: regex (10,812), embedding_adjacent (7,493), title_lookup_llm (5,061).

docs.title: 5. False-positive and false-negative rates (LLM cross-check)
docs.text:
Where both rule-based and LLM labels are available (36,057 rows):

LLM: SWE LLM: SWE_ADJACENT LLM: NOT_SWE Rule: SWE (9,659) 7,493 (77.6%) 861 (8.9%) 1,305 (13.5%) Rule: not-SWE (26,398) 2,236 (8.5%) 3,922 (14.9%) 20,240 (76.7%)

Estimated FP rate: 13.5% (rule=SWE, LLM=NOT_SWE). An additional 8.9% are reclassified to SWE_ADJACENT. Estimated FN rate: 8.5% (rule=not-SWE, LLM=SWE).

FP rate by tier:

Tier SWE (agree) SWE_ADJACENT NOT_SWE (disagree) Total FP rate regex 7,042 545 1,095 8,682 12.6% title_lookup_llm 378 274 186 838 22.2% embedding_high 73 42 24 139 17.3%

title_lookup_llm has the highest FP rate (22.2%), followed by embedding_high (17.3%). Even regex has 12.6% disagreement with LLM, though some of this may be LLM error rather than rule error.

docs.title: 6. Dual-flag violations
docs.text:
Zero violations. No row has more than one of (is_swe, is_swe_adjacent, is_control) set to True.

1,105,570 rows (82.2%) have none of the three flags set (general LinkedIn postings not in any study category).

docs.title: 7. Boundary-case temporal consistency
docs.text:
SWE classification rates for key boundary roles across sources:

Role Arshkon SWE% Asaniczka SWE% Scraped SWE% Temporal Drift? ML Engineer 90.9% 96.7% 93.8% No Data Engineer 91.6% 85.5% 93.2% Minor (+7.7pp arshkon vs asaniczka) DevOps 97.7% 99.3% 99.3% No Platform Engineer 90.7% 86.3% 85.2% Minor drift down (-5.5pp) Cloud Engineer 98.1% 96.2% 91.2% Moderate drift down (-6.9pp) AI Engineer 87.9% 98.6% 85.7% Moderate (-12.9pp asaniczka to scraped)

Key finding: No catastrophic temporal drift. Most roles are stable within +/-5pp. AI Engineer shows the largest instability: 98.6% SWE in asaniczka (small N=72) vs 85.7% in scraped (N=1,302). This is likely driven by the emergence of "AI Engineer" as a distinct non-SWE role category in 2026 (e.g., prompt engineering, AI evaluation roles that do not write software). Cloud Engineer similarly drops from 98.1% to 91.2%, possibly as cloud infrastructure roles diversify.

docs.title: 8. SWE confidence distribution
docs.text:
Confidence bin SWE count Non-SWE count 0.0-0.3 0 1,016,355 0.3-0.5 0 76,153 0.5-0.7 0 50,554 0.7-0.85 0 18,305 0.85-0.95 5,363 5,311 0.95-1.0 57,931 114,469

The classifier uses an effective 0.85 threshold: all SWE rows have confidence >= 0.85. The 0.85-0.95 bin contains 5,363 SWE and 5,311 non-SWE rows, indicating this band is essentially a coin flip. Above 0.95, the ratio shifts heavily toward correct classification.

docs.title: Surprises and Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. QA/test roles are the dominant source of SWE classification noise. 46% of borderline SWE titles are QA, test, or automation roles. This is a substantive research decision (should QA engineers count as SWE?), not just a technical classification error.

  2. The LLM finds 2,236 SWE rows among rule-classified non-SWE postings. These are not in the current SWE sample. If the FN rate of 8.5% holds for the full non-SWE population, approximately 100K+ rows may be misclassified as non-SWE. However, the LLM sample is not random -- it is drawn from the LLM-selected frame which may over-represent borderline cases.

  3. The title_lookup_llm tier's uniform 0.90 confidence is misleading. All sampled borderline SWE rows have exactly 0.90 confidence, suggesting the tier assigns a fixed score rather than a calibrated probability. This means confidence cannot distinguish good from bad title_lookup_llm classifications.

  4. "AI Engineer" classification is becoming less stable over time. In 2024 asaniczka data, 98.6% of AI Engineer titles were classified as SWE. In 2026 scraped data, this drops to 85.7%. The emergence of non-coding AI roles (prompt engineering, AI ethics, AI operations) is creating a new boundary that did not exist at the time of the classifier's design.

  5. 1.1 million rows have no classification flag at all (not SWE, not adjacent, not control). These are the general job posting population. This is expected but worth noting -- 82% of the filtered dataset is background.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
1. FP/FN rates are estimated from LLM cross-check, which itself has unknown error rates. The true FP/FN rates may differ.
  2. LLM labels are available for only 9,659 SWE rows (15.3% of SWE population). The FP/FN estimates assume this subset is representative.
  3. Borderline samples are not strictly random -- they oversample title_lookup_llm tier postings. The 46% suspicious-title rate applies to this borderline set, not to all SWE rows.
  4. The regex-tier FP rate of 12.6% seems high for a pattern-matching approach. This may include LLM classification errors rather than regex errors (e.g., the LLM may incorrectly classify some genuine SWE roles as NOT_SWE).

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
1. The current SWE classification is adequate for primary analyses. 91.4% of SWE labels come from high-confidence regex, and overall accuracy is reasonable. The ~13.5% FP rate is a known limitation that should be reported but does not invalidate the sample.

  2. For sensitivity analyses, consider excluding title_lookup_llm tier (removes 4,459 rows / 7% of SWE) to test whether findings are robust to the highest-FP-rate tier.

  3. QA/test contamination should be acknowledged in the methods section. Consider creating a "core SWE" subsample that excludes QA/test titles for robustness checks.

  4. Monitor AI Engineer classification over time. As AI roles diversify, the current regex patterns may need updating to distinguish coding AI engineers from non-coding AI roles.

  5. Do not use swe_confidence for filtering within tiers -- the title_lookup_llm tier assigns uniform 0.90, making confidence useless for separating good from bad cases within that tier.

docs.title: Action Items
docs.text:
1. Report the 13.5% FP rate in any analysis that uses the SWE sample. Frame it as a known limitation.
  2. Run a sensitivity check excluding title_lookup_llm tier rows from downstream analyses.
  3. Create a "QA-excluded SWE" subsample for analyses where the distinction between test code and production code matters.
  4. Cross-validate boundary role classification for AI Engineer in future pipeline runs -- this is the most temporally unstable classification.
  5. Flag the uniform 0.90 confidence issue for title_lookup_llm as a pipeline improvement opportunity.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The three datasets differ substantially on multiple dimensions, but within-2024 calibration reveals that much of the divergence reflects collection-method artifacts rather than temporal change. The two 2024 sources (arshkon vs asaniczka) already show significant differences on description length (KS=0.18), geographic distribution, and company composition -- establishing a high baseline of cross-source variability. Cross-time differences (2024 vs 2026) are larger on most metrics, but only ~1.5-2x the within-2024 baseline, suggesting the real temporal signal is modest relative to source noise.

Most trustworthy cross-time comparisons: Title vocabulary (top-50 Jaccard actually higher for arshkon-scraped than arshkon-asaniczka), YOE distributions within matched title x seniority cells (median YOE is remarkably stable), and the platform labeling stability test (native seniority labels for the same titles are broadly consistent). Least trustworthy: Description length (collection method confound), company overlap (Jaccard ~0.11-0.14 everywhere, reflecting sampling rather than market structure), and raw seniority distributions (dominated by the asaniczka entry-level labeling gap).

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Population: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true
  • Pairwise comparisons across three sources: arshkon (2024-04, n=5,019), asaniczka (2024-01, n=23,213), scraped (2026, n=35,062)
  • Statistical tests: KS tests for continuous distributions, chi-squared with Cramer's V for categorical distributions, Jaccard similarity for set overlap
  • Seniority ablation: all analyses repeated with seniority_llm, seniority_native, seniority_final, and seniority_imputed

docs.title: 1. Description Length
docs.text:
Comparison KS (full desc) KS (core) Cohen's d (full) Arshkon vs Asaniczka (within-2024) 0.184 0.050 -0.35 Arshkon vs Scraped (cross-time) 0.339 0.335 -0.84 Asaniczka vs Scraped (cross-time) 0.212 0.379 -0.52

Median description lengths: arshkon 2,954 chars, asaniczka 3,825, scraped 5,175. The scraped 2026 descriptions are ~57% longer in median than the arshkon 2024 baseline. This is a large effect (Cohen's d = -0.84).

Artifact assessment: The within-2024 KS of 0.18 shows that different Kaggle datasets from the same era already differ substantially. The cross-time KS is 1.84x the within-2024 baseline, suggesting roughly half the observed difference may be real (longer postings in 2026) and half is collection-method noise. Core-length KS is much smaller within-2024 (0.05) but larger cross-time (0.34-0.38), suggesting the boilerplate removal step normalizes within-era artifacts but the real content difference in 2026 is genuine.

docs.title: 2. Company Overlap
docs.text:
Comparison Jaccard (all) Shared Jaccard (top 50) Shared Arshkon vs Asaniczka 0.114 741/6,503 0.136 12/88 Arshkon vs Scraped 0.117 914/7,834 0.220 18/82 Asaniczka vs Scraped 0.136 1,366/10,030 0.163 14/86

Company overlap is low across all pairs (~11-14% Jaccard), with the within-2024 pair showing essentially the same overlap as cross-time pairs. This indicates that company sampling is the dominant source of variation, not temporal change. The job posting market has a very long tail of companies.

Notable: Top-50 Jaccard is actually highest for arshkon vs scraped (0.22), suggesting the largest employers are more stable across time than across collection methods. Asaniczka's top companies are dominated by aggregators (Recruiting from Scratch, ClearanceJobs, ClickJobs.io), which inflates its apparent company diversity.

docs.title: 3. Geographic Distribution
docs.text:
Comparison Chi-squared Cramer's V Arshkon vs Asaniczka (within-2024) 224.8 0.108 Arshkon vs Scraped (cross-time) 267.3 0.090 Asaniczka vs Scraped (cross-time) 1,611.0 0.187

All pairwise differences are statistically significant, but the within-2024 Cramer's V (0.11) is comparable to the arshkon-scraped V (0.09). The asaniczka-scraped difference is larger (V=0.19), likely driven by asaniczka's different search query design.

Key state-level shifts: California share increases from 20-21% (2024) to 25% (2026). Washington increases from 5-6% to 8%. Florida and Ohio drop. These could reflect real geographic concentration of SWE hiring in tech hubs, or differential scraper coverage.

docs.title: Entry-level share across operationalizations
docs.text:
Variable Arshkon 2024 Asaniczka 2024 Scraped 2026 seniority_llm (primary) 16.7% (84/504) 6.4% (39/613) 27.7% (180/649) seniority_native 22.3% (769/3,444) 0.0% (0/23,213) 13.7% (3,972/29,077) seniority_final 21.0% (848/4,035) 0.6% (129/23,213) 14.2% (4,656/32,808) seniority_imputed 6.7% (151/2,238) 1.3% (218/16,151) 6.7% (1,288/19,337)

CRITICAL FINDING -- directional disagreement across operationalizations:

  • seniority_llm shows entry share increasing from 16.7% (arshkon 2024) to 27.7% (scraped 2026) -- a +11 pp increase
  • seniority_native shows entry share decreasing from 22.3% to 13.7% -- a -8.6 pp decrease
  • seniority_final similarly shows a decrease from 21.0% to 14.2% -- a -6.8 pp decrease
  • seniority_imputed shows no change (6.7% in both periods)

This is the most important finding in the task. The direction of the entry-level trend depends on the seniority operationalization. The LLM classifier (which uses uniform explicit-signal-only logic) sees an increase; the native platform labels see a decrease. This could indicate: 1. LinkedIn's platform labeling has shifted (more roles that the LLM considers entry are being labeled mid-senior natively) 2. The LLM classifier is more generous with entry-level labels than the native platform 3. The LLM labeled sample is small (504 / 613 / 649 rows with known seniority) and may not be representative

Small-N warning for seniority_llm: The LLM-labeled entry counts are 84, 39, and 180. These are too small for confident trend estimates. The seniority_native and seniority_final results are based on 10-60x more observations.

docs.title: Chi-squared tests
docs.text:
Variable Comparison Cramer's V seniority_final Arshkon vs Asaniczka 0.391 seniority_final Arshkon vs Scraped 0.083 seniority_final Asaniczka vs Scraped 0.252 seniority_llm Arshkon vs Asaniczka 0.223 seniority_llm Arshkon vs Scraped 0.207 seniority_llm Asaniczka vs Scraped 0.319

The within-2024 seniority_final Cramer's V (0.39) is much larger than the arshkon-scraped V (0.08), confirming the asaniczka entry-level gap dominates seniority comparisons. For seniority_llm, the within-2024 V (0.22) is comparable to arshkon-scraped (0.21), suggesting the LLM variable partially resolves this artifact but introduces its own cross-source variability.

docs.title: 5. Title Vocabulary
docs.text:
Comparison Jaccard (all) Jaccard (top 50) Arshkon vs Asaniczka 0.048 0.389 Arshkon vs Scraped 0.030 0.429 Asaniczka vs Scraped 0.040 0.266

Full-title Jaccard is very low (~3-5%) reflecting the enormous long tail of unique titles. But top-50 Jaccard is substantial (27-43%), and importantly the arshkon-scraped top-50 overlap (0.43) is the highest of all pairs. This indicates that the core SWE title vocabulary is stable across time.

12,971 titles appear uniquely in scraped (not in either 2024 source). Many contain AI-specific language ("ai and distributed systems backend engineer", "genai python systems engineer", "ai data engineer") or consulting-firm standardized formats with level suffixes ("- manager", "- consultant", "- director"). 8,316 titles appear in 2024 but not in scraped.

docs.title: 6. Industry Comparison (Arshkon vs Scraped)
docs.text:
The largest industry shift: "IT Services and IT Consulting" dropped from 36.3% to 16.2%, while "Software Development" increased from 23.6% to 37.5%. "Technology, Information and Internet" doubled from 3.7% to 7.8%. Chi-squared: 1,294 (p < 1e-267), Cramer's V = 0.22.

Artifact assessment: This shift could reflect LinkedIn reclassifying industries, companies changing their LinkedIn profiles, or a genuine recomposition of the SWE hiring market away from IT consulting toward product companies. The absence of asaniczka industry data prevents within-2024 calibration.

docs.title: 7. Artifact Diagnostic Summary
docs.text: Metric Within-2024 baseline Cross-time signal Trustworthiness Description length KS=0.18 KS=0.34 (1.8x) Medium -- real lengthening likely, but magnitude uncertain Company overlap Jaccard=0.11 Jaccard=0.12 Low -- no signal above baseline noise Geographic shares V=0.11 V=0.09-0.19 Medium -- CA/WA increases may be real Seniority (native) V=0.39 (artifact) V=0.08 Medium -- arshkon-scraped is well-behaved; asaniczka confounds everything Seniority (LLM) V=0.22 V=0.21-0.32 Low-Medium -- small N, directional disagreement with native Title vocabulary (top 50) J=0.39 J=0.43 High -- core titles are stable YOE per title/seniority Stable Stable High -- most stable metric across all comparisons

docs.title: 8. Within-2024 Calibration
docs.text:
The within-2024 comparison (arshkon vs asaniczka) establishes that datasets from the same era can diverge substantially: - Description length KS = 0.18 (significant) - Company Jaccard = 0.11 - Geographic Cramer's V = 0.11 - Seniority dominated by asaniczka's missing entry labels

This means any cross-time difference must exceed these baselines to be interpreted as temporal change.

docs.title: 9. Platform Labeling Stability Test
docs.text:
For the top 20 shared titles between arshkon and scraped, the native seniority label distributions are broadly similar but show systematic differences:

  • "software engineer": entry share drops from 16.2% (arshkon) to 20.6% (scraped) -- actually a slight increase on this anchor title
  • "data engineer": entry drops from 22.2% to 14.4%
  • "devops engineer": entry drops from 14.3% to 13.3% (essentially stable)
  • "machine learning engineer": entry increases from 5.4% to 12.9%
  • "embedded software engineer": entry drops from 22.6% to 13.2%
  • "test engineer": entry drops from 36.0% to 31.0%
  • "software developer": entry drops from 37.5% to 26.0%

For the flagship "software engineer" title, entry share slightly increased. But for several other titles, entry share declined. The pattern is mixed, not uniformly in one direction, which argues against a simple platform relabeling artifact.

YOE stability: Within matched title x seniority cells, YOE distributions are remarkably stable. For "software engineer" mid-senior, median YOE is 5.0 in both periods. For "software engineer" entry, median YOE is 2.0 in both periods. This consistency suggests that when a role has the same title and native seniority label, its actual experience requirements have not changed -- the shifts are in composition (which seniority levels are posted for which titles), not content (what each level requires).

Indeed cross-validation: Indeed scraped data (using seniority_imputed, the only available method for Indeed) shows entry share of 7.3%, very close to LinkedIn's seniority_imputed of 6.7%. This similarity across platforms suggests the seniority_imputed baseline is platform-independent and thus not a LinkedIn-specific artifact.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Directional disagreement on entry-level trend is the most consequential finding. seniority_llm shows +11 pp entry increase; seniority_native shows -8.6 pp decrease. This must be resolved before RQ1 analysis can proceed.

  2. Asaniczka's top "companies" are aggregators and data artifacts. "Recruiting from Scratch" (1,699 SWE postings), "ClearanceJobs" (920), and "ClickJobs.io" (483) are not real employers but job aggregators that passed through the canonicalization pipeline. "We are deploying software-defined" (196) and "Commitment Full-time" (189) appear to be description text fragments misclassified as company names.

  3. Description length increased 57% in median (2,954 to 5,175 chars). This is a large, real effect that will bias any text-density metric. All text-based analyses must control for description length.

  4. AI engineer counts exploded. "ai engineer" went from 6 postings in arshkon to 321 in scraped -- a 50x increase. This title barely existed in 2024 and is now a major category.

  5. Top-50 company overlap is highest for the cross-time pair (arshkon-scraped at 0.22 > arshkon-asaniczka at 0.14), suggesting the large employers are more stable across time than the full company distribution.

  6. Industry recomposition is dramatic: "IT Services and IT Consulting" halved its share while "Software Development" grew by 14 pp. If real, this represents a structural shift in which types of companies hire SWEs.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
1. seniority_llm sample sizes are binding constraints. Only 504/613/649 non-unknown labeled rows per source. Trend estimates from these have very wide confidence intervals.
  2. Asaniczka has company name data quality issues. Several "companies" are text fragments, not real employers. Any analysis involving asaniczka company-level grouping should filter for known-good company names.
  3. Remote work flags are 0% across all sources (data artifact, not real). Do not use for any analysis.
  4. Description length differences confound all text-based cross-source comparisons. Use density metrics (per 1K chars) or length-matched samples.
  5. Industry data is available only for arshkon and scraped. The asaniczka gap prevents within-2024 industry calibration.

docs.title: Action Items for Downstream Tasks
docs.text:
1. Resolve the entry-level directional disagreement before proceeding with RQ1. The most promising approach: expand the seniority_llm sample size (currently small) and test whether the disagreement persists. If it does, the analysis must present both findings with appropriate caveats.
  2. All text-density analyses must normalize for description length. The 57% length increase makes raw counts unreliable.
  3. Consider excluding asaniczka from entry-level analyses entirely and relying solely on arshkon as the 2024 baseline -- the entry-level labeling gap makes asaniczka a liability.
  4. Company-level analyses should exclude known aggregator artifacts from asaniczka (Recruiting from Scratch, ClearanceJobs, etc.) or use the is_aggregator flag consistently.
  5. The stable YOE pattern within title x seniority cells is a strong anchor for analysis: use this to argue that composition changes (which seniority levels are posted) rather than content changes (what each level requires) are the primary signal.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text:
The SWE posting market is moderately concentrated but no single company dominates enough to bias aggregate findings. Top-1 company share ranges from 3.3% (arshkon) to 7.3% (asaniczka); top-20 share ranges from 15.8% to 31.4%. Asaniczka is the most concentrated due to aggregator inflation (Recruiting from Scratch alone accounts for 7.3%).

The within-company decomposition is the key analytical result: Using seniority_native (84 overlap companies), the entry-level share declined from 28.7% to 12.4% (-16.3 pp) among companies present in both periods. 90% of this decline is within-company (existing companies posting fewer entry roles), with only 11% from between-company composition effects. This robustly establishes that the entry decline is not an artifact of changing employer composition -- the same companies are posting fewer junior roles. The seniority_final operationalization confirms this (89% within-company).

However, seniority_llm has insufficient overlap company coverage (only 3 companies with >=3 known-seniority postings in both periods) to perform a meaningful decomposition. The LLM-labeled decomposition should not be interpreted.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Population: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true
  • Concentration metrics: HHI, Gini coefficient, top-k share, Lorenz curves
  • Within-company decomposition: shift-share analysis on companies with >=5 known-seniority postings in both arshkon and scraped
  • Sensitivity: company-capped analysis (max 10 postings per company)
  • Aggregator analysis: comparison of aggregator vs direct employer profiles
  • New entrants: companies in scraped with no arshkon match

docs.title: 1. Concentration Metrics
docs.text:
Metric Arshkon (2024-04) Asaniczka (2024-01) Scraped (2026) N companies 2,298 4,946 6,450 N postings 4,974 23,213 35,062 HHI 0.0029 0.0140 0.0040 Gini 0.466 0.710 0.711 Top-1 share 3.3% 7.3% 3.7% Top-5 share 8.3% 21.9% 10.3% Top-10 share 11.2% 26.3% 14.4% Top-20 share 15.8% 31.4% 20.2%

All three datasets have low HHI (well below the 0.15 "moderately concentrated" threshold). Arshkon is the least concentrated; asaniczka is the most concentrated due to aggregator dominance. Scraped falls between them with moderate Gini (0.71) reflecting the long tail of companies.

docs.title: 2. Companies with >3% SWE Share
docs.text:
Only 4 companies exceed the 3% threshold in any dataset:

Source Company Share Arshkon DataAnnotation 3.3% Asaniczka Recruiting from Scratch 7.3% Asaniczka Capital One 7.2% Asaniczka ClearanceJobs 4.0% Scraped Jobs via Dice 3.7%

All four of asaniczka's >3% companies and scraped's sole >3% company are aggregators. DataAnnotation (arshkon's largest) is a data labeling company, not a traditional SWE employer.

docs.title: 3. Within-Company vs Aggregate Decomposition (Core Analytical Output)
seniority_native (84 overlap companies with >=5 postings in both periods)
docs.text:
Metric Arshkon Scraped Change Overlap companies: entry share 28.7% 12.4% -16.3 pp Full-sample: entry share 22.4% 13.7% -8.7 pp

Shift-share decomposition:

Component Effect Share of total Total change -16.31 pp 100% Within-company -14.68 pp 90% Between-company -1.86 pp 11% Interaction +0.23 pp -1%

Interpretation: The within-company decline (-14.7 pp) exceeds the full-sample aggregate decline (-8.7 pp). This means that among companies present in both periods, the entry-level decline is actually larger than the market-wide average. Composition effects (new entrant companies being more junior-friendly) are dampening the signal, not inflating it.

docs.title: seniority_final (100 overlap companies)
docs.text:
Component Effect Share of total Total change -14.73 pp 100% Within-company -13.08 pp 89% Between-company -5.17 pp 35% Interaction +3.51 pp -24%

Confirms the seniority_native finding: 89% of the entry decline is within-company.

docs.title: seniority_llm (NOT USABLE)
docs.text: Only 3 companies had >=3 known-seniority LLM-labeled postings in both periods (total of 11 and 10 postings respectively). The resulting decomposition is meaningless due to extreme sparse coverage. This is a binding limitation of the current LLM budget.

docs.title: 4. Company-Capped Sensitivity (cap = 10 postings/company)
docs.text:
Variable Source Uncapped Capped Delta seniority_llm Arshkon 16.7% 16.5% -0.2 pp seniority_llm Asaniczka 6.4% 7.4% +1.1 pp seniority_llm Scraped 27.7% 24.0% -3.7 pp seniority_native Arshkon 22.3% 20.7% -1.7 pp seniority_native Scraped 13.7% 12.7% -1.0 pp seniority_final Arshkon 21.0% 19.5% -1.5 pp seniority_final Scraped 14.2% 13.8% -0.4 pp

Capping reduces entry shares modestly across all sources and operationalizations. The direction and rough magnitude of trends is preserved. For seniority_native: arshkon drops from 22.3% to 20.7%, scraped drops from 13.7% to 12.7% -- the gap narrows slightly but the decline persists. No single company is driving the results.

For seniority_llm: the cap has a larger effect on scraped (-3.7 pp), suggesting a few companies with many LLM-labeled entry postings are boosting the scraped entry share. Still, the capped scraped rate (24.0%) remains well above capped arshkon (16.5%).

docs.title: 5. Aggregator Analysis
docs.text:
Source Total SWE Aggregator Agg % Arshkon 5,019 591 11.8% Asaniczka 23,213 6,340 27.3% Scraped 35,062 5,026 14.3%

Asaniczka has 2.3x the aggregator rate of arshkon. This is a major source artifact.

Aggregator vs direct employer profiles:

Dimension Aggregator Direct Difference Description length (arshkon median) 1,646 3,133 Aggregators ~47% shorter Description length (scraped median) 3,578 5,385 Aggregators ~34% shorter Median YOE 5.0 5.0 No difference

Aggregator seniority is sharply different from direct: - In arshkon: aggregators are 46.2% entry vs direct at 18.1% entry - In scraped: aggregators are 25.5% entry vs direct at 12.2% entry - Aggregators have consistently higher entry-level share across all periods

Implication: Aggregator prevalence shifts can create artificial seniority composition effects. Asaniczka's high aggregator rate (27.3%) combined with its missing entry labels makes it doubly problematic. Consider running seniority analyses with aggregators excluded for robustness.

Top aggregators by source: - Arshkon: DataAnnotation (163), Apex Systems (52), Motion Recruitment (42) - Asaniczka: Recruiting from Scratch (1,699), Capital One (1,622*), ClearanceJobs (920) - Scraped: Jobs via Dice (1,296), Actalent (166), Motion Recruitment (161)

*Note: Capital One appears as an aggregator in asaniczka (1,622 SWE postings), which is likely a classification issue -- Capital One is a large direct employer, not a staffing agency. This should be investigated.

docs.title: 6. New Entrants
docs.text:
Metric Count Companies in scraped 6,450 Companies in arshkon 2,298 New entrants (scraped only) 5,536 (85.8%) Returning (in both) 914 (14.2%) New entrant posting volume 19,692 / 35,062 = 56.2% Returning posting volume 15,370 / 35,062 = 43.8%

The vast majority (86%) of companies in scraped are "new entrants" not found in arshkon, and they account for 56% of scraped postings. However, much of this reflects arshkon's smaller sample rather than true market entry.

Seniority profile of new vs returning companies (seniority_final):

Company type Entry Associate Mid-senior Director New entrant 15.3% 2.6% 81.0% 1.1% Returning 12.6% 3.6% 82.7% 1.1%

New entrant companies have higher entry share (15.3%) than returning companies (12.6%), a +2.7 pp difference. This is consistent with the within-company decomposition: new entrants are slightly more junior-friendly, which dampens the aggregate decline driven by existing companies cutting entry roles.

Seniority profile (seniority_llm, small N):

Company type Entry Associate Mid-senior Director New entrant 24.1% 2.2% 71.7% 1.9% Returning 32.3% 7.6% 52.1% 8.0%

The LLM labels show a different pattern (returning companies have higher entry share), but with only 361/288 observations, this is too noisy to interpret. The returning company director share of 8.0% vs 1.1% in seniority_final is suspicious and may reflect LLM classification peculiarities.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. Within-company decline is LARGER than aggregate decline. The 16.3 pp within-company drop exceeds the 8.7 pp full-sample drop. New companies entering the market are partially offsetting the decline. This is the opposite of what would be expected if the entry decline were driven by compositional shifts (e.g., more senior-heavy companies entering).

  2. Capital One is classified as an aggregator in asaniczka with 1,622 SWE postings. This is anomalous and may indicate a data quality issue in asaniczka's company name field or in the aggregator detection pipeline.

  3. Aggregators are disproportionately entry-level. In arshkon, aggregators are 46% entry vs 18% for direct employers. This creates a leverage point: changes in aggregator prevalence can shift the aggregate entry rate significantly. The decline in aggregator share from 27.3% (asaniczka) to 14.3% (scraped) mechanically reduces the entry rate even if direct-employer behavior is unchanged.

  4. 86% of scraped companies are "new." This is partly an arshkon sample-size artifact (only 2,298 companies), but it also means the within-company decomposition is based on a relatively narrow overlapping set (914 companies). The 84 companies meeting the >=5 threshold for the decomposition represent only a subset of returning companies.

  5. Asaniczka's concentration is 2-4x higher than other sources. This is almost entirely driven by aggregator postings. After removing aggregators, the three sources would likely have comparable concentration.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
1. seniority_llm overlap is too sparse for decomposition. Only 3 companies have >=3 LLM-labeled known-seniority postings in both periods. The LLM decomposition result should be discarded.
  2. The "new entrant" count is inflated by arshkon's small sample. Many "new" companies likely existed in 2024 but were not captured by arshkon. The 86% "new" rate should not be interpreted as a real market entry rate.
  3. Capital One aggregator classification in asaniczka needs investigation. If this is a pipeline error, it affects the aggregator analysis for asaniczka.
  4. Asaniczka company names include text fragments ("We are deploying software-defined", "Commitment Full-time") that are not real employers. These inflate company counts.

docs.title: Action Items for Downstream Tasks
docs.text:
1. Use the within-company decomposition as an anchor for RQ1. The 90% within-company share of the entry decline (seniority_native) is a robust finding that holds under seniority_final as well. This should be the primary evidence that the entry decline is real and not compositional.
  2. Run all seniority analyses with and without aggregators. The aggregator entry-level skew (46% vs 18% in arshkon) means aggregator prevalence shifts can confound trends.
  3. Expand LLM budget for the decomposition. The 3-company overlap for seniority_llm is a binding constraint. The decomposition cannot be replicated with the primary seniority variable until LLM coverage increases.
  4. Investigate Capital One aggregator classification in asaniczka. If this is a pipeline error, fix it and rerun.
  5. The new-entrant entry share being higher (15.3% vs 12.6%) should be explored further -- are these startups, consulting firms, or specific sectors? This could inform RQ1 about which types of employers are driving the entry-level trend.

docs.title: Headline Finding
docs.text: The sample is geographically representative (r = 0.985 vs BLS OES) and all main aggregate comparisons are well-powered, but entry-level analyses under seniority_llm are severely underpowered (n = 84 arshkon entry, n = 180 scraped entry). The binding constraint for the entire project is entry-level sample size under the LLM seniority variable. Under seniority_final, entry comparisons are adequate (n = 848 vs 4,656), and the observed 2024-to-2026 entry share decline (16.9% -> 13.3%) is already detectable. Metro-level analyses require pooling asaniczka into 2024, which is valid only for non-entry-level metrics unless seniority_llm resolves asaniczka's missing entry labels.

1. Actual Group Sizes
docs.text:
Source Period Total SWE Entry (sf) Mid-Senior (sf) Entry (llm) Mid-Senior (llm) kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 5,019 848 2,924 84 404 kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 23,213 129* 21,542 39 498 scraped 2026-03+ 35,062 4,656 26,805 180 409

LLM classification coverage (SWE, labeled): arshkon 3,133; asaniczka 2,777; scraped 3,749. The LLM-labeled subsample is much smaller than the full SWE set because of budget constraints and the rule_sufficient routing.

docs.title: 3. Entry-Level Share MDE (the central RQ1 metric)
docs.text:
Seniority Variable Comparison n1 n2 Baseline Entry Share MDE (abs) MDE (rel %) seniority_final arshkon vs scraped 5,019 35,062 16.9% 1.6pp 9.4% seniority_final pooled_2024 vs scraped 28,232 35,062 3.5%* 0.4pp 11.8% seniority_llm (labeled) arshkon vs scraped 3,133 3,749 2.7% 1.1pp 40.9% seniority_llm (labeled) pooled_2024 vs scraped 5,910 3,749 2.1% 0.8pp 40.1% seniority_native arshkon vs scraped 3,444 29,077 22.3% 2.1pp 9.4%

Pooled 2024 under seniority_final has an artificially low 3.5% entry rate because asaniczka contributes ~0.6% entry. This is a known measurement artifact.

Key implication: Under seniority_final, the observed entry share decline from arshkon (16.9%) to scraped (13.3%) = 3.6pp is well above the MDE of 1.6pp and is statistically detectable. Under seniority_llm, the very low entry classification rate (~2.7%) and small labeled sample mean the MDE is 40% of the baseline -- only very large relative changes would be detectable.

docs.title: 5. Metro-Level Feasibility
docs.text:
Threshold per period Metros (arshkon & scraped) Metros (pooled_2024 & scraped) >= 30 SWE 22 26 >= 50 SWE 18 26 >= 100 SWE 8 25 >= 200 SWE 3 23

Recommendation: Metro-level analysis requires pooling asaniczka into 2024. With pooled 2024, all 26 scraped metros have >= 50 SWE in both periods. For arshkon-only metro analysis, only 8 metros have >= 100 SWE in both periods (SF Bay Area, Washington DC, Seattle, Dallas-Fort Worth, Boston, LA, Chicago, and Austin).

Top 5 metros by scraped volume: San Francisco Bay Area (4,426), Washington DC (2,527), Seattle (2,005), New York City (1,862), Dallas-Fort Worth (1,755).

Full metro table: exploration/tables/T07/metro_feasibility.csv

docs.title: 6. Company Overlap Panel Feasibility
docs.text:
Threshold Overlapping Companies arshkon Postings scraped Postings >= 1 SWE 914 3,019 15,370 >= 3 SWE 228 1,987 10,113 >= 5 SWE 120 1,598 8,418 >= 10 SWE 46 1,120 5,844

Pooled 2024 vs scraped (>= 3 SWE): 541 companies (13,423 + 15,072 postings). This is a strong panel for T16 within-company analysis.

docs.title: 7. Geographic Representativeness vs BLS OES
docs.text:
State-level correlation between our SWE posting counts and BLS OES May 2023 employment for SOC 15-1252 (Software Developers):

Metric Value N states 51 (all states + DC) Pearson r (raw) 0.985 (p = 2.5e-39) Pearson r (log-log) 0.972 (p = 2.8e-32) Spearman rho 0.969 (p = 2.6e-31)

This exceeds the r > 0.80 target by a wide margin. Our LinkedIn sample is highly representative of the geographic distribution of SWE employment as measured by BLS. The log-log correlation confirms this relationship holds across the full range of state sizes (from Wyoming with 670 BLS employees to California with 228,350).

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/state_bls_correlation.png

Note: BLS OES employment numbers are from the published May 2023 release for SOC 15-1252 (Software Developers). Direct download from BLS was blocked by their CDN; numbers were entered from published tables. The BLS API does not serve OES cross-sectional data as time series.

docs.title: 8. JOLTS Information Sector Context
docs.text:
JOLTS data (seasonally adjusted, series JTS510000000000000JOL) provides hiring-cycle context:

Period JOLTS Info Openings (000s) Our Data 2022 peak average 224K (pre-data) Jan 2024 160K Asaniczka period Apr 2024 87K Arshkon period 2024 full-year average 123K -- Late 2025 (Sep-Dec avg) 109K Closest to scraped Mar 2026 (not yet released) Scraped period

Key observation: Our two 2024 data points straddle a significant JOLTS swing. Asaniczka (Jan 2024) was collected during a relative uptick (160K openings), while arshkon (Apr 2024) was collected near a trough (87K, the lowest single month in 2024). This means:

  1. Comparing asaniczka to scraped captures a different part of the hiring cycle than arshkon to scraped.
  2. The late-2025 JOLTS level (~109K avg) is below Jan 2024 (160K) but above Apr 2024 (87K), so the scraped period is roughly in the middle of the 2024 range.
  3. From the 2022 peak (~224K avg), information-sector openings fell 45% by 2024 -- our data captures the post-peak period, not the peak itself.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/jolts_context.png

docs.title: 9. Industry Distribution
docs.text:
Industry labels are available for 99.2% of arshkon and 100% of scraped SWE (0% for asaniczka). Top industries are consistent across periods:

Rank Arshkon (2024) Scraped (2026) 1 IT Services & IT Consulting (24.4%) Software Development (25.7%) 2 Software Development (15.8%) IT Services & IT Consulting (11.1%) 3 Financial Services (9.5%) Financial Services (8.6%) 4 Staffing and Recruiting (5.5%) Technology, Information & Internet (5.4%) 5 Information Services (3.1%) Defense & Space Manufacturing (3.4%)

The shift from IT Services dominance in arshkon to Software Development dominance in scraped may reflect either real industry composition change or differences in how LinkedIn categorizes employers between the two collection periods. This should be flagged as a potential confound for industry-stratified analyses.

docs.title: 10. Sample Representativeness Framing
docs.text:
What our sample represents: - LinkedIn-posted SWE job openings across 26 major US metros - Three time snapshots: Jan 2024 (asaniczka, large volume), Apr 2024 (arshkon, smaller but richer labels), Mar 2026+ (scraped, daily collection) - Geographic coverage spans all 50 states + DC, with 77.7% of scraped SWE assigned to one of the 26 study metros

What our sample does NOT represent: - All US SWE positions (LinkedIn captures a subset; Indeed, company websites, and internal postings are excluded) - The full SWE labor market (postings != actual employment or hiring) - Non-US labor markets - The universe of tech job titles (our SWE classifier selects a specific occupation slice) - Ghost/phantom postings are included in the count (our ghost assessment flags these but does not remove them)

Population inference framework: Our sample should be described as "a convenience sample of LinkedIn SWE postings across major US metros, highly correlated with BLS state-level SWE employment (r = 0.985)" rather than as a representative sample of the SWE labor market. The geographic representativeness claim is strong; the platform-coverage claim is necessarily limited.

docs.title: Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. The seniority_llm entry-level crisis is worse than anticipated. Only 84 arshkon entry and 180 scraped entry in the labeled LLM subset. This is not just "thin" -- it is below the threshold for any reasonable effect detection. The LLM classifier's explicit-signal-only approach produces ~2.7% entry rate vs seniority_final's ~16.9%. Most entry-level postings apparently lack explicit entry signals in the title/description and get classified as "unknown." This is a fundamental tension: the most methodologically rigorous seniority variable is also the least powerful for the project's central question.

  2. Arshkon was collected during a JOLTS trough. The Apr 2024 JOLTS reading (87K) is the lowest single month in 2024, while Jan 2024 (asaniczka) shows 160K. Any comparison between arshkon and scraped is confounded by this hiring-cycle position. Pooling the two 2024 sources would partially average this out, but the asymmetric timing should be noted in any cross-period comparison.

  3. Industry distribution shifted between arshkon and scraped. IT Services was the dominant industry in arshkon (24.4%) but fell to second place in scraped (11.1%), while Software Development rose from 15.8% to 25.7%. This could represent real market shifts, LinkedIn platform changes, or differences in how employers self-categorize. This is a potential confound for any analysis that assumes stable industry composition across periods.

  4. New York City metro is dramatically underrepresented in arshkon. Only 11 SWE postings vs 1,862 in scraped. This is likely a search-query artifact in the original Kaggle dataset. NYC metro-level comparisons should use asaniczka (55 SWE) or pooled 2024 (66 SWE), but even pooled, this is thin.

  5. Scraped data now spans two periods (2026-03 and 2026-04). The scraped source contains 24,054 SWE from 2026-03 and 11,008 from 2026-04, indicating the scraper has been running for over a month. This provides some within-2026 temporal variation for the scraped period.

docs.title: Data Quality Caveats
docs.text:
• BLS OES numbers are hand-entered from published May 2023 tables because the BLS CDN blocks automated downloads and the BLS public API does not serve OES cross-sectional data as time series. Values should be verified against BLS OES 15-1252 if results are used in publication.
  • JOLTS data is for the Information sector (NAICS 51), not specifically software development. Information sector includes publishing, broadcasting, telecommunications, and data processing -- a broader category than our SWE sample.
  • Power calculations assume independent observations. If postings cluster by company (i.e., one company posts 50 similar positions), the effective sample size is smaller than the nominal count, and MDEs are underestimates. This is especially relevant for the company panel analysis.
  • MDE calculations use standard normal approximations (z-test for proportions, t-test for means). For very small cells (associate seniority_llm = 15, director = 1), these approximations are unreliable and exact tests should be used.

docs.title: Action Items for Downstream Tasks
docs.text:
1. T08 (Entry share trend): Proceed with seniority_final as the primary variable (well-powered). Present seniority_llm as a sensitivity check with explicit caveat about low power. The observed 3.6pp entry share decline under seniority_final is above the MDE (1.6pp).

  2. T09 (Junior scope inflation): YOE within entry-level is feasible under seniority_final (488 arshkon, 1,595 scraped entries with YOE; MDE d = 0.145). Under seniority_llm, this analysis has approximately 84 entry rows with unknown YOE coverage -- likely infeasible.

  3. T10 (Senior archetype shift): Well-powered under seniority_final (2,924 vs 26,805 mid-senior). Keyword prevalence MDE = 1.6pp at baseline 10%. Even under seniority_llm, the 404 vs 409 mid-senior comparison is marginal (MDE d = 0.20) -- can detect medium effects.

  4. T11-T12 (Metro-level analysis): Use pooled 2024 (arshkon + asaniczka) for metro comparisons. All 26 metros are viable at >= 50 threshold. For entry-level metro comparisons, only feasible under seniority_final with arshkon-only (or if seniority_llm gets budget expanded).

  5. T16 (Company panel): 228 companies at >= 3 threshold; 541 with pooled 2024. Well-powered for paired within-company tests (MDE d = 0.186 for 228 companies). Proceed as planned.

  6. LLM budget recommendation: The seniority_llm power gap is the single biggest methodological bottleneck. If LLM budget can be expanded to increase labeled coverage of arshkon SWE from 3,133 to ~5,000 and scraped from 3,749 to ~10,000+, the entry-level seniority_llm cells would grow proportionally and potentially become viable. Alternatively, including rule_sufficient rows (arshkon +1,882, asaniczka +5,094, scraped +3,538) would triple the seniority_llm usable set -- but those rows have null seniority_llm values and would need to fall back to seniority_final, which undermines the purpose of the LLM variable.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Power calculations: Standard two-sample z-test for proportions and t-test for means, at 80% power, alpha = 0.05 (two-sided). MDE_binary computed at p_baseline = 0.30 for the feasibility table, and at actual observed baselines for the entry-share-specific table.
  • Geographic correlation: Pearson r, Pearson r (log-log), and Spearman rho between our state-level SWE posting counts (all sources pooled) and BLS OES May 2023 employment for SOC 15-1252.
  • JOLTS data: Seasonally adjusted series JTS510000000000000JOL from BLS API. Values are in thousands.
  • BLS OES state data: Published May 2023 employment for SOC 15-1252 from bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes151252.htm.
