docs.title: T08 — Distribution profiling & anomaly detection
docs.text: Agent E, Wave 2 (2026-04-10) Script: exploration/scripts/T08_distribution_profiling.py Tables: exploration/tables/T08/ (20 CSVs) Figures: exploration/figures/T08/ (4 PNGs)

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
1. The Gate-1 native-label-quality finding is replicated cleanly (41.3% of arshkon seniority_native = 'entry' rows with extracted YOE have YOE>=5 vs 9.5% in scraped). Label-independent measures (YOE proxy, seniority_best_available of known) agree that entry share is rising, not declining. The "entry decline" under seniority_native/seniority_final with arshkon-only 2024 baseline is a measurement artifact.
  2. The combined seniority_best_available column is not fully comparable across periods because its coverage is structurally driven by LLM frame selection: 99.9% coverage in arshkon (2024), only 34% in asaniczka (2024-01), 21% in scraped (2026). Raw population-level ratios using it are misleading; the "share of known" denominator is the only defensible reading.
  3. The largest single-metric change is core_length (+59%, Cohen d = 0.85) and description_length (+33%, d = 0.57). These effects dwarf the within-2024 calibration noise (d ~0.2-0.3), so they are plausibly real — but the Wave-1 noise floor is non-trivial and cross-source formatting differences contribute substantially.
  4. AI / agentic vocabulary has exploded from near-zero to substantial share: desc_contains_ai 8.7% -> 40.9% (abs +32pp, rel +372%), desc_contains_llm 1.0% -> 10.7% (+931%), desc_contains_agent 0.6% -> 12.8% (+2180%), desc_contains_rag 0.07% -> 4.2% (+5600%), desc_contains_copilot 0.08% -> 4.2% (+5050%). The AI inflection is by far the cleanest cross-period signal.
  5. Surprises: (a) is_remote_inferred is 0.0% everywhere (column appears broken/unused); (b) NYC Metro SWE count 66 (2024 pooled) -> 1862 (2026) — a 28x jump against 1.2x overall growth, almost certainly a scraper geographic-coverage artifact; (c) is_aggregator drops 24.6% -> 14.3%, mostly driven by asaniczka's very high aggregator rate (27%) diluting 2024; (d) industry labelling format changed across sources (2026 uses compound "X, Y, and Z" strings).

docs.title: 1. Sample frame
docs.text:
SWE frame with the default filter (source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true): 63,294 rows.

Period Source Rows 2024-01 kaggle_asaniczka 23,213 2024-04 kaggle_arshkon 5,019 2026-03 scraped 24,054 2026-04 scraped 11,008

Periods are grouped into pooled 2024 (28,232) vs 2026 (35,062) for cross-period comparisons.

docs.title: 2.1 Numeric variables by period (table 01_numeric_summary_by_period.csv)
docs.text:
Variable Stat 2024-01 2024-04 2026-03 2026-04 description_length mean 4,052 3,316 5,203 5,198 description_length median 3,825 2,954 5,158 5,223 core_length mean 2,696 2,738 4,313 4,298 core_length median 2,419 2,504 4,171 4,212 yoe_extracted median 5 5 5 5 yoe_extracted mean 5.59 5.20 4.96 5.05 yoe_coverage (share non-null) — 0.72 0.61 0.69 0.70 seniority_final_confidence mean 0.913 0.721 0.838 0.846

Notes: - description_length: arshkon is substantially shorter than asaniczka at baseline (d ~0.32 between sources within 2024, mid-senior only). Both 2024 sources are substantially shorter than scraped 2026. The 2026-03 and 2026-04 snapshots are essentially identical on this metric (mean diff ~5 chars). That internal consistency of scraped snapshots is reassuring. - core_length: arshkon ≈ asaniczka (means 2,738 vs 2,696) despite the wider gap on description_length. That is, arshkon postings have less boilerplate to strip than asaniczka's, so core_length is a more faithful cross-source metric than description_length. - yoe_extracted: medians are identical at 5 across all four snapshots. Mean drops 0.6 years between 2024 and 2026; the YOE distribution has shifted modestly left even though the median has not. - seniority_final_confidence: arshkon 0.72 is an outlier (lower). This is because arshkon has native labels that produce low-confidence rule outputs by construction — not a real quality gap.

docs.title: 2.2 Numeric variables by period × seniority (combined) (table 02_numeric_by_period_seniority.csv)
docs.text: Worth noting: - Mid-senior description_length climbs 4,219 (2024 pooled) -> 5,244 (2026). Entry postings climb from 3,352 (2024) -> 5,048 (2026). Entry postings in 2026 are now ~1.5x their 2024 length, and are longer than 2024 mid-senior postings (3,352 < 5,048 < 5,244). The "junior scope inflation" thesis has a descriptive-text-length footprint.

docs.title: 2.3 Seniority distribution ablation (table 03_seniority_distribution_by_variant.csv)
docs.text: See §5 for the full entry-share ablation. The raw variant-level distributions confirm: - seniority_native: 0% entry in asaniczka (structural zero), 15.3% in arshkon, 11.3% in scraped. - seniority_final: 0.6% entry in asaniczka (artifact from rule imputation), 16.9% arshkon, 13.3% scraped. - seniority_best_available: 0.85% (of all, pooled 2024) -> 1.11% (2026) — but dominated by the coverage gap.

docs.title: 2.4 Aggregator share (table 04_aggregator_share_by_period.csv)
docs.text:
Period Source Aggregator share 2024-01 asaniczka 27.3% 2024-04 arshkon 11.8% 2026-03 scraped 14.6% 2026-04 scraped 13.7%

Asaniczka is an outlier on aggregator share. The pooled-2024 rate is 24.6%; the 2026 rate is 14.3%; the 10.2pp decline is almost entirely a source-composition artifact. Arshkon and scraped are closely aligned at 12-15%.

docs.title: 2.5 Metro top-15 (table 05_metro_top15_by_period.csv)
docs.text: Consistent leaders across all periods: SF Bay Area, Washington DC, Seattle, Dallas-Fort Worth, Boston, LA, Chicago. Anomaly: NYC Metro shows 55 (asaniczka), 11 (arshkon) -> 1,231 (scraped 2026-03), 631 (scraped 2026-04). Pooled: 66 -> 1,862 (28x). Other metros grew ~1.2x. This is almost certainly a scraper geographic-coverage artifact; the Kaggle sources appear to undersample NYC.

docs.title: 2.6 Company industry top-15 (table 06_industry_top15_by_period.csv)
docs.text: Arshkon leader: IT Services and IT Consulting (24% of labeled rows). Scraped 2026-03 leader: Software Development (26%). This is a real compositional shift in industry coverage across sources, not a market change. Format anomaly: 2026 sources contain compound industry strings like "Information Services and Technology, Information and Internet" and "Accounting, IT Services and IT Consulting, and Business Consulting and Services" — these appear to be LinkedIn's multi-industry tags and are absent from arshkon's label format. Any industry-level cross-period analysis must harmonize these labels.

docs.title: 2.7 SWE classification tier (table 07_swe_tier_by_period.csv)
docs.text:
Period regex embed_high title_lookup_llm regex share 2024-01 20,411 395 2,407 87.9% 2024-04 4,477 77 465 89.2% 2026-03 22,659 322 1,073 94.2% 2026-04 10,332 162 514 93.8%

Regex share of SWE classifications is +5pp higher in 2026. Consistent with T04's finding that title naming conventions are becoming more standardized. Also means less noise in the 2026 SWE sample.

docs.title: 2.8 Misc flags (table 08_misc_flags_by_period.csv)
docs.text:
Period remote_share ghost_nonlow ghost_llm_nonreal ghost_llm_coverage yoe_coverage 2024-01 0.00 0.09% 0.73% 11.9% 72.2% 2024-04 0.00 6.56% 4.72% 62.4% 60.6% 2026-03 0.00 0.82% 0.63% 10.9% 68.6% 2026-04 0.00 0.80% 0.73% 10.3% 70.1%

Anomaly 1 — is_remote_inferred is 0.0% everywhere. Confirmed by raw inspection: 0 true values out of 63,294. Meanwhile is_remote (source-provided) has 22.5% true in 2026-03, 21.6% in 2026-04, and 0 in 2024. The inferred column is effectively broken or never populated; is_remote is the only usable remote flag, and it's source-only.

Anomaly 2 — ghost flags. Rule-based ghost_job_risk is 6.6% non-low in arshkon but < 1% in every other snapshot. The rule-based ghost heuristic keys on seniority_native = 'entry' + YOE contradictions, so arshkon (the only source with meaningful native entry labels plus inflated-YOE-for-entry rows) is the only source where it fires. Use ghost_assessment_llm for any cross-period comparison; LLM coverage is 62% on arshkon but only 11% on the others, which means ghost-job analyses are underpowered outside the arshkon subset.

docs.title: 3. Anomaly flags (summary)
docs.text:
# Anomaly Evidence A1 is_remote_inferred never populated 0/63,294 true across all periods A2 NYC Metro 28x growth against 1.2x overall 66 -> 1,862 A3 ghost_job_risk rule heuristic fires only in arshkon 6.6% vs <1% elsewhere A4 Industry label format differs across sources Compound multi-industry strings only in 2026 A5 seniority_best_available coverage drops 99.9% (arshkon) -> 21% (scraped 2026) Driven by LLM frame selection A6 Arshkon seniority_final_confidence mean 0.72 vs ~0.85 elsewhere Rule output for native-backfill rows runs at lower confidence A7 Asaniczka aggregator share 27.3% vs ~14% elsewhere Source characteristic, not a period change A8 Arshkon seniority_native='entry' has 41.3% YOE>=5 Replicates Wave-1 measurement deep-dive A9 AI/agentic vocabulary explodes from near-zero desc_contains_{ai,llm,agent,rag,copilot} all +1000%+

Distributions were not strongly bimodal. Skewness is mild (description_length skew 0.3-0.8, yoe_extracted skew 1.0-1.6) — no hidden subpopulation on these metrics.

docs.title: 4. Native-label quality diagnostic — replication and extension (table 11_native_label_yoe_profile.csv, figure fig3_native_entry_yoe_profile.png)
docs.text:
Replicating the Wave-1 finding with clean "share of rows with extracted YOE" denominator (not "share of all rows"):

Source Level n n_with_yoe share YOE<=2 (of yoe) share YOE>=5 (of yoe) arshkon 2024-04 entry 769 475 29.7% 41.3% scraped 2026-03 entry 2,785 1,070 81.2% 9.5% scraped 2026-04 entry 1,187 482 ~76% ~10%

Confirmed. Arshkon's "entry" label is structurally contaminated — 41% of its native-entry rows with extracted YOE demand 5+ years. Scraped entry labels look like actual entry (81% have YOE<=2). Any "entry decline 2024 -> 2026" computed with seniority_native or seniority_final (using arshkon-only 2024 baseline) is driven largely by the 2024 baseline including a large contingent of senior roles mislabeled as "entry" on the LinkedIn platform side.

Extending to other levels:

Source Level n yoe_mean yoe_median share YOE>=5 (of yoe) arshkon associate 275 3.85 3 17.5% (of yoe) arshkon mid-senior 2,344 5.56 5 45.2% (of yoe) arshkon director 25 7.79 8 68.0% (of yoe) scraped associate 795 4.26 3 26.4% (of yoe) scraped mid-senior 15,574 5.37 5 46.4% (of yoe) scraped director 241 7.83 8 73.0% (of yoe)

Reassuring: mid-senior and director YOE profiles are nearly identical across sources (5.4 vs 5.6 mean, 46 vs 45% YOE>=5 for mid-senior). The label drift is concentrated in entry — arshkon's native-entry label is corrupt; mid-senior and up are well-behaved. Label-based analyses of mid-senior and above should be safer than label-based entry analyses.

associate is also noisy: arshkon 17.5% YOE>=5 vs scraped 26.4%. The associate label is unreliable across both sources but the drift direction is opposite to entry.

docs.title: 5. Within-2024 calibration — mid-senior only (tables 12, 13; extends the Agent-Prep calibration table)
docs.text:
Mid-senior SWE rows under seniority_best_available:

Metric arshkon asaniczka scraped d(within-2024) d(asa -> scraped) ratio description_length 3,823 4,495 (5,200) -0.32 -0.59 1.83 core_length 3,133 2,801 (4,308) +0.18 -1.03 5.59 yoe_extracted 5.98 6.05 — -0.02 0.13 5.85

(Scraped values use the full scraped frame for the cross-time comparator; n_scr = 3,639 mid-senior labeled rows in the combined-column frame.)

Interpretation. Description-length growth is the only metric where the within-2024 noise floor (d = 0.32) is substantial relative to the cross-period signal (d = 0.59). For core_length and yoe_extracted, cross-time effects dwarf within-2024 noise by 5-6x. That means the core_length growth (+59%) is credibly real and not an artifact of one source running longer than another.

Also see the Agent-Prep calibration table (exploration/artifacts/shared/calibration_table.csv) for the full 34-metric set. Every binary metric with a calibration-ratio > 3 (tech_python 157, has_llm_text 3.64, has_ai_keyword 16, etc.) crosses the "materially sensitive" threshold by our rule, but for almost all of them the within-2024 difference is near zero and the ratio blows up because the denominator is tiny — the cross-time signals are large in absolute terms.

docs.title: 6. Entry share under full ablation (tables 14, 15, 20; figure fig2_entry_share_ablation.png)
6.1 Label-based variants
docs.text: Variant 2024 n 2024 share_all 2024 share_known 2026 n 2026 share_all 2026 share_known seniority_best_available (2024 pooled) 28,232 0.85% 2.98% 35,062 1.11% 9.31% seniority_native (arshkon-only 2024) 5,019 15.32% 22.33% 35,062 11.33% 13.66% seniority_final (arshkon-only 2024) 5,019 16.90% 21.02% 35,062 13.28% 14.19% seniority_imputed (2024 pooled) 28,232 1.31% 2.01% 35,062 3.67% 6.66%

docs.title: 6.2 Label-independent YOE proxy
docs.text: Period n_all n_with_yoe share YOE<=2 of yoe share YOE<=3 of yoe 2024 pooled 28,232 19,795 10.34% 24.00% 2026 pooled 35,062 24,209 16.61% 33.62%

docs.title: 6.3 Single-source cleanest contrast (arshkon-2024 vs scraped-2026)
docs.text: Source Period entry best of known entry native of known entry final of known YOE<=2 share arshkon 2024-04 7.4% 22.3% 21.0% 15.0% scraped 2026-03 11.2% 14.0% 14.7% 17.1% scraped 2026-04 5.5% 12.9% 13.0% 15.7%

docs.title: 6.4 Verdict
docs.text:
• seniority_native and seniority_final, arshkon-only 2024 baseline: entry share declines 22% -> 14% (of known). This is the Gate-1 result. The deep-dive showed it is mostly a label-quality artifact: 41% of the 2024 baseline is mislabeled senior.
  • seniority_best_available of known: entry share rises 3.0% -> 9.3% (+6.3pp). But the 2024 pooled denominator is heavily biased by asaniczka's tiny LLM-frame subset; the arshkon-only 2024 reading is 7.4% -> 11.2% (scraped 2026-03), which is a ~4pp rise.
  • seniority_imputed of known: entry share rises 2.0% -> 6.7% (+4.6pp).
  • YOE proxy: entry (YOE<=2) rises 10.3% -> 16.6% (+6.3pp) of YOE-known rows. YOE<=3 rises 24% -> 34% (+10pp).

Three of four operationalizations agree: entry share is rising, and the one dissenter (native/final with arshkon-only 2024) is the one we already know has a contaminated baseline.

The scraped 2026-04 snapshot has a slightly lower entry share than 2026-03 (11.2% -> 5.5% under best-available). Without more months we cannot tell if this is monthly volatility or a systematic drift; worth monitoring.

Best defensible claim: entry share has risen modestly in 2026 relative to 2024, by roughly 4-6 percentage points (of known-seniority rows), with the exact magnitude depending on operationalization. This directly contradicts the naive "AI eats juniors" narrative and aligns with the Gate-1 revised reading.

docs.title: 7. Ranked changes 2024 -> 2026 (table 16_ranked_changes_2024_to_2026.csv, figure fig4_ranked_changes.png)
docs.text:
Top 20 metrics by absolute change (continuous metrics use Cohen's d; binary metrics use absolute diff in share):

# Metric Kind 2024 2026 Δ effect 1 core_length cont 2,704 4,308 +59% -0.85 2 description_length cont 3,921 5,201 +33% -0.57 3 desc_contains_ai bin 8.7% 40.9% +32.2pp +0.32 4 yoe_extracted (mean) cont 5.53 4.99 -9.7% +0.19 5 midsenior_rate (best) bin 27.3% 10.4% -16.9pp -0.17 6 desc_contains_lead bin 41.4% 58.0% +16.6pp +0.17 7 desc_contains_python bin 36.3% 50.8% +14.5pp +0.15 8 desc_contains_scope bin 18.6% 32.1% +13.4pp +0.13 9 desc_contains_ownership bin 10.5% 22.8% +12.3pp +0.12 10 desc_contains_agent bin 0.6% 12.8% +12.2pp +0.12 11 is_aggregator bin 24.6% 14.3% -10.2pp -0.10 12 entry_rate_final bin 3.5% 13.3% +9.8pp +0.10 13 desc_contains_llm bin 1.0% 10.7% +9.7pp +0.10 14 yoe_le3_share bin 16.8% 23.2% +6.4pp +0.06 15 desc_contains_kubernetes bin 17.4% 22.5% +5.1pp +0.05 16 desc_contains_terraform bin 7.6% 12.6% +5.1pp +0.05 17 desc_contains_typescript bin 9.9% 14.5% +4.7pp +0.05 18 desc_contains_react bin 15.2% 19.6% +4.3pp +0.04 19 yoe_le2_share bin 7.3% 11.5% +4.2pp +0.04 20 desc_contains_rag bin 0.07% 4.2% +4.2pp +0.04

Narrative groupings. - Description length is the single biggest continuous shift. Core length's +59% is larger than description length's +33%, implying boilerplate-to-substance ratio has fallen (or the LLM boilerplate stripper is more conservative in 2026 — worth a sensitivity check). - AI vocabulary (ai, llm, agent, rag, copilot, prompt) is the dominant binary cluster. Relative changes are >900% for the core AI terms. These are also the binary keywords with the lowest within-2024 noise (Agent-Prep calibration shows d < 0.07 within 2024). - Scope / ownership / lead language rose together. Consistent with "junior scope inflation" hypothesis H1, but also consistent with any general inflation of aspirational job-posting language. Decomposition by seniority (deferred to T09/T16) will be needed to separate these. - Tech stack (Python, Kubernetes, Terraform, Typescript, React) has all moved up — suggesting growing posting breadth rather than a narrowing specialization. Python's +14pp to 50.8% is notable: it is now reliably mentioned in every other SWE posting. - midsenior_rate under best-available appears to collapse -17pp, but this is an artifact of the differential seniority_best_available coverage across periods (§5, A5). Same for entry_rate_final under arshkon-only 2024 — the Wave-1 artifact.

docs.title: 8. Domain × seniority decomposition
docs.text: Skipped — T09 archetype labels are not yet available (exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet does not exist). Re-run this step after T09 completes; the decomposition is the key test for H1 (within-archetype vs between-archetype entry-share change).

docs.title: 9. Company size stratification (arshkon only, table 17)
docs.text:
Within arshkon (n=4,974 with company_size populated):

Quartile Size range n entry_best_share entry_native_share yoe_le2_share desc_len_mean ai_kw_share 1 0 - 104 1,244 2.7% 4.4% 6.3% 2,231 1.5% 2 104 - 627 1,244 2.5% 12.1% 7.0% 2,544 0.7% 3 627 - 9,287 1,243 4.3% 24.5% 9.2% 3,749 1.4% 4 9,287 - 748,312 1,243 4.3% 20.8% 14.0% 4,783 2.2%

Surprises: - Largest companies post the most entry roles and the longest descriptions. Smallest companies have 1/4 the entry share of largest. This is counter to the "startups hire juniors more" folk wisdom. (Caveat: seniority_native is LinkedIn's label, and large companies may just be better at explicitly tagging seniority.) - AI keyword share is highest at the largest companies (2.2% vs 0.7-1.5%). - yoe_le2_share doubles from smallest (6.3%) to largest (14.0%) quartile.

docs.title: 10. Posting volume per company across periods (table 18)
docs.text:
Period mean posts/co median p90 max n companies 2024 pooled 4.3 1 6 1,794 6,503 2026 pooled 5.4 1 9 1,296 6,450

Companies post more SWE roles each on average in 2026 (+1.1 postings/company, +25%). The median is still 1 — the growth is entirely in the upper tail. Number of distinct companies is essentially unchanged (6,503 vs 6,450) despite the row count growing 1.24x — the expansion happens within the existing company set. This is a useful control for compositional-change arguments.

docs.title: 11. Sensitivities (tables 19, 20)
docs.text:
Cut 2024 entry_best (of all) 2026 entry_best (of all) Δ Unrestricted 0.85% 1.11% +0.26pp Aggregators excluded 1.07% 1.09% +0.02pp Company-capped to 20 1.20% 0.99% -0.22pp
  • Aggregator exclusion moves the 2024 baseline more than the 2026 value — consistent with asaniczka having many aggregator postings that are biased toward mid-senior labelling.
  • Company capping moves the 2026 share down, suggesting a small number of high-volume companies are disproportionately driving the 2026 entry-share rise. This is a materially sensitive finding under our 30% rule and warrants inspection in T16/T17.

The arshkon vs scraped single-source contrast (table 20) is the cleanest cut and gives 7.4% -> 11.2% under best-available (of known). This contrast bypasses the asaniczka pooling ambiguity entirely and should be the primary statement.

docs.title: 12. Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
Ordered by how much they change the default reading:

  1. Entry share is rising, not falling (§6). Three of four operationalizations plus the YOE-based proxy agree. The one dissenting operationalization is the one we already know has a contaminated 2024 baseline.
  2. Description length growth (+59% core, +33% full) is the largest single-metric change and dwarfs within-2024 calibration noise for the core-length variant. Cohen d = 0.85 is "large" by Cohen's rubric — this is a real, sizeable change in how postings are written.
  3. AI vocabulary explosion — desc_contains_agent at 12.8%, desc_contains_llm at 10.7%, and desc_contains_rag at 4.2% from near-zero. The biggest relative changes in the dataset, and the cleanest cross-period signals (near-zero within-2024 noise).
  4. Largest companies post the most entry roles in arshkon. Q4-by-size has 1.6x the entry share of Q1. Runs against the folk wisdom of startups hiring juniors.
  5. Company set is stable (6,503 -> 6,450 companies) while posting volume per company rises 25%. The expansion is intensive, not extensive. Useful for H1 analysis: within-company dynamics dominate between-company composition.
  6. Mid-senior and director native labels are clean across sources (45-46% YOE>=5 for mid-senior, 68-73% for director). Only entry and associate have the label-drift problem. Label-based analyses of mid-senior+ roles can probably be trusted more than previously assumed.
  7. is_remote_inferred is broken (0/63,294 true) — column never populated. Downstream analyses cannot rely on it; use source-provided is_remote (available for 2026 only, so no cross-period remote analysis is possible without re-running the text inference).
  8. NYC Metro 28x growth versus the overall 1.2x — scraper geographic-coverage artifact, not a market change. Same-metro cross-period comparisons should exclude or downweight NYC.
  9. Industry label format changes across sources (compound multi-industry strings in 2026). Any cross-period industry analysis needs harmonization.
  10. Asaniczka's high aggregator share (27%) drives most of the apparent "aggregator decline" between periods. The real between-period delta is much smaller (11.8% arshkon vs 14.3% scraped — essentially flat).
  11. Scraped 2026-04 entry share (5.5% under best-available) is much lower than 2026-03 (11.2%) — worth monitoring with more months of scraped data.

docs.title: 13. Sensitivity framework applied
docs.text: Dimension Applied Finding (a) Aggregator exclusion Yes 2024 baseline moves more than 2026; the "aggregator decline" is mostly an asaniczka artifact (b) Company capping Yes Entry-share rise reverses sign under capping; materially sensitive (c) Seniority operationalization (full ablation) Yes Three of four methods + YOE proxy agree: entry rising. Only native/final (with contaminated 2024 baseline) disagrees (d) Description text source Partial Used raw description for binary keyword checks to maximize recall; noted boilerplate-denominator issue for length metrics (e) Source restriction Yes Single-source arshkon vs scraped comparison given throughout (f) Within-2024 calibration Yes Extended Agent-Prep table with mid-senior-only Cohen's d; core_length cross-time signal dwarfs noise 5.6x (g) SWE classification tier Noted Regex share +5pp in 2026, sample is cleaner (i) Indeed cross-platform Not run T08 is LinkedIn-only per spec

docs.title: Artefacts
docs.text:
Figures (exploration/figures/T08/): - fig1_numeric_distributions.png — description_length, core_length, yoe_extracted, seniority_final_confidence histograms by period - fig2_entry_share_ablation.png — entry share over 2024->2026 under full ablation set + YOE proxy - fig3_native_entry_yoe_profile.png — native-entry label quality diagnostic by source - fig4_ranked_changes.png — top 15 metrics by 2024->2026 change

Tables (exploration/tables/T08/): 20 CSVs covering numeric summaries, seniority variant distributions, aggregator/industry/metro composition, anomaly notes, native-label diagnostic, within-2024 calibration (Cohen's d), entry-share ablation, YOE proxy, ranked changes, company size stratification, posting volume, and sensitivities.

docs.title: Follow-ups for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T09 (archetype labels): needed to re-run step 7 (domain × seniority decomposition). The within-vs-between decomposition is the cleanest test of H1.
  • T16: re-run company-level entry-share analysis with seniority_best_available + YOE proxy; the Wave-1 T06 result is based on contaminated inputs.
  • T17 (keyword validation): the "desc_contains_*" family here was used as a coarse screen — a 50-sample-per-pattern precision check is owed (AI, LLM, agent, rag, copilot, ownership, scope, lead, cursor).
  • Remote-work analysis: is_remote_inferred column needs fixing (preprocessing) before any cross-period remote analysis is feasible.
  • Industry harmonization: need a mapping from 2026 compound industry strings to 2024 canonical industries before industry-level cross-period analysis.
  • NYC downweighting: recommend excluding or separately profiling NYC for same-metro cross-period comparisons.
  • Scraped snapshot drift: flag 2026-03 vs 2026-04 entry-share gap for monitoring once more scraper months accumulate.

docs.title: T09: Posting Archetype Discovery — Methods Laboratory
Headline Finding
docs.text:
Unsupervised topic discovery surfaces a clean tech-domain partition of the SWE posting market, and the single largest 2024→2026 shift is the rise of an AI/ML archetype (4.1% → ~15%, +11 pp), with simultaneous double-digit-percent declines in .NET, frontend/web, and Spring/Java stacks. BERTopic (min_topic_size=30, primary) yields 23 interpretable topics with 27.8% noise and strong core-only seed stability (mean ARI = 0.93). NMF (k=12, comparison) agrees on the main tech-domain axis (BERTopic↔NMF NMI = 0.33; 5 of 23 BERTopic topics have a ≥0.15 top-term-Jaccard match to an NMF component), but NMF's low-k components are heavily contaminated by company-specific boilerplate signatures. Clusters do not align with seniority (NMI vs combined seniority = 0.0155) or with the YOE-based junior proxy (NMI = 0.0078) — the dominant structure of the SWE market in this embedding is tech domain, not career level.

Surprise highlights (expanded below): - Two topics exist only in 2026 because unsupervised topic modeling recovered scraper/preprocessing boilerplate artifacts the cleaning pipeline missed (Dice reposts, Northrop Grumman footers). - BERTopic and NMF agree where BERTopic has high-coherence technical topics (AI/ML, cloud/devops, data eng, web/react, android/mobile), but NMF spends 4 of its 12 components on boilerplate signatures that BERTopic either merges into noise or isolates into small, explicitly identifiable artifact topics — a useful diagnostic by itself. - The combined best-available seniority column and the YOE-based junior proxy disagree sharply at the archetype level (pooled: entry share 7.7% vs YOE-junior share 18.2%), and the disagreement is not uniform across clusters.

docs.title: 1. Sample Construction
docs.text:
Target: up to 8,000 SWE LinkedIn postings, balanced across three period buckets (2024, 2026-03, 2026-04) with within-period stratification by the combined best-available seniority column. Prefer text_source='llm' and prefer arshkon over asaniczka for 2024.

Filters: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true, joined with swe_cleaned_text.parquet.

Pool sizes: 2024 = 28,232 rows (arshkon 5,019 + asaniczka 23,213), 2026-03 = 24,054, 2026-04 = 11,008. Full SWE LinkedIn pool after joining cleaned text = 63,294.

Final sample: 8,000 rows, with per-period target 2,700 (lightly downsampled after the 2024 arshkon-first fill).

period_bucket source n 2024 kaggle_arshkon 2,664 2024 kaggle_asaniczka 0 (arshkon saturated the 2024 target) 2026-03 scraped 2,666 2026-04 scraped 2,670

Text source composition:

period_bucket llm rule Note 2024 2,664 0 arshkon is 99% llm 2026-03 2,431 235 llm pool had 4,921 usable rows 2026-04 1,287 1,383 llm pool only 2,329 rows — had to fall back to rule-based text for 52% of 2026-04

This is a caveat: the 2026-04 half of the sample is half rule-based text, which is materially different in length and boilerplate retention from llm text. It could either amplify or mute cluster signal for 2026-04-only topics. See "Sensitivities" for an llm-only re-run.

Combined seniority composition (known labels only):

period_bucket entry associate mid-senior director unknown missing (None) 2024 93 8 1,156 7 1,400 0 2026-03 35 5 264 8 2,100 254 2026-04 19 5 301 11 946 1,388 Total 147 18 1,721 26 4,446 1,642

Most 2026 rows have no combined-seniority label because most scraped rows fall in not_selected LLM coverage and have no rule_sufficient routing. Known-label count is ~22% of sample. This is the same sparsity landscape documented in T07/T06 carried forward to the clustering sample.

docs.title: 2. Shared Artifacts Used
docs.text:
• swe_cleaned_text.parquet (company-stripped cleaned text)
  • swe_embeddings.npy + swe_embedding_index.parquet (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim, L2-normalized)
  • swe_tech_matrix.parquet (153 technology regex flags, used for characterization and NMI)

Embeddings were re-used (not recomputed), so the BERTopic results are deterministic-by-seed with respect to the shared representation.

docs.title: 3.1 min_topic_size sweep
docs.text:
min_topic_size n topics noise pct noise 20 58 2,925 36.6% 30 (primary) 23 2,223 27.8% 50 5 445 5.6%

At mts=20 many near-duplicate niche topics appear (e.g., multiple Java/Spring variants). At mts=50 HDBSCAN collapses everything into 5 coarse clusters that lose the domain granularity that is the core finding. mts=30 is the interpretability optimum.

docs.title: 3.2 Stability (primary mts=30, 3 seeds)
docs.text:
seeds ARI all docs ARI core-only (exclude noise) n core 42 vs 7 0.640 0.893 5,000 42 vs 2026 0.592 0.897 4,805 7 vs 2026 0.735 0.990 4,709 mean 0.656 0.927

Clusters assigned to the non-noise core are highly stable across seeds. The all-docs ARI is substantially lower because HDBSCAN's noise boundary is seed-sensitive — i.e., the same documents sometimes flip in/out of noise, but when they are clustered they land in the same topic. For downstream analyses, core-only assignments can be trusted; noise should be treated as an "unclassified" stratum, not as a 24th cluster.

docs.title: 3.3 Topic coherence
docs.text: Mean UMass coherence across the 23 primary topics = -1.342. On our vocabulary this is in the normal-to-good range for short, dense technical documents; we use it as a comparative rather than absolute reference.

docs.title: 3.4 Primary topic list (mts=30)
docs.text:
Full top-20 per topic in exploration/tables/T09/bertopic_topic_terms.csv; summary below (top 8 terms, n, period shares):

T n Top terms (c-TF-IDF) pct 2024 pct 2026-03 pct 2026-04 -1 2,223 (noise) 34.3 33.3 32.4 0 1,018 software, technical, development, design, team, business, experience, years 34.1 29.5 36.4 1 912 ai, learning, machine learning, machine, ml, models, model, aiml 12.0 44.4 43.6 2 712 cloud, infrastructure, devops, azure, kubernetes, security, terraform, aws 33.9 35.5 30.6 3 663 data, analytics, data engineer, data engineering, pipelines, data pipelines, databricks, sql 36.2 33.3 30.5 4 516 frontend, web, react, backend, javascript, stack, css, nodejs 41.1 31.6 27.3 5 312 spring, java, spring boot, angular, java developer, microservices 43.3 32.1 24.7 6 222 android, mobile, ios, native, app, kotlin, react native 33.3 39.6 27.0 7 209 equipment, sales, customer, manufacturing, mechanical, electrical 41.2 34.0 24.9 8 165 test, testing, automation, qa, selenium, test cases 30.9 32.1 37.0 9 165 net, aspnet, net core, sql server, net developer 70.9 10.9 18.2 10 150 embedded, firmware, linux, kernel, hardware, embedded linux 25.3 40.0 34.7 11 134 aws, services, golang, software development, architecture 42.5 25.4 32.1 12 102 flight, software, vehicle, space, aerospace, ground, embedded 17.6 36.3 46.1 13 75 radar, clearance, missile, systems engineering, dod, secret 26.7 40.0 33.3 14 73 dice, experts stage, leading career, dice today, apply dice 0.0 16.4 83.6 15 65 site reliability, sre, observability, incident 41.5 18.5 40.0 16 57 python, python developer, django, flask 61.4 22.8 15.8 17 51 typescript sql, javascript java, kubernetes variety, databases container 64.7 17.6 17.6 18 40 systems engineering, systems engineer, mbse, requirements 50.0 22.5 27.5 19 40 servicenow, itsm, servicenow platform, cmdb 65.0 20.0 15.0 20 35 salary, employees, depending, salary range, shift differential 0.0 17.1 82.9 21 31 expedia, travel, cash, commerce team 22.6 35.5 41.9 22 30 test, manufacturing, tessent, fixtures, dft 30.0 43.3 26.7

Bold values mark topics that are materially skewed across periods.

docs.title: 4. Method B — NMF (Comparison)
docs.text:
NMF ran on a TF-IDF matrix (1-2grams, min_df=10, max_df=0.6, sublinear_tf, L2-normalized). Top-8 terms per component at each k in exploration/tables/T09/nmf_topic_terms.csv; summary of k=12 (the best-interpreted k):

C top terms (8) Note 0 product, building, build, ll, scale, platform, help, engineers Generic "help us build" — weak signal 1 chatbots, html sql, proficient programming, javascript html, language python, python javascript, proficient, progress Boilerplate artifact: AI-trainer chatbot-labeling contract postings 2 content, degree phd, opportunities switch, enthusiastic new, qualities enthusiastic Boilerplate artifact: an asaniczka-era LinkedIn boilerplate 3 ai, ml, machine learning, learning, machine, models, model, ai ml AI/ML — matches BERTopic T1 4 web, java, applications, developer, spring, react, using, angular Web + Java combined (matches BERTopic T4 + T5) 5 ubuntu, openstack, upstream, open source, ceph, openstack kubernetes, lxc lxd, lxd Boilerplate artifact: Canonical job postings 6 infrastructure, cloud, devops, ci, cd, ci cd, terraform, automation Cloud/DevOps — matches BERTopic T2 7 open source, open, tech trends, external technology, experimenting learning, communities mentoring Boilerplate artifact: a growth-culture boilerplate 8 data, data engineer, analytics, data engineering, etl, data pipelines, sql, pipelines Data eng — matches BERTopic T3 9 test, requirements, support, degree, hardware, required, testing, analysis QA/Test/Requirements — matches BERTopic T8 loosely 10 qualifications capabilities, capabilities skills, chase, software applications, operational stability Boilerplate artifact: JPMorgan Chase template 11 responses, model responses, responses coding, coding software, evaluation, strengths, reasoning, clarity Boilerplate artifact: LLM-evaluation contract postings

Key methodological finding: 6 of 12 NMF components (1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 11) are boilerplate/company signatures that encode the residual company-specific text that the shared cleaning did not strip. NMF's raw TF-IDF objective is more susceptible to these than BERTopic's embedding+HDBSCAN path, because sentence-transformer embeddings place similar boilerplate patterns close enough to merge into HDBSCAN's noise stratum or to be absorbed by larger on-domain topics.

k=5 and k=8 look worse (the top components are dominated by boilerplate). k=15 adds a clean clearance/embedded component and an "agentic/govcloud" micro-topic but also adds two more boilerplate components.

docs.title: 5.1 Alignment by top-term Jaccard (BERTopic ↔ NMF k=12)
docs.text:
Best NMF match per BERTopic topic (full table at method_best_nmf_per_bertopic.csv):

BERTopic best NMF Jaccard Interpretation T3 data C8 0.50 strong match T2 cloud/devops C6 0.43 strong match T1 AI/ML C3 0.36 strong match T5 Spring/Java C4 0.20 partial — NMF merges T4 web with T5 Java T4 frontend/web C4 0.15 partial — same as above all others — < 0.15 either BERTopic-specific or NMF-specific

Method-robust topics (Jaccard ≥ 0.15): 5 of 23. These are the five biggest mainstream technical axes: AI/ML, cloud/devops, data engineering, and web+Java. Cross-method NMI(BERTopic, NMF-k12) = 0.326; ARI = 0.143.

Why only five? Not because BERTopic and NMF disagree on the "what" — they largely agree that AI/ML, cloud, data, and web are the main structures — but because: 1. BERTopic splits finer than NMF in the middle of the size distribution (mobile, embedded, SRE, .NET, servicenow, systems eng, python/django are distinct BERTopic topics but get folded into NMF's generic "applications" or "infrastructure" components). 2. NMF spends 6/12 components on boilerplate that BERTopic absorbs into noise or isolates in small dedicated topics (T14 Dice, T20 salary/shift boilerplate). 3. BERTopic's noise stratum (28% of docs) has no NMF analog — NMF assigns every doc to some component.

docs.title: 5.2 Methods comparison table
docs.text:
Method # topics pct noise stability (ARI core) mean UMass coh. interpretability BERTopic mts=20 58 36.6% — — Fragmented; many duplicate niches BERTopic mts=30 23 27.8% 0.927 -1.34 Clean domain clusters; primary choice BERTopic mts=50 5 5.6% — — Over-merged; loses structure NMF k=5 5 — — — 3 of 5 components are boilerplate NMF k=8 8 — — — Mixes domain and boilerplate NMF k=12 12 — — — Best NMF; 6/12 domain + 6/12 boilerplate NMF k=15 15 — — — Adds clearance + agentic micro-topics; boilerplate persists

Interpretability ranking (subjective): BERTopic mts=30 > NMF k=12 > NMF k=15 > NMF k=8 > BERTopic mts=20 > BERTopic mts=50 > NMF k=5.

Recommendation for downstream use: BERTopic mts=30. NMF k=12 is useful as a robustness check and as a company-boilerplate detector.

docs.title: 5.3 Noise characterization
docs.text:
The 2,223 BERTopic noise documents (27.8%) are not concentrated in any period (762/740/721 across 2024/2026-03/2026-04) or source. Manual inspection of 10 samples shows: - Genuine niche roles that don't cluster (radar, embedded-RTOS, robotics perception). - Fragmentary descriptions that survived cleaning with odd token sequences. - Descriptions heavy on company-team boilerplate (PwC, Scribd team descriptions) where the technical content is a short tail.

The seniority distribution of noise is similar to the overall known distribution (20% mid-senior, 2% entry, 0.3% director). Noise is not a junior/senior-specific bucket — it is semantic-diversity-specific.
