(Archetypes 11, 13, 16, 17, 18, 22 omitted from this table for brevity — all are smaller than T10 and documented fully in the CSV.)

docs.title: 6.1 Entry-level share disagreements
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T09/entry_share_by_archetype_period.csv shows that, for every major archetype, the combined best-available entry share and the YOE-based junior share disagree in magnitude and sometimes in direction across periods:

  • T00 software/development, 2024→2026-03: combined entry drops 13.1% → 10.5% (n_known 213→57), while YOE-junior share almost doubles 23.4% → 43.0% (n_known 252→223). These moves are in opposite directions.
  • T03 data eng, 2024→2026-03: combined entry 2.9% → 24.2%, YOE-junior 11.7% → 23.9%. The combined jump is driven by a tiny known-label denominator (33).
  • T05 Spring/Java: combined entry ~4% in 2024, 0% in 2026 (n_known 5–7); YOE-junior 11% → 14% → 8%. Small n_known makes combined unreliable.

Interpretation: the combined column's known-label denominator collapses under archetype stratification (often <30), and under those conditions its entry-share estimate is dominated by sampling noise. For archetype-level seniority claims, the YOE proxy is more defensible — but even there, T07's caveat about differential YOE extraction quality across snapshots applies (see seniority_measurement_investigation.md). Downstream tasks should report both and avoid drawing strong archetype-level seniority conclusions from the combined column alone.

docs.title: 7. Temporal Dynamics (within-period share)
docs.text:
Growers (largest increases 2024 → mean(2026-03, 2026-04)):

T name 2024 2026-03 2026-04 delta 1 AI/learning/machine learning 4.09% 15.19% 14.91% +10.96 pp 14 dice/experts stage (artifact) 0.0% 0.45% 2.28% +1.37 pp 12 flight/software/vehicle 0.68% 1.39% 1.76% +0.90 pp 10 embedded/firmware 1.43% 2.25% 1.95% +0.67 pp 20 salary/shift (artifact) 0.0% 0.23% 1.09% +0.66 pp 13 radar/clearance/missile 0.75% 1.13% 0.94% +0.28 pp 8 test/testing/automation 1.91% 1.99% 2.28% +0.22 pp

Shrinkers:

T name 2024 2026-03 2026-04 delta 9 .NET/aspnet/net core 4.39% 0.68% 1.12% -3.49 pp 4 frontend/web/react 7.96% 6.11% 5.28% -2.26 pp 5 Spring/Java/spring boot 5.07% 3.75% 2.88% -1.75 pp 3 data/analytics/data engineer 9.01% 8.29% 7.57% -1.08 pp 7 equipment/sales/customer 3.23% 2.66% 1.95% -0.92 pp 16 python/python developer/django 1.31% 0.49% 0.34% -0.90 pp 17 typescript sql / javascript java 1.24% 0.34% 0.34% -0.90 pp

Stable: T2 cloud/devops (~9%), T0 generic "software/development" (~13%), T6 mobile (~3%), noise (~28%).

docs.title: 7.1 Archetypes that exist in only one period
docs.text:
• T14 Dice boilerplate (73 docs): 0 in 2024, 12 in 2026-03, 61 in 2026-04. This is a preprocessing artifact: Dice cross-posts carry an "Apply via Dice today" header whose company-name stripping leaves the boilerplate intact. The ratcheting concentration in 2026-04 suggests this template was introduced to Dice's feed after 2026-03 or a scraper change began retaining more Dice rows. Recommended action: add "dice leading career destination" / "apply via dice" to the boilerplate-removal patterns for Stage 3.
  • T20 salary/shift boilerplate (35 docs): 0 in 2024, 6 in 2026-03, 29 in 2026-04. Manual inspection: these are Northrop Grumman / Firefly Aerospace defense-contractor postings with a consistent "CLEARANCE TYPE / TRAVEL / Description / employees incredible opportunities" preface. The 2024 snapshot did not sample Northrop postings densely; the 2026 snapshot does.
  • T21 expedia/travel/cash (31 docs): 7 in 2024, 24 in 2026 — mostly a company-signature cluster (Expedia).

These three small topics together account for ~140 documents. Their existence is a process finding about residual boilerplate, not a labor-market signal. Downstream analyses should flag them (dropping the noise topic plus the three artifact topics reduces the clean archetype set to 20).

docs.title: 8. UMAP & PCA Visualizations
docs.text:
Three projection figures per method, colored by (a) BERTopic clusters, (b) period, (c) seniority:

  • exploration/figures/T09/umap_clusters.png, umap_period.png, umap_seniority.png
  • exploration/figures/T09/pca_clusters.png, pca_period.png, pca_seniority.png

What UMAP shows: clear, well-separated topic islands (AI/ML, cloud, data, web/java, embedded, mobile, noise) — consistent with the high core-only ARI. Period coloring overlays roughly uniformly on the topic islands except the AI/ML island, which is clearly denser with 2026-03/04 points and sparser in 2024. Seniority coloring is essentially featureless — there is no visible "junior island" or "senior island"; known seniority labels are scattered across every domain. This is the visual analog of the near-zero NMI with seniority.

What PCA shows: a single dense blob with only the tails of outlier clusters (Canonical/Ubuntu, Dice boilerplate) visible. PCA captures document-length and boilerplate-magnitude directions rather than semantic domain. The visual story changes substantially: if the analyst looked only at PCA, they would conclude the sample is unstructured. This is a clean argument for manifold methods over linear projections on sentence-transformer embeddings.

Additional figures: - bertopic_topterms_grid.png — 4×4 bar chart of top 8 terms for the 16 largest topics. - archetype_period_heatmap.png — within-period share per archetype, rows sorted by 2024→2026 delta. - archetype_sensitivities.png — archetype share at baseline vs no_aggregator vs llm_text_only.

docs.title: 9. Alignment of Cluster Structure With Known Labels (NMI)
docs.text:
Target NMI n seniority_combined (known only) 0.0155 1,912 yoe_junior (<=2 vs >2, yoe known) 0.0078 5,222 period_bucket 0.0219 8,000 primary_language (python/java/js/ts/rust/csharp) 0.1119 8,000 text_source (llm vs rule) 0.0219 8,000 data source (arshkon/asaniczka/scraped) 0.0215 8,000 is_aggregator 0.0190 8,000

The dominant latent structure of the SWE posting market in this embedding is primary programming language / tech domain, not seniority, not period, not data source. NMI with primary language is 5× NMI with period and 7× NMI with seniority. This is consistent with the visual story from UMAP and with the BERTopic topic list.

Implications for RQ1 (employer-side restructuring): - Clustering will not give us a "junior cluster vs senior cluster" decomposition. - It will give us a tech-domain partition within which we can ask how the junior share moved inside each domain. The AI/ML growth (+11 pp) plus flat YOE-junior share within the AI/ML cluster (17.2%) plus its lower-than-average combined entry share (6.0%) means the AI/ML growth is not arriving as a flood of junior AI postings — it arrives in the same seniority mix as the rest of the market, which in level terms means AI/ML is making the overall job market less junior-heavy via composition (even if each domain held its junior share constant).

docs.title: 10.1 Aggregator exclusion (sensitivity (a))
docs.text: Removing is_aggregator=true rows changes the maximum-single-archetype share by ≤2.5 pp (max shift: noise at 2.5 pp). The AI/ML archetype moves by less than 1 pp. Not materially sensitive.

docs.title: 10.2 Text source: llm-only (sensitivity (d))
docs.text:
Restricting to text_source='llm' (drops ~1,618 rule-text rows, nearly all from 2026-04) changes archetype shares by at most 2.3 pp (T14 Dice boilerplate). The AI/ML, cloud, data, and web archetypes move by less than 1.3 pp each. Not materially sensitive at the archetype-composition level. However, 2026-04's within-period-share estimates are noisier because the llm-only subset of 2026-04 is only n=1,287.

Full sensitivity table: exploration/tables/T09/archetype_sensitivities.csv; summary at archetype_sensitivity_summary.csv.

docs.title: 10.3 SWE classification tier (sensitivity (g))
docs.text: Not stratified in this run because the sample was constructed with a mix of tiers. If downstream tasks need a tier-restricted run, re-run T09 with swe_classification_tier IN ('regex','strong_keyword') and re-sample.

docs.title: 11. Surprises & Unexpected Patterns
docs.text:
1. AI/ML is the only large grower. Every other archetype that gains share grows by <1.5 pp. The AI/ML jump of +11 pp absorbs essentially all the share that .NET (-3.5), web/react (-2.3), Spring/Java (-1.8), data eng (-1.1), equipment/sales (-0.9), python-django (-0.9), and misc typescript/sql (-0.9) give up. The story is a stack-migration story, not a diffusion story.

  2. Primary language is the single strongest latent dimension of the embedding — 5× more explanatory than period or seniority. If you want to predict what cluster a job posting lands in, the most informative feature is which programming language it mentions.

  3. Two clusters (T14 Dice, T20 Northrop) exist only in 2026 — not because the labor market changed, but because scraper boilerplate changed. Unsupervised topic modeling is acting as a preprocessing QA tool here: it surfaces residual boilerplate that the Stage 3 patterns missed. Recommended follow-up: add "dice leading career destination" / "apply via dice today" / "clearance type: [polygraph|top secret|secret]" to boilerplate removal.

  4. NMF dedicates half its components to company-specific boilerplate at k=12, even though those postings are numerically a small minority. This means NMF is a poor primary choice for this corpus, but it is an excellent boilerplate auditor: the components it surfaces are literally a ranked list of residual boilerplate signatures in the corpus.

  5. Clusters do not align with seniority. This is the most important negative result. The "archetype" structure of the SWE market is fundamentally a tech-stack structure, and the junior/senior distinction cuts across all stacks without producing its own cluster. This constrains how we can use the archetype labels in RQ1: they are a control-for-composition variable, not an outcome variable.

  6. Combined-seniority and YOE-junior disagree strongly at the archetype level. In the T00 "generic software" archetype, combined entry share drops 2024→2026-03 while YOE-junior share nearly doubles. At archetype granularity, the combined column has too few known labels to be reliable (n_known often <30 per archetype×period cell), and the two proxies should be reported together, never collapsed.

  7. BERTopic's noise stratum is 28% — not small, not growing, not seniority-biased. This is healthy: a large noise bucket is the method's honest admission that the SWE market has a semantic long tail. It is not a failure mode — forcing mts=50 drops noise to 5.6% but destroys the topic granularity that makes the AI/ML finding visible.

  8. Cluster stability is 0.93 on the core but 0.66 when noise is included — meaning the cluster content is stable, the cluster boundary is not. For downstream analyses, noise should be treated as its own "unclassified" bucket, not merged into the nearest cluster.

  9. The "data engineer" archetype is flat or slightly declining (-1.1 pp). This is a mild surprise given the broader narrative that data engineering is growing — in this SWE-only slice, it is the AI/ML cluster that is growing, and data eng is holding roughly steady.

  10. Median YOE is 4 or 5 across virtually every archetype, with only T09 .NET (5), T05 Spring/Java (6), and T11 AWS-noninternship (3) deviating. YOE is homogeneously mid-career across the posting pool; junior-heavy clusters don't really exist.

docs.title: 12. Downstream Artifact
docs.text:
exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet — 8,000 rows, columns uid, archetype (int, -1 for noise), archetype_name (e.g., T01_ai / learning / machine learning). T08, T11, T16, T20 and any downstream task that wants an archetype column can join on uid.

Caveats for downstream users: 1. Labels cover only the 8,000-doc sample, not the full 63,294 SWE pool. To extend: run BERTopic's .transform on the full pool using the saved model (not persisted in this run — rerun T09 with a save step if needed). 2. Archetypes 14, 20, and 21 are company/scraper boilerplate artifacts, not labor-market signals. Exclude or flag them before any composition analysis. 3. The noise topic (-1, 28%) is genuine semantic long-tail; treat as an "unclassified" stratum, not as a 24th cluster. 4. Archetype-level seniority claims should report both the combined best-available column and the YOE-based junior proxy, and should disclose the known-label denominator per cell.

docs.title: 13. Recommendation for Downstream Use
docs.text: Use BERTopic mts=30 labels as the primary archetype variable. NMF k=12 is a useful robustness check and boilerplate auditor, but it should not be the primary assignment. For composition analyses (RQ1-style), use the 23 BERTopic topics (or aggregate the three artifact topics into "boilerplate" and drop noise) as a domain control — not as a seniority proxy.

docs.title: 14. Artifact Index
docs.text:
Scripts (exploration/scripts/): - T09_01_sample.py — sample construction and composition tables - T09_02_bertopic.py — BERTopic sweep, primary model, stability, coherence - T09_03_nmf.py — NMF at k ∈ {5, 8, 12, 15} - T09_04_compare_and_characterize.py — method comparison, NMI, temporal dynamics, sensitivities, archetype labels artifact - T09_05_visualize.py — UMAP/PCA scatter plots, top-terms bar chart, heatmap, sensitivity bars

Tables (exploration/tables/T09/): - sample.parquet, sample_composition_{source,textsource,seniority}.csv, period_pool_sizes.csv - bertopic_topic_info.csv, bertopic_topic_terms.csv, bertopic_topics.parquet, bertopic_min_topic_size_sweep.csv, bertopic_stability_ari.csv, bertopic_topic_coherence.csv, bertopic_summary.json - nmf_topic_terms.csv, nmf_summary.csv, nmf_assignments.parquet, nmf_assignments_wide.parquet - methods_comparison.csv, method_alignment_jaccard.csv, method_best_nmf_per_bertopic.csv, method_best_bertopic_per_nmf.csv, method_cross_nmi.json - archetype_characterization.csv, archetype_temporal_dynamics.csv, entry_share_by_archetype_period.csv, nmi_scores.csv - archetype_sensitivities.csv, archetype_sensitivity_summary.csv

Figures (exploration/figures/T09/): - umap_clusters.png, umap_period.png, umap_seniority.png - pca_clusters.png, pca_period.png, pca_seniority.png - bertopic_topterms_grid.png, archetype_period_heatmap.png, archetype_sensitivities.png

Downstream artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet

docs.title: T10 — Title taxonomy evolution (SWE, LinkedIn)
docs.text: Status: complete (2026-04-09) Script: exploration/scripts/T10_title_evolution.py Tables: exploration/tables/T10/ Figures: exploration/figures/T10/

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• Default filter: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true.
  • Two period buckets: 2024 (kaggle_arshkon Apr + kaggle_asaniczka Jan) vs 2026 (scraped Mar+Apr).
  • Two sensitivity variants throughout:
  • combined — all 2024 SWE (n=28,232) vs all 2026 SWE (n=35,062)
  • arshkon_only — arshkon 2024 only (n=5,019) vs all 2026, to rule out aggregator-volume confounding (asaniczka is aggregator-heavy)
  • Seniority uses the augmented combined best-available column (LLM where labeled, seniority_final fallback otherwise).
  • Titles analyzed in two forms: title_normalized (seniority-stripped, lowercased, used for vocabulary/concentration) and title_clean (lowercased raw title with salary/parenthetical stripped, used for seniority-marker detection).

docs.title: Key numbers
docs.text:
Metric 2024 combined 2024 arshkon 2026 scraped n postings 28,232 5,019 35,062 Unique titles (after normalization) 8,866 2,100 13,268 Unique per 1K postings 314 418 378 Top-10 title share 19.3% 23.1% 20.1% Top-50 title share 37.4% 43.6% 30.5% HHI 0.0086 0.0126 0.0114

Interpretation: The SWE title space is more fragmented in 2026, not more standardized. Unique titles per 1K grew 21% vs the combined 2024 baseline (314 → 378), and top-50 title share dropped from 37.4% to 30.5%. Arshkon-only shows even more fragmentation in 2026. The top tail remains moderately concentrated (HHI ~0.01).

docs.title: 1. Vocabulary comparison
docs.text:
Frequency threshold: title appearing ≥3 times in the period.

Variant Unique (freq≥3) 2024 Unique (freq≥3) 2026 New in 2026 Disappeared from 2024 Shared combined 1,641 1,616 1,301 676 315 arshkon_only 215 1,616 1,477 76 139

Under arshkon_only, 91% of 2026 titles (freq≥3) do not appear in the 2024 baseline — consistent with both a real fragmentation effect and the small 2024 title pool (arshkon is only 5K SWE postings). Under combined 2024, ~80% of 2026 frequent titles are still novel.

See exploration/tables/T10/new_titles_{variant}.csv and disappeared_titles_{variant}.csv.

docs.title: Notable new titles (combined variant, 2026)
docs.text:
• ai ml engineer (n=139), ai software engineer (n=53), applied ai engineer (n=35), ai data engineer consultant (n=34), ai data engineer manager (n=28), genai python systems engineer director (n=28), oracle ai engineer manager (n=27), manager genai full stack developer (n=27), associate director ai application engineer (n=32)
  • data engineer manager (n=65), data engineer associate (n=33), software engineer associate (n=33)
  • software engineer [multiple positions available] (n=61) — aggregator template artifact
  • forward deployed software engineer palantir foundry director/manager — company-specific templated titles
  • servicenow developer consultant (n=34), servicenow software engineer (n=28) — ServiceNow/consulting bucket growth

docs.title: Notable disappeared titles (combined variant, from 2024)
docs.text:
• c# .net developer (n=129), dotnet developer (n=96), php developer (n=104), java architect (n=52) — legacy-stack framings
  • founding software engineer seed stage (n=91), backend engineer startup (n=86), fullstack engineer startup (n=75), founding fullstack engineer seed (n=74), founding fullstack engineer ai startup (n=69) — startup-branded roles; essentially absent in 2026 scraped
  • machine learning engineer series a (n=95), machine learning engineer ai (n=52), software engineer artificial intelligence (n=60) — rebranded as "AI engineer" / "AI ML engineer" in 2026
  • server infrastructure engineer (n=103) — replaced by "platform engineer" / "infrastructure engineer"
  • experienced associate software engineer (python sql) (n=62) — appears to be a single-company 2024 template

docs.title: 2. Concentration
docs.text: See Key numbers above. In one sentence: the top few titles are still dominant, but the long tail grew.

docs.title: 3. AI/ML in titles
docs.text:
Titles containing AI, ML, LLM, data, agent, NLP, deep learning, GPT, etc.:

Variant 2024 share 2026 share combined 9.8% (2,777 / 28,232) 23.9% (8,381 / 35,062) arshkon_only 12.0% (603 / 5,019) 23.9%

The AI/ML term is present in ~1 in 4 SWE titles in 2026, roughly 2.5× the 2024 share. This is the clearest title-level signal of role redefinition.

(Caveat: the pattern includes data, which is broad. Removing data still leaves roughly double growth for strict AI/ML/LLM terms; see ai_terms_in_title.csv for the full list.)

docs.title: 4. Title-content alignment for top-10 shared titles
docs.text:
TF-IDF cosine similarity (stop words removed) between the concatenated 2024 and 2026 descriptions for each shared title:

Title n_2024 n_2026 Cosine similarity software engineer 2,075 3,365 0.9496 data engineer 732 1,042 0.9628 systems engineer 724 176 0.9578 devops engineer 278 515 0.9415 machine learning engineer 189 399 0.9005 full stack engineer 154 423 0.8834 embedded software engineer 299 255 0.8935 software developer 323 204 0.9288 test engineer 260 222 0.9623 site reliability engineer 186 255 0.9187

All top-10 shared titles remain highly similar between periods (cosine ≥ 0.88). The two softest pairs are machine learning engineer (0.9005) and full stack engineer (0.8834), suggesting the content for these titles is drifting most. This is consistent with the emergence of specialized AI/ML titles peeling off "ML engineer" content into "AI engineer / applied AI / GenAI" subsets, and with "full stack" absorbing more AI/agentic product work.

docs.title: 5. Seniority markers in the raw title
docs.text:
Strict regex detection (excludes bare "associate" and single-letter "I" to avoid noise):

Variant Year n Junior share (junior/jr/entry/intern/new grad/trainee) Senior share (senior/sr/staff/principal/lead/distinguished/fellow) combined 2024 28,232 0.75% 58.85% combined 2026 35,062 2.47% 43.71% arshkon_only 2024 5,019 1.89% 36.82% arshkon_only 2026 35,062 2.47% 43.71%
  • Junior marker share tripled under combined (0.75% → 2.47%) and grew 30% under arshkon_only (1.89% → 2.47%). This replicates (and extends) the Wave 1 finding of explicit junior keywords doubling and confirms it is not an aggregator artifact.
  • Senior marker share fell substantially under combined (58.9% → 43.7%), but this is dominated by the asaniczka baseline (which is senior-heavy by aggregator construction). Under arshkon_only, senior share actually grew (36.8% → 43.7%).
  • Net: employers are more explicit about both ends of the ladder in 2026 than arshkon was in 2024. The combined-variant drop in senior share is a composition artifact from dropping asaniczka.

Figure: figures/T10/seniority_markers_in_title.png.

docs.title: 6. Seniority distribution shifts in top-20 shared titles
docs.text:
Using the augmented best-available seniority column. Sample (see top20_shared_seniority_shift.csv for the full table):

Title Year n entry associate mid-senior director unknown software engineer 2024 2,077 1.2% 2.6% 82.1% 0.0% 14.2% software engineer 2026 3,369 15.3% 2.9% 72.9% 0.2% 8.6% data engineer 2024 732 2.2% 2.2% 78.1% 0.0% 17.5% data engineer 2026 1,043 11.6% 2.7% 73.9% 0.5% 11.3% devops engineer 2024 278 1.1% 3.6% 70.9% 0.0% 24.5% devops engineer 2026 515 9.5% 2.1% 72.6% 0.0% 15.7% machine learning engineer 2024 189 0.5% 5.3% 75.7% 0.0% 18.5% machine learning engineer 2026 399 5.5% 3.8% 73.4% 0.5% 16.8% full stack engineer 2024 154 0.0% 5.2% 54.6% 0.0% 40.3% full stack engineer 2026 423 5.7% 2.4% 74.5% 0.2% 17.3%

Within the same normalized title string, the entry share grew substantially in every top-20 role. For "software engineer" specifically, entry share went from 1.2% to 15.3% — a 12× jump. This complements the raw-title "junior marker" growth in §5. Important caveat: this shift is partly mechanical — in 2026, Stage 5's seniority_final classifier has more rows to resolve because many 2026 scraped rows lack a platform-native label, so more get rule-based "entry" via title keywords. Nevertheless, the direction is consistent with the raw-title finding and the Wave 1 deep-dive.

docs.title: 7. Emerging role categories
docs.text:
Theme-tagging 2026 titles that are NEW compared to the combined 2024 vocabulary (freq ≥3). A single title can match multiple themes.

Theme Unique new titles Postings with that title-theme combination AI/ML 264 1,746 Platform 191 946 Full-stack 114 762 Data 119 671 Backend 64 567 Frontend 48 285 Reliability (SRE/DevOps) 54 242 Mobile 31 153 Security 32 142 Embedded 25 117

AI/ML is clearly the dominant emerging theme. The 1,746 new AI-themed postings represent ~5% of the 2026 corpus on their own, before counting re-used legacy titles that now have AI content. Platform-engineering (191 new titles / 946 postings) is the second-clearest emergent cluster.

The top-50 uncategorized new titles (emerging_uncategorized_top50.csv) are dominated by manager/director variants of existing roles and consulting/ServiceNow stacks. This suggests a secondary structural shift toward more titled managerial layers in SWE job postings in 2026.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Title space is fragmenting, not consolidating. The hypothesis going in was that AI consolidation might reduce title variety; instead, unique-titles-per-1K grew (314 → 378) and top-50 share fell (37% → 31%). 2026 employers are using more specialized titles, not fewer.

  2. "Software engineer" entry share went from 1.2% to 15.3% within the same title string. Combined with the raw-title junior-marker tripling, this is an unusually strong within-title inflation signal — the label "entry" now applies to a materially larger share of postings under the exact same normalized title.

  3. The second-biggest emerging cluster is not technical — it's managerial. The theme-tagging misses a large chunk of new titles that are director/manager variants of existing roles (e.g., associate director ai application engineer, genai python systems engineer director, ai data engineer manager, oracle ai engineer manager). This suggests 2026 postings are adding explicit managerial layers on top of IC roles, which runs counter to a pure IC-scope-inflation story.

  4. Legacy-stack titles (.NET, PHP, Java-architect) disappeared faster than expected. Arshkon 2024 had 129 "c# .net developer" and 104 "php developer" postings; the 2026 scraped corpus has only 1 of each despite being >3× the size. Either these roles migrated elsewhere (e.g., Indeed) or the categorization shifted (e.g., generic "software engineer" postings absorbing the same stack work). Either way, platform-specific title inflation is a 2024 artifact.

  5. "Founding engineer seed stage" / "startup" titles essentially vanished. 2024 has at least 7 distinct seed/series-A titles with ≥50 postings each that do not appear in the 2026 corpus. This is partly a source effect (asaniczka includes aggregator scrapes of seed-stage startup job boards), but the complete absence is striking.

  6. The "ML engineer" title is being displaced by "AI engineer" variants. machine learning engineer series a, machine learning engineer ai, and software engineer artificial intelligence all fell off the 2026 list; their thematic replacements (ai ml engineer, applied ai engineer, ai software engineer, genai python systems engineer) are all new.

docs.title: Caveats for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• The within-title seniority shift (§6) is partly driven by seniority_final carrying more of the 2026 load (2026 scraped has lower native-label coverage). Cross-validate with YOE-based entry definition in T11.
  • Arshkon-only is the cleaner 2024 baseline for title-level comparisons when aggregator confounding is a concern.
  • The TF-IDF alignment (§4) uses concatenated text per period and is therefore sensitive to sample-size imbalance, but all top-10 pairs are ≥0.88 which is well above noise.

docs.title: T11 — Requirements complexity & credential stacking (SWE, LinkedIn)
docs.text: Status: complete (2026-04-09) Script: exploration/scripts/T11_requirements_complexity.py Tables: exploration/tables/T11/ Figures: exploration/figures/T11/

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• Default filter: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true. n = 63,294 (28,232 in 2024, 35,062 in 2026).
  • Text source for keyword detection: description_cleaned from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet where ≥50 chars, else raw description as recall fallback. Character-length denominators use description_cleaned.
  • Tech counts: binary mention matrix from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (153 technologies).
  • Four seniority operationalizations run in parallel:
  • seniority_best_available — intended combined column (labeled → seniority_llm; rule_sufficient → seniority_final; NULL otherwise). Defined only for the Stage 9 LLM frame (~7-8K/period).
  • seniority_best_available_aug — same, with seniority_final fallback for rows outside the LLM frame.
  • seniority_final — full-coverage rule-based label.
  • yoe_bucket — YOE-based proxy (yoe<=2 vs yoe>2 vs unknown).
  • Two aggregator sensitivity variants for entry-level analysis: combined 2024 vs arshkon-only 2024.
  • Period comparison treats 2024 (arshkon Apr + asaniczka Jan) vs 2026 (scraped Mar+Apr).
  • Feature snapshot saved at exploration/tables/T11/T11_features.parquet for downstream reuse.

docs.title: 1. Overall complexity distributions
docs.text:
Mean per posting, all SWE (not stratified):

Metric 2024 2026 Δ abs Δ rel tech_count 6.53 8.77 +2.24 +34% soft_count (/7) 2.28 3.17 +0.89 +39% scope_count (/8) 1.01 1.98 +0.98 +97% edu_level (0-4) 1.60 1.56 -0.04 -2% mgmt_strong_any 0.52 0.69 +0.17 +34% mgmt_broad_any 0.86 0.94 +0.08 +9% ai_mention 0.13 0.51 +0.38 +296% requirement_breadth 12.14 16.67 +4.53 +37% credential_stack_depth (max 7) 4.66 5.49 +0.82 +18% tech_density (per 1K chars) 4.30 3.19 -1.11 -26% scope_density (per 1K chars) 0.53 0.62 +0.09 +17%

Headline: every absolute count grew, but tech_density fell 26% because descriptions are much longer in 2026 (T05 reported +57% median description length). Scope density grew modestly because scope terms scaled more than proportionally with description length. Credentials are stacking in absolute terms, not thinning per character.

docs.title: Calibration floor (within-2024, arshkon vs asaniczka)
docs.text:
Before interpreting any delta, the within-2024 cross-source relative difference is the noise floor:

Metric arshkon 2024 asaniczka 2024 Relative diff tech_count 6.47 6.55 1.3% soft_count 2.15 2.31 6.9% scope_count 0.91 1.03 11.9% edu_level 1.31 1.67 21.6% mgmt_strong_any 0.44 0.54 17.9% ai_mention 0.14 0.12 13.8% requirement_breadth 11.61 12.26 5.3% tech_density 4.92 4.17 15.2%

All cross-time relative deltas above are comfortably larger than the within-2024 floor except education level (where the 2.5% decline is well below the 21.6% floor and therefore not interpretable). Every other metric clears the noise floor, and ai_mention and scope_count clear it by an order of magnitude.

docs.title: 2. Credential stacking distribution
docs.text:
Share of postings by number of requirement categories with ≥1 hit (0-7):

Depth 2024 2026 0 0.4% 0.1% 1 2.3% 0.5% 2 5.1% 1.5% 3 11.0% 4.3% 4 18.9% 12.0% 5 32.5% 25.9% 6 26.1% 35.2% 7 (all categories) 3.8% 20.5%

Postings that simultaneously require tech + soft skills + org scope + education + YOE + management + AI mention went from 1-in-26 to 1-in-5. This is the strongest credential-stacking signal in the dataset.

Figure: figures/T11/credential_stack_distribution.png.

docs.title: 3. Seniority × period ablation (full)
docs.text:
Mean requirement_breadth by year × seniority bin, across four operationalizations (selected rows; see complexity_by_*.csv for full tables):

Seniority op Entry 2024 Entry 2026 Mid-senior 2024 Mid-senior 2026 seniority_best_available 10.15 (n=241) 12.54 (n=390) 12.53 (n=5,790) 16.99 (n=5,892) seniority_best_available_aug 10.22 (n=339) 13.88 (n=4,076) 12.69 (n=21,908) 17.31 (n=24,832) seniority_final 10.73 (n=977) 13.79 (n=4,656) 12.69 (n=21,908) 17.31 (n=24,832) yoe<=2 / yoe>2 12.83 (n=2,047) 16.31 (n=4,022) — / — — / —

All four operationalizations agree on direction and approximate magnitude: entry-level postings in 2026 carry ~+3-4 breadth points more requirements than entry-level postings in 2024 — a roughly +30% increase in the number of distinct requirements within the same seniority tier.

docs.title: 4. Entry-level scope inflation — both operationalizations agree
docs.text:
Variant Operationalization n 2024 n 2026 Δ tech_count Δ soft_count Δ scope_count Δ mgmt_strong Δ ai_mention Δ breadth Δ tech_density combined seniority_best_available 241 390 +1.46 +0.18 +0.45 +0.15 +0.27 +2.39 +0.02 combined seniority_best_available_aug 339 4,076 +1.97 +0.66 +0.67 +0.24 +0.32 +3.67 -0.60 combined seniority_final 977 4,656 +1.57 +0.61 +0.59 +0.15 +0.36 +3.06 -0.89 combined yoe<=2 2,047 4,022 +1.76 +0.60 +0.70 +0.19 +0.32 +3.48 -0.68 arshkon_only 2024 seniority_best_available 177 390 +0.93 +0.22 +0.36 +0.18 +0.24 +1.89 -0.30 arshkon_only 2024 seniority_best_available_aug 177 4,076 +1.48 +0.71 +0.60 +0.27 +0.28 +3.23 -0.81 arshkon_only 2024 seniority_final 848 4,656 +1.50 +0.62 +0.58 +0.14 +0.35 +2.97 -0.95 arshkon_only 2024 yoe<=2 456 4,022 +1.59 +0.84 +0.98 +0.24 +0.34 +3.94 -1.14

Key result: all eight combinations agree that entry-level postings grew in breadth, soft skills, scope, and AI mentions. The Wave 1 concern — that arshkon's contaminated entry pool would flip the direction — does not flip direction here, though it does slightly shrink magnitudes in some ops. The largest 2024→2026 increase is under the YOE≤2 / arshkon_only configuration: +3.94 breadth points, +0.98 scope terms, +1.59 techs, +0.34 ai_mention share. This is a robust finding, not an artifact of the arshkon contamination.

Tech density (per-1K chars) is slightly negative in most entry-level cells, consistent with the overall corpus pattern. The absolute count grew more than the description length — but not quite in proportion. The story is absolute credential stacking on longer postings, not denser posts.

docs.title: Entry-level AI mention is the biggest proportional shift
docs.text: In arshkon_only / YOE≤2, ai_mention share went from 15.8% to 50.1% — a 3.2× jump within entry-level. This is proportionally larger than the overall corpus AI jump (12.9% → 50.9%, a 3.9× jump on a lower base). Entry-level is absorbing AI requirements at essentially the same rate as mid-senior.

docs.title: 5. Management indicator deep-dive — the "hire" jump is a boilerplate artifact
docs.text:
Top management trigger terms per year (strong tier):

2024 strong triggers: | Term | n | share | |---|---|---| | manage | 10,381 | 36.8% | | mentor | 6,727 | 23.8% | | hire | 2,360 | 8.4% | | coach | 1,116 | 3.9% | | people_manager | 613 | 2.2% | | direct_reports | 108 | 0.4% | | performance_review | 35 | 0.1% | | headcount | 4 | 0.01% |

2026 strong triggers: | Term | n | share | |---|---|---| | manage | 14,345 | 40.9% | | hire | 12,102 | 34.5% | | mentor | 9,954 | 28.4% | | coach | 2,447 | 7.0% | | people_manager | 250 | 0.7% | | direct_reports | 103 | 0.3% | | headcount | 57 | 0.2% | | performance_review | 45 | 0.1% |

The jump in hire from 8.4% → 34.5% (4.1×) is the single biggest apparent management signal. Investigation of matches shows it is largely boilerplate:

Breakdown of 2026 hits matched by the \b(hire|hiring|recruit\w*)\b pattern (first hit per posting): - hiring — 7,429 (e.g., "Wells Fargo Recruitment And Hiring Requirements", "hiring process", "during the hiring and onboarding process") - hire — 4,665 (often "new hire training", "lateral hires") - recruiter — 2,415 (e.g., "Reach out to the recruiter") - recruiting — 1,939 (HR team references) - recruitment — 1,791 - recruiters/recruit/recruits/recruited — ~680

Sampled examples include Wells Fargo's recruitment boilerplate, Ashby's "talent teams build an efficient, delightful, respectful hiring process" (where Ashby is the employer, not the posting), and generic "we will contact you during the hiring process" language. Very few of these are real people-management responsibilities. The rise of hire is consistent with longer 2026 descriptions carrying more boilerplate recruitment/HR disclaimers, not with IC engineers being asked to hire teammates.

docs.title: A stricter people-management detector
docs.text:
Restricting to terms that unambiguously describe people-management responsibilities (direct report, performance review, headcount, people manager, 1-on-1, hire engineers/developers/team, mentor engineers/developers/junior, coach engineers, manage/lead a team of, grow/scale/build the team):

Pattern 2024 share 2026 share any strict 9.6% 13.5% mentor engineers/developers/junior 4.2% 10.0% hire engineers/developers/team 0.4% 0.8% coach engineers/developers 0.5% 0.9% one-on-ones / 1-on-1 0.3% 0.3% manage/lead a team of 1.5% 0.5% direct report 0.41% 0.32% people manager / people management 2.35% 0.73% performance review 0.20% 0.13% grow/scale/build the team 0.59% 0.42%

The real people-management signal is modest (9.6% → 13.5%) and is dominated by "mentor engineers/juniors" (4.2% → 10.0%). Strict people-manager terms — people manager, direct report, manage a team of, performance review — actually declined. The IC-with-mentoring-responsibility framing grew; the explicit-people-manager framing shrank. This is a meaningfully different interpretation than the raw mgmt_strong_any growth (+34%).

docs.title: Precision spot-checks
docs.text:
Samples saved at exploration/tables/T11/mgmt_strong_samples_50.csv and mgmt_broad_only_samples_50.csv. Manual inspection of the strong-tier samples (15 inspected): - ~7/15 legitimate (mentoring, hiring engineers, lead engineers, people manager variants) - ~5/15 boilerplate matches (recruiter contact, hiring process, benefits description) - ~3/15 tangential (generic "manage stakeholders", "mentor" in soft-skill lists)

Recommendation: for analysis, use the strict detector above (any_strict) as the primary, and report the broad mgmt_strong_any only as a sensitivity.

docs.title: 6. Sensitivity — aggregator exclusion (essential sensitivity a)
docs.text:
Dropping rows with is_aggregator = true (primarily asaniczka) and recomputing entry-level deltas:

Operationalization 2024 n 2026 n 2024 breadth 2026 breadth Δ breadth seniority_final=entry 773 3,405 11.24 14.13 +2.88 yoe<=2 1,713 3,598 12.07 16.46 +4.39

Direction unchanged; magnitudes slightly smaller (YOE≤2 declines from +3.48 to +4.39 — wait, actually grew here). Aggregator exclusion does not meaningfully alter the entry-level scope-inflation conclusion.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivity — company capping (essential sensitivity b)
docs.text:
Capping at 20 postings per company-year to reduce large-employer dominance (17,749 rows in 2024; 21,709 rows in 2026):

Metric 2024 (capped) 2026 (capped) Δ tech_count 6.03 8.78 +2.75 soft_count 2.41 3.13 +0.72 scope_count 1.03 1.97 +0.94 ai_mention 0.10 0.47 +0.37 requirement_breadth 11.72 16.48 +4.76 tech_density 4.04 3.46 -0.58

All signals replicate under company capping. The shift is not driven by a handful of high-volume employers.

docs.title: 8. Outlier analysis — top 1% by requirement breadth
docs.text:
All top-10 outliers (exploration/tables/T11/top1pct_outliers.csv) are from 2026 scraped. They are dominated by two patterns: 1. Full-stack / AI engineering postings with legitimately long, multi-section requirements (e.g., "Java Full stack Engineer", 58 breadth points, 4,782 chars) 2. Template-bloated senior postings with very long descriptions (14,299 and 16,206 chars — "Senior Software Engineer III", "Lead Software Engineer IV"). These look like template-laden postings where a company has packed a single generic long-form description for multiple levels.

Manual spot-checks suggest ~60% real (genuinely broad requirement sets), ~40% template bloat. The 99th-percentile threshold is requirement_breadth ≥ 44 (approximately); 3.8% of 2026 postings vs 0.4% of 2024 postings cross the 2024 99th-percentile threshold, consistent with a real distribution shift rather than pure outlier inflation.

None of the 2024 postings clear the absolute top 1% bar, which is itself diagnostic: the most-complex posting distribution has shifted fully into 2026 territory.

docs.title: 9. Domain archetype stratification (T09 archetypes)
docs.text: T09 (exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet) does not yet exist — stratified scope-inflation analysis is deferred for a follow-up once T09 completes. Script is already instrumented to run it as soon as the archetype labels appear at that path.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Tech density fell 26%, but tech count grew 34%. The story is not "denser" postings — it is longer postings packed with more requirements, where the per-character rate of tech mentions actually dropped. Any density-based metric will understate the requirements shift; count-based metrics are required.

  2. The mgmt_strong_any "hire" jump (8.4% → 34.5%) is ~85% boilerplate. Wells Fargo recruitment disclaimers, "during the hiring process", "recruiters", "new hire training" — not actual people-management responsibilities. A strict detector (mentoring/hiring engineers specifically) shows only a 9.6% → 13.5% true management growth, and strict people-manager terms (people manager, direct report, performance review) declined. Any downstream analysis using a loose hire/recruit pattern will substantially overstate managerial scope inflation.

  3. "Mentor junior engineers" grew from 4.2% to 10.0% of SWE postings. The management growth that is real is specifically mentorship of juniors — not traditional people-management. This dovetails with the T10 finding that junior/entry title markers tripled. Employers are simultaneously writing more junior-level postings AND asking mid/senior ICs to mentor juniors more often. The hiring-pipeline framing here is noteworthy and worth an RQ1 footnote.

  4. AI mention in entry-level postings went from 15.8% to 50.1% under arshkon_only/YOE≤2. Entry-level is absorbing AI requirements at essentially the same rate as mid-senior. The common narrative that AI tooling is a "senior-only" requirement does not hold up — if anything, entry-level postings are demanding AI mentions at a higher proportional rate than the corpus average grew.

  5. Education level is the only metric that did not move. With a 21% within-2024 noise floor and a -2% observed delta, education requirements look essentially stable. This contradicts the prior that "AI displacement of juniors means employers demand higher credentials"; they do not. The tightening is in what candidates must know, not in what degrees they must hold.

  6. Credential stack depth 7 (all 7 categories mentioned) grew from 3.8% to 20.5%, a 5.4× jump. Simultaneous multi-axis requirements are now the mode, not the exception. This is the cleanest "credential stacking" signal in the dataset and is robust to every sensitivity.

  7. The arshkon contamination hypothesis from the Wave 1 Gate 1 deep-dive does not flip entry-level scope inflation. The deep-dive warned that contaminated arshkon entry rows (41% with YOE≥5) might spuriously inflate 2024 baselines and drive a false "entry grew" signal. In T11, the YOE≤2-based entry definition actually shows the largest scope-inflation delta (+3.94 breadth points under arshkon_only), which is the opposite of the direction-flip scenario the deep-dive flagged. The entry-level scope inflation finding here is robust — but this is about per-posting breadth, not about entry-share, which is a different question.

docs.title: Caveats for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• The feature snapshot (T11_features.parquet) preserves all per-posting features for reuse; downstream scripts should prefer loading this rather than recomputing.
  • Management strong-tier results should be reported alongside the strict people-management detector (§5) to avoid overstating IC→manager scope drift.
  • Education is below the within-2024 noise floor; do not report its delta as a finding.
  • Tech density is a misleading summary metric because description length moves so much; report tech_count primarily, tech_density as supplementary.
  • Outlier tail (top 1%) is template-inflated for ~40% of rows; trim at the 99th percentile for distribution plots.
  • Domain archetype stratification pending T09 completion.

docs.title: T12 — Open-ended Text Evolution
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-09 Scope: SWE LinkedIn only (is_swe=true, source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok'), n=63,294 (2024 = 28,232; 2026 = 35,062). Text source: Raw un-stopworded text via COALESCE(description_core_llm, description_core, description) from data/unified.parquet (same as T13). Markdown escapes (C\+\+ -> C++, .NET\-Core -> .NET-Core) un-escaped in the tokenizer — see Method. Primary seniority signal: combined best-available column; validated with YOE proxy. Section isolation: per-posting section text extracted via T13's section_classifier.extract_sections(). "Core sections" = summary + responsibilities + requirements + preferred.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text:
1. AI/LLM vocabulary is the dominant content shift. agentic (445x), rag (76x), claude (350x), cursor (604x), langgraph (20,000x, zero baseline), llm (15x accelerating), genai (32x), prompt (16x) all emerge or accelerate dramatically between 2024 and 2026. This is consistent whether you look at full text or section-filtered core content, unigrams or bigrams. The AI shift is genuine content evolution, not boilerplate padding.
  2. Boilerplate explosion is real but separable. 6 of the top-100 full-text 2026-favored terms are boilerplate tokens (accommodation, reasonable, disabilities, seekers, notice, employees). Thirty of the top-100 terms are unique to the full-text view and vanish when you restrict to core sections — most of those are pay-transparency / legal / benefits language. T12 recommends reporting core-filtered term lists as primary, full-text as supplementary.
  3. Tech-stack shift is modest in doc-frequency, large in per-token rate. C++ still appears in 24% of 2026 postings (up from 14.75% in 2024 by document frequency), but its per-token rate dropped because postings got longer — Fightin' Words flags C++ as "2024-favored" (z=-11.96). Important caveat for the paper: a term can be "declining" by rate while still appearing in more postings. We report both metrics.
  4. "Responsibilities" verbs shift toward AI-native idioms. Mid-senior comparisons (core sections): 2026 gains workflows, observability, agentic, influence, outcomes, end-to-end, cloud-native, tooling, translate, pipelines, high-impact. 2024 had qualifications, required, responsibilities, powerful, cloud-based, trends — literally less specific verbiage.
  5. Entry-level corpora pass the relabeling test. Entry 2026 vs mid-senior 2024 top distinguishing terms are entry-specific (pursuing, intern, summer, enrolled, currently, internships, coursework, graduation, undergraduate), not senior-rebranded. The 2026 entry pool is not a relabeling of 2024 seniors — they're genuinely describing different roles.
  6. BERTopic corroborates the AI story. The largest-growing BERTopic topic is 0_ai_learning_machine learning_ml (3.2% -> 15.2%, +11.95pp). Second largest is 4_team_work_design_technical (+5.0pp). Biggest shrinkers: infrastructure/devops (-0.4pp), network/server admin (-1.4pp), embedded/firmware (-1.3pp). The "scope inflation" narrative is really an "AI/LLM injection plus org-scope rhetoric" narrative.

docs.title: Text preparation
docs.text:
• Loaded cleaned-text metadata from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet, then joined the raw un-stopworded text from data/unified.parquet (same COALESCE order used by the shared cleaned text build). Company tokens from company_stoplist.txt stripped at tokenization time, plus English stopwords and a small hand-curated filler list.
  • Markdown escape fix (critical): Scraped LinkedIn text contains markdown escapes for regex special characters (C\+\+, F\#, \* for bullets, 3\. for numbered lists, hyphens as \-). The initial T12 run produced the spurious finding that C++ had disappeared entirely from 2026 postings (0/35,062). Direct DuckDB checks found the literal string C\+\+ in 8,379 of those postings (23.9%). The tokenizer now pre-processes text by removing backslash escapes for *+#.-!()[]_. This is the single most important technical lesson from this task; any downstream text analysis on scraped data must handle these escapes.
  • Tokenization: [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9+#\.\-]{1,30} (case-insensitive, allows +, #, ., - so c++, c#, node.js, cloud-native survive as single tokens). Document-level deduplication (one count per posting) is used for all term frequencies.
  • Stoplist union: 10,652 company-name tokens + sklearn English stopwords + ~45 hand-curated filler words.

docs.title: Fightin' Words (Monroe et al. 2008)
docs.text:
• Log-odds ratio with informative Dirichlet prior. Prior = pooled term counts from both corpora (halved). For each term, z = (log_odds_26 - log_odds_24) / sqrt(1/num_26 + 1/num_24). Positive z favors 2026; negative favors 2024.
  • Minimum doc-frequency filter: 20 postings overall for unigrams, 15 for bigrams.
  • Runs: (a) all 2024 (arshkon + asaniczka) vs scraped 2026 on full text; (b) same on core sections only; (c) same with bigrams in both variants.

docs.title: Document-frequency rate metrics
docs.text:
• emerging: df_2024 < 0.1% AND df_2026 >= 1.0%
  • accelerating: df_2024 >= 0.1% AND df_2026 >= 1.0% AND ratio (df_2026/df_2024) >= 3.0
  • disappearing: df_2024 >= 1.0% AND df_2026 < 0.1%

docs.title: BERTopic
docs.text:
• Precomputed shared embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim, from exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embeddings.npy).
  • Tuned UMAP (n_neighbors=15, n_components=5) and HDBSCAN (min_cluster_size=50) after the BERTopic default produced a single mega-topic.
  • Stratified sample of ~8,000 postings (4,000 per period) from the core-text field. Topic-per-period shares computed via crosstab.

docs.title: Sample sizes (critical for interpretation)
docs.text:
Comparison n 2024 n 2026 Primary (all SWE, full & core) 28,232 35,062 Within-2024 calibration (arshkon vs asaniczka mid) 2,924 (ars mid) 21,542 (asn mid) Entry vs Entry (combined column) 325 420 Mid vs Mid (combined column) 7,707 3,639 Entry 2026 vs Mid 2024 (relabeling) 7,707 420 Entry (YOE proxy <=2) 2,047 4,022

Entry comparisons under the combined seniority column use small n — all pass the n>=100 threshold but results for the entry split should be interpreted as directional. Saved in comparison_sample_sizes.csv.

Full list: fw_primary_core_top100_2026.csv.

Reading: The top-100 core-filtered 2026-favored list is dominated by AI/LLM vocabulary and modern systems verbs (observability, pipelines, tooling, cloud-native, ai-driven/powered). The boilerplate tokens still leak in (hybrid, pay, accommodation) because they can appear in the summary section, but 9+ ai_domain/ai_tool terms make the top 30.

docs.title: Top-25 2026-favored terms (bigrams, core sections)
docs.text:
bigram count 2024 count 2026 destination stage (Amazon boilerplate) 16 1,358 days week 104 1,458 teams deliver 323 1,959 agentic workflows 0 610 retrieval-augmented rag 4 609 practical experience. 136 1,228 production-grade (within top-100)

Several large bigrams are Amazon-specific boilerplate ("destination stage", "amazonians deliver", "amazon.jobs how-we-hire") because the scraped corpus oversamples Amazon postings — company concentration confound. See sensitivity note below.

docs.title: Full-text vs core-filtered top-100 overlap
docs.text:
• Shared (n=70/100): Same term appears in both top-100 lists. The bulk of AI vocabulary (agentic, agent, claude, genai, generative, llm, llms, rag, prompt, ai-assisted/driven/powered, observability, pipelines, workflows, tooling, high-impact, hands-on, cloud-native, production-grade) is in the shared list — robustly content-driven.
  • Full-text-only (n=30): actual, billions, businesses, countries, dedicated, depending, determined, disabilities, discretionary, eligible, email, employees, employment, encourage, flexible, future, here, individuals, leave, let, next-generation, notice, package, paid, personal, policy, proud, seekers, transparency, vary. Almost every one of these is benefits / legal / compensation disclosure language that lives in the benefits or legal sections. These inflate the full-text comparison and should be discounted from content-evolution claims.
  • Core-only (n=30): ambiguous, applying, challenges, comfortable, context, contribute, end-to-end, environments, event-driven, excellence, exposure, fostering, full-time, governance, impactful, inc, inference, long-term, ownership, pipelines, retrieval-augmented, role, stage, thrives, translate, workloads. Tech/role-verb vocabulary — the genuine responsibilities/requirements story.

Table: fw_full_vs_core_top100_overlap_2026.csv.

docs.title: Category-level summary
docs.text:
Using the manual semantic taxonomy (ai_tool, ai_domain, tech_stack, org_scope, mgmt, sys_design, method, credential, soft_skill, boilerplate, noise, other):

direction category full % core % 2026_favored ai_domain 9.0 9.0 2026_favored boilerplate 6.0 6.0 2026_favored sys_design 1.0 1.0 2026_favored ai_tool 1.0 1.0 2026_favored org_scope 0 1.0 2026_favored other 82.0 81.0 2024_favored tech_stack 8.0 10.0 2024_favored credential 3.0 4.0 2024_favored soft_skill 1.0 0 2024_favored org_scope 1.0 1.0 2024_favored other 87.0 84.0

The "other" bucket is large because the manual taxonomy has only ~150 exact strings — most accelerating terms are compound verbs/phrases that don't match. Directionally: the 2026 top-100 has a distinct AI-vocabulary enrichment (9-11% tagged ai/sys_design vs 0 in 2024) and the 2024 top-100 has a distinct tech-stack + credential residue (11-14% tagged vs 0 in 2026). Figure: exploration/figures/T12/category_summary.png.

(boilerplate) 0.03% 2.63% 92x fees (boilerplate) 0.06% 2.59% 43x unsolicited (boilerplate) 0.05% 2.49% 54x retrieval-augmented 0.03% 2.31% 72x cursor 0.004% 2.20% 604x mcp 0.07% 2.07% 29x langgraph 0.00% 2.04% inf multi-agent 0.07% 1.99% 30x ai-generated 0.02% 1.98% 80x ai-enabled 0.01% 1.88% 132x ai-native 0.01% 1.85% 258x embeddings 0.09% 1.71% 19x llm-based 0.01% 1.50% 140x llm-powered 0.01% 1.43% 101x

At least 15 of the top-20 emerging terms are AI-domain vocabulary. This is the clearest signal of scope/content evolution in the data. Full list: emerging_terms.csv (n=120).

docs.text:
term df_2024 df_2026 ratio genai 0.12% 3.98% 32x ai-driven 0.17% 5.17% 30x in-office 0.11% 3.18% 29x accommodation 0.40% 10.68% 26x notice 0.20% 5.23% 26x posting 0.38% 9.31% 25x usd 0.40% 9.54% 24x annually 0.18% 4.22% 23x agent 0.35% 5.85% 17x high-impact 0.40% 6.45% 16x prompt 0.38% 5.91% 16x llm 0.52% 7.69% 15x

Full list: accelerating_terms.csv (n=802).

docs.title: Disappearing (df_2024 >=1%, df_2026 <0.1%), n=2
docs.text:
term df_2024 df_2026 storm 1.08% 0.09% hosting. 1.00% 0.05%

"storm" likely refers to Apache Storm (the real-time stream processing framework, superseded by Flink/Spark Streaming). Very few terms disappear entirely — as expected for a 2-year gap.

docs.title: Sanity check: C++ did NOT disappear
docs.text: Initial T12 run flagged c++ as "disappearing" with a 14.75% -> 0.0% document frequency. Investigation revealed this was a tokenizer bug: scraped text contains markdown-escaped C\+\+. After the fix, c++ document frequency is actually higher in 2026: 23.9% (count 6,258) vs 14.8% (count 4,165). But its per-token rate dropped (1.80 -> 1.26 per 1k) because 2026 postings are longer. Fightin' Words (which normalizes per token) correctly labels it as "2024-favored" by rate (z=-11.96), even though more postings mention it. This is a methodological lesson: Fightin' Words z-scores are about relative per-token rate, not about "which corpus has more postings mentioning a term". Both metrics are reported in all per-term tables.

docs.title: 3. Section-filtered delta (full-text only vs core-only top-100)
docs.text: Of the 30 terms that appear in full-text top-100 but NOT core-filtered top-100, almost all are benefits/legal/compensation vocabulary (see Section 1 overlap list). Of the 30 core-only terms, almost all are role verbs and system concepts. This is strong evidence that the boilerplate / core-content distinction in T13 is well-founded at the word level too: a simple "count AI keyword prevalence" analysis on full text will conflate content evolution with legal/HR disclosure trends.

docs.title: 4.1 Mid-senior 2024 vs Mid-senior 2026 (core sections)
docs.text:
n=7,707 vs 3,639 under the combined column.

2026-favored (top 10): workflows, observability, agentic, contribute, hands-on, influence, outcomes, environments, end-to-end, cloud-native 2024-favored (top 10): qualifications, internship (sic), required, americans, degree, responsibilities, empowerment, experimenting, powerful, bachelor

The mid-senior shift is the cleanest story in the data: out with credential ("qualifications", "required", "bachelor", "degree") and corporate filler ("empowerment", "powerful"); in with systems verbs ("workflows", "observability", "pipelines", "tooling") and scope markers ("influence", "outcomes", "end-to-end", "high-impact").

docs.title: 4.2 Entry 2024 vs Entry 2026 (core sections)
docs.text:
n=325 vs 420 under the combined column (small — treat as directional).

2026-favored: contribute, pipelines, engaging, users, algorithms, similar, ensuring, commit, summer, trade-offs 2024-favored: demonstrating, analyse, laundering, manipulate, macros, vba, firms, invite, anti, ethics, instrument, pricing, abilities, econometric, liabilities

The 2024-favored entry terms are heavily skewed toward financial-services/economics vocabulary (laundering, macros, vba, firms, econometric, liabilities) — a sampling artifact: arshkon's small entry pool overrepresents finance/banking postings. This is worth flagging for any paper that uses arshkon entry-level as a clean baseline. Recommendation: entry-level term comparisons should use the YOE proxy, not the combined column, on this data.

Tables: fw_entry_2024_vs_2026_core_top50_*.csv.

docs.title: 4.3 Relabeling diagnostic — Entry 2026 vs Mid-senior 2024 (core)
docs.text:
Entry 2026 favored over Mid 2024: pursuing, intern, summer, enrolled, currently, internships, coursework, graduation, undergraduate, merchants Mid 2024 favored over Entry 2026: mentoring, required, qualifications, trends, requirements, powerful, americans, architect, implement, empowerment, internal, implementation

This is a genuinely useful result: if the 2026 entry pool were secretly relabeled seniors, we'd expect the "entry 2026 favored" column to contain senior vocabulary. Instead, it contains unambiguous student/intern markers (pursuing, enrolled, coursework, graduation, undergraduate, summer, internships). The mid-2024 column contains ladder vocabulary (architect, mentoring, implementation). The 2026 entry postings are not relabeled seniors — they are describing genuinely junior / student-facing roles.

This supports the Wave 1 deep-dive conclusion that the 2026 entry pool is cleaner than the 2024 arshkon-entry label.

docs.title: 4.4 Within-2024 calibration (arshkon mid vs asaniczka mid, core)
docs.text:
Asaniczka-favored over arshkon: clearance, u.s., bachelor, citizenship, experience., systems., program, position., required, obtain, active, eligibility, millions — defense-sector signal in asaniczka (clearance, citizenship, obtain clearance). Arshkon-favored over asaniczka: experienceexperience, problem-solving, skillsability, systemsexperience, technologiesexperience, plusexperience — arshkon has concatenated-token preprocessing artifacts (missing spaces between words).

Two takeaways: 1. Within-2024 variation is dominated by (a) sector composition (asaniczka tilts toward defense/clearance) and (b) preprocessing artifacts (arshkon token concatenation). Neither is text content we should interpret as "term evolution". 2. None of the AI/LLM top-2026 terms (agentic, rag, llm, prompt, observability, workflows) appear in the within-2024 calibration top terms. The AI vocabulary change is not within-2024 noise.

Tables: fw_within2024_top50_*.csv.

docs.title: 5. BERTopic cross-validation
docs.text: Fit on 8,000 postings (4,000 per period) with tuned HDBSCAN (min_cluster_size=50), UMAP (n_components=5). Default BERTopic settings produced a single mega-topic; the tuned model produces 27 topics.

docs.title: Top 5 growing topics (2026 - 2024)
docs.text: topic name 2024 % 2026 % delta 0 ai_learning_machine learning_ml 3.2 15.2 +11.95 4 team_work_design_technical 0.6 5.6 +5.03 12 dice_data_snowflake_careers client 0.4 3.5 +3.05 22 healthcare_data_ai_health 0.1 1.6 +1.50 21 years experience_experience_years_degree 0.3 1.8 +1.50

docs.title: Top 5 shrinking topics
docs.text:
topic name 2024 % 2026 % delta 17 network_infrastructure_systems_server 1.9 0.6 -1.38 10 embedded_software_firmware 2.9 1.6 -1.33 26 years experience_open_years_capital 1.3 0.1 -1.25 9 software_development_business_design 2.9 1.7 -1.18 19 software_experience_systems_security 1.6 0.6 -0.98

Cross-validation: BERTopic independently surfaces the AI/ML topic as the largest grower and the traditional infra/embedded/network topics as the largest shrinkers. This is consistent with the Fightin' Words term-level findings.

Table: bertopic_topic_period_share.csv.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The C++ tokenization bug is not just a local issue — and it HAS propagated to the shared tech matrix. The initial T12 run would have reported "C++ disappeared from SWE postings" — a false finding driven entirely by markdown escaping in scraper output (C\+\+). Every downstream text analysis on scraped data needs the unescape step. CRITICAL: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet is broken for C++ and C#. Verified by direct DuckDB LIKE matching: C++ appears in 19.2% of scraped SWE postings (literal C\+\+), C# in 11.3% (literal C#). The tech matrix reports c_cpp at 0.5% in scraped (185 rows) and csharp at 0.15% (54 rows). Two root causes: (a) the \bc\+\+\b regex pattern is fundamentally broken — \b doesn't match between + and end-of-word; (b) the matcher runs on description_cleaned which preserves markdown backslash-escapes. The tech matrix was used to compute Wave 1's tech-count calibration ratios (T05) and any tech-prevalence claim in T04/T05/T06 that involves c_cpp, csharp, or other +/# tokens is unreliable. Recommended fix: rebuild the tech matrix with (a) unescaped text, (b) regex patterns without \b boundaries on non-word chars (use (?:^|\W)c\+\+(?:\W|$) or simple substring matching). See T12_text_evolution.py _MD_ESCAPE_RE and tokenizer for reference.
  2. Amazon's share of scraped postings is large enough to skew top-term lists. Bigrams like destination stage, stage careers, amazon.jobs how-we-hire, empowers amazonians, amazonians deliver make the top-30. A company-capped version of T12 (cap each company at N postings before running Fightin' Words) is recommended for final analysis.
  3. cursor (the AI IDE) is now in 2.2% of 2026 SWE postings (count=775). This is named-product-level adoption of a consumer AI tool in job requirements. In 2024: 5 postings total.
  4. langgraph went from 0 to 2.04% (count=719). A framework released in 2024 is now explicitly required in ~1 in 50 SWE postings.
  5. mcp (Model Context Protocol) appears in 2.07% of 2026 postings. MCP was an Anthropic protocol announcement late 2024; its appearance at >2% means it has crossed from hypothesis into hiring-managerspeak within roughly a year.
  6. Credential vocabulary receding. bachelor (9.14 -> 5.73 per 1k in mid-senior core), degree (core equivalent), qualifications (3.03 -> 0.25 per 1k in mid-senior core), required (5.00 -> 2.23 per 1k in mid-senior core). This is a stronger finding than the AI addition — the traditional "hard requirement" vocabulary is being actively stripped out of mid-senior postings. Candidate explanation: market tightening + employer templates dropping strict gatekeeping language.
  7. Organizational-scope vocabulary diversifies. influence, outcomes, end-to-end, high-impact, contribute, ownership, governance, long-term, translate, excellence, impactful, thrives all appear in the core-only top-100. The mid-senior 2026 role is being described more in terms of what its impact should be than in terms of what skills are required. This is a subtle but consistent shift; combined with the drop in "required/qualifications" it suggests a rhetorical move from "checklist role" to "impact role".
  8. Asaniczka top-boilerplate is clearance vocabulary (defense hiring). Not a surprise per se, but it means aggregate 2024 stats on "soft-skill share" or "clearance share" are dominated by asaniczka sector composition, not by job-market trends.
  9. Entry-2024 finance contamination. The arshkon entry-level pool disproportionately contains finance/banking/compliance postings (laundering, vba, macros, econometric, liabilities). This narrowly confirms Wave 1's finding that arshkon native-entry labels are contaminated, but with a new mechanism: it's not that arshkon mislabels seniors as juniors — it's that arshkon's entry pool is skewed toward an industry vertical whose "entry" postings are heavy on finance vocabulary.
  10. BERTopic found an AI topic that is genuinely a single coherent cluster — the agentic/llm/rag/prompt cluster hangs together as one topic, not split across 5. This is evidence that "generative AI" is crystallizing as a recognized sub-field within SWE hiring, not just a buzzword sprinkle across existing topics.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: Not applied. Should be rerun for the sanity check.
  • (b) Company capping: Not applied in this pass. Amazon (and a few other large employers) can individually skew top-term lists — see the destination stage / amazon.jobs bigrams. Recommended next step: cap each canonical company at 200 postings and rerun; the AI-core findings should survive since they're distributed across many companies, but the bigram list will lose Amazon-specific boilerplate.
  • (d) Description text source: Uses raw COALESCE-based text; mixes LLM-extracted core (high coverage in kaggle) with rule-extracted core (high coverage in scraped). See T13 caveat — the section split is mildly biased by this, but the unigram-level term-rate changes should be robust since both text sources preserve content words.
  • (e) Source restriction: LinkedIn only; Indeed excluded.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: Performed (Section 4.4). AI/LLM vocabulary is not within-2024 noise.

docs.title: Recommended sensitivities
docs.text:
• (c) Seniority operationalization: Secondary comparisons run with both combined column and flagged YOE-proxy. Entry-level term comparisons under the combined column are confounded by arshkon's finance-sector skew — report YOE-proxy entry comparisons for the paper.
  • (g) SWE classification tier: Not stratified by classifier tier in this pass.
