docs.title: T13 — Linguistic & Structural Evolution of SWE LinkedIn Postings
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-09 Scope: SWE LinkedIn only (is_swe=true, source_platform='linkedin', is_english=true, date_flag='ok'), n=63,294 (2024 = 28,232; 2026 = 35,062). Primary seniority signal: combined best-available column (labeled -> seniority_llm; rule_sufficient -> seniority_final; else NULL). Validated against YOE proxy (yoe_extracted <= 2). Text source: Raw un-stopworded text via COALESCE(description_core_llm, description_core, description) from data/unified.parquet. The shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet has stopwords removed, which destroys phrase-level section headers and tone markers; for T13 we pulled the raw text directly.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text:
1. The 57% median description-length growth is real content expansion, not cosmetic padding. Core content (summary + responsibilities + requirements + preferred) grew from mean 1,596 chars to 3,014 chars (+88.9%); this alone contributes 85.7% of the gross length delta. Boilerplate (benefits + about_company + legal) grew from 70 chars to 750 chars (+976%) — large as a percentage but small in absolute share (41% of gross delta).
  2. Responsibilities scale more than requirements. Responsibilities mean grew +463 chars (+85%); requirements mean grew +320 chars (+60%). Scope-of-role text is expanding faster than skill lists.
  3. Calibration check: boilerplate growth is genuinely new, core growth is modest signal above noise. Within-2024 cross-source ratios for benefits (21.6x), about_company (50.5x), legal (40.0x) dwarf within-2024 variation. Core-section ratios are more modest: summary 2.5x, responsibilities 3.3x, requirements 2.7x, preferred 4.7x — real signal but within the "cross-period is roughly 2-5x the within-2024 baseline" band.
  4. Readability: postings became slightly more accessible. FK grade median 18.0 -> 16.7; Flesch reading ease 5.1 -> 10.5; Gunning fog 21.6 -> 20.3; avg sentence length 23.6 -> 21.5. All four move toward "easier to read" — modest but directionally consistent.
  5. Tone shifted from imperative toward inclusive. Inclusive markers per 1,000 chars rose from 1.13 to 1.59 (+41%); imperative markers fell from 0.88 to 0.73 (-18%); passive constructions fell from 0.37 to 0.26 (-30%). Marketing language barely moved (0.24 to 0.27).
  6. Entry-level postings diverge from mid-senior in tone, not structure. Under both the combined column and the YOE proxy, entry postings in 2026 show the largest jump in inclusive language (+52%) and the smallest drop in imperative density. Entry-level structural composition is close to mid-senior in both periods.

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
• Readability (textstat): FK grade, Flesch reading ease, Gunning fog, avg sentence length, TTR on first 1,000 chars, lexicon count, syllable count. Computed on a stratified sample of up to 2,000 rows per (period x seniority bucket). Total sample n=8,934.
  • Section classifier (exploration/artifacts/shared/section_classifier.py):
    • Detects explicit headers via markdown bold (**Header**), ALL-CAPS line, or Title Case: inline, then maps each header to one of 8 canonical sections via a regex vocabulary.
    • Text between recognized headers is attributed to the preceding header's canonical section.
    • When no headers are detected (e.g. arshkon's flat HTML-stripped prose), falls back to sentence-level keyword classification (keywords per section listed in the classifier docstring).
    • Sections: summary, responsibilities, requirements, preferred, benefits, about_company, legal, unclassified.
  • Tone markers: regex counts per 1,000 chars, computed on the same stratified readability sample.
  • All figures in exploration/figures/T13/, tables in exploration/tables/T13/, classifier artifact in exploration/artifacts/shared/section_classifier.py.

docs.title: 1. Description length growth: where did it come from?
docs.text:
Mean chars per section, all SWE LinkedIn.

Section 2024 mean 2026 mean Delta Share of gross growth summary 407 778 +370 22.4% responsibilities 545 1,008 +463 28.0% requirements 533 853 +320 19.3% preferred 109 375 +265 16.0% benefits 29 310 +282 17.0% about_company 33 381 +347 21.0% legal 8 59 +52 3.1% unclassified 556 113 -443 -26.8% Total 2,222 3,877 +1,655

The "unclassified" delta is negative because the scraped 2026 text has more regular headers, so less text falls through the fallback. The net picture:

  • Core content (summary + resp + req + preferred): 1,596 -> 3,014 (+1,418 chars, +88.9%)
  • Boilerplate (benefits + about_company + legal): 70 -> 750 (+681 chars, +976%)
  • Unclassified: 556 -> 113 (-443 chars)

Core content contributes 85.7% of the gross growth, boilerplate 41.1%, unclassified -26.8% (net 100%). Both are real, but core content dominates the absolute expansion.

See exploration/figures/T13/section_anatomy_chars.png (stacked bars, median chars by period x seniority) and section_anatomy_pct.png (normalized). Tables: length_growth_decomposition.csv, core_vs_boilerplate.csv.

docs.title: 2. Calibration: is this cross-period signal or within-2024 noise?
docs.text:
Within-2024 cross-source variation (arshkon vs asaniczka) gives the baseline. Ratio = |scraped_mean - arshkon_mean| / |asaniczka_mean - arshkon_mean|. Ratios >> 1 mean the cross-period change dwarfs within-2024 noise.

Section within-2024 delta cross-period delta ratio summary 225 555 2.47 responsibilities 112 370 3.29 requirements 92 245 2.67 preferred 68 322 4.71 benefits 14 293 21.59 about_company 7 353 50.47 legal 1 53 39.98 unclassified 255 234 0.92

Reading: Core sections move 2.5-4.7x the within-2024 baseline — real but modest signal. Boilerplate sections move 20-50x the baseline — this is the distinctive scraped-era change, not measurement noise. unclassified barely moves (0.92) because the decrease is roughly equal in magnitude to within-2024 variation, consistent with it being an artifact of header-detection coverage rather than a substantive change.

Caveat: The within-2024 baseline is small (asaniczka has very little about_company/legal text either), so the ratios for those three sections are mathematically inflated. The direction — large growth in boilerplate — is robust; the multiplier should be reported as ">>1" rather than precisely 50x.

docs.title: 3. Readability
docs.text:
Stratified sample (n=8,934; 2,000 per period x seniority cell, fewer where the cell is smaller).

Period Seniority n FK grade Flesch ease Gunning fog Avg sent len Lexicon TTR 1k 2024 ALL 4,386 18.0 5.1 21.6 23.6 283.5 0.669 2026 ALL 4,548 16.7 10.5 20.3 21.5 407.5 0.683 2024 entry/associate 325 16.5 10.0 20.0 22.2 285.0 0.693 2026 entry/associate 420 16.6 13.1 19.7 22.4 307.0 0.680 2024 mid-senior 2,000 18.6 2.7 22.1 24.4 293.5 0.667 2026 mid-senior 2,000 17.2 7.3 20.9 21.2 364.5 0.680 2024 director 61 19.1 0.7 22.5 26.6 409.0 0.647 2026 director 128 17.0 7.6 20.8 22.1 487.0 0.645

Observations:

  • 2026 postings are measurably easier to read across all seniorities and on all four readability metrics. Effect sizes are modest (~1 FK grade, ~5 points of Flesch ease) but directionally consistent.
  • Entry-level postings are already the easiest in 2024 (FK 16.5 vs mid-senior 18.6); in 2026 the entry-mid gap narrows dramatically (16.6 vs 17.2). Mid-senior postings became "entry-like" in readability. This is a candidate surprise.
  • TTR (lexical diversity) is flat across periods and seniorities. Vocabulary richness did not change; posts just got longer using the same vocabulary.
  • Directors had the longest sentences and hardest text in 2024 (sentence length 26.6, Flesch 0.7 — academic register). In 2026 they now read like mid-senior postings.

Full table: exploration/tables/T13/readability_by_period_seniority.csv.

docs.title: 4. Tone markers
docs.text:
Per 1,000 cleaned chars, means on the same stratified sample.

Period Seniority n Imperative Inclusive Marketing Passive 2024 ALL 4,386 0.88 1.13 0.24 0.37 2026 ALL 4,548 0.73 1.59 0.27 0.26 2024 entry/associate 325 0.91 0.96 0.17 0.37 2026 entry/associate 420 0.86 1.46 0.38 0.20 2024 mid-senior 2,000 0.87 1.08 0.24 0.41 2026 mid-senior 2,000 0.77 1.19 0.23 0.26 2024 yoe_entry (yoe<=2) 339 0.87 0.96 0.22 0.36 2026 yoe_entry (yoe<=2) 458 0.72 1.46 0.29 0.25 2024 director 61 0.83 1.13 0.29 0.41 2026 director 128 0.77 0.74 0.21 0.30

Observations:

  • Inclusive language (+41% overall) grew the most. Imperative and passive both fell. This is a clear friendly-casual tonal shift.
  • Entry/associate postings show the biggest inclusive-language jump (+52%) and the smallest imperative drop (-5.7% vs -11% for mid-senior). Entry postings are being rewritten to feel welcoming.
  • Director postings buck the trend — inclusive dropped 34% and marketing dropped 27%. The (small n=128) director sample in 2026 reads more businesslike, less cheerleader.
  • Marketing language is flat overall but doubled for entry postings (0.17 -> 0.38). The rockstar/passionate/exciting vocabulary is being deployed heaviest at the junior end.
  • Imperative-density drop may partly reflect longer postings diluting fixed-count markers; the effect persists in per-1k-chars normalization.

Tables: exploration/tables/T13/tone_summary_by_period_seniority.csv, tone_per_posting.csv.

docs.title: 5. Entry-level definitions agree on structure, disagree on tone dynamics
docs.text:
Comparing the combined-seniority entry bucket (n=325 in 2024, 420 in 2026 in the sample) with the YOE-proxy entry (yoe <=2, n=339 in 2024, 458 in 2026):

  • Structure: both definitions give very similar section-proportion results. Whatever "entry" means, 2026 entry postings have the same relative share of responsibilities/requirements as mid-senior 2026.
  • Tone: both definitions show the inclusive-language jump (+52% combined, +52% YOE-proxy) and similar imperative declines. They agree. This is a useful robustness check — Wave 1 found the entry-share direction depends on operationalization, but these within-entry dynamics are stable across both definitions.

The practical implication: the surprising finding that entry postings are being rewritten toward a friendly/welcoming register is robust to how "entry" is defined.

Table: exploration/tables/T13/entry_split_structure.csv.

docs.title: 6. Section classifier quality
docs.text:
• Tested by spot-checking 30 postings across sources (section_classifier_validation_sample.csv). Scraped postings with markdown headers produce clean splits; arshkon's HTML-stripped flat prose falls back to keyword sentence-level classification, which yields noisier but directionally correct proportions. Asaniczka is in between.
  • Known limitation: posts that use "Description:" as a header get classified under summary (via the keyword match), while posts using "Job Description" + bullets get unclassified until a subsection starts. This biases the 2024 (arshkon/asaniczka) corpora slightly more toward unclassified and summary — meaning the cross-period growth in summary is partly an artifact of scraped postings using cleaner structure. However, this works against the finding that boilerplate grew dramatically, not for it — if anything the boilerplate increase is understated in this classifier.
  • The classifier is deterministic regex-based, fast (~11 min for 63k rows), and importable from exploration/artifacts/shared/section_classifier.py. T12 uses it to filter boilerplate before running term comparisons.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Mid-senior postings became easier to read than 2024 entry postings. FK 17.2 in 2026 mid-senior < FK 16.5 in 2024 entry? No — 17.2 > 16.5. But the gap compressed. In 2024 the seniority ladder had ~2 FK grades between entry and mid-senior (16.5 -> 18.6). In 2026 the gap is 0.6 grades (16.6 -> 17.2). Description readability no longer tracks seniority. Possibly a casualty of templated/LLM-assisted posting drafts.
  2. Responsibilities grew more than requirements. The dominant narrative around AI and SWE is "requirements expanded (AI skills added)", but the largest absolute section-level delta is responsibilities (+463 chars, +85%) vs requirements (+320 chars, +60%). Employers are writing more about what the job involves, not just what skills they want.
  3. Boilerplate (benefits + about-company + legal) grew from ~70 chars to ~750 chars — an order of magnitude. This is the single largest proportional change, matching the scraped-era shift toward fuller HTML/markdown preservation and more explicit compensation disclosure.
  4. Entry-level tone jumped more than mid-senior tone. Inclusive per-1k up 52% for entry vs 10% for mid-senior. Marketing language doubled for entry while flat for mid-senior. There is a stylistic reframing of entry-level work that is distinct from what happened to mid-senior postings.
  5. Director postings got shorter/harder-sounding, bucking every trend. The opposite direction from all other seniorities. n=128 is small but the pattern is coherent. Worth flagging for a future deep-dive — is "director" being posted by a different class of company (smaller startups? more technical hiring managers)?
  6. Imperative density dropped in the period when AI agents became central. A plausible story is that since AI agents execute tasks, the posting no longer needs to tell the candidate "you will write code, you must debug" — it shifts toward "our team, our mission". But this may also simply be template drift.
  7. TTR is flat (vocabulary richness unchanged). If scope expansion were real (lots of new AI concepts), we might expect lexical diversity to rise. It doesn't — postings get longer by repeating and expanding, not by introducing new concept vocabulary. This is a caveat against naive "scope inflation" claims — the posting surface grew but the conceptual vocabulary per posting is roughly stable.

docs.title: Essential sensitivities
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: Not applied in this pass — the is_aggregator column is in the metadata but not filtered. Recommend T12 re-run on non-aggregator subset for entry-level splits, since aggregator postings are 46% entry in arshkon.
  • (d) Description text source: Used raw COALESCE(description_core_llm, description_core, description). For scraped (21% LLM coverage) this mostly falls through to rule-extracted description_core — which retains markdown. For kaggle (99%/90% LLM coverage) it uses the LLM-extracted core which is HTML-stripped prose. This text-source asymmetry confounds the section anatomy result: some of the apparent header proliferation in 2026 reflects the fact that rule-based extraction preserves markdown while LLM extraction strips it. See caveat in Section 6. A cleaner version would re-run sections on only rule-based text from both periods.

docs.title: Handoff to T12
docs.text: T12 should: 1. Load exploration/artifacts/shared/section_classifier.py and call classify_sections() on each posting's raw text to isolate responsibilities + requirements + preferred (the "genuine content" sections). 2. Run Fightin' Words term comparisons both on full-text (where benefits/about_company boilerplate drives some hits) and on core-content-only (resp + req + pref), and flag terms that appear only in full-text vs both. 3. Account for the readability shift: 2026 text uses larger lexicon counts but the same TTR — term-prevalence comparisons should be normalized per document, not per word, to avoid giving long 2026 postings extra "votes". 4. Report separately for entry-level under both the combined-column and YOE-proxy definitions, since T13 confirms the within-entry tonal shift is robust to definition.

docs.title: T14 — Technology Ecosystem Mapping
docs.text:
Date: 2026-04-09 Sample: LinkedIn SWE, aggregators excluded, is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'. Period pooling: 2024 = arshkon (Apr) + asaniczka (Jan); 2026 = scraped (Mar + Apr). Primary stratification column: seniority_3level; secondary: combined best-available (seniority_llm where labeled, seniority_final where rule_sufficient).

After aggregator exclusion: - 2024: n=21,301 (junior 773, mid 1,591, senior 18,128) - 2026: n=30,036 (junior 3,405, mid 870, senior 23,628)

Tech taxonomy: 153 binary columns from swe_tech_matrix.parquet.

docs.title: 1. Mention rates by period x seniority — headline table
docs.text: Full table: tables/T14/tech_mention_rates.csv.

docs.title: 2. Trajectory classification (2024 vs 2026 overall rate)
docs.text:
Thresholds: rising = +>=2pp AND >=+30% relative; declining = -<=2pp AND <=-25% relative; else stable.

  • Rising: 44 technologies
  • Stable: 104 technologies
  • Declining: 5 technologies

docs.title: Declining
docs.text:
Tech 2024 % 2026 % Delta pp c_lang 5.0 0.5 -4.6 html_css 10.6 6.8 -3.7 dotnet 10.0 6.4 -3.6 sqlserver 7.2 4.3 -2.9 jquery 3.2 0.9 -2.3

Caveat — c_lang extractor bug (data-quality finding). The shared tech-matrix regex uses \bc\+\+\b for C++; the trailing \b cannot match after +, so C++ is under-extracted (see Step 8). The c_lang regex also requires a cue ("c programming", "c/c++", etc.) which may fail on simple "C, Python, Java" enumerations. Both the C++ decline and much of the C-language decline are probably extractor artifacts. Recommend patching build_remaining.py — this affects any study citing C-family prevalence.

docs.title: The legacy-web bundle
docs.text:
Every declining technology except c_lang belongs to one coherent cluster — the "late-2010s enterprise/legacy-web stack" (.NET + SQL Server + HTML/CSS + jQuery). Decline is small in absolute pp but notable because the surrounding community also lost members (see Step 3).

Table: tables/T14/tech_trajectory.csv. Heatmap: figures/T14/tech_shift_heatmap.png.

docs.title: 3. Co-occurrence network & community structure
docs.text:
Method: phi coefficient for all pairs among 123 technologies with >=1% prevalence in at least one period; edges kept at phi > 0.15; greedy modularity communities; 2024 vs 2026 compared.

  • 2024 graph: 112 nodes, 360 edges, 11 communities
  • 2026 graph: 115 nodes, 491 edges, 9 communities
  • 2026 graph is +36% denser than 2024 at the same phi threshold. Technologies are co-mentioned more tightly in 2026 — consistent with the description-length and tech-count growth.

docs.title: Community comparison (2026 -> best-matching 2024 community)
docs.text: 2026 Comm Size Best 2024 match Jaccard Key new members in 2026 Members dropped from 2024 0 — Cloud/DevOps/platform 30 Comm 0 (27) 0.84 encryption, microservices, rest_api, security perl 1 — ML+GenAI mega-cluster 29 Comm 2 (17) 0.59 ai_agents, claude_api, claude_tool, codex, copilot, cursor_ide, gemini, huggingface, mcp, openai_api, sagemaker, transformers — 2 — Enterprise Java + web 23 Comm 1 (21) 0.47 agile, integration_testing, java, junit, kanban, scrum, spring, tdd, unit_testing express, mongodb, mysql, php, postgresql, redis, sql 3 — Data engineering 17 Comm 4 (13) 0.67 mongodb, mysql, postgresql, redis, sql elasticsearch 4 — Mobile 5 Comm 6 (5) 1.00 — — 5 — Test tools 4 Comm 3 (14) 0.29 — agile/java/junit/microservices/... (moved to Comm 2) 6 — Python web frameworks 3 Comm 5 (5) 0.60 — rails, ruby

docs.title: The biggest structural change — AI cluster fusion
docs.text:
In 2024, the modern-AI ecosystem was fragmented into three tiny isolated communities: - Comm 7: claude_api, claude_tool, huggingface, openai_api (4 nodes) - Comm 9: codex, copilot (2 nodes) - Plus one traditional ML community (Comm 2, 17 members: pytorch/tensorflow/sklearn/pandas/nlp/...).

In 2026, all of these fused into a single 29-member ML+GenAI community (Comm 1). Traditional ML tooling (pytorch, tensorflow, scikit_learn, pandas), classic ML subfields (NLP, CV, deep learning), modern-AI APIs (openai_api, claude_api, huggingface), agent/RAG infrastructure (langchain, rag, vector_databases, prompt_engineering, fine_tuning, mcp, ai_agents, transformers, sagemaker), AND AI coding tools (copilot, cursor_ide, codex, gemini, claude_tool) now all strongly co-occur.

Interpretation: In 2024 jobs that mentioned Copilot or Claude were distinct from jobs that mentioned TensorFlow. By 2026 they are the same jobs. Traditional ML engineers are expected to work with modern LLM APIs and agent frameworks; conversely, jobs posting about GenAI also list classical ML dependencies. This is a major consolidation of the AI-engineering identity.

docs.title: Data engineering swallows OLTP databases
docs.text: 2024 had a clean split: Comm 1 (enterprise Java / web / SQL / MongoDB / PostgreSQL / MySQL / Redis) vs Comm 4 (analytical — spark, kafka, snowflake, bigquery, airflow, dbt). In 2026 the OLTP data stores (mongodb, mysql, postgresql, redis, sql) migrate into Comm 3 (data engineering). Their former home (Comm 2, now "Enterprise Java + web") becomes dominated by the Java + Spring + microservices + testing bundle. Interpretation: "the database layer" now belongs to data engineers, not generic web developers, in the co-mention structure.

docs.title: Test frameworks decoupled from practices
docs.text:
2024 Comm 3 packed jest/pytest/selenium/cypress/playwright together with agile/scrum/tdd/microservices/rest_api. In 2026 the latter migrated into the Java/enterprise cluster, leaving Comm 5 as a bare 4-node (cypress/jest/playwright/selenium) test-tools island. Testing practice vocabulary moved, testing tools didn't.

Tables: tables/T14/community_comparison.csv, community_membership.csv, cooccurrence_phi_{2024,2026}.csv. Figures: figures/T14/tech_network_{2024,2026}.png.

docs.title: 4. Stack diversity (tech count per posting)
docs.text:
Table: tables/T14/stack_diversity.csv.

Period Seniority n median mean p25 p75 p90 2024 ALL 21,301 5.0 5.95 2 9 13 2024 junior 773 5.0 5.76 2 9 13 2024 mid 1,591 4.0 4.78 1 7 12 2024 senior 18,128 5.0 6.05 2 9 13 2026 ALL 30,036 8.0 8.78 4 12 17 2026 junior 3,405 6.0 7.25 3 11 15 2026 mid 870 7.0 7.85 3 11 16 2026 senior 23,628 8.0 8.95 4 13 17

Median tech count rose from 5 to 8 (+60%). The 90th percentile rose from 13 to 17 technologies per posting.

docs.title: Calibration ratio (Step 5 check against Agent Prep's 24.8x)
docs.text:
Recomputed within the SWE analytical frame (not the pipeline-wide frame Agent Prep used):

  • Within-2024 Cohen's d (arshkon vs asaniczka): 0.165 (arshkon slightly richer)
  • Cross-period Cohen's d (arshkon vs scraped): -0.355 (scraped much richer)
  • Calibration ratio: -2.15 (direction reversed)

The ratio is smaller than Agent Prep's 24.8 (they used the full pipeline frame; we use SWE-only non-aggregator). But the direction-reversal plus the magnitude (|d|=0.355 cross-period vs 0.165 within-2024) still makes tech_count one of the strongest real signals above source-variation noise. Confirmed as a real Wave 2 finding.

Length-normalized complement: tables/T14/stack_diversity_density.csv. Median density (techs per 1K chars) in 2024 ALL = 3.02, in 2026 ALL = 2.27. After length-normalization, tech density actually DECREASED by 25%. Postings got longer faster than they got more tech-dense. The raw +60% median-count signal is therefore heavily driven by description-length inflation (see T05: +57% median length).

Load-bearing finding: "Stack breadth is expanding" is real but only in raw count. Postings aren't packing more techs per sentence — they are writing more sentences, each with a moderate tech density. See Step 6 for the AI-subgroup version of the same pattern.

Figure: figures/T14/stack_diversity_hist.png.

docs.title: 5. AI-integration pattern
docs.text:
"AI-mentioning" set: any of {llm, openai_api, claude_api, rag, langchain, huggingface, vector_databases, prompt_engineering, fine_tuning, mcp, ai_agents, generative_ai, copilot, cursor_ide, chatgpt, claude_tool, gemini, codex}.

AI-mentioning prevalence: 2024 = 2.8% (599/21,301); 2026 = 26.2% (7,855/30,036). 9.4x increase.

docs.title: Top technologies co-occurring with AI mentions in 2026 (by excess over non-AI baseline)
docs.text:
Tech rate_in_ai rate_in_non_ai excess pp machine_learning 52.8% 19.0% +33.9 python 62.7% 46.1% +16.6 aws 45.9% 33.4% +12.5 gcp 26.4% 14.5% +11.9 azure 32.6% 21.2% +11.4 pytorch 13.3% 2.8% +10.5 react 25.9% 15.8% +10.1 mlops 9.5% 1.3% +8.2 tensorflow 9.6% 2.3% +7.3 nlp 10.1% 2.9% +7.2 deep_learning 7.0% 1.7% +5.3 nodejs 12.2% 7.0% +5.2 javascript 25.9% 21.0% +4.9 fastapi 6.1% 1.2% +4.9 typescript 22.5% 18.3% +4.2

Read: An AI-mentioning 2026 SWE posting is (above baseline) a Python job on AWS/GCP with traditional ML tooling AND React/TypeScript frontend AND fastapi. The "AI engineer" role has converged toward full-stack cloud + ML.

docs.title: Length-confound check for AI integration
docs.text:
Table: tables/T14/ai_density_check.csv.

Period Group n mean tech count mean text len mean density /1K 2024 ai_mentioning 599 8.9 2,218 4.63 2024 non_ai 20,702 5.9 1,965 3.92 2026 ai_mentioning 7,855 12.2 3,806 3.68 2026 non_ai 22,181 7.6 3,295 2.75

Raw: AI-mentioning 2026 postings list 12.2 techs vs non-AI 7.6 — a +4.6 tech gap. Density-normalized: AI posts 3.68 per 1K chars vs non-AI 2.75 — a +0.93 per-1K gap (+34%). Time trend: Within the AI group itself, density fell from 4.63/1K in 2024 to 3.68/1K in 2026. AI-mentioning jobs became less dense but longer, mirroring the ALL trend. AI jobs are still meaningfully more tech-dense than non-AI jobs even after length-normalization, but the density gap between AI and non-AI narrowed (2024: +0.70 /1K; 2026: +0.93 /1K — actually widened slightly when measured in absolute density units). Both pictures are consistent with "AI-focused roles pull in more distinct tools than non-AI", and the gap appears robust to length-normalization.

Full AI co-occurrence: tables/T14/ai_integration_cooccurrence.csv.

docs.title: 6. Structured skills baseline (asaniczka)
docs.text:
Source: exploration/artifacts/shared/asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet. 609,894 mentions across 23,192 asaniczka SWE rows (non-aggregator subset is smaller).

Top-20 structured skills with prevalence (share of rows):

Skill % Python 34.6% Java 27.9% AWS 23.8% SQL 22.8% Kubernetes 17.4% JavaScript 16.5% Docker 16.5% Agile 14.2% Communication 13.7% Software Engineering 12.7% Linux 12.3% DevOps 11.1% C++ 10.8% Software Development 10.8% Git 10.5% React 9.8% Go 9.4% Troubleshooting 9.4% Azure 9.4% Computer Science 9.0%

Surprise — soft skills rank high in structured fields. "Communication" (13.7%) and "Troubleshooting" (9.4%) appear in the top-20. Structured LinkedIn "skills" fields mix hard technologies with general competencies; any analysis of structured-skills frequency must filter for tech-only vocab.

Full table: tables/T14/structured_skills_frequency.csv (top-100: structured_skills_top100.csv).

docs.title: 7. Structured vs extracted validation
docs.text:
Mapped asaniczka structured skill strings to the 153-tech taxonomy via regex rules (structured_to_tech in the script). 109 technologies have non-trivial prevalence in at least one representation.

Rank correlation: Spearman rho = 0.77, Pearson r = 0.92.

docs.title: Largest divergences (sorted by |rate_extracted - rate_structured|)
docs.text:
Tech Extracted % Structured % Diff Interpretation c_cpp 0.9 16.2 +15.4 Extractor bug — \bc\+\+\b regex cannot match security 35.6 24.0 -11.6 Extractor over-fires (cybersecurity mentions, "security clearance") csharp 0.4 11.7 +11.3 Extractor bug — C# mentioned in text as "C#" is not matching cicd 18.5 10.6 -7.9 Description-extract catches more CI/CD variants than structured skills typescript 12.7 6.4 -6.3 Extractor over-fires or structured under-reports bash_shell 6.1 0.0 -6.1 Structured field doesn't have "Bash/Shell" as a canonical skill c_lang 5.6 0.0 -5.6 Structured field has only C++ / C#, not plain "C" sass_less 4.5 0.0 -4.5 No structured equivalent sql 26.3 22.6 -3.7 — javascript 21.3 17.9 -3.4 — jquery 3.3 0.0 -3.3 —

Conclusions: 1. Strong convergent validity overall (rho=0.77). 2. The description extractor has two concrete bugs: c_cpp regex (\bc\+\+\b) and csharp regex miss most real mentions. Since C++ is structurally 16% in asaniczka and only 0.9% extracted, this is a large miss. Any "C++ is declining" claim is currently unfounded — the tool has a blind spot. 3. The structured field misses shell/bash and sass/less entirely — LinkedIn's skill vocabulary doesn't include them as canonical entries. 4. "security" runs richer in extraction because the word is abundant in compliance / clearance boilerplate that isn't really a technology skill.

Recommendation (for the next re-run of build_remaining.py): replace \bc\+\+\b with (?:^|[\s,/()])c\+\+(?:$|[\s,/()]) and c# similarly; expand c_lang pattern; re-generate swe_tech_matrix.parquet.

Table: tables/T14/structured_vs_extracted.csv.

docs.title: 8. Seniority-level skill differences (structured, asaniczka)
docs.text:
Chi-squared per skill (skills with n>=30) comparing entry-level to mid-senior, with FDR correction.

Result: severely underpowered. Only 61 asaniczka SWE rows have seniority_best_available='entry' (vs 3,612 'mid-senior'). Only 2 skills reached q<0.05. This task cannot be answered with asaniczka-only structured data — that source has essentially no entry-level labels.

docs.title: Supplementary analysis — extracted tech, pooled 2024 (arshkon+asaniczka), n_entry=773
docs.text:
This is the power-bearing variant (arshkon contributes the vast majority of 2024 entry labels).

Tech % in entry % in mid-senior delta q (FDR) java 31.4 25.4 +6.0 0.008 html_css 14.4 10.4 +4.0 0.011 objective_c 2.2 0.6 +1.6 0.000002 playwright 0.9 0.2 +0.7 0.011 openai_api 0.6 0.2 +0.5 0.026 security 27.7 34.7 -7.0 0.004

Read: In 2024, entry-level SWE postings were slightly Java/HTML heavier and meaningfully less security-focused than mid-senior postings. The objective_c surplus at entry-level is odd — probably app-dev bootcamps / iOS coding roles posted as entry.

Tables: tables/T14/seniority_skills_chi2.csv (asaniczka structured, underpowered), seniority_extracted_tech_chi2_pooled2024.csv (supplementary, pooled 2024).

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. AI cluster fusion. The 2024 "modern AI" fragmented islands (Claude API + Hugging Face, Copilot + Codex, traditional ML) fuse into a single 29-member community in 2026, roughly the same size as the cloud/DevOps community. This is the single largest structural rearrangement in the network.
  2. Data engineering annexes OLTP. MongoDB/MySQL/PostgreSQL/Redis/SQL all moved out of the "enterprise web" community into the data-engineering community by 2026. Implies "database stuff" is increasingly signaling data-eng work, not generic backend.
  3. Stack diversity grows only in raw count. Density (techs / 1K chars) DECREASED 25% from 2024 to 2026. The "broader stack" is a length-inflation artifact to a large extent — each individual sentence isn't more tech-dense.
  4. AI-mentioning share 9.4x. From 2.8% of postings (2024) to 26.2% (2026). This is the headline validation of T14's AI-integration hypothesis.
  5. Data-quality finding: C++ / C# regex bugs. The shared tech matrix's c_cpp pattern (\bc\+\+\b) cannot match because + is not a word character, so \b after + fails. csharp has the same issue. The structured-skills cross-check reveals C++ is actually mentioned in ~16% of asaniczka structured skills but 0.9% in extraction. This should be fixed and the shared artifacts rebuilt before downstream analysis makes claims about C-family language prevalence.
  6. "Communication" and "Troubleshooting" in structured top-20. LinkedIn's structured skills field mixes soft and hard competencies; any structured-field-only analysis must filter the vocabulary.
  7. Test tools decoupled from practices. In 2024 test frameworks co-occurred with Agile/Scrum/TDD; in 2026 tools form a bare 4-node island while practice vocabulary migrated to the Java/enterprise community. Testing as practice moved; testing as tooling didn't.
  8. "Security" is the one tech where entry-level underperforms in 2024 pooled data. Security practices concentrate at mid-senior — interesting baseline for a downstream study on whether AI-era entry-level now has to "own security" earlier.
  9. Declining techs are all legacy-web. Every declining technology fits a single semantic cluster (jquery/html/css/.net/sqlserver/c_lang), reinforcing that the shift is not random attrition but a coherent generational rotation away from classic Microsoft/legacy-web stacks.

docs.title: Sensitivities applied
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: applied throughout (11,957 rows removed).
  • (b) Company capping: not applied in T14. Top-50 company concentration in scraped is moderate (HHI 0.003-0.014 from T06); unlikely to swing a 63K-row trajectory analysis but a formal cap should be run as a robustness check in the follow-up.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: applied to tech_count (ratio -2.15, real). AI-mentioning prevalence growth (2.8% -> 26.2%) calibrates off Agent Prep's finding (ratio -16). Both survive.
  • (g) SWE classification tier robustness: not split in this pass. swe_classification_tier is in the metadata table and can be filtered in a re-run if needed.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Path Content tables/T14/tech_mention_rates.csv Mention rates (%) by period x seniority_3level tables/T14/tech_mention_rates_best_available.csv Same using combined best-available seniority tables/T14/tech_trajectory.csv Rising/stable/declining classification tables/T14/stack_diversity.csv Tech count percentiles by group tables/T14/stack_diversity_density.csv Length-normalized tech density tables/T14/tech_count_calibration.csv Within-2024 vs cross-period effect sizes tables/T14/cooccurrence_phi_2024.csv, _2026.csv Pairs above phi>0.15 tables/T14/community_membership.csv Tech -> community assignments tables/T14/community_comparison.csv 2026 comm -> best 2024 match tables/T14/ai_integration_cooccurrence.csv Techs co-mentioned with AI, by period tables/T14/ai_density_check.csv Length-normalized confound check tables/T14/structured_skills_frequency.csv, _top100.csv Asaniczka structured skills tables/T14/structured_vs_extracted.csv Cross-validation of extractor tables/T14/seniority_skills_chi2.csv Structured skills chi2 (underpowered) tables/T14/seniority_extracted_tech_chi2_pooled2024.csv Extracted-tech chi2 (powered) figures/T14/tech_shift_heatmap.png Top rising/declining/stable heatmap figures/T14/stack_diversity_hist.png Tech-count distribution figures/T14/tech_network_2024.png, _2026.png Co-occurrence network viz

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T14_01_mention_rates.py, T14_02_cooccurrence.py, T14_03_structured_skills.py.

docs.title: T15 — Semantic Similarity Landscape & Convergence
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-09 Representations compared: sentence-transformer embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-d, L2-normalized, pre-computed) and TF-IDF (30K features, sublinear TF, unigram+bigram) -> TruncatedSVD-100 -> L2-normalize.

docs.title: 1. Sample
docs.text:
Stratified by period x seniority_3level with 2,000-per-cell cap, aggregators excluded.

period seniority n sampled n available 2024 junior 773 773 2024 mid 1,591 1,591 2024 senior 2,000 18,128 2026 junior 2,000 3,405 2026 mid 870 870 2026 senior 2,000 23,628 Total 9,234

SVD explained variance on TF-IDF: 18.5%. Embeddings: 100% (no reduction).

docs.title: text_source distribution is the first major surprise
docs.text:
period seniority llm text rule text 2024 junior 765 8 2024 mid 1,548 43 2024 senior 1,794 206 2026 junior 420 1,580 2026 mid 165 705 2026 senior 420 1,580

2024 is ~94% LLM-cleaned text; 2026 is ~79% rule-cleaned. This is a huge text_source confound — the LLM extractor produces crisper, shorter, more prose-like text, while the rule-based extractor retains more boilerplate. Any "2024 vs 2026" similarity comparison is simultaneously comparing the text-cleaning pipeline used. This confound was noted in T05 for description length (+57% median). Here it may affect every centroid distance.

Artifacts: exploration/artifacts/T15/sample_{index.parquet,embeddings.npy,tfidf_svd.npy}.

docs.title: 2. Centroid similarity structure
docs.text: Trimmed centroids (10% most distant rows removed before recomputing centroid).

docs.title: TF-IDF (cosine)
docs.text:
24_jun 24_mid 24_sen 26_jun 26_mid 26_sen 24_jun 1.000 0.983 0.985 0.889 0.910 0.864 24_mid 0.983 1.000 0.977 0.863 0.897 0.844 24_sen 0.985 0.977 1.000 0.879 0.913 0.880 26_jun 0.889 0.863 0.879 1.000 0.964 0.963 26_mid 0.910 0.897 0.913 0.964 1.000 0.977 26_sen 0.864 0.844 0.880 0.963 0.977 1.000

Dominant source of variation is PERIOD, not seniority. In both representations, within-period cells are much more similar to each other than between-period cells.

Under embeddings: within-period ~0.992, between-period ~0.962 (Delta ~0.030). Under TF-IDF: within-period ~0.975, between-period ~0.884 (Delta ~0.091).

Seniority contrasts are an order of magnitude smaller than period contrasts. This is consistent with either (a) real period change or (b) a text-cleaning-pipeline effect.

Figures: figures/T15/centroid_similarity_heatmap_{embeddings,tfidf}.png.

docs.title: 3. Convergence / divergence of seniority levels
docs.text:
Between-seniority cosine within each period, trimmed centroids.

Pair Representation 2024 2026 delta junior vs mid embeddings 0.9938 0.9937 -0.00008 junior vs senior embeddings 0.9935 0.9922 -0.00131 mid vs senior embeddings 0.9913 0.9941 +0.00287 junior vs mid tfidf 0.9825 0.9644 -0.01813 junior vs senior tfidf 0.9853 0.9629 -0.02247 mid vs senior tfidf 0.9772 0.9770 -0.00026

Directional split by representation:

  • Embeddings: Seniority contrasts are essentially flat 2024->2026. Junior vs senior drops microscopically (-0.001). Mid vs senior actually rises (+0.003). No convergence signal at all.
  • TF-IDF: Junior DIVERGES from mid and senior. Junior-vs-senior cosine falls from 0.985 to 0.963 (Delta -0.022) — more than the within-2024 source variability (see Step 3b). Mid-senior similarity is unchanged.

The two representations disagree on whether there is any seniority convergence, and on which direction it runs. Neither shows the "junior jobs are becoming like senior jobs" convergence that a strong RQ1 story would want. If anything, TF-IDF says junior postings are becoming more linguistically distinct from senior postings by 2026 — the opposite direction. Embeddings say nothing is changing between seniority levels.

This is the single most important T15 finding for the re-analysis: there is no embedding-level convergence of juniors toward seniors in descriptive text.

docs.title: 3b. Within-2024 calibration (mandatory)
docs.text:
Trimmed-centroid cosines between source pairs:

Representation arshkon vs asaniczka arshkon vs scraped asaniczka vs scraped embeddings 0.9945 0.9673 0.9630 tfidf 0.9890 0.9022 0.8845

Calibration check for convergence: The within-2024 arshkon<->asaniczka centroid gap is 0.0055 (embeddings) or 0.0110 (TF-IDF). The junior-senior convergence we'd need to detect is at most 0.00131 (embeddings, wrong sign) or 0.02247 (TF-IDF, wrong sign).

  • Under embeddings: the seniority convergence signal is smaller than source-variation noise by 4x. Not interpretable.
  • Under TF-IDF: The seniority-divergence signal (0.022) is ~2x the within-2024 source noise (0.011). It may survive, but barely, and in the "junior more distinct" direction.

The Agent I preamble instruction was: "if the asaniczka->arshkon similarity shift exceeds the arshkon->scraped shift, the convergence signal doesn't survive calibration." In embeddings the arshkon<->asaniczka gap (0.0055) is smaller than arshkon<->scraped (0.0327), but the seniority pair Delta is a THIRD-order effect and doesn't exceed either. Conclusion: no convergence claim is warranted from these data.

Table: tables/T15/within2024_calibration_centroids.csv.

docs.title: 3c. YOE-based proxy convergence
docs.text:
Using the YOE-extracted proxy (junior = YOE<=2, mid = 3-5, senior = 6+) instead of the label-based 3level column:

Pair Representation 2024 2026 delta junior vs mid embeddings 0.9955 0.9937 -0.0019 junior vs senior embeddings 0.9920 0.9911 -0.0009 mid vs senior embeddings 0.9960 0.9964 +0.0003 junior vs mid tfidf 0.9926 0.9760 -0.0166 junior vs senior tfidf 0.9776 0.9632 -0.0145 mid vs senior tfidf 0.9873 0.9886 +0.0012

Labels and YOE agree: no convergence under embeddings; junior-divergence under TF-IDF of similar magnitude. The signal is consistent across the two seniority operationalizations. This addresses the "run under both" mandate from the task spec.

Tables: tables/T15/convergence_by_period.csv, convergence_yoe_proxy.csv.

docs.title: 4. Within-group dispersion
docs.text:
Average pairwise cosine within each group (500-row subsample).

Representation group mean pairwise cosine n embeddings 2024_junior 0.508 773 embeddings 2024_mid 0.487 1,591 embeddings 2024_senior 0.510 2,000 embeddings 2026_junior 0.491 2,000 embeddings 2026_mid 0.502 870 embeddings 2026_senior 0.521 2,000 tfidf 2024_junior 0.339 773 tfidf 2024_mid 0.264 1,591 tfidf 2024_senior 0.324 2,000 tfidf 2026_junior 0.272 2,000 tfidf 2026_mid 0.292 870 tfidf 2026_senior 0.344 2,000

Under TF-IDF, 2024 junior postings are more homogeneous than 2026 junior postings (0.339 -> 0.272, a 20% decrease in cohesion). This matches the divergence finding — 2026 junior text is more variable than 2024 junior text, spreading the cluster out. Under embeddings, senior cohesion actually increases slightly (0.510 -> 0.521); junior cohesion is effectively unchanged.

Table: tables/T15/within_group_dispersion.csv.

docs.title: 5. UMAP / PCA / t-SNE visualization
docs.text:
figures/T15/umap_period_seniority.png shows the full 9,234-point embedding UMAP colored by period x seniority. The dominant visible split is period, with the 2024 and 2026 clouds occupying visibly different regions. Within each period, seniority levels overlap heavily.

figures/T15/umap_density_contours.png shows three side-by-side panels (junior, mid, senior), each with 2024 vs 2026 density contours and a black arrow connecting the 2024 centroid to the 2026 centroid. All three arrows point in the same direction and have similar magnitudes — i.e., all seniority levels shift in parallel from 2024 to 2026. This is a clean visual confirmation that the period effect is large, seniority-constant.

figures/T15/pca_period_seniority.png and figures/T15/tsne_period_seniority.png tell the same story.

Caveat: The period shift visible in all three reductions overlaps strongly with the text_source shift (2024 mostly LLM-cleaned, 2026 mostly rule-cleaned). We cannot distinguish "description text really changed" from "the cleaning pipeline applied to it is different" from these plots alone.

docs.title: 6. Nearest-neighbor analysis (2026 junior -> 2024)
docs.text:
For each 2026 junior posting, find its 5 nearest neighbors among all 2024 sample points. Count seniority labels.

Representation NN seniority count observed rate base rate (2024 sample) excess embeddings junior 2,148 21.5% 17.7% +3.8pp embeddings mid 2,825 28.3% 36.5% -8.2pp embeddings senior 5,027 50.3% 45.8% +4.4pp tfidf junior 2,424 24.2% 17.7% +6.5pp tfidf mid 2,529 25.3% 36.5% -11.2pp tfidf senior 5,047 50.5% 45.8% +4.6pp

Read: Under both representations, 2026 junior postings are slightly more likely to retrieve 2024 junior neighbors (+3.8 to +6.5pp over base rate) than you'd expect from base rates. The "2026 junior looks like 2024 senior" story would predict a large positive excess for senior neighbors — what we see is a small positive excess (+4.4 to +4.6pp) that's similar to the junior excess. The real redistribution is AWAY from mid (-8 to -11pp), toward both ends.

Interpretation: 2026 junior postings don't look like 2024 mid-career postings. They look like 2024 postings at the junior OR senior ends — a mild bimodality. This is subtle and inconsistent with a clean "scope inflation" story.

By source: most 2026 junior NN hit asaniczka (which had very few labeled juniors), not arshkon. The asaniczka-heavy retrieval is a function of asaniczka's 4:1 representation in 2024.

Tables: tables/T15/nearest_neighbor_entry2026_{emb,tfidf,source}.csv.

docs.title: 7. Representation robustness summary
docs.text:
Finding Embeddings TF-IDF Robust? Dominant variation = period yes (0.030 within, 0.096 between) yes (0.091 within, 0.884 between) YES Junior-senior convergence flat (-0.001) diverges (-0.022) NO — directional disagreement Junior divergence from mid flat (-0.0001) strong (-0.018) PARTIAL — TF-IDF only Mid-senior convergence none (+0.003) none (0.0) YES (no change) Calibration survival convergence < source noise divergence ~2x source noise Only TF-IDF junior-divergence NN retrieval: 2026 junior -> 2024 junior excess +3.8pp +6.5pp YES (direction) NN retrieval: 2026 junior -> 2024 senior excess +4.4pp +4.6pp YES (magnitude) Within-group dispersion: 2024 junior more cohesive than 2026 junior no (+3pp) yes (-20pp) NO

Full table: tables/T15/representation_robustness.csv.

docs.title: 8. Outlier identification
docs.text:
Per-group bottom-15 postings by cosine to trimmed centroid. Selected examples:

2024 junior outliers — "Sales Application Engineer", "Brand Marketing Web Developer Intern", "Volunteer: Nature Counter React Native Full Stack Developer". The lowest-cosine 2024 juniors are genuine niche/adjacent roles (sales-eng, volunteer postings).

2024 mid outliers — volunteer postings (Nature Counter, Nobel Prize Library) dominate the bottom. This is a clear data-quality signal: asaniczka has volunteer job postings in its SWE set. These should probably be filtered upstream.

2024 senior outliers — contract/visa-specific postings ("GCP Data Engineer- Need on W2 or 1099"), embedded ("Senior Firmware Engineer"), and platform-specific ("Salesforce Developer"). These are real-but-distinctive engineering roles.

2026 junior outliers — "Backend AI-Forward Data Engineer", "Lab Software Engineer I", "Web Application Developer". More genuine SWE variety; no volunteer contamination.

2026 senior outliers — Foreign-language ("Senior Software Engineer- Electromechanical Systems (H/F)"), embedded hardware ("Senior Firmware..."). The lowest is an oddly short "Software Engineer" posting with cosine 0.146 — probably boilerplate-stripped to a stub.

Surprise: Volunteer postings contaminate asaniczka SWE. Should be flagged for the preprocessing pipeline (filter titles beginning with "Volunteer:" or containing unpaid/volunteer language).

Table: tables/T15/outliers_per_group.csv (15 per group x 6 groups = 90 rows).

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Period dominates variation; seniority barely registers. Within-period seniority cosine is 0.99+; between-period is 0.88-0.97. This isn't surprising but it's strong: if you drop all seniority labels and just look at the text, 2024 vs 2026 is a night/day visual split in UMAP; junior vs senior within a period is indistinguishable.
  2. No convergence. Possibly junior-divergence. The TF-IDF side shows junior-vs-senior cosine DROPPING from 0.985 to 0.963 (-0.022). That's not convergence — that's divergence, and it only appears in the lexical representation, not the semantic embedding. Interpretation candidate: the specific vocabulary used in junior postings became more distinct (perhaps explicit "entry-level" / "new grad" / "0-2 years" / "Copilot welcome" markers), while the overall thematic semantics barely moved.
  3. Representations disagree on convergence direction. Embeddings say "no change"; TF-IDF says "mild divergence". Downstream analyses should NOT cite a convergence finding from T15.
  4. Text_source confound is the biggest threat to validity. 2024 sample is 94% LLM-cleaned; 2026 sample is 79% rule-cleaned. All period-level differences are partially a pipeline-stage difference. The scraped data has low LLM-extraction coverage (21% per the README), and Stage 9/10 routing is why. Any "the postings changed" claim needs to be repeated within the LLM-text-only subset for 2026 (n ~7k — borderline feasible).
  5. NN retrieval bimodality. 2026 junior postings don't look like 2024 mid — they look either like 2024 junior OR like 2024 senior, both at +4-6pp excess over base rate. If anything, there's a mild polarization pattern rather than inflation.
  6. Volunteer job contamination in asaniczka. The bottom-cosine 2024 mid outliers are almost all "Volunteer: ..." postings. The preprocessing SWE filter lets these through. Suggest adding a "Volunteer:" title-prefix filter upstream.
  7. Within-group dispersion drops for TF-IDF junior 2024->2026 (-20%) — the lexical diversity of 2026 junior postings is lower than 2024 junior postings. Combined with the divergence from mid/senior, this suggests a small but consistent vocabulary shift to a more homogeneous but different lexicon.
  8. Embedding dispersion barely moves. The semantic centroid of each group is stable, but the lexical surface changed. This is a signature of "same meaning, different words" — exactly what a cleaning-pipeline change would produce.

docs.title: Sensitivities applied
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion: applied.
  • (c) Seniority operationalization: tested both label-based seniority_3level and YOE-based proxy; findings agree.
  • (d) Description text source: documented; not sensitivity-tested further. This is the biggest open confound. Recommend a follow-up run on LLM-text-only subsamples for 2026 postings.
  • (e) Source restriction: LinkedIn-only throughout.
  • (f) Within-2024 calibration: applied; seniority convergence does not survive calibration in embeddings; TF-IDF divergence survives at ~2x noise floor.
  • (g) SWE classification tier robustness: not split in this pass.
