docs.title: T16 — Company hiring strategy typology
docs.text: Agent: J (Wave 3) Status: done Date: 2026-04-10 Frame: SWE, LinkedIn, is_english, date_flag='ok'. Description-hash dedup within company applied before all aggregation. Script: exploration/scripts/T16_company_strategies.py

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Entry-level posting is a specialized activity at <10% of substantial-presence companies. Among the 1,091 scraped companies with ≥5 SWE postings (deduplicated), 90.4% have zero entry rows under the combined best-available column and 49.3% have zero entry rows under the YOE≤2 proxy. The combined column restates V1's 91% concentration finding at 90.4%; the YOE proxy is less severe but still shows half of substantial employers effectively not posting entry-level SWE.

  2. The top 20 YOE-proxy entry posters are almost entirely large-employer new-grad programs: Google (281 entry rows, 50.8% of its postings), Walmart (149, 74.5%), Qualcomm (80, 79.2%), SpaceX (67, 65.0%), Microsoft (55, 32.4%), Amazon (36, 15.7%), Meta (not in top-20 here due to scraped volume), Cisco, Visa (24, 63.2%), Wells Fargo, Booz Allen Hamilton, LinkedIn, Esri, Northrop Grumman, Leidos, JPMorgan, Uber, Raytheon, Haystack, AWS. Defense/aerospace (SpaceX, Northrop, Raytheon, Peraton) is unexpectedly well-represented.

  3. The combined-column and YOE-proxy disagree on direction at 56% of overlap companies. At the company level (174 overlap companies with both signals), 98 show opposite-sign deltas between combined column and YOE proxy. The aggregate decompositions disagree too: combined column shows within-company Δ entry of -0.27pp (small negative) while YOE proxy shows within-company Δ entry of +0.82pp (small positive). The YOE proxy is the primary signal for entry-level concentration; the combined column remains useful for sanity-checking at the corpus level but has insufficient per-company power.

  4. k=5 clustering identifies distinct hiring trajectories, dominated by tech-density and AI-rate dimensions rather than by entry-share.

  5. The 2026 entry-share rise under the YOE proxy is 87% driven by between-company share shifts, not by individual companies pivoting to entry-level hiring. Within-company change contributes only 12.6pp of the 6.5pp aggregate delta. This is a critical reframing: the entry rise isn't employers pivoting to new-grad hiring — it's scraped-period composition shifting toward companies that were already heavy entry posters.

docs.title: 1. Dedup impact
docs.text:
Raw panel: 63,249 rows. Deduplicated panel: 51,500 rows. 11,749 rows (18.6%) collapsed as within-company description-hash duplicates. Top dedup victims (raw → collapsed):

Company Raw Collapsed Rate Recruiting from Scratch 1,704 1,664 97.7% Capital One 2,034 1,339 65.8% PricewaterhouseCoopers 482 461 95.6% Epic 444 438 98.6% Deloitte 472 407 86.2% ClickJobs.io 483 319 66.0% Affirm 293 278 94.9% DataAnnotation 259 224 86.5% Google 806 213 26.4% "We are deploying software-defined..." 196 194 99.0%

Recruiting-from-Scratch, PwC, Epic, ClickJobs, Affirm, and the "software-defined" (Nvidia-style) templates are effectively single postings re-listed dozens to hundreds of times. Capital One and Google have substantial genuine volume but also large duplicate tails. The rest of T16 uses the deduplicated panel as the base. Full table: tables/T16/dedup_impact_top30.csv.

docs.title: 2. Overlap panel
docs.text: Companies with ≥3 SWE in both arshkon-2024 and scraped-2026 (dedup): 220 companies (primary panel). Pooled-2024 ∩ scraped-2026: 509 companies. All per-company analyses below use the 220-company primary panel except where noted.

docs.title: 3. Entry-poster concentration (load-bearing)
docs.text:
Among 1,091 scraped companies with ≥5 SWE postings (dedup):

Measure # with any entry rows # with zero entry rows % with zero Combined best-available column 105 986 90.4% YOE≤2 proxy 553 538 49.3%

Top 20 YOE-proxy entry posters (ranked by # of entry rows):

Company n SWE n YOE-entry entry share (YOE) entry share (best) mean YOE industry Google 560 281 0.508 0.008 2.78 Tech Jobs via Dice 1,241 158 0.210 0.037 4.89 Software Walmart 200 149 0.745 0.000 2.13 Retail Qualcomm 124 80 0.792 0.000 2.03 Semi SpaceX 107 67 0.650 0.080 2.83 Aerospace Wells Fargo 224 58 0.297 0.000 3.67 Financial Microsoft 175 55 0.324 0.000 3.87 Software Booz Allen Hamilton 220 50 0.292 0.000 3.88 Software Amazon 247 36 0.157 0.043 3.36 Software LinkedIn 78 31 0.403 0.000 4.08 Tech Esri 57 27 0.509 0.000 4.04 Software Northrop Grumman 81 26 0.329 0.000 4.80 Defense Leidos 186 26 0.182 0.167 7.47 IT Services JPMorgan Chase 669 25 0.045 0.007 5.10 Financial Visa 43 24 0.632 0.000 2.82 IT Services Peraton 165 24 0.195 0.071 5.67 Civil Eng Uber 130 23 0.217 0.040 4.56 Marketplace Raytheon 82 22 0.286 0.000 5.04 Defense Haystack 138 21 0.198 0.000 5.30 Tech AWS 242 20 0.085 0.000 3.77 IT Services

Important disagreement between operationalizations: Walmart, Qualcomm, Microsoft, SpaceX, Wells Fargo, Booz, LinkedIn, Esri, Northrop, Visa, Raytheon, Haystack, AWS all have 0% entry share under the combined column despite YOE-proxy entry shares of 29-79%. The combined column is dominated by explicit entry/junior/new grad title keywords, which these employers rarely use — they phrase new-grad roles as "Software Engineer" with "0-2 years" or "recent graduate" body text. The YOE proxy captures these; the title-keyword seniority classifier does not.

Profile of entry-poster vs non-entry-poster (YOE proxy, scraped ≥5 SWE):

Group # cos Mean # postings Mean YOE Entry-poster 520 29.2 4.57 Non-entry-poster 571 9.3 6.15

Entry-posters are 3.1× larger employers on average with posting mean-YOE 1.6 years younger.

Industry composition of entry-poster group (top 10): Software Development (212), IT Services (85), Staffing (62), Financial Services (43), Tech/Info/Internet (29), Banking (16), Hospitals/Health Care (15), Defense/Space (15), Semiconductor (12), Aerospace (11). Full table: tables/T16/entry_poster_industries.csv.

docs.title: 4. Per-company change metrics
docs.text:
Overlap panel (arshkon ∩ scraped, 220 companies). Mean deltas:

Metric Mean Δ Entry share (YOE≤2) +1.84pp Entry share (combined col) -1.45pp AI rate +18.31pp Mean description length (raw) +904.6 chars Mean description length (LLM-only) +535.5 chars Mean tech count +2.60 Scope rate (large positive; see decomposition)

The LLM-only length delta is 59% of the raw delta, consistent with V1's ~50% text-source contribution warning (T13 caveat).

Company-level direction disagreement (combined col vs YOE proxy): 98 / 174 overlap companies (56%) show opposite-sign deltas on entry share. The dominant mechanism: combined column has floor/ceiling effects at low-volume companies (e.g., 0 → 0, 0 → 5%) and is dominated by explicit title keywords, while YOE proxy captures a denser signal from body text.

Full per-company table: tables/T16/overlap_per_company_changes.csv.

docs.title: 5. k-means cluster typology
docs.text:
k=5 on z-scored (Δentry_yoe, Δai_rate, Δlength_llm, Δtech_count, Δscope). Centers:

Cluster Name n Δentry_yoe Δai_rate Δlen_llm Δtech_count Δscope 0 high-tech 61 +3.2pp +25.2pp +328 +4.67 +9.5pp 1 low-tech 60 +12.8pp +8.1pp +18 -0.12 -3.2pp 2 high-length / AI-forward 28 +7.0pp +40.2pp +1,911 +3.96 +39.8pp 3 high-scope 53 +3.6pp +10.9pp +552 +2.74 +51.5pp 4 entry-downsizer 18 -53.8pp +16.5pp +676 +2.10 +15.9pp

Cluster interpretations:

  • Cluster 0 (high-tech, n=61): Staffing and IT-services firms that responded to the AI wave by quadrupling technology-stack keyword density without changing job descriptions much otherwise. Exemplars: Mastech Digital, UST, American Unit Inc, Apex Systems, Cognizant. The "keyword stuffing" response.
  • Cluster 1 (low-tech, n=60): Mild AI adoption, no meaningful scope/length/tech changes, but a meaningful +12.8pp entry share rise (YOE proxy). Exemplars: Aditi Consulting, Piper Companies, TikTok, Experis, DataAnnotation. The "status-quo" group — mostly staffing/contract firms plus TikTok and one labeling-data outlier (DataAnnotation).
  • Cluster 2 (high-length / AI-forward, n=28): Massive length growth (+1,911 chars LLM-only, on top of 2024 baseline ~2K), largest AI-rate jump (+40pp), moderate tech & scope gains. Exemplars: Talent Groups, Fanatics, Unisys, Veridian, hackajob. Among top AI deltas: ServiceNow (+100pp AI), eTeam, Verizon, Capgemini, Procore. This is the "re-wrote the job description for the AI era" cluster.
  • Cluster 3 (high-scope, n=53): Largest scope-mention rise (+51.5pp — e.g., "own the end-to-end", "cross-functional", "lead projects"), moderate tech/AI/length changes. Exemplars: Zillion Tech, Actalent, Solomon Page, AVEVA, Meta. Top AI-delta exemplars include Wells Fargo (+36pp AI, +17pp entry YOE) and CarMax. The "expanding role scope language" cluster.
  • Cluster 4 (entry-downsizer, n=18): Unique — -53.8pp entry YOE share (i.e., these 18 companies stopped posting entry-level entirely). Exemplars: General Dynamics Mission Systems, WEX, Leidos, Radley James, Ledgent Tech. Top AI-delta: Talener, Macquarie Group (-66pp entry, +7 tech count), LanceSoft, Honeywell. These are defense/financial/staffing firms where the 2024 baseline had unusually high entry share and 2026 reverted to mid-senior. Mechanism is likely composition: 2024 arshkon snapshot caught an entry-heavy moment at these firms that did not repeat.

Figure: figures/T16/cluster_boxplots.png. Full cluster assignments: tables/T16/overlap_per_company_changes_with_clusters.csv.

docs.title: 6. Within/between decomposition
docs.text:
Shift-share decomposition of the 2024→2026 aggregate change within the 220-company overlap panel (company weights at period-specific shares, within = shared avg share × Δmetric, between = shared avg metric × Δshare):

Metric agg 2024 agg 2026 total Δ within between within % between % Entry share (combined col) 0.034 0.042 +0.88pp -0.27pp +1.15pp -31% 131% Entry share (YOE≤2) 0.127 0.192 +6.50pp +0.82pp +5.68pp 13% 87% AI rate 0.026 0.219 +19.3pp +17.6pp +1.8pp 91% 9% Desc length (raw) 3,520 5,412 +1,892 +958 +934 51% 49% Desc length (LLM-only) 1,931 2,634 +703 +523 +180 74% 26% Tech count 6.15 8.32 +2.16 +2.44 -0.28 113% -13% Scope rate 0.275 0.494 +21.9pp +17.3pp +4.5pp 79% 21% Entry share (native, arshkon-only) 0.205 0.091 -11.4pp -9.1pp -2.3pp 80% 20% Entry share (seniority_final) 0.218 0.118 -9.9pp -8.5pp -1.5pp 85% 15%

Critical contradiction across seniority operationalizations:

  • YOE proxy: entry-share rise (+6.5pp aggregate) is 87% between-company — i.e., 2026 reweights toward companies that already had high entry shares in 2024.
  • Native / seniority_final: entry-share decline (-9.9pp to -11.4pp) is 80-85% within-company — companies are posting less entry under these labels.
  • Combined best-available: small positive (+0.88pp) but with opposite-sign within/between contributions; within is slightly negative (-0.27pp).

The YOE-proxy and the native-label stories point in opposite directions and disagree about whether the change is within or between companies. Per the seniority measurement investigation (Wave 1) and V1 verification, the native labels are known to be contaminated by employer labeling conventions — 41% of arshkon native-entry rows have YOE≥5. The YOE proxy is label-independent and does not suffer from this.

Interpretation (reconciled): the 2026 YOE-proxy entry-share rise is driven by (a) between-company composition — 2026 reweights toward Google, Walmart, Qualcomm, SpaceX etc. and away from finance/consulting/staffing that was over-represented in 2024 arshkon — and (b) the companies that did most of the entry posting in 2024 are still doing most of it in 2026 (the within contribution is small and positive). This is NOT a story of employers pivoting to new-grad hiring. It is a story of survivor bias and concentration at a stable small set of companies running formal new-grad programs. The "entry share is rising" framing needs to be reframed as "between-company reweighting + a small within effect at new-grad-program employers".

AI rate (+19.3pp) is 91% within-company — the AI adoption story is genuine, cross-company transformation. Same companies, adding AI language.

Tech count (+2.16) is 113% within-company — real thickening of tech stacks at the same employers, with a small offsetting between-effect.

Scope rate (+21.9pp) is 79% within-company — same companies, expanded scope language. Matches the T11 within-company scope inflation story.

Desc length under LLM-only control is 74% within-company (vs 51% under raw desc) — confirms the length growth is substantially real but smaller than T13 headlined, per V1.

Archetype-stratified decomposition (2-way into within-cell and between-cell using company×archetype cells):

Metric within-cell between-cell total Entry (YOE≤2) +3.3pp +5.4pp +8.7pp AI rate +12.4pp +2.4pp +14.8pp

The between-cell component for entry (+5.4pp, dominant) captures the archetype re-weighting — 2026 has more AI/ML archetype rows, and those have different entry shares than the 2024 archetype mix. For AI rate the story is within-cell (same companies in same archetypes, adopting AI language).

Full tables: tables/T16/decomposition.csv, tables/T16/decomposition_archetype.csv.

docs.title: 7. Within-company entry scope inflation
docs.text:
Restricting to entry-level (YOE≤2) postings within the overlap panel: only 24 companies have ≥2 entry-level rows in both arshkon and scraped. Within those:

  • Δ scope rate (entry-only, within-company): +17.8pp
  • Δ strict mentor rate (entry-only, within-company): +1.6pp

The scope inflation signal at entry level is real within-company at the 24 companies that sustain entry posting across periods. The strict mentor signal (requiring "mentor" + target) is essentially flat, consistent with V1/T11 — the "mentoring" narrative is mostly driven by aggregate-level pattern matching, not specifically by entry-level role descriptions within-company. Table: tables/T16/within_company_entry_scope.csv.

Caveat: n=24 is small. T22's validated scope/mentor patterns were not available at this task's execution time; we used approximations. When T22 ships, re-run this subtask with its strict detectors.

docs.title: 8. New market entrants
docs.text:
Companies appearing in scraped 2026 but with zero presence in either 2024 source:

  • New entrants (≥5 SWE scraped): 483 companies
  • Returners (≥5 SWE, present in 2024): 608 companies
Metric New entrants Returners Diff AI rate 0.254 0.200 +5.4pp Desc length 4,830 4,787 +43 YOE-entry rate 0.087 0.097 -1.0pp Scope rate 0.558 0.510 +4.8pp

New entrants are modestly more AI-forward and more scope-heavy than returners, but less entry-heavy. Top new-entrant industries: Software Development (169), IT Services (57), Tech/Info/Internet (35), Financial Services (31), Defense/Space (11), Hospitals/Health Care (9), Business Consulting (7), Staffing (7), Telecom (6). Full table: tables/T16/new_entrants_top_industries.csv.

This says the 2026 scraped corpus isn't massively different from 2024 in company composition — it's an expansion (+483 new companies past the ≥5 cutoff) with a mild AI/scope lean and slightly less entry-level activity in the new cohort. The entry-share rise is NOT being driven by new entrants.

docs.title: 9. Aggregator vs direct employer
docs.text:
Aggregators dropped from ~14% (arshkon 2024, 431/4,620 = 9.3%) and ~15% (asaniczka 2024, 2,724/17,774) to 16.2% (scraped 2026, 4,715/29,106) of SWE volume. Within each period, aggregators show lower AI rate, lower YOE-entry rate, and lower scope rate than direct employers. The absolute direction of change is similar (both groups rising on all three dimensions), so the aggregator share shift is not a dominant confound for corpus-level metrics.

Sensitivity: dropping aggregators from the overlap panel (194 cos remaining), the YOE-entry decomposition is within=-0.99pp, between=+6.48pp. Same story: between-company dominates.

Full table: tables/T16/aggregator_vs_direct.csv.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
(a) Aggregator exclusion: checked — direction preserved, between-company dominance preserved (see §9).

(c) Seniority operationalization: reported full ablation in the decomposition table. Direction disagrees between YOE proxy and native/final labels (YOE up, native down). Resolution per V1 and the seniority investigation: native labels are contaminated by employer labeling conventions; YOE is label-independent. Use YOE as primary.

(b) Company capping: not applied as primary per the task spec (per-company metrics should not be capped). For the cluster analysis, capping was not applied either — we use company as the unit and each company contributes one change vector.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Defense/aerospace is over-represented in entry-level posting. SpaceX (65%), Northrop Grumman (33%), Raytheon (29%), Peraton (19.5%), Booz Allen (29%), Leidos (18%). This contradicts the default assumption that entry-level new-grad programs are concentrated in "tech" firms (Google, Meta, etc.). Defense contractors run formal new-grad pipelines at scale, partly driven by clearance-track recruitment. Suggests a follow-up: is the "new-grad program" definition too narrow if we're missing defense?

  2. 90.4% combined-column concentration vs 49.3% YOE-proxy concentration. These differ by 41pp. The combined column is so tight because almost no company uses explicit "entry"/"junior"/"new grad" title keywords for their SWE roles — they use "Software Engineer" + YOE body text. The combined-column "entry" signal is a measure of titling conventions, not of actual entry hiring. This is itself a methodological finding: the combined column underestimates entry-level activity by roughly 2×. Any analysis that uses combined-column entry share as an indicator of "how much new-grad hiring is happening" will systematically miss half of it.

  3. YOE proxy shows the 2026 entry rise is 87% between-company. This is a substantial reframing of the Wave 2 / V1 narrative. The entry rise is REAL but not a market-wide behavior change — it's composition. 2026 scraped reweights toward companies that were already entry-heavy in 2024. This has implications for the paper's narrative: the entry rise is not "employers are hiring more juniors" but "the 2026 data sample is weighted toward the employers who have always hired juniors." The 13% within-company component represents actual change at stable entry posters — small but positive.

  4. Combined-column within-company entry share is slightly negative (-0.27pp). Under the combined column, overlap-panel companies are individually posting slightly less entry than they were in 2024. It's the between-company share shift (+1.15pp) that pushes the aggregate positive. The YOE proxy shows a small positive within-component (+0.82pp), not negative, so the disagreement is small in magnitude. Both are consistent with "most of the signal is between-company".

  5. One cluster is an entry-downsizer cluster (n=18). These 18 companies (General Dynamics, WEX, Leidos, Radley James, Ledgent, Talener, Macquarie, LanceSoft, Honeywell) had unusually high entry YOE share in 2024 and near-zero in 2026. Mechanism is likely arshkon snapshot effect — 2024 caught an entry-heavy moment that didn't repeat. Worth checking whether this is explained by aggregator volume (staffing firms).

  6. ServiceNow hit +100pp AI rate (0% → 100%). Every single 2026 ServiceNow posting in the overlap panel mentions validated AI terms. This is an extreme exemplar of the "re-write JDs for the AI era" cluster 2.

  7. DataAnnotation ended up in cluster 1 (low-tech, entry-rising) rather than AI-forward. DataAnnotation is Scale AI's RLHF-labelers business. Their postings are intentionally low-tech (they want non-specialist freelancers) and entry-focused — which is consistent with cluster 1. It's a reminder that "AI-forward" on the demand side includes a labor force that doesn't look like traditional SWE.

  8. The combined-column "entry share rose +0.88pp" finding has within=-0.27pp. V1 reported combined-column entry rise at +3.48pp corpus-wide; on the overlap panel it's only +0.88pp. The difference is composition — companies that are ONLY in scraped (new entrants) contribute to the corpus-wide delta but not the overlap panel. And within the overlap panel, the within-company component is slightly negative. So the combined-column story depends entirely on between-company composition (both at the overlap level and the corpus level).

docs.title: Critical implications for the paper narrative
docs.text:
The Wave 2 / V1 headline "entry share is rising" survives verification, but T16's within/between decomposition refines it substantially:

  • Under the YOE proxy, the rise is +6.5pp aggregate, of which +0.82pp is within-company (same employers posting more entry) and +5.68pp is between-company (2026 reweights toward entry-heavy employers).
  • Under the combined column, the rise is +0.88pp aggregate, of which -0.27pp is within-company and +1.15pp is between-company. Combined column has essentially zero within-company signal.
  • Under the native/seniority_final labels (contaminated), the change is negative, and 80-85% within-company.

Recommended paper framing: "The 2026 entry-share rise is driven by composition — specifically, the 2026 corpus samples more heavily from a small set of large-employer new-grad programs (Google, Walmart, Qualcomm, SpaceX, Microsoft, Amazon, defense contractors) that have always been heavy entry posters. 90% of other substantial-presence employers post zero entry-level roles. Within-company entry share is essentially flat under the YOE proxy. This is a concentration story, not a market-wide hiring pivot."

This does not contradict V1 — it extends it with the decomposition evidence. V1 established the rise is real and concentration-driven at ~12 large employers; T16 establishes that within that concentrated set, even those employers aren't doing more entry hiring than they were in 2024 — they are simply being sampled more heavily in 2026.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• Script: exploration/scripts/T16_company_strategies.py
  • Figures: exploration/figures/T16/cluster_boxplots.png
  • Tables: exploration/tables/T16/ (dedup_impact_top30.csv, top20_entry_posters_scraped.csv, entry_poster_industries.csv, overlap_per_company_changes.csv, overlap_per_company_changes_with_clusters.csv, cluster_centers.csv, decomposition.csv, decomposition_archetype.csv, within_company_entry_scope.csv, new_entrants_vs_returners.csv, new_entrants_top_industries.csv, aggregator_vs_direct.csv)

docs.title: T17 — Geographic market structure
docs.text: Agent: J (Wave 3) Status: done Date: 2026-04-10 Frame: SWE, LinkedIn, is_english, date_flag='ok'. Description-hash dedup within company applied. Metros with ≥50 SWE in both periods. Script: exploration/scripts/T17_geographic_structure.py

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. 26 metros qualify for comparison (≥50 SWE both periods). AI-rate gains range from +11.2pp (Detroit) to +30.3pp (Tampa Bay). Change is broad but not uniform — Tampa, Salt Lake City, Miami, NYC, SF Bay all jumped +25-30pp; Detroit, DC, LA, Denver, San Diego lagged at +11-14pp.

  2. AI gain does NOT correlate with entry share gain across metros. Pearson r=-0.09 (p=0.65) under YOE proxy, r=-0.20 (p=0.32) under combined column. The AI surge and entry rise are geographically decoupled — they are not the same phenomenon unfolding city by city.

  3. AI gain DOES correlate with scope-rate gain across metros (Pearson r=+0.43, p=0.030; Spearman r=+0.44, p=0.023). Metros that saw larger AI adoption also saw larger scope-language expansion. This is a meaningful signal — AI adoption and scope inflation are the same metro-level phenomenon.

  4. Remote share jumped from ~0% (2024) to 22.3% (2026) corpus-wide. Remote vs onsite postings have nearly identical AI rates (22.9% vs 24.2%) and scope rates (54.7% vs 53.0%). Remote is not an AI-differentiated segment — it's a transformation that happened uniformly across both kinds of roles.

  5. AI/ML archetype is concentrated in SF Bay Area (26.7%), Boston (17.9%), Seattle (16.3%), San Diego, Austin. Surprisingly, Houston (15.6%) ranks 4th — not a traditional AI hub, likely driven by energy-sector AI/ML or medical AI (MD Anderson, Baylor). Houston is a surprise that warrants a sanity check.

  6. NYC scraped count is 26× arshkon (64 → 1,644). The NYC changes cannot be interpreted as a real labor market shift because the 2024 arshkon baseline is essentially a 64-posting sample. Asaniczka contribution partially mitigates this but the pooled 2024 NYC count is still small. All NYC-specific claims are flagged as likely coverage artifacts.

docs.title: 1. Metro-level metrics (26 metros, dedup panel)
docs.text:
Top 5 and bottom 5 metros on each change dimension:

Δ AI rate: - Top: Tampa Bay +30.3pp, Salt Lake City +26.6pp, Miami +25.8pp, NYC +25.7pp, SF Bay +25.6pp - Bot: Detroit +11.2pp, DC +11.4pp, LA +11.7pp, Denver +11.9pp, San Diego +14.4pp

Δ entry share (YOE≤2): - Top: San Diego +25.8pp, SF Bay +12.5pp, Seattle +12.4pp, Philadelphia +12.0pp, LA +11.9pp - Bot: Houston -7.3pp, Detroit -6.1pp, Tampa Bay -1.5pp, Dallas-FW -1.3pp, Salt Lake City -0.4pp

Δ entry share (combined col): - Top: Miami +12.1pp, Portland +9.4pp, San Diego +5.5pp, Philadelphia +5.4pp, Nashville +5.4pp - Bot: NYC -4.6pp, Charlotte -3.4pp, Atlanta -1.9pp, Dallas-FW -1.8pp, Phoenix -1.1pp

Δ scope rate: - Top: Atlanta +29.3pp, Portland +29.3pp, NYC +29.0pp, Salt Lake City +23.9pp, Austin +23.7pp - Bot: Denver +12.6pp, Pittsburgh +14.9pp, LA +15.5pp, Dallas-FW +16.0pp, Nashville +16.5pp

Δ median description length: - Top: Philadelphia +1,889, NYC +1,724, San Diego +1,660, Detroit +1,634, Minneapolis-St.Paul +1,578 - Bot: Salt Lake City +181, Denver +565, Pittsburgh +567, Raleigh-Durham +621, Columbus +775

Salt Lake City is notable — biggest AI gain and biggest scope gain in the top 5, yet smallest length gain (+181 chars). It achieved AI/scope growth without substantial length expansion. Likely a concise-description local convention.

Full tables: tables/T17/metro_changes.csv, tables/T17/metro_metrics_both_periods.csv.

docs.title: 2. Hub vs non-hub
docs.text:
Five hubs defined: SF, NYC, Seattle, Austin, San Jose (matched by first city substring):

Group Δentry_yoe Δentry_best Δai_rate Δscope Δmedian_len Δmedian_tech Hub +6.90pp +1.78pp +22.11pp +25.71pp +1,393 +2.75 Non-hub +4.97pp +2.80pp +19.06pp +20.33pp +1,115 +2.66

Hubs lead non-hubs on entry (YOE) by 2pp, on AI by 3pp, on scope by 5pp, on length by 278 chars, on tech count by 0.1. Hub leads are modest. The 2026 transformation is broad, not concentrated in hubs.

Interestingly, non-hubs lead on entry share under the combined column (+2.80pp vs +1.78pp). This is consistent with T16: the combined-column story is dominated by titling conventions, and hubs use "Software Engineer" titles more than explicit "Junior" titles. Table: tables/T17/hub_vs_nonhub.csv.

docs.title: 3. Metro-level correlations
docs.text:
x y Pearson r p Spearman r p Δ AI rate Δ entry (YOE) -0.093 0.651 -0.065 0.754 Δ AI rate Δ entry (best) -0.204 0.317 -0.244 0.229 Δ AI rate Δ scope rate +0.426 0.030 +0.443 0.023 Δ AI rate Δ median length -0.074 0.719 -0.066 0.749 Δ entry (YOE) Δ scope rate +0.134 0.515 +0.063 0.759

Only AI↔scope is statistically significant at α=0.05 (n=26). All other correlations are indistinguishable from zero at this sample size.

Interpretation: At the metro level, AI adoption and scope expansion are coupled (same underlying driver — role enlargement + AI language). But AI adoption and entry-share rise are uncorrelated geographically, meaning the "AI-forward employers hire more juniors" hypothesis does not hold at the geographic-aggregate level. The metros with the biggest AI surges (Tampa Bay -1.5pp entry, Salt Lake City -0.4pp) did not see the biggest entry gains. The metros with the biggest entry gains (San Diego, SF Bay, Seattle) are not the biggest AI gainers.

The AI and entry phenomena are driven by different mechanisms: AI is a broad corpus-wide text shift; entry-level concentration is a company-mix story (see T16).

Figure: figures/T17/metro_correlations.png (scatter with metro labels).

Full table: tables/T17/metro_correlations.csv.

docs.title: 4. Remote work dimension
docs.text:
Data limitation: scraped remote postings (7,809 rows) have metro_area = NULL because the scraper's geographic routing excludes them. We cannot compute per-metro remote shares from this dataset. Instead, we compute corpus-level remote share and compare remote vs onsite characteristics.

Corpus-level remote share: - 2024: 0.0035% (1 remote row in 28,232; essentially zero — neither kaggle dataset flagged remote) - 2026: 22.3% (7,809 remote of 35,062)

This is a jump, but the 2024 baseline is itself a data artifact (kaggle datasets don't flag remote). We cannot distinguish real growth in remote from better remote tagging. The 2026 22.3% figure is a reasonable estimate of current state; the "change" is not interpretable.

Remote vs onsite profile (2026):

Period is_remote n AI rate scope rate 2024 False 28,231 2.0% 29.8% 2024 True 1 — — 2026 False 27,253 24.2% 53.0% 2026 True 7,809 22.9% 54.7%

Remote and onsite are near-identical on AI and scope rates in 2026. Remote is not an AI-differentiated segment. This contradicts a plausible hypothesis ("remote attracts more AI-forward roles"). Tables: tables/T17/remote_vs_onsite.csv, tables/T17/remote_share_by_period.csv.

docs.title: 5. Archetype geographic distribution
docs.text:
T09 archetype labels are available for 8,000 rows total, of which 5,108 are in the metro panel (after dedup). 17 metros have ≥30 archetype-labeled rows in both periods.

The single AI-related archetype in T09's output is T01_ai / learning / machine learning. Top 10 metros by AI/ML archetype share (2026):

Metro AI/ML share San Francisco Bay Area 26.7% Boston Metro 17.9% Seattle Metro 16.3% Houston Metro 15.6% Chicago Metro 15.1% San Diego Metro 14.5% Austin Metro 14.4% Atlanta Metro 13.6% Minneapolis-Saint Paul 13.5% Dallas-Fort Worth Metro 11.8%

SF Bay is the overwhelming leader. Boston, Seattle, San Diego, Austin are traditional AI/ML hubs. Houston at rank 4 (15.6%) is a surprise — not a traditional AI hub. Possible drivers: energy-sector ML (oil/gas forecasting, seismic imaging), medical AI (MD Anderson, Texas Medical Center, Baylor), NASA JSC ML work. Warrants spot-checking a sample of Houston AI/ML postings to confirm this isn't a classifier artifact from the archetype model.

Delta table (archetype share change 2024→2026) saved at tables/T17/archetype_delta_by_metro.csv. Full snapshots at tables/T17/archetype_by_metro_2024.csv, tables/T17/archetype_by_metro_2026.csv.

docs.title: 6. Metro heatmap
docs.text:
Figure: figures/T17/metro_heatmap.png. Rows = 26 metros, columns = 6 change metrics (entry_yoe, entry_best, ai_rate, scope_rate, median_len, median_tech), values normalized per-column to [-1,1] for visual comparability with raw deltas annotated.

Visible patterns: - SF Bay, Seattle, San Diego, Philadelphia cluster in the "high on everything" corner. - Denver, Pittsburgh, Salt Lake City show weak length gains but strong AI. - Detroit, Houston, Dallas-FW, Tampa Bay show weak entry changes but varied AI. - No clean "eastern rust belt vs coastal tech hubs" split emerges — the transformation is pan-geographic with noisy rankings.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivity: aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Dropping aggregators, 26 metros still qualify. Aggregate medians essentially unchanged:

  • ΔAI median: 0.188 → 0.193 (no-agg)
  • ΔYOE-entry median: 0.039 → 0.043 (no-agg)

The metro-level changes are not meaningfully driven by aggregator composition. Table: tables/T17/metro_changes_no_aggregator.csv.

docs.title: 8. NYC coverage caveat (explicit check)
docs.text:
NYC SWE postings by source: - arshkon: 11 - asaniczka: 53 - scraped: 1,644

Pooled 2024 baseline is 64 rows. Scraped/pooled ratio is 26×. Any NYC-specific change metric is dominated by sample expansion, not by real market shifts. In particular, the "NYC is a top-5 AI gainer" finding (+25.7pp) should be treated as "consistent with the broader trend at a metro where the 2024 baseline is statistically thin."

The two NYC-specific claims in this report are flagged accordingly: - NYC top-5 AI gain → plausible but baseline-thin. - NYC top-3 scope gain → plausible but baseline-thin. - NYC is the WORST metro on combined-column entry (-4.6pp) → probably the most interpretable NYC signal, because it's directionally consistent with T16's "combined-column has low power" story.

Other metros (SF, Seattle, Boston, DC, Dallas-FW) have ≥700 rows in both periods and are interpretation-safe.

docs.title: Sensitivities checklist
docs.text: (a) Aggregator exclusion: checked, §7. Direction preserved. (b) Company capping: not applied. For metro-level aggregates, company capping would defeat the purpose because large-employer hubs are part of what defines the metros. Per T16, capping is only necessary for cross-period metro comparisons dominated by one employer. Spot check: NYC 2026 is not Amazon/Google dominated (no single employer >10%). (c) Seniority operationalization: reported both combined col and YOE proxy in all metro rankings. They disagree on some metros (e.g., Miami: combined col +12.1pp, YOE +8.6pp, same direction; NYC: combined col -4.6pp, YOE +2.9pp, opposite direction). The general T16 caveat applies: YOE proxy is the more reliable entry signal.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. AI gain and entry gain are geographically uncorrelated (r=-0.09). A strong prior would be that AI-forward metros would also be the ones hiring more juniors (because Wave 2 showed entry postings added AI language faster). The geographic data rejects this at the metro-aggregate level. The two phenomena have different mechanisms — AI is a cross-the-board text shift; entry is a company-concentration story. This is a significant finding for the paper's narrative. It means "AI is making it harder for juniors" cannot be supported by a geographic argument (metros with more AI would then have fewer juniors — correlation would be negative, p=0.65 — not zero, not positive).

  2. AI↔scope correlation is +0.43 (p=0.03). This is the strongest metro-level link in the analysis. AI adoption and scope-language expansion travel together geographically. Both are text-level shifts driven by the same underlying force (employers rewriting JDs around AI capability + expanding role scope), consistent with T16's within-company decomposition showing both are within-company stories (91% within for AI, 79% within for scope).

  3. Tampa Bay and Salt Lake City lead AI gains (+30pp, +27pp). Not traditional AI hubs. Tampa's tech scene has grown substantially (USF, Jabil, local SaaS firms); SLC is the "Silicon Slopes" (Qualtrics, Domo, Pluralsight, local AI startups). Both are smaller metros with ~200-1,300 postings in 2026 — volatility is higher but the direction is believable. SLC's +26.6pp AI with near-zero length gain is a distinctive profile — they changed content without padding.

  4. Houston at rank 4 for AI/ML archetype (15.6%). Surprising. Should be spot-checked — may indicate energy-sector ML, medical AI, or classifier misfire. Archetype sample size at Houston metro is moderate (~100 rows in the 5,108-row panel).

  5. San Diego has the largest YOE-entry gain (+25.8pp) — more than SF Bay (+12.5pp) or Seattle (+12.4pp). Qualcomm (124 SWE, 80 entry rows, 79% entry) is based in San Diego and is the single biggest YOE-proxy entry employer in the dataset. San Diego's entry rise is almost certainly a Qualcomm single-employer effect. Plus SpaceX has a Hawthorne / LA footprint but some San Diego presence. Recommendation: re-run San Diego metro with Qualcomm excluded to test robustness.

  6. Remote and onsite have identical AI/scope rates in 2026. The "remote attracts AI-forward roles" hypothesis is rejected. Remote transformation happened uniformly.

  7. Portland's scope rate jumped +29.3pp (tied for top) with only 121→145 rows. Small-sample volatility is high but tied for the top is still notable — Intel, Nike, and a growing set of local tech firms.

  8. NYC scope-rate gain (+29.0pp, top 3) despite the coverage caveat. Even if the magnitude is noisy, the direction is consistent with the hypothesis that scope language expanded uniformly across metros. The NYC entry/length numbers are less interpretable.

  9. Detroit and Houston both have negative entry-YOE deltas (-6.1pp, -7.3pp). Traditional manufacturing/energy metros. Possibly reflects a shift toward senior-only postings in automotive and oil/gas, or composition of 2024 arshkon snapshot. The direction is the opposite of the SWE-sector narrative.

  10. The "coastal tech hub vs rust belt" divide does NOT cleanly appear in the heatmap. The 2026 AI/scope transformation is broadly distributed — rust-belt metros (Detroit, Pittsburgh, Cleveland-not-sampled) show meaningful AI gains too. The story is less "Silicon Valley and the rest" and more "everywhere, with hubs leading slightly."

docs.title: T18 — Cross-occupation boundary analysis
docs.text: Last updated: 2026-04-09 (Agent K, Wave 3)

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The AI/ML restructuring is real and SWE-adjacent, but NOT field-wide. Over 2024→2026, SWE AI-keyword prevalence rose from 1.9% to 24.8% (+22.9pp). SWE-adjacent rose nearly identically: 1.6%→20.6% (+19.0pp). Control occupations rose only 0.08%→1.27% (+1.2pp). Difference-in-differences (SWE − control) = +21.7pp AI rate and +7.6pp agentic. The AI story is a tech-sector phenomenon that extends beyond the SWE title boundary to data-scientist / ML-engineer / security-engineer / data-analyst, but does NOT reach accountants, nurses, electrical/mechanical engineers, or financial analysts at more than token rates. The "SWE-specific" framing should be weakened to "tech-cluster-specific."

Boundary textual similarity (TF-IDF centroid cosine on balanced 400-doc samples):

Pair 2024 2026 Δ SWE ↔ adjacent 0.830 0.808 −0.022 SWE ↔ control 0.418 0.497 +0.079 adjacent ↔ control 0.473 0.478 +0.005

The SWE ↔ adjacent boundary did NOT blur — it slightly diverged. But SWE became meaningfully more similar to control postings (+0.079 cosine, ~19% relative increase), driven by shared length expansion, shared inclusive-language shifts, and moderate diffusion of scope vocabulary into control. The cross-occupation convergence is "SWE is writing more like everyone else," not "adjacent is writing more like SWE." This inverts the obvious hypothesis.

docs.title: Inputs and filters
docs.text:
• data/unified.parquet, Wave 1.5 unified file.
  • Filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok', no is_swe filter. Three groups via is_swe, is_swe_adjacent, is_control.
  • 2024 period = period IN ('2024-01','2024-04') (pooled); 2026 period = period IN ('2026-03','2026-04') (pooled).
  • Sample sizes:
Group 2024 2026 SWE 28,232 35,062 SWE-adjacent 13,543 9,823 control 120,525 31,686
  • Note on control composition: top titles include accountant (7k), registered nurse (3.7k), electrical/mechanical/civil engineer, financial analyst, accounting manager, NP. These are intentionally chosen non-tech occupations so the control captures "general white-collar / professional" trends.
  • Seniority column: combined best-available (seniority_llm where labeled else seniority_final where rule-sufficient else NULL); YOE proxy as co-equal validator.
  • AI-keyword regex: agentic|multi-agent|AI agent|llm|rag|generative ai|copilot|claude|ai-powered|large language model — follows V1's agent-contamination warning (bare agent excluded).

docs.title: 1. Parallel trends (baseline)
docs.text:
All values in pp unless noted. Full table at exploration/tables/T18/trends_baseline.csv.

Metric SWE 2024 SWE 2026 adj 2024 adj 2026 ctrl 2024 ctrl 2026 entry_share (combined) 1.87% 5.35% 1.95% 4.14% 12.93% 10.64% YOE ≤ 2 10.34% 16.61% 12.42% 17.16% 60.89% 44.62% AI rate 1.86% 24.76% 1.58% 20.56% 0.08% 1.27% agentic (strict) 0.02% 8.02% 0.01% 7.28% 0.00% 0.40% scope language 29.06% 48.01% 29.49% 55.78% 5.23% 18.98% median desc len (raw chars) 2049 3737 2339 3088 2122 2909 median desc len (LLM-text only) 2028 2555 2320 2948 1739 2055 mean tech count (15-tech safe list) 2.44 2.69 1.04 1.78 0.016 0.041

Figures at exploration/figures/T18/: - ai_gradient.png — AI and agentic rates across all four native periods by group. - did_bars.png — 2024→2026 Δ per metric × group. - length_by_group.png — raw vs LLM-text-controlled median length. - boundary_similarity.png — TF-IDF cosine over time.

docs.title: Sensitivity (a) — aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Excluding is_aggregator=true rows tightens the SWE and adjacent samples but leaves the DiD qualitatively unchanged. SWE AI Δ is 22.9pp in baseline and 23.4pp no-aggregator; agentic Δ is 8.0 → 8.3pp. Control AI Δ stays at ~1.2pp. See did_no_agg.csv.

docs.title: Sensitivity (g) — exclude SWE title_lookup_llm tier
docs.text: This tier had the highest FP rate (22.2%, T04). Excluding it drops 1,587 SWE 2026 rows and 1,566 adjacent 2026 rows. Headline Δ for AI rate becomes 23.2pp (vs 22.9 baseline), agentic 8.1pp (vs 8.0), and scope 18.7pp (vs 19.0). The AI/scope DiD is not driven by borderline-classified SWE rows. See did_no_title_lookup_llm.csv.

docs.title: 2. Difference-in-differences table
docs.text:
From did_baseline.csv (pp or absolute units):

Metric SWE Δ adj Δ ctrl Δ DiD (SWE−ctrl) DiD (SWE−adj) entry_share (combined) +3.48 +2.19 −2.28 +5.77 +1.29 YOE ≤ 2 +6.27 +4.74 −16.28 +22.55 +1.53 AI rate +22.90 +18.98 +1.19 +21.71 +3.91 agentic (strict) +8.01 +7.27 +0.40 +7.60 +0.73 scope language +18.95 +26.29 +13.75 +5.20 −7.34 median desc len (raw chars) +1688 +749 +788 +900 +939 median desc len (LLM-text only) +527 +629 +316 +211 −102 mean tech count (safe list) +0.25 +0.74 +0.025 +0.22 −0.49 AI rate (LLM-text only) +19.61 +18.63 +0.59 +19.02 +0.98

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
1. AI restructuring survives every DiD framing as the dominant SWE-specific signal vs control, but becomes small-to-null vs adjacent. SWE−control = +21.7pp; SWE−adjacent = only +3.9pp. The AI story's "specific to SWE" framing is WEAKER than anticipated — SWE-adjacent tech roles share nearly the full shift. The right framing is "tech-occupation cluster," not "SWE." The agentic strict keyword confirms this with a sharper signal: +8.0pp SWE, +7.3pp adjacent, +0.4pp control.

  2. Entry-level rise is SWE + adjacent, NOT control. SWE combined-column entry Δ +3.5pp, adjacent +2.2pp, control −2.3pp. Under the YOE proxy the divergence is even larger: SWE +6.3pp, adjacent +4.7pp, control −16.3pp. Control's YOE≤2 drop is large — partially driven by the control sample being heavily nurse/accountant, where the asaniczka January 2024 sample has unusually high YOE≤2 and/or unusually many "no years required" postings; the cross-time drop of 16pp should be treated as source-composition noise rather than a real 2024→2026 decline in entry demand for nurses. Even so, the SWE−control DiD on YOE proxy is +22.5pp — the strongest entry-rise signal in the DiD table.

  3. Scope language DiD is surprisingly NEGATIVE vs adjacent (−7.3pp). SWE-adjacent grew scope vocabulary faster than SWE (+26.3pp vs +19.0pp). This is consistent with data/security/solutions-architect adjacent roles absorbing "stakeholder/roadmap/cross-functional" framing. Against control, SWE scope growth is only +5.2pp — the scope-inflation finding (T08, T11) is only weakly SWE-specific vs control, and NOT SWE-specific vs adjacent.

  4. Length growth (raw) is strongest for SWE (+1688 chars median) and weaker for adjacent (+749) and control (+788). Under LLM-text-source control, the picture flips dramatically: SWE +527, adjacent +629, control +316. Adjacent has the largest text-source-controlled length growth, not SWE. Per V1, the raw SWE +1688 is partly composition (2024 91% LLM-cleaned, 2026 21% LLM-cleaned); the true content expansion in SWE is closer to +527 median (LLM-text-only), which is roughly what adjacent also shows. The length-inflation story is a tech-cluster phenomenon, not SWE-specific.

  5. Tech-count DiD is weakly positive for SWE vs control (+0.22) but negative vs adjacent (−0.49). Adjacent grew its mean tech count more than SWE did (+0.74 vs +0.25) — consistent with data-science and security-engineering postings absorbing broader tech stacks. SWE tech count was already near-saturated in the safe-list definition.

docs.title: 3. Boundary similarity analysis
docs.text:
Method: from each (group, 2-period bucket) draw a balanced 400-doc sample (minimum description length 500 chars), build a single TF-IDF(1-2 grams, 10k features, min_df=3, max_df=0.8, basic English + company-stoplist subset) on the combined corpus, compute centroid cosine between group means. The T12-identified markdown backslash escapes (C\+\+, C\#, .NET\-Core) are pre-stripped.

From boundary_similarity.csv:

Pair 2024 2026 Δ SWE ↔ adjacent 0.830 0.808 −0.022 SWE ↔ control 0.418 0.497 +0.079 adjacent ↔ control 0.473 0.478 +0.005

The SWE ↔ adjacent boundary did NOT blur in text space. Cosine slightly diverged (−0.022). The naive hypothesis was that data/ML/security engineers would become more SWE-like as tooling democratized. Instead, they became mildly less SWE-like — likely because SWE postings themselves adopted distinctive agentic/RAG/copilot vocabulary that adjacent picked up only partially, while SWE boilerplate and inclusive-language shifts moved it toward control norms. The magnitude is small (−0.022 over 2 years) but the direction is against expectation.

The most striking cross-boundary shift is SWE ↔ control: +0.079 cosine, +19% relative. SWE postings in 2026 are noticeably more similar to accountant/nurse/financial-analyst postings than they were in 2024. Driven by: (1) shared length expansion (SWE 2049→3737, control 2122→2909 median chars) crowding the centroid toward shared whitespace/structural vocabulary; (2) shared inclusive-language shifts; (3) shared growth of "cross-functional/stakeholders/roadmap" vocabulary (+14pp control, +19pp SWE — control catching up).

This is NOT "SWE is becoming generic" — the text still carries heavy tech-keyword weight — but it IS evidence that some share of the observed SWE text changes reflect cross-occupation genre drift rather than SWE-specific restructuring. Any claim that "SWE postings diverged from general white-collar writing" is contradicted by this finding.

Top-term migration analysis is in boundary_top_terms.csv (top 25 distinguishing terms per pair per period). Notable: by 2026, terms like agentic, llm, rag, copilot, multi-agent, langchain are top-SWE-distinguishing vs both adjacent and control — they did NOT migrate to adjacent at the full SWE magnitude.

docs.title: 4. Specific adjacent titles
docs.text:
Top 5 adjacent titles by volume: network_engineer (1,359), quality_engineer (1,228), data_scientist (1,199), data_analyst (1,011), architect (538). Profile changes by period in adjacent_titles_profile.csv:

Title Period AI rate Python rate median_len network engineer 2024-01 0.0% 8.9% 2142 network engineer 2026-03 2.8% 25.3% 2983 quality engineer 2024-01 0.0% 1.3% 2391 quality engineer 2026-03 0.0% 7.5% 2704 data scientist 2024-01 11.7% 83.7% 2242 data scientist 2026-03 29.4% 87.5% 2897 data analyst 2024-01 0.8% 43.3% 2176 data analyst 2026-03 1.8% 34.9% 2356
  • data_scientist is the clearest AI-ward movement (AI rate 11.7%→29.4%, +17.6pp). Data scientist postings are the most SWE-adjacent-becoming-SWE-like title.
  • network_engineer and data_analyst are NOT AI-adopting (both <3%). These adjacent titles are not part of the AI-cluster shift.
  • quality_engineer grew a small Python footprint but no AI mentions — still adjacent, not absorbing SWE content.
  • "architect" collapsed from n=510 (2024-01) to n=3 (2026-03). Almost all 2026 "architects" are being classified as SWE rather than adjacent — a classifier boundary shift, not necessarily a real labor shift.

No top-5 adjacent title became indistinguishable from SWE in 2026. Data scientist is the closest and still has a distinctive Python-heavy / non-agentic profile.

docs.title: 5. AI Engineer content evolution (T05 follow-up)
docs.text:
T05 noted "AI Engineer" title exploded 6→321. Re-profiled in ai_engineer_profile.csv:

Period n mean_len agentic rate pytorch rate llm_stack rate swe_frac 2024-01 77 2446 0.0% 22.1% 26.0% 98.7% 2024-04 36 2304 2.8% 41.7% 30.6% 86.1% 2026-03 1,042 4167 37.8% 29.0% 66.1% 86.9% 2026-04 531 4325 31.3% 29.9% 65.7% 88.9%

"AI Engineer" in 2024 was a PyTorch/ML-modeling role; by 2026 it is an LLM-agent orchestration role. agentic keyword went 0→37.8%; llm_stack (langchain/langgraph/rag/llm) went 26%→66%; pytorch rate stayed roughly flat at 22%→29%. Title volume exploded ~14× (total across 2026: 1,573 vs 113 in 2024). The 2026 AI Engineer title is a distinct role archetype, not a relabeling of ML Engineer. Descriptions are also ~80% longer (median 2446→4167). It remains classified as SWE at 87-89% — i.e. the content still reads as "build software that uses LLMs" rather than "research ML."

docs.title: 6. Cross-validation of the AI/ML finding
docs.text:
The critical validity question for T18: does the T09/T12/T14 AI/ML finding appear at the same magnitude in adjacent and control?

Source SWE 2024→2026 AI rate Δ adj Δ ctrl Δ Wave 2 (T08 keyword scan) +32.2pp (baseline) — — T18 (AI family, baseline) +22.90pp +18.98pp +1.19pp T18 (agentic strict) +8.01pp +7.27pp +0.40pp T18 (AI, LLM-text-only sub) +19.61pp +18.63pp +0.59pp

Answer: (a) Adjacent shows the same pattern at 83% of SWE magnitude (+18.98/+22.90 = 0.83); (b) Control shows the pattern at 5% of SWE magnitude (+1.19/+22.90 = 0.05).

The correct framing is: AI restructuring is a TECH-CLUSTER phenomenon covering both SWE and SWE-adjacent (data scientist, ML/AI engineer, security engineer, systems admin with automation), NOT a pure SWE phenomenon, and NOT a general labor-market phenomenon. The SWE-specific DiD exists (+3.9pp vs adjacent) but is small relative to the SWE-vs-control DiD (+21.7pp). Paper framing should:

  • Keep the strong claim "AI coding tools are restructuring technology work."
  • Weaken the specific claim "SWE roles are being restructured" to "the SWE and technology-adjacent role cluster is being restructured, and the restructuring has not spread to non-tech white-collar occupations at more than token rates."
  • Note that the ~+1pp control rate is not zero — by 2026-04 control AI rate is 1.79% (up from 0.02% in 2024-04), and the agentic control rate reached 1.04% in 2026-04 from zero. There is a small non-tech diffusion underway.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected findings
docs.text:
1. SWE ↔ adjacent boundary DIVERGED slightly (−0.022), did not blur. Naive prediction was convergence; data shows stable-or-mild-divergence.
  2. SWE ↔ control CONVERGED (+0.079). Larger than the SWE ↔ adjacent change. Cross-occupation genre drift (length, inclusivity, scope language) partially explains the SWE 2024→2026 text changes.
  3. AI adjacent catch-up is ~83% of SWE magnitude — higher than expected. "SWE-adjacent" is nearly as AI-saturated as SWE.
  4. "AI Engineer" title became an LLM-agent role, not an ML-modeling role between 2024 and 2026. Agentic keyword 0→38%. Useful concrete example of within-title content transformation.
  5. Adjacent scope language growth (+26.3pp) EXCEEDS SWE growth (+19.0pp). The scope/org language finding from T08/T11 is MORE SWE-adjacent than SWE. Should be reframed.
  6. Text-source-controlled length growth is actually LARGER in adjacent than SWE (+629 vs +527 median chars). Another finding that softens SWE specificity.
  7. Control YOE≤2 cross-period drop is large (−16.3pp) — plausible source-composition artifact of asaniczka nurse/accountant pool having inflated YOE≤2; should be interpreted cautiously.
  8. Mean tech count grew faster in adjacent than SWE (+0.74 vs +0.25). SWE tech list was near-saturated; adjacent is catching up.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Length comparisons are sensitive to the 2024/2026 LLM-text composition imbalance (V1). Reported both raw and LLM-text-only; the SWE-specificity conclusion holds under both.
  • Control titles are concentrated in accountant/nurse/engineer/financial-analyst — different from BLS control-group choices; not a representative sample of all non-tech labor. The control is the most conservative test available within this dataset.
  • Sample sizes for control 2024-04 in arshkon are only 8.4k postings vs 112k from asaniczka — within-2024 differences between accountants-in-January and nurses-in-April could inflate control noise.
  • The tech-count safe list intentionally excludes tools beyond 15 common ones; absolute magnitudes are not comparable to Wave 2 tech_count metrics (which had the c_cpp regex bug).
  • The 400-doc balanced boundary sample is deliberately small to keep cosine stable; increasing to 1000+ doesn't change the direction (spot-checked).

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T18/trends_baseline.csv — full parallel trends
  • exploration/tables/T18/trends_no_agg.csv, trends_no_title_lookup_llm.csv — sensitivities
  • exploration/tables/T18/did_baseline.csv, did_no_agg.csv, did_no_title_lookup_llm.csv — DiD tables
  • exploration/tables/T18/boundary_similarity.csv, boundary_top_terms.csv — TF-IDF cosine
  • exploration/tables/T18/ai_engineer_profile.csv — AI Engineer title time series
  • exploration/tables/T18/adjacent_titles_profile.csv — top-5 adjacent title profiles
  • exploration/tables/T18/ai_gradient_by_period.csv — AI rate by group × period
  • exploration/figures/T18/ai_gradient.png, did_bars.png, length_by_group.png, boundary_similarity.png

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T18_01_parallel_trends.py, T18_02_boundary_similarity.py, T18_03_specific_titles.py, T18_04_plots.py.

docs.title: T19 — Temporal patterns and rate-of-change estimation
docs.text: Last updated: 2026-04-09 (Agent K, Wave 3)

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Our data is three discrete snapshots, not a time series. asaniczka covers 6 days in Jan 2024 (12-17), arshkon covers ~2 weeks in Apr 2024 (5-20), scraped spans ~8 days in late March 2026 (20-31) plus ~6 days in early April 2026 (1-6). No continuous coverage. Any "annualized rate" is a two-point linear extrapolation, not a fit.

Within the constraint, the cross-period AI rate is accelerating ~4× faster than the within-2024 rate, and agentic is accelerating ~14× faster. Within-2024 annualized AI delta: +2.86pp/year; cross-period annualized AI delta: +11.64pp/year. For agentic: +0.30pp/year (2024 from ~0 base) → +4.14pp/year (cross-period). The within-2024 baseline is so low that the acceleration ratio is noisy for most metrics, but the direction is unambiguous: every AI/scope/length metric's cross-period slope is larger in absolute magnitude than its within-2024 slope.

The 2026 snapshot is stable across the March/April periods on every key metric. AI rate 24.79% → 24.69%; agentic 7.75% → 8.62%; median length 3735 → 3743; YOE≤2 17.1% → 15.7%. The only notable drift between 2026-03 and 2026-04 is the combined best-available entry share (6.34% → 3.25%), which is a known preprocessing dedup artifact (V1) — likely fewer Affirm/Canonical/Epic duplicate entries in April.

Within-2024 source differences behave more like source heterogeneity than genuine temporal change. Arshkon (Apr 2024) AI rate 2.45% vs asaniczka (Jan 2024) 1.74%; median length 1784 vs 2102 (arshkon SHORTER despite being 3 months later). Arshkon is more entry-heavy (22% native entry vs 0%) purely because asaniczka has no native entry labels. Most of the within-2024 variance is source-composition, not temporal signal.

docs.title: Rate-of-change comparison table (SWE, linkedin/en/ok)
docs.text:
From exploration/tables/T19/rates_annualized.csv. All values for SWE sample only.

Metric asaniczka (2024-01) arshkon (2024-04) scraped (2026-03) scraped (2026-04) scraped 2026 pooled Within-2024 annualized Cross-period annualized Accel. ratio entry_native 0.00% 22.33% 14.02% 12.88% 13.66% +89.31pp/yr −4.52pp/yr −0.05 entry_final 0.56% 16.90% 13.74% 12.26% 13.28% +65.36pp/yr −1.89pp/yr −0.03 entry_best (combined) 0.81% 3.53% 6.34% 3.25% 5.37% +10.87pp/yr +0.96pp/yr +0.09 YOE ≤ 2 9.50% 15.00% 17.06% 15.67% 16.62% +22.02pp/yr +0.85pp/yr +0.04 AI rate 1.74% 2.45% 24.79% 24.69% 24.76% +2.86pp/yr +11.64pp/yr 4.07 agentic rate 0.004% 0.08% 7.75% 8.62% 8.02% +0.30pp/yr +4.14pp/yr 13.74 median desc length 2102 1784 3735.5 3743 3738 −1272 chars/yr +1019 chars/yr — median tech count 2.0 2.0 3.0 2.0 2.69 0 +0.36/yr — mean tech count 2.49 2.57 2.75 2.69 2.73 +0.31/yr +0.09/yr 0.28 scope rate 31.51% 32.78% 51.81% 52.85% 52.14% +5.05pp/yr +10.10pp/yr 2.00

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
1. The within-2024 rate estimates are dominated by source heterogeneity, not real temporal change. arshkon's entry_native rate is 22.33% while asaniczka's is 0.00% — but asaniczka has no native entry labels by construction (see T02). The +89pp/year within-2024 rate is a labeling artifact, not a market signal. Same issue for median length: arshkon is SHORTER than asaniczka (1784 vs 2102), producing a "−1272 chars/year" within-2024 rate that reflects source text-cleaning differences, not a declining labor market.

  2. The cleanest rate-of-change comparison uses metrics that are source-robust: AI rate, agentic rate, scope rate, mean tech count. On these:

  3. AI rate: within-2024 +2.86pp/yr, cross-period +11.64pp/yr, accel ratio 4.07. Acceleration is real and large.
  4. agentic: within-2024 +0.30pp/yr, cross-period +4.14pp/yr, accel 13.74 (noisy because baseline is ~0).
  5. scope: within-2024 +5.05pp/yr, cross-period +10.10pp/yr, accel 2.00.

  6. mean tech count: within-2024 +0.31/yr, cross-period +0.086/yr, accel 0.28 — tech count growth was actually FASTER within 2024 than cross-period. But the within-2024 signal itself may be source artifact (arshkon tech matrix is slightly denser than asaniczka).

  7. Entry share is tricky. Under entry_native, within-2024 is the asaniczka-labeling artifact, and cross-period shows a decline (which contradicts the combined-column rise — the measurement investigation story). Under entry_best and yoe_le2, the cross-period rate is small-positive (+1pp/yr and +0.85pp/yr), and the within-2024 rate is +10.87 and +22pp/yr — again the within-2024 is dominated by source-composition (asaniczka YOE≤2 is 9.5%, arshkon is 15.0%, driven by different job populations in Jan vs Apr).

  8. The acceleration ratios should be reported with a strong caveat: both the numerator and denominator are two-point estimates with heavy source-composition noise in the denominator. The reliable statement is: the cross-period rates for AI, agentic, and scope language are substantially larger than any within-2024 fluctuation can explain, while entry-share and tech-count cross-period rates are within-noise.

docs.title: Within-arshkon stability
docs.text:
Arshkon's scrape_week column is null for all rows (exploration/tables/T19/arshkon_within_stability.csv), so week-level bucketing via that column is not available. Using date_posted instead, we compare the early window (April 5-12, 1,308 SWE rows) to the late window (April 13-20, 3,711 SWE rows):

Window n entry_native AI rate median_len wk1 (Apr 5-12) 1,308 17.58% 2.29% 1804 wk2 (Apr 13-20) 3,711 14.52% 1.91% 1774
  • Arshkon has meaningful internal heterogeneity. Entry_native drops 3pp across two weeks; AI rate drops 0.4pp; median length drops 30 chars. Over 2 weeks, a 3pp entry-share drift is not large but it demonstrates that the "April 2024 snapshot" is not a point estimate — there is meaningful within-snapshot variance that we report as zero. Any claim that compares arshkon to scraped at <3pp resolution on entry share is within within-arshkon noise.
  • wk2 has 2.8× the postings of wk1, skewed toward Apr 17-19 (1,600 + 1,219 + 352 postings on those three days). The arshkon "snapshot" is effectively a three-day collection window, not a uniform April sample.

docs.title: Scraped daily stability
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T19/scraped_march_daily.csv and scraped_april_daily.csv. 2026-03 covers Mar 20-31, 2026-04 covers Apr 1-6.

Volume pattern (SWE, linkedin/en/ok):

Date n AI rate agentic entry_native entry_best yoe≤2 median_len agg rate 2026-03-20 3,043 26.9% 6.7% 12.6% 5.7% 17.2% 3829 13.8% 2026-03-21 1,415 25.3% 5.7% 15.0% 6.1% 16.4% 3757 19.8% 2026-03-22 1,209 22.8% 5.6% 10.5% 2.8% 14.8% 3829 7.9% 2026-03-23 863 22.8% 6.0% 13.8% 10.2% 16.3% 3015 5.6% 2026-03-24 2,677 22.5% 6.1% 15.7% 5.2% 18.5% 3459 16.6% 2026-03-25 2,801 23.3% 7.0% 16.4% 15.3% 19.0% 3732 15.0% 2026-03-26 2,595 25.4% 9.5% 12.1% 4.7% 17.9% 3838 14.2% 2026-03-27 2,699 27.7% 11.2% 14.4% 3.9% 15.7% 3921 14.4% 2026-03-28 2,312 23.7% 8.1% 14.7% 4.7% 17.9% 3809 13.7% 2026-03-29 1,080 27.3% 10.2% 14.2% 4.7% 17.1% 3982 13.1% 2026-03-30 926 20.8% 4.9% 17.3% 6.8% 15.5% 3722 31.9% 2026-03-31 2,434 25.3% 8.6% 12.1% 6.1% 15.3% 3711 12.1% 2026-04-01 2,773 24.9% 9.1% 12.1% 2.1% 18.6% 3707 12.4% 2026-04-02 1,651 24.5% 10.0% 13.3% 4.8% 15.7% 3619 19.3% 2026-04-03 2,481 26.6% 10.0% 12.5% 2.4% 16.2% 3783 14.1% 2026-04-04 2,369 23.1% 7.1% 15.8% 2.3% 15.1% 4002 14.2% 2026-04-05 1,138 24.9% 6.5% 10.9% 2.7% 11.7% 3898 10.3% 2026-04-06 596 22.0% 6.7% 9.3% 5.3% 10.6% 3054 7.6%

Observations:

  1. The first scrape day (2026-03-20) is NOT a backlog with distinct content. AI rate 26.9% (highest of the month), median length 3829 (typical), agg rate 13.8% (typical). No evidence of "stock vs flow" contamination on day 1.
  2. Scrape volume oscillates heavily — from 596 to 3,043 rows per day (5×). The Apr 5-6 tail drops off sharply because our data ends 2026-04-06. Large-volume days correlate with mid-week posting cycles (Tue/Wed/Thu).
  3. AI rate is very stable: all days between 20.8% and 27.7%, no monotonic drift. agentic is noisier (4.9-11.2%) because of the smaller base, but also no monotonic drift.
  4. entry_best (combined) is the noisiest metric: 2.1% to 15.3% day-to-day. This reflects both the small denominator (the entry_best column is sparse) and the dedup artifact (some days have more duplicate-template entries than others — Mar 25 at 15.3% is an outlier driven by V1's documented Affirm/Canonical/Epic clones).
  5. entry_native is noticeably more stable (10.5%-17.3% range, centered ~13-15%) and does not show the dedup artifact because the affected templates don't all carry native entry labels.
  6. Mar 30 has a 31.9% aggregator rate (vs typical 12-17%) — a scraping-day artifact where the scraper picked up unusually many Dice/Indeed reposts. Not enough to shift content metrics; median length and AI rate for Mar 30 are actually LOWER than average (3722 chars, 20.8% AI), consistent with aggregator reposts being shorter and more legacy.
  7. Median length is very stable across all days (3054-4002, with the low end being short-sample days like 2026-04-06 with only 596 postings).

The scraped snapshot does NOT show stock-vs-flow bias on day 1 or systematic drift across days. It can be treated as a roughly uniform sample of the March-April 2026 LinkedIn SWE posting stream, modulo the ~6pp swing in entry_best driven by dedup template artifacts.

docs.title: 2026-03 vs 2026-04 direct comparison
docs.text:
From march_vs_april_2026.csv:

Metric 2026-03 2026-04 Δ n 24,054 11,008 — AI rate 24.79% 24.69% −0.10pp agentic 7.75% 8.62% +0.87pp entry_best 6.34% 3.25% −3.09pp YOE ≤ 2 17.06% 15.67% −1.39pp entry_native 14.02% 12.88% −1.14pp median_len 3735.5 3743 +7.5 mean_tech 2.75 2.69 −0.07

Verdict: stable on all content metrics. AI rate and median length are essentially unchanged. agentic drifted up slightly (+0.87pp, consistent with the real trend but within week-to-week noise from the daily view). entry_best drifted down 3.1pp — this is the largest swing and is almost certainly the V1-documented duplicate-template dedup artifact resolving itself as the April scrape picked up fewer Affirm/Canonical/Epic clones. YOE≤2 and entry_native both drifted down modestly (~1pp each), within day-to-day noise.

Implication: the "2026" endpoint is measurement-stable across the available 18 days of data. Any cross-period metric we report for 2026 should pool both March and April — or alternatively use only one and note that the choice does not materially move content metrics.

docs.title: Posting age analysis
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T19/posting_age.csv:

  • Coverage is essentially zero — only 179 of 24,054 March rows (0.7%) and 110 of 11,008 April rows (1.0%) have posting_age_days populated.
  • Every populated row has posting_age_days = 1 (median and p90 both = 1).
  • The scraper captures fresh postings; the posting_age_days field is not a meaningful analysis variable under current coverage. Report as "not usable."

docs.title: Day-of-week analysis for scraped
docs.text:
From day_of_week.csv. DuckDB's dayofweek() returns 0-6 where 0=Sunday through 6=Saturday.

2026-03:

DOW Day n AI rate entry_native median_len 0 Sun 2,289 24.9% 12.3% 3923 1 Mon 1,789 21.8% 15.6% 3313 2 Tue 5,111 23.9% 14.0% 3586 3 Wed 2,801 23.3% 16.4% 3732 4 Thu 2,595 25.4% 12.1% 3838 5 Fri 5,742 27.3% 13.4% 3879 6 Sat 3,727 24.3% 14.8% 3774
  • Posting volume concentrates Tue (5,111) and Fri (5,742); lowest on Mon (1,789). This is the opposite of the naive "new week = new postings" pattern — Tuesday/Friday bursts are consistent with scraper run timing and batched LinkedIn posting.
  • AI rate is highest on Fri (27.3%) and lowest on Mon (21.8%). Range across days: 21.8-27.3%, a 5.5pp spread. Non-trivial day-of-week effect.
  • entry_native is highest on Wed (16.4%) and lowest on Thu (12.1%). ~4pp spread.
  • median length is lowest on Mon (3313) and highest on Sun (3923). ~600 chars spread.

Implication: pooled-week metrics are more reliable than any single-day metric, and comparisons between the 2026-03 (12 days, full week coverage) and 2026-04 (6 days, partial week coverage) snapshots can carry a few pp of day-of-week bias. The 2026-04 sample spans Wed-Mon (not a symmetric week), so its composition is slightly Tue/Wed/Thu-heavy. This is consistent with 2026-04 having slightly lower AI rate (24.7%) than 2026-03 (24.8%) — April's day-of-week mix under-samples Friday, the highest-AI day.

docs.title: Timeline contextualization
docs.text:
See exploration/figures/T19/timeline.png. Our three snapshots (Jan 2024, Apr 2024, Mar-Apr 2026) sit at the following qualitative points in the AI tooling timeline:

  • asaniczka Jan 2024: 1 month before Claude 3 release (Mar 2024). GPT-4 has been out 10 months. No production agent frameworks. LangChain is ~1.5 years old, LangGraph does not yet exist. This is the "pre-agentic" baseline.
  • arshkon Apr 2024: 1 month after Claude 3 release, ~1 month before GPT-4o (May 2024). LangGraph is ~3 months old. This is approximately "early assistants" era.
  • scraped Mar 2026: The following tools have been released in the intervening 23 months: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Jun 2024), o1 (Sep 2024), DeepSeek V3 (Dec 2024), GPT-4.5 (Feb 2025), Claude 3.6 Sonnet (Apr 2025), Claude 4 Opus (Sep 2025), Gemini 2.5 Pro (Mar 2026). This is "mature agentic" era — Cursor/Claude Code/Windsurf are mainstream; multi-agent frameworks (CrewAI, LangGraph) are established.
  • scraped Apr 2026: Essentially same tool landscape as March, one month after Gemini 2.5 Pro release.

The 23-month gap between arshkon and scraped spans 8 major model releases plus the full "agent" tooling wave. This qualitatively explains why our two snapshots show the largest change (+22.9pp AI, +8.0pp agentic for SWE): the 2024 snapshot predates the agentic tooling era entirely, while the 2026 snapshot occurs mid-plateau. Any observed "acceleration ratio" between within-2024 and cross-period is thus confounded with the density of tool releases in each interval — within-2024 had zero major agent frameworks in the 3-month gap; cross-period had 8 major releases plus the entire Cursor/Windsurf ecosystem. The 4× acceleration ratio on AI rate is almost certainly an underestimate of what a continuous time series would show.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected findings
docs.text:
1. Within-2024 arshkon is SHORTER than asaniczka (median 1784 vs 2102 chars). Shorter postings in April 2024 than January 2024, by the same platform, three months apart. This is pure source heterogeneity (asaniczka had more LLM cleaning, different selection) and demonstrates how thoroughly within-2024 comparisons are confounded by source.
  2. The first scrape day (2026-03-20) has the HIGHEST AI rate in March (26.9%) and a normal median length. If the first day were a backlog, we'd expect either older stale content (lower AI) or longer extracts from Dice/Indeed (different length). Neither shows up. Day 1 looks like a normal high-volume scrape day.
  3. Day-of-week effects span ~5pp on AI rate, ~4pp on entry_native, ~600 chars on median length. Single-day snapshots could be meaningfully biased. All published period comparisons should pool across weeks.
  4. agentic drifted UP from March to April 2026 (7.75% → 8.62%, +0.87pp in ~18 days). Extrapolating linearly: +18pp/year rate of agentic keyword growth within 2026-03 to 2026-04. Noisy, but consistent with AI vocabulary still expanding rapidly during our 2026 scrape window.
  5. entry_best dropped 3.09pp between March and April 2026 — larger than any other metric's March-vs-April shift. This is almost certainly the V1-documented Affirm/Canonical/Epic duplicate-template artifact resolving as the April scrape picked up fewer duplicates. Underlines why YOE proxy is safer than combined-column entry share.
  6. Mar 30 had 31.9% aggregator rate vs ~14% baseline — a 2x spike. Content metrics absorbed it (AI rate -4pp that day), showing aggregator composition does propagate to content. Good datapoint for sensitivity (a) if any analysis is heavily Mar-30-weighted.
  7. Mean tech count is NEARLY FLAT across all three snapshots (2.49 → 2.57 → 2.73). This contradicts T11/T14's "tech count grew +34-60%" claims — once you use a safe 15-tech detection list that avoids the c_cpp/csharp regex bug, mean tech count is roughly stable. The big tech-count growth reported by Wave 2 may be tech-matrix artifact. Flagged for T22/T24 follow-up.
  8. Arshkon is not uniformly distributed across its Apr 5-20 window — 1,600 of 5,019 SWE postings are stamped Apr 18 alone, and 2,819 of 5,019 (56%) are on three days (Apr 17-19). Arshkon is effectively a three-day snapshot, not a two-week one.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Two-point linear annualization is crude; reported as directional only.
  • asaniczka has NO native entry labels, so any within-2024 entry comparison with arshkon is an artifact.
  • posting_age_days coverage is ~1%; unusable.
  • scrape_week column is null for arshkon — within-arshkon weekly buckets had to be inferred from date_posted.
  • Day-of-week effects are computed on dayofweek of scrape_date (when the scraper ran), not when the posting was originally listed. The SWE postings surfaced on a given scrape day are a mix of fresh postings and slightly older ones that the scraper surfaced late — but since posting_age is nearly always 1, the distinction doesn't matter here.
  • All metrics use SWE only. Repeating T19 on SWE-adjacent and control would require separate runs.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T19/snapshot_metrics.csv — raw snapshot metrics
  • exploration/tables/T19/rates_annualized.csv — within-2024 vs cross-period rates
  • exploration/tables/T19/arshkon_within_stability.csv — within-arshkon bucketing
  • exploration/tables/T19/scraped_march_daily.csv, scraped_april_daily.csv — daily stability
  • exploration/tables/T19/march_vs_april_2026.csv — direct period comparison
  • exploration/tables/T19/posting_age.csv — posting age coverage
  • exploration/tables/T19/day_of_week.csv — DOW effects
  • exploration/figures/T19/scraped_daily.png — daily volume and content
  • exploration/figures/T19/rates_annualized.png — within-2024 vs cross-period bar chart
  • exploration/figures/T19/timeline.png — AI tool releases + our snapshots
  • exploration/figures/T19/march_vs_april_2026.png — snapshot stability

Scripts: exploration/scripts/T19_01_rates.py, T19_02_plots.py.

docs.title: T20 — Seniority boundary clarity (feature-based)
docs.text: Status: complete (2026-04-09) Script: exploration/scripts/T20_seniority_boundaries.py Tables: exploration/tables/T20/ Figures: exploration/figures/T20/

docs.title: Scope and rationale
docs.text:
T15 already rejected semantic convergence between seniority tiers (junior↔senior cosine flat or slightly diverging, robust under text-source control). T20 is the structured-feature complement: it asks whether seniority tiers are becoming harder to tell apart when you give a linear classifier an engineered feature vector built from YOE, tech breadth, AI mention (validated), scope density, mentoring density (strict detector), people-management density (strict detector), description length, and education level.

  • Default filter: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true. n = 63,294 (28,232 in 2024; 35,062 in 2026).
  • Features reuse the T11 feature snapshot (T11_features.parquet), with strict mentoring and strict people-management densities recomputed from scratch per the Wave 2 + V1 guidance (the V1-verified strict detector, ~100% precision; no naive hire/recruit pattern), and AI mention recomputed using the validated AI pattern set (agentic, ai agent, llm, rag, copilot, chatgpt, claude, langchain, langgraph, etc. — never bare agent).
  • Seniority operationalizations, all run in parallel:
  • seniority_best_available_combined (labeled → seniority_llm; rule_sufficient → seniority_final; NULL otherwise) — defined only for rows in the LLM classification frame.
  • seniority_best_available_aug (same with seniority_final fallback for rows outside the LLM frame).
  • seniority_final — full-coverage.
  • YOE buckets (yoe≤2 vs 2<yoe≤5 vs yoe>5) — label-independent.
  • Boundaries tested: entry↔associate, associate↔mid-senior, mid-senior↔director.
  • Classifier: L2-regularized logistic regression on standardized features, 5-fold stratified CV, AUC the metric.
  • Strict-detector regex asserts run at the top of every script execution.

docs.title: 1. Headline: most boundaries did NOT blur — but the mid-senior↔director boundary did
docs.text:
Under the primary combined column, the signal goes the "expected" direction only for associate↔mid-senior (which slightly blurred) while director-specific and entry-level boundaries moved by different, smaller amounts. Under seniority_final (full coverage), the dominant movement is mid-senior↔director AUC falling from 0.75 → 0.69, while entry↔associate stayed flat around 0.55 and associate↔mid-senior actually sharpened slightly (0.69 → 0.71).

AUC deltas by operationalization:

Boundary seniority_final 2024 → 2026 Δ AUC best_avail_combined 2024 → 2026 Δ AUC entry↔associate 0.554 → 0.552 -0.002 0.780 → 0.680 -0.099 associate↔mid-senior 0.690 → 0.711 +0.021 0.825 → 0.777 -0.049 mid-senior↔director 0.751 → 0.686 -0.065 0.720 → 0.711 -0.009

The combined column shows larger shifts at entry↔associate but on very small n (84 associate in 2024, 30 in 2026) — the wide confidence bands make the big-number moves unreliable. seniority_final is better powered.

Interpretation: Under the full-coverage operationalization, the only boundary that meaningfully blurred is mid-senior↔director. Feature-based separation fell 6-7 AUC points (from 0.75 to 0.69) — directors are becoming harder to tell apart from mid-seniors on structured features. This is consistent with T11's reframing (senior roles moving toward IC + mentoring rather than toward people management): if directors used to be distinguishable by people-management signals and scope, and that signal is fading across the senior tier, then the director/mid-senior line blurs.

The entry↔associate boundary was already very blurry in 2024 (AUC 0.55) under seniority_final and stayed that way. This matches the T08 finding that 2026 entry postings are now longer than 2024 mid-senior postings: along structured features, entry and associate have always been hard to separate, and the "blur" question as usually posed (entry becoming mid-senior-like) is more accurately described as a low-signal boundary in both periods.

Table: boundary_auc_deltas.csv. Figure: boundary_auc_seniority_final.png.

docs.title: 2. What features actually separate levels, and which features shifted
docs.text:
Top 5 standardized coefficients by boundary (from boundary_auc_deltas.csv, seniority_final):

Boundary 2024 top features 2026 top features entry↔associate tech_count −0.19, text_len +0.14, scope_density +0.08, people_mgmt_density −0.05, ai_mention −0.04 text_len +0.14, edu_level −0.11, scope_density +0.11, tech_count +0.07, mentor_density +0.04 associate↔mid-senior yoe +0.66, people_mgmt +0.43, tech_count +0.39, mentor +0.26, edu +0.14 yoe +0.86, mentor +0.28, text_len +0.21, scope +0.14, edu +0.13 mid-senior↔director yoe +0.43, edu +0.39, scope +0.34, text_len +0.22, tech_count −0.18 yoe +0.38, scope +0.30, edu +0.23, mentor_density −0.22, tech_count +0.19

Three substantive observations about feature drift:

  1. At the associate↔mid-senior boundary, people-management density falls out of the top 5 entirely in 2026. In 2024 it was the #2 discriminator (coef +0.43); in 2026 it doesn't make the top 5. Mentoring density stays in the top 5 across both periods, and YOE becomes more important (+0.66 → +0.86). This is a clean structural echo of T11: the senior tier is no longer distinguished from mid-career by people-management language; it is increasingly distinguished by tenure and mentoring.

  2. At the mid-senior↔director boundary, mentoring density flips sign. In 2024 mentoring did not make the top 5. In 2026, mentoring density enters at −0.22 — meaning lower mentoring density is associated with being director (higher mentor density is associated with being mid-senior). This is the clearest single-feature evidence that mentoring has drifted down the ladder from director to mid-senior in the 2026 corpus. Mentoring language used to be neutral-to-slight-director-signal; it is now a mid-senior signal.

  3. At entry↔associate, the boundary is just noise in both periods (AUC ≈ 0.55). None of the structured features discriminates these levels well, either year. If there is a real difference, it is not in YOE, tech breadth, scope, AI, mentoring, people-management, length, or education.

docs.title: 3. YOE-based proxy (label-independent cross-validation)
docs.text:
Using YOE buckets directly (yoe≤2 = yoe_low, 2<yoe≤5 = yoe_mid, yoe>5 = yoe_high), with YOE itself excluded from the feature set so the classifier cannot cheat:

Boundary 2024 AUC 2026 AUC Δ yoe_low ↔ yoe_mid 0.599 0.636 +0.037 yoe_mid ↔ yoe_high 0.578 0.572 −0.006

The YOE-proxy evidence goes the opposite direction from the label-based mid-senior↔director blurring: in 2026, non-YOE features actually separate early-career (yoe_low) from mid-career (yoe_mid) better than in 2024. Top 2026 discriminators: edu_level −0.41 (low YOE requires education less than mid YOE in 2026), mentor_density +0.20, tech_count +0.15, ai_mention +0.08.

Sensitivity disagreement is itself a finding: The label-based and YOE-based operationalizations give different stories about which boundaries blurred and which sharpened.
