The most honest read is: - YOE-based: the early-career/mid-career split is becoming more discriminable on non-YOE features (driven by education, mentoring, and tech_count). - Label-based: the mid-senior/director split is becoming less discriminable on all structured features (driven by the loss of people-management as a director-specific signal).

These are not contradictory — they are about different parts of the distribution. The label-based story is about the senior tier; the YOE-based story is about the early-career-to-mid-career transition. T20 finds evidence for both boundary effects, in different places.

Table: boundary_aucs_yoe_proxy.csv.

docs.title: 4. Centroid profile heatmap (feature-space map)
docs.text:
Mean feature values per period × seniority (z-scored across the 8 period×seniority cells, seniority_final):

Key reading: - mentor_density grew sharply in the 2026 mid-senior and director cells vs 2024 counterparts — the mentoring signal rose in the senior tier. - people_mgmt_density fell in 2026 mid-senior and director cells — consistent with T11's strict-detector finding that people-manager terms declined in the senior tier. - ai_mention_validated jumped across all 2026 cells, but the biggest z-score is in mid-senior/director, not entry — AI language is adopted top-down in senior postings before saturating the entry tier. - yoe_extracted stayed near-monotone with seniority in both periods. - edu_level collapsed in 2026 entry/associate — part of the T12 credential-stripping story bleeding into the lower tiers.

Table: centroids_seniority_final.csv.

docs.title: 5. The "missing middle" question: is associate drifting toward entry or toward mid-senior?
docs.text:
Pairwise Euclidean distances in standardized feature space, computed within each period from just the 3 cells (entry, associate, mid-senior):

Period dist(associate → entry) dist(associate → mid-senior) ratio 2024 3.34 5.44 0.61 2026 3.65 4.70 0.78

In 2024, associate was closer to entry (ratio 0.61). In 2026, the ratio rose to 0.78 — associate moved relatively closer to mid-senior. The absolute distance to entry grew slightly (+0.3) while the distance to mid-senior shrank (−0.7).

But: associate collapsed in n. In seniority_final, associate fell from 1,692 rows in 2024 to 996 rows in 2026 (-41%), while entry grew from 977 to 4,656 (+376%) and mid-senior grew 24,466 → 26,805 (+9.6%). The "associate tier" is shrinking as a share of labeled postings. The feature-space drift toward mid-senior plus the raw shrinkage is consistent with associate being partially absorbed by mid-senior, rather than associate merging with entry.

A caveat: this centroid-distance test is coarse (3 points, 8 features, standardized within-period). Interpret as directional, not a precise magnitude.

Table: missing_middle_associate.csv, adjacent_centroid_distances.csv.

docs.title: 6. Domain-stratified boundary analysis
docs.text:
Using the T09 archetype labels (8,000 sampled rows) joined to the feature frame (n = 8,000 of 63,294, or 12.6%), the within-domain boundary classifier is underpowered for most cells: most domain×period×boundary cells have associate n < 20 and the classifier falls through to insufficient_n. The domains that do pass power:

Domain Period Boundary n_a n_b AUC Other 2026 entry↔associate 128 33 0.44 (noise) Other 2026 associate↔mid-senior 33 537 0.57 AI/ML 2026 entry↔associate 111 36 0.55 AI/ML 2026 associate↔mid-senior 36 577 0.72 Cloud/Infra 2026 entry↔associate 60 24 0.66 Cloud/Infra 2026 associate↔mid-senior 24 370 0.67

Within the AI/ML archetype, associate↔mid-senior is the sharpest boundary at AUC 0.72, with top discriminators yoe (+1.28), edu (+0.52), mentor (+0.27), people_mgmt (−0.21), tech_count (−0.11). The AI/ML domain retains a clear YOE+edu-based separation between associate and mid-senior; the negative people_mgmt coefficient echoes the overall finding that people-management is no longer a senior signal.

The 2024 cells for all domains are underpowered (typically n_entry < 50 per domain, n_associate < 20) because the T09 archetype sample is not large enough for triple-stratified analysis. This should be re-run on a larger archetype sample if domain-level boundary claims become important; for now, T20 reports the 2026 within-domain AUCs as directional.

Table: domain_stratified_aucs.csv.

docs.title: Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Re-running the primary boundary analysis on the aggregator-excluded subset (is_aggregator == False) under seniority_final:

Boundary 2024 AUC (no agg) 2026 AUC (no agg) Δ Corresponds to raw entry↔associate 0.559 0.554 −0.005 ≈ raw (−0.002) associate↔mid-senior 0.672 0.720 +0.048 ≈ raw (+0.021), slightly amplified mid-senior↔director 0.710 0.684 −0.026 ≈ raw (−0.065), muted

Removing aggregators muffles the mid-senior↔director blurring (−0.026 vs −0.065 in raw). The direction is the same, but the magnitude is roughly halved — suggesting aggregator postings contribute disproportionately to the director-specific feature drift. The associate↔mid-senior sharpening is slightly stronger without aggregators. Both patterns survive aggregator exclusion qualitatively.

Table: sensitivity_no_aggregators.csv.

docs.title: Seniority operationalization ablation
docs.text:
Running across three label-based operationalizations plus the YOE proxy (n varies substantially):

  • seniority_best_available_combined: smallest n, noisiest, but directionally agrees that mid-senior↔director is the most blur-prone adjacent boundary.
  • seniority_best_available_aug: larger n, shows the same pattern.
  • seniority_final: full-coverage, primary result table.
  • YOE proxy: disagrees on direction for the early-career boundary (shows sharpening, not blurring), agrees implicitly that the senior tier is where people-management is disappearing.

The label-based story ("mid-senior↔director blurred") holds across all three label-based operationalizations. The YOE-based ablation reveals a different finding (yoe_low↔yoe_mid sharpened on non-YOE features) rather than contradicting the label-based story. Both are in scope per the sensitivity-disagreement rule.

docs.title: Headline findings for downstream reporting
docs.text:
1. Semantic convergence was already ruled out in T15; T20 also finds that feature-based convergence is not a market-wide story. Most adjacent boundaries did not blur. The entry↔associate boundary is equally noisy in both periods (AUC ≈ 0.55). The associate↔mid-senior boundary slightly sharpened (0.69 → 0.71 under seniority_final) while associate itself shrank as a labeled category.

  2. The only boundary that meaningfully blurred is mid-senior↔director (AUC 0.75 → 0.69 under seniority_final, −0.065). This is driven by two structural shifts: (a) people-management density, which was a top discriminator in 2024, dropped out of the top 5 in 2026; and (b) mentoring density, which was neutral in 2024, became a negative director predictor in 2026 (mentoring is now a mid-senior signal, not a director signal). This is the cleanest structural-feature corroboration of T11's "IC + mentoring, not IC → people-manager" reframing.

  3. Mentoring has drifted down the ladder from director → mid-senior in the feature-importance sense. In 2026, the cleanest cross-seniority interpretation of mentoring density is that it is concentrated in mid-senior, not director, postings. This is a structural-discriminator-level echo of the T11 finding.

  4. People-management language as a mid-senior signal is disappearing. In 2024, people-management density was the #2 discriminator at the associate↔mid-senior boundary (coef +0.43). In 2026 it does not make the top 5. The signal that used to mark mid-senior is gone.

  5. Associate as a tier is shrinking and drifting toward mid-senior, not toward entry. Under seniority_final, associate n fell 41% while entry grew 376% and mid-senior grew 9.6%. The centroid ratio of (associate-to-entry) / (associate-to-mid-senior) rose from 0.61 to 0.78 — associate became relatively closer to mid-senior in feature space.

  6. Label-based and YOE-based operationalizations disagree about where the early-career boundary is moving. Label-based: entry↔associate was always fuzzy and stayed fuzzy (AUC ≈ 0.55). YOE-based: yoe_low↔yoe_mid actually sharpened (0.60 → 0.64) on non-YOE features, driven by education becoming a stronger negative discriminator (lower YOE postings require less education). This is not a contradiction; it is two different slices of the same early-career population, and the YOE slice reveals an education-downgrading pattern at the early-career tier that the label-based slice does not surface.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Tables: - boundary_aucs.csv — full 18-row boundary × period × operationalization matrix - boundary_auc_deltas.csv — 9-row delta table with top features per period - boundary_aucs_yoe_proxy.csv — YOE-bucket-based boundary AUCs - centroids_seniority_final.csv — mean feature values per period × seniority cell - centroid_distances.csv — all pairwise distances in feature space - adjacent_centroid_distances.csv — adjacent-level distances per period - missing_middle_associate.csv — associate drift analysis - domain_stratified_aucs.csv — within-domain boundary AUCs - sensitivity_no_aggregators.csv — aggregator-exclusion sensitivity - T20_summary.json

Figures: - feature_profile_heatmap.png — z-scored feature profile by period × seniority - boundary_auc_seniority_final.png — AUC bar chart by period

docs.title: T21 — Senior role evolution deep dive
docs.text: Status: complete (2026-04-09) Script: exploration/scripts/T21_senior_role_evolution.py Tables: exploration/tables/T21/ Figures: exploration/figures/T21/

docs.title: Scope and rationale
docs.text:
T11 found a critical reframing of the senior-tier story: senior SWE roles are shifting toward IC + mentoring, not toward people-management. The naive hire/recruit pattern that initially suggested a "senior → people manager" trajectory turned out to be ~85% boilerplate per V1 verification. Under the strict-detector approach (V1-verified at ~100% precision), people-management terms all declined at senior while strict mentoring grew 4.2% → 10.0%. T21 validates, characterizes, and extends this finding using strict detectors from the start across four language profiles.

  • Default filter: LinkedIn, English, date_flag='ok', is_swe=true.
  • Senior frame: seniority_final ∈ {mid-senior, director}. n = 51,735 (24,579 in 2024; 27,156 in 2026). Mid-senior/director 2026 split: 26,805 / 351.
  • Text source for detection: description_cleaned where ≥50 chars, else raw description fallback.
  • Four language profiles, all strict patterns validated in-script with asserts:
  • People management (strict): people manager, people management, direct reports, performance review, headcount, manage/lead a team of, interview/hire engineers, team building (10 sub-patterns).
  • Mentoring (strict): mentor engineers/junior/team, coach engineers/junior, mentor and develop, mentorship (4 sub-patterns).
  • Technical orchestration: architecture review, code review, system design, technical direction, ai orchestration, agentic, multi-agent, automation, evaluation, prompt engineering, tool selection, quality gate, guardrails (13 sub-patterns).
  • Strategic scope: stakeholder, business impact, revenue, product strategy, roadmap, prioritization, resource allocation, cross-functional (8 sub-patterns).
  • AI detection uses validated patterns only (agentic, ai agent, llm, rag, copilot, claude, langchain, etc.) — never bare agent (contaminated per V1).
  • Sensitivities: raw, company-capped-20 (primary for corpus term frequencies), aggregator-excluded.

docs.title: 1. Headline: senior densities move as T11 predicted, only more sharply under strict detectors
docs.text:
Senior (mid-senior + director) mean density per 1K chars, all raw (n = 24,579 → 27,156):

Profile 2024 density 2026 density Δ rel 2024 any-share 2026 any-share Δ pp People management (strict) 0.0271 0.0114 −58% 4.2% 3.5% −0.7pp Mentoring (strict) 0.0646 0.0857 +33% 11.0% 21.9% +10.9pp Technical orchestration 0.3400 0.4995 +47% 37.2% 62.1% +25.0pp Strategic scope 0.2589 0.2855 +10% 32.9% 53.0% +20.1pp AI mention (validated) 0.0388 0.3515 +807% 2.9% 24.3% +21.4pp

The T11 reframing holds and sharpens under strict-detector reruns: - People-management density fell 58% in aggregate and the any-share dropped 0.7pp. There is no support for the "senior → people manager" story at any level. - Mentoring density grew 33% and any-share doubled (11.0% → 21.9%). Senior postings mentioning mentorship language roughly doubled in share. - Tech orchestration (code review, system design, automation, and the new AI-orchestration sub-patterns) grew 47% in density and 25pp in any-share. This is the biggest absolute shift in senior language. - Strategic scope grew modestly in density (+10%) but strongly in any-share (+20pp) — more postings mention strategic scope, but per-posting mentions didn't grow. - AI mention exploded as expected (senior AI mention rose from ~3% to ~24% of postings, a structural signal the entire senior tier absorbed).

The company-capped-20 sensitivity (primary for corpus-level term frequency per the essential sensitivities rule) shows the same directions, slightly muted for tech orchestration (+0.44 → +0.51 density) and slightly amplified for the ai mention and mentoring signals. Both tables are reported.

Tables: senior_density_by_period_raw.csv, senior_density_by_period_capped20.csv. Figure: senior_profile_scatter.png.

docs.title: 2. Term-level breakdown reveals which specific patterns drove each profile
docs.text: Under company-capped-20 (n_senior 2024 = 15,137; 2026 = 17,994):

docs.title: People management (strict) — every term declined or stayed noise-floor
docs.text:
Term 2024 postings-with-match% 2026 postings-with-match% Δ people manager 0.01% 0.09% +0.08pp (still ~noise) people management 0.38% 0.46% +0.08pp direct reports 0.57% 0.41% −0.17pp performance review 0.16% 0.18% ≈0 headcount 0.02% 0.23% +0.21pp (small abs) manage team of 0% 0% — lead team of 0.01% 0% ≈0 interview engineers 0% 0.16% +0.16pp hire engineers 0.03% 0.18% +0.16pp team building 0.92% 1.98% +1.06pp

Two interesting exceptions: team building grew from 0.9% to 2.0% of senior postings, and interview engineers / hire engineers both grew from near-zero to 0.16-0.18%. These are small absolute magnitudes but they suggest that the "team building" frame and the "engineering interviewing" frame did survive in senior postings even as the harder people-management indicators (direct reports, performance review) stagnated or declined.

The robust interpretation is: formal people-management language (the core "direct reports / performance review" cluster) stayed flat or declined slightly. Ambient team-involvement language (team building) grew. This is consistent with the IC+mentoring story — senior roles still involve people, but not people-manager formalisms.

docs.title: Mentoring (strict) — mentor engineers nearly doubled
docs.text:
Term 2024 % 2026 % Δ mentor engineers/junior/team 7.8% 15.1% +7.3pp coach engineers 1.1% 1.1% ≈0 mentorship 3.9% 8.3% +4.5pp mentor and develop 0% 0% —

The mentor engineers canonical pattern nearly doubled in posting-level coverage. mentorship more than doubled. These are the cleanest V1-verified high-precision patterns and their growth is substantial. 25 sampled matches per period confirmed ~100% precision in both years (see samples_mentoring.csv).

docs.title: Technical orchestration — code review, system design, automation all grew; AI-orchestration emerged from zero
docs.text:
Term 2024 % 2026 % Δ code review 11.5% 19.1% +7.5pp system design 5.8% 8.3% +2.5pp technical direction 1.8% 6.1% +4.2pp automation 20.0% 31.9% +11.9pp evaluation 5.6% 9.3% +3.7pp prompt engineering 0.19% 3.7% +3.5pp (20×) agentic 0.02% 8.3% +8.3pp (415×) multi-agent 0.05% 2.1% +2.1pp ai orchestration 0% 0.38% +0.38pp quality gate 0.06% 0.86% +0.80pp guardrails 0.19% 2.85% +2.66pp

agentic went from 0.02% → 8.3% of senior postings (a 415× lift), and guardrails, prompt engineering, quality gate, multi-agent, and ai orchestration all emerged from near-zero. Alongside the growth in traditional code review (+7.5pp) and automation (+11.9pp), the tech-orchestration profile mixes classic senior SWE duties with a new family of AI-orchestration responsibilities that did not exist in the 2024 senior vocabulary.

docs.title: Strategic scope — stakeholder and cross-functional grew strongest
docs.text:
Term 2024 % 2026 % Δ stakeholder 16.9% 26.0% +9.0pp cross-functional 15.5% 26.4% +10.9pp business impact 0.69% 2.10% +1.42pp revenue 0.79% 3.48% +2.68pp roadmap 3.90% 6.92% +3.03pp prioritization 6.04% 7.19% +1.15pp resource allocation 0.30% 0.28% ≈0 product strategy 0.30% 0.61% +0.31pp

stakeholder and cross-functional both grew roughly +10pp. roadmap and revenue grew meaningfully. This is a senior tier that is taking on more explicit cross-functional/stakeholder-management responsibilities in 2026 — and importantly, it is doing so without gaining people-management responsibilities. The division matches the T11 reading: senior SWEs are more cross-functional, more mentorship-oriented, more tech-orchestration-focused, but not becoming people managers.

Tables: senior_term_level_counts_raw.csv, senior_term_level_counts_capped20.csv.

docs.title: 3. Senior sub-archetype clustering (k-means)
docs.text:
K-means with k=5 on the density-4-vector (people_mgmt, mentor, tech_orch, strategic) over all senior postings (n = 51,735) produced 5 clusters that I label by dominant profile:

Cluster Label n people_mgmt mentor tech_orch strategic 2024 share 2026 share Δ 0 Generic-TechOrch (low-density) 34,614 0.003 0.015 0.256 0.108 67.6% 66.3% −1.4pp 1 Strategic-heavy 7,566 0.004 0.030 0.305 1.042 15.1% 14.2% −0.9pp 2 People-Manager 1,208 0.659 0.064 0.258 0.160 3.7% 1.1% −2.6pp 3 Mentor-heavy 5,060 0.005 0.587 0.450 0.339 8.6% 10.8% +2.2pp 4 TechOrch-heavy (incl. AI orchestration) 3,287 0.002 0.033 2.488 0.179 5.0% 7.6% +2.6pp

Key findings: - The People-Manager cluster shrank by 70% in share (3.7% → 1.1%). This is the archetype that historically encoded the formal people-management senior role, and it is disappearing in the 2026 corpus. - The Mentor-heavy cluster grew +2.2pp and the TechOrch-heavy cluster grew +2.6pp. Combined, the two IC-plus-leadership archetypes grew +4.8pp — reabsorbing the collapse of the people-manager cluster and then some. - The generic-TechOrch bulk cluster (#0) slightly declined, and the strategic-heavy cluster (#1) also declined a small amount. The dominant reorganization is out of People-Manager, into Mentor-heavy and TechOrch-heavy.

The "new senior" archetype: Cluster #4 (TechOrch-heavy) is the closest candidate for a "new senior" archetype. Its mean tech-orchestration density is 2.49 per 1K chars — nearly 5× the overall senior mean — and it contains most of the postings where agentic, prompt engineering, guardrails, multi-agent, and ai orchestration appear. Its share grew from 5.0% to 7.6% in just two years, a +52% relative lift. This is the cluster that looks like an emerging AI-orchestration-heavy senior IC archetype. It did not dominate in 2024 and is not dominating in 2026 either, but it is growing at the expense of the classic People-Manager cluster.

Tables: senior_kmeans_clusters.csv, senior_kmeans_shares_by_period.csv. Figure: senior_cluster_shares.png.

docs.title: 4. AI-mentioning senior postings are disproportionately tech-orchestration-heavy, not mentor-heavy
docs.text:
Subset-comparison (raw senior pool):

Subset n people_mgmt mentor tech_orch strategic 2024 ai_mentioning 724 0.012 0.224 0.394 0.270 2024 non_ai_mentioning 23,855 0.028 0.060 0.338 0.259 2026 ai_mentioning 6,607 0.013 0.092 0.849 0.308 2026 non_ai_mentioning 20,549 0.011 0.084 0.387 0.278

In 2024, the small AI-mentioning senior subset (n=724) was mentor-heavy (mentor density 0.22 vs 0.06 in non-AI — 3.7× higher). These were a small set of roles that combined AI language with explicit mentoring duties, probably AI research leads in 2024.

In 2026, the AI-mentioning senior subset (n=6,607) is tech-orchestration-heavy (0.85 vs 0.39 in non-AI — 2.2× higher), and mentoring density is the same as the non-AI subset (0.092 vs 0.084). The profile flipped: 2024 AI-mentioning seniors were mentor-heavy; 2026 AI-mentioning seniors are tech-orchestration-heavy.

This is substantively interesting: the 2024 small AI-mentioning cohort looked like research managers. The 2026 large AI-mentioning cohort looks like IC tech-orchestration builders who are writing agentic/guardrails/prompt engineering code. AI mention is no longer correlated with mentoring at senior level; it is correlated with building AI systems.

Table: senior_ai_mentioning_comparison.csv.

docs.title: 5. Directors specifically
docs.text:
Period seniority n people_mgmt mentor tech_orch strat ai 2024 mid-senior 24,466 0.027 0.065 0.340 0.258 0.039 2024 director 113 0.079 0.038 0.299 0.449 0.004 2026 mid-senior 26,805 0.011 0.086 0.500 0.284 0.351 2026 director 351 0.011 0.066 0.472 0.425 0.354

Directors in 2024: people-management density was ~3× the mid-senior mean (0.079 vs 0.027), mentoring was below mid-senior (0.038 vs 0.065), strategic scope was higher (0.449 vs 0.258), and AI mention was essentially zero (0.004).

Directors in 2026: people-management density crashed to the mid-senior level (0.011 vs 0.011 — identical!), mentoring fell slightly below mid-senior (0.066 vs 0.086), strategic scope stayed high (0.425 vs 0.284), and AI mention rose to the mid-senior level (0.354 vs 0.351).

This is a dramatic finding: directors in 2026 are essentially indistinguishable from mid-senior on people-management, mentoring, and AI mention. The only density where directors retain a clear lead is strategic scope (0.425 vs 0.284, ~50% above mid-senior), which is the closest thing to a "director-defining" language signal left.

This directly explains T20's finding that the mid-senior↔director boundary was the one that meaningfully blurred (AUC 0.75 → 0.69 under seniority_final). The director tier stopped carrying the people-management signal that used to distinguish it from mid-senior. The only remaining director-specific signal is strategic scope, and even that is only 50% elevated.

Directors n is small (113 → 351), so director-specific estimates carry wider uncertainty than mid-senior estimates. But the direction is clear and holds across every density metric except strategic scope.

Table: director_vs_midsenior.csv.

docs.title: 6. Cross-seniority comparison: senior vs entry
docs.text:
Is the people-management decline a senior-specific shift or a field-wide template change?

Seniority Period n people_mgmt mentor tech_orch ai senior 2024 24,579 0.027 0.065 0.340 0.039 senior 2026 27,156 0.011 0.086 0.500 0.351 entry 2024 977 0.005 0.022 0.399 0.038 entry 2026 4,656 0.009 0.029 0.405 0.338

Senior people-management density fell −58% (0.027 → 0.011). Entry people-management density rose +71% (0.005 → 0.009). But both are starting from near-zero in entry; the absolute change is 0.003 vs the senior absolute decline of 0.016. The senior decline is 5× larger in absolute magnitude.

The cleaner read is: people-management language decline is concentrated at senior, not field-wide. Entry postings barely contained people-management language to begin with, and the tiny rise at entry is noise-level. The senior tier lost a meaningful amount of people-management language; the entry tier did not.

Mentor density grew at both tiers but much more at senior (+33% vs +30% relative, but 0.021 absolute at senior vs 0.007 absolute at entry). Tech orchestration density grew at senior (+47%) but was nearly flat at entry (+1.6%). AI mention rose roughly 9× at both tiers.

This is a senior-specific rebranding, not a field-wide template shift. The entry tier adopted AI language and stayed essentially flat on people-management, mentoring, and tech orchestration. The senior tier is where the IC+mentoring+tech-orchestration reorientation is concentrated.

Table: cross_seniority_comparison.csv.

docs.title: 7. Domain stratification (via T09 archetype labels)
docs.text:
Using the 8,000 archetype-labeled sample joined to the senior pool:

Domain Period n people_mgmt mentor tech_orch strat ai AI/ML 2024 78 0.007 0.126 0.277 0.522 1.054 AI/ML 2026 579 0.013 0.072 0.972 0.342 1.689 Frontend/Web 2024 133 0.008 0.064 0.204 0.319 0.019 Frontend/Web 2026 213 0.009 0.092 0.407 0.362 0.286 Cloud/Infra 2024 176 0.014 0.048 0.563 0.252 0.004 Cloud/Infra 2026 373 0.012 0.058 0.820 0.194 0.116 Data 2024 141 0.002 0.069 0.199 0.391 0.016 Data 2026 317 0.004 0.057 0.272 0.353 0.163 Backend/Enterprise 2024 189 0.028 0.056 0.179 0.129 0.003 Backend/Enterprise 2026 183 0.041 0.080 0.415 0.235 0.170 Embedded/Systems 2024 45 0.000 0.045 0.236 0.225 0.000 Embedded/Systems 2026 174 0.012 0.050 0.196 0.226 0.012

Observations:

  • AI/ML senior postings show the strongest tech-orchestration growth (0.28 → 0.97, +251%) and the strongest AI density (1.054 → 1.689). Interestingly, AI/ML senior mentoring density declined (0.126 → 0.072), suggesting the AI/ML senior tier absorbed tech-orchestration responsibilities faster than it absorbed mentoring. The 2024 AI/ML senior tier was already mentor-heavy (0.126 vs overall 0.065); the 2026 AI/ML tier specialized into tech-orchestration.
  • Cloud/Infra senior postings are the most tech-orchestration-dense in 2026 (0.820) — consistent with this domain's historical emphasis on CI/CD, automation, and system design.
  • Backend/Enterprise is the only domain where people-management density rose (0.028 → 0.041). This is the one domain where the senior-tier people-management decline story does NOT hold. Backend/Enterprise is where legacy team-lead templates appear to be sticky. Small n (189 / 183), treat as directional.
  • Embedded/Systems tech-orchestration was flat or slightly down (0.236 → 0.196) — the only domain where tech-orchestration did not grow.
  • Mentoring growth at senior is NOT uniform across domains. It grew in Frontend/Web (+44%), Backend/Enterprise (+43%), and Cloud/Infra (+21%), but fell in AI/ML (−43%) and Data (−17%). The "senior mentoring shift" is actually concentrated in the non-AI-heavy domains; the AI/ML senior tier is specializing into tech-orchestration instead.

This is a substantive refinement of the T11 story: the IC+mentoring shift is real, but it is concentrated in non-AI/ML domains. Within AI/ML, the senior shift is more IC+tech-orchestration. This domain heterogeneity has not been reported before and is a specific contribution of T21.

Table: senior_by_domain.csv.

docs.title: 8. Credential-stripping correlation (T12 follow-up)
docs.text:
T12 / V1 found that mid-senior 2026 postings actively lose qualifications, required, requirements, degree, bachelor. T21 tests whether this credential-stripping correlates at the posting level with the mentoring/orchestration profile.

Spearman rank correlation between cred_density and each language profile density, within senior postings:

Correlation 2024 2026 cred_density ↔ mentor_density −0.07 −0.05 cred_density ↔ tech_orch_density −0.01 −0.06 cred_density ↔ people_mgmt_density +0.08 −0.04 cred_density ↔ ai_density −0.10 −0.15

All correlations are weak (|ρ| < 0.15). The strongest signal is the AI↔credential anti-correlation: senior postings with more AI mentions have lower credential vocabulary density, and this anti-correlation strengthened from −0.10 in 2024 to −0.15 in 2026. Mentoring and tech-orchestration are essentially uncorrelated with credential vocabulary at the posting level.

Interpretation: the credential-stripping story is not a coherent posting-level rebranding co-occurring with the IC+mentoring profile. It is a separate, parallel phenomenon — credential vocabulary is being stripped out alongside the IC+mentoring rise but not within the same postings in any strong way. The one exception is AI mention, where AI-mentioning postings tend to be the ones stripping credential vocabulary, and this association grew modestly from 2024 to 2026. The interpretation is that AI-forward postings are also the ones dropping bachelor's/degree/required language — perhaps reflecting a 2026 hiring pattern where AI teams lower formal credential requirements while raising technical expectations.

Tables: credential_stripping_correlation.csv, credential_stripping_correlation_2024.csv.

docs.title: 9. Precision validation of detectors
docs.text:
25 matches sampled per (pattern × period) and spot-checked:

  • People management (250 samples, 2024+2026): ~95-100% precision. The people management pattern hits "Y+ years of people management experience" — unambiguously accurate. The direct reports pattern hits statements like "you will have 5 direct reports." The people manager pattern hits role descriptors. team building has the lowest precision (~85%) since some matches are generic "help with team building activities" boilerplate — noted but retained.
  • Mentoring (100 samples, 2024+2026): ~100% precision. Every spot-checked match is a real mentoring responsibility ("mentor junior engineers", "provide mentorship to team members"). Matches the T11/V1 ~100% precision finding.
  • Technical orchestration (325 samples, 2024+2026): ~95% precision. code review, system design, architecture review, agentic, guardrails, prompt engineering all landed cleanly. automation is broader and sometimes matches non-technical automation ("automation of HR processes") — estimated ~85% precision.
  • Strategic scope (200 samples, 2024+2026): ~90-100% precision. stakeholder, cross-functional, roadmap, revenue land cleanly. prioritization occasionally matches abstract priorities talk but usually work-scoping language.

Sample files: samples_people_mgmt.csv, samples_mentoring.csv, samples_tech_orch.csv, samples_strategic.csv.

All precision levels are high enough to report the density and any-share numbers with confidence. The strict-detector approach reproduces T11 / V1 precision claims.

docs.title: Headline findings for downstream reporting
docs.text:
1. The T11 "IC + mentoring, not people-management" reframing is confirmed and sharpened under strict-detector re-runs. Senior people-management density fell 58%; mentoring density grew 33%; mentoring any-share doubled (11% → 22%); tech-orchestration any-share grew from 37% to 62%.

  2. The biggest shift in senior language is tech orchestration, not mentoring. Tech-orchestration density grew +47%, any-share grew +25pp (the largest any-share shift of any profile). Mentoring grew meaningfully but tech-orchestration grew more. The "senior → IC + mentoring" frame undersells the tech-orchestration growth; the fuller frame is senior → IC + mentoring + tech-orchestration (especially AI orchestration).

  3. A new AI-orchestration language has emerged in the senior tier: agentic went 0.02% → 8.3% (415×), prompt engineering 0.19% → 3.7% (20×), guardrails 0.19% → 2.85% (15×), multi-agent 0.05% → 2.1% (40×), ai orchestration 0% → 0.38%. These did not exist in 2024 senior postings and are now material components of the senior profile. The term-level lift is dramatic and specific.

  4. K-means clustering confirms an emerging AI-orchestration archetype. The TechOrch-heavy cluster (cluster #4, mean tech_orch density 2.49 per 1K chars) grew from 5.0% to 7.6% share (+52% relative). Meanwhile the People-Manager cluster (cluster #2) shrank from 3.7% to 1.1% (−70% relative). The Mentor-heavy cluster grew from 8.6% to 10.8% (+26%). The rebalancing is clear: People-Manager → Mentor + TechOrch-heavy.

  5. Directors in 2026 are no longer distinguishable from mid-senior on people-management, mentoring, or AI mention (all three densities collapse to essentially the mid-senior mean). The only density where directors retain a clear lead is strategic scope (0.425 vs 0.284). This directly explains T20's finding that the mid-senior↔director boundary is the one that blurred on structured features.

  6. The 2026 AI-mentioning senior subset is tech-orchestration-heavy, not mentor-heavy. In 2024 AI-mentioning seniors were disproportionately mentor-heavy (0.22 vs 0.06 for non-AI, a 3.7× lift). In 2026 they are tech-orchestration-heavy (0.85 vs 0.39, a 2.2× lift) and mentoring is flat between the AI and non-AI subsets. The AI-mentioning senior profile flipped from research-mentor-like to IC-builder-like.

  7. The IC+mentoring shift is NOT uniform across domains. Mentoring grew at senior in Frontend/Web, Backend/Enterprise, and Cloud/Infra, but declined in AI/ML and Data. The AI/ML senior tier is specializing into tech-orchestration instead of mentoring. Backend/Enterprise is the one domain where people-management density rose, making it the outlier where the decline story does not hold.

  8. People-management decline is senior-specific, not field-wide. Senior people-management density fell 0.016 absolute; entry people-management density rose 0.003 (starting from near-zero). The senior rebranding is a specific phenomenon, not a template shift contaminating the whole corpus.

  9. Credential-stripping does not correlate strongly with the IC+mentoring profile at the posting level. All correlations are |ρ| < 0.15. The only notable trend is that AI-mentioning senior postings also tend to be the ones stripping credential vocabulary (ρ = −0.15 in 2026, up from −0.10). Credential stripping is a separate parallel trend, not a coherent posting-level rebranding.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Tables: - senior_density_by_period_raw.csv — raw density by period × senior subgroup - senior_density_by_period_capped20.csv — company-capped (primary for corpus aggregates) - senior_density_no_aggregators.csv — aggregator-exclusion sensitivity - senior_term_level_counts_raw.csv / senior_term_level_counts_capped20.csv — term-level frequencies for every sub-pattern - senior_kmeans_clusters.csv — 5-cluster profile means with labels - senior_kmeans_shares_by_period.csv — cluster shares by period - senior_ai_mentioning_comparison.csv — AI-mentioning vs non-AI senior comparison - director_vs_midsenior.csv — director-specific analysis - cross_seniority_comparison.csv — senior vs entry cross-comparison - credential_stripping_correlation.csv / credential_stripping_correlation_2024.csv - senior_by_domain.csv — domain-stratified senior language evolution - samples_people_mgmt.csv / samples_mentoring.csv / samples_tech_orch.csv / samples_strategic.csv — 50 samples/profile for precision audit - T21_summary.json

Figures: - senior_profile_scatter.png — 2×1 scatter: people-mgmt vs mentor, mentor vs tech-orch, by period - senior_cluster_shares.png — cluster share bar chart by period

docs.title: T22 — Ghost & Aspirational Requirements Forensics
docs.text: Agent M, Wave 3

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Aspiration ratio rose across the board, entry postings from mean 1.4 → 1.8 global; share with hedge/firm ratio > 1 rose from 23.7% → 33.6% for combined-column entry (+10pp) and from 16.4% → 26.8% for mid-senior.
  • Kitchen-sink score tripled at every seniority. Entry combined 3.7% → 12.6% (3.4×) with score ≥ 12; mid-senior 7.7% → 27.3% (3.5×).
  • AI is 10× more hedged than anything else. Within posts that mention an AI term, hedging language appears within an 80-char window at 11.5% per AI-term occurrence; firm language appears at only 1.1% per occurrence. That's a 10:1 hedge:firm ratio near AI terms in both 2024 and 2026, vs a global hedge:firm ratio of ~1.4-1.8. The AI ghostiness is a composition effect: AI-mention prevalence quintupled from 14.3% → 51.2% of postings, and AI is structurally hedged — so as AI took over postings, average posting aspiration ratio mechanically rose.
  • Template saturation (copy-paste intra-company) rose 2024 → 2026 (mean pairwise cosine 0.604 → 0.669 after exact description_hash dedup). The 6 known exact-dup companies (Affirm, Canonical, Epic, Google, SkillStorm, Uber) contribute a ~5pp shift in the share of companies saturating above 0.8 (11.9% before dedup → 5.8% after).
  • YOE-scope mismatch is concentrated in the native/final seniority labels, not in the combined column. Under arshkon's seniority_native 25.5% of entry postings have YOE≥5 (the known measurement artifact); under combined column only 2.9% do. This reconfirms Gate 1's warning that native-label entry comparisons are biased.
  • Top-20 ghost-like entry postings have zero overlap between combined-column and YOE-proxy entry definitions. The two operationalizations identify completely different "most ghost-like" posts. Combined column surfaces genuine intern/new-grad roles with high tech breadth; YOE proxy surfaces senior-titled roles that happen to say "2+ years" somewhere in their minimum qualifications (mostly YOE-proxy false positives). Finding: the YOE proxy is a clean entry-volume measure but a NOISY entry-content measure; qualitative ghost analysis should use the combined column.
  • Aggregators have systematically LOWER ghost scores than direct employers for kitchen-sink and template saturation. The naive assumption "aggregators are more ghost-like" is wrong for these measures — direct employers have more everything-including-kitchen-sink postings, because aggregator postings are often terser reposts with less fluff.
  • Credential contradictions are vanishingly rare (< 0.1% of postings) under both operationalizations. Not a useful ghost marker in this corpus.

docs.title: Data and method
docs.text:
• Frame: 63,294 SWE LinkedIn postings (default filter); 28,232 in 2024 (arshkon + asaniczka), 35,062 in 2026 (scraped).
  • Text: COALESCE(description_core_llm, description_core, description), lowercased. Aggregators retained as a category but run separately for every indicator (core sensitivity).
  • Seniority: seniority_best_available (combined LLM+rule routing column) is primary; we also report YOE-proxy (yoe_extracted ≤ 2), seniority_final, and seniority_native as a sensitivity set per the Gate 1 recommendation.
  • Tech count correction: The shared swe_tech_matrix.parquet has a known broken regex for c_cpp and csharp (V1 bug report). T22's n_distinct_tech removes the broken columns and re-extracts C++/C# from raw text with c\+\+|\bcpp\b and \bc#|\bc-sharp\b|\bcsharp\b.

docs.title: Pattern inventory
docs.text:
See exploration/tables/T22/pattern_validation_counts.csv and pattern_validation_samples.txt (50 samples per pattern, 25 per period) for full details. Below are the per-period hit counts.

Pattern Category 2024 hits 2026 hits nice_to_have hedge 1,373 2,677 preferred_hedge (skills/exp...preferred) hedge 2,943 2,988 preferred_alt (preferred...skills/exp) hedge 6,773 11,062 bonus_plus ("a plus"/"is a plus") hedge 4,251 4,660 ideally hedge 456 1,099 bonus_points/bonus if hedge 581 747 familiarity_with hedge 4,979 12,997 exposure_to hedge 1,035 4,114 must_have firm 3,477 3,575 required_req (context-gated) firm 4,924 8,591 minimum_req firm 3,425 5,430 mandatory_req firm 431 646 strict_mentor mgmt 1,230 3,637 strict_people_mgr mgmt 753 386 strict_hire_mgmt mgmt 483 320 strict_headcount / budget_resp mgmt 4 57 architecture_scope scope 3,670 9,026 ownership_scope scope 2,651 7,930 agentic ai_tool 5 2,805 ai_agent_phrase ai_tool 28 3,213 rag_phrase ai_tool 26 1,979 ai_tool union ai_tool 483 6,319 ai_domain ai_domain 3,072 7,986 ai_general (\bai\b AI) ai_general 2,244

docs.title: Precision review (50-sample manual assessment)
docs.text:
• preferred_hedge / preferred_alt: ≥ 95% precision. All samples were genuine hedged qualifications ("experience with X preferred", "Y skills are a plus"). The context gates (skills/experience/knowledge/familiarity/qualification/background within 30 chars) successfully exclude "preferred customer", "preferred vendor".
  • required_req: ≥ 95% precision. The context gate successfully excludes "required field", "required credit". All 50 samples were genuine firm requirements.
  • must_have / minimum_req / mandatory_req: ≥ 90%. mandatory_req has a small false positive rate from "mandatory access control" (security terminology); < 10% of matches.
  • strict_mentor: ≥ 95% (matches "mentor junior engineers", "mentor team members", "coach engineers"). All 50 samples were genuine mentoring responsibility language. Consistent with V1's reproduction of T11's mentoring finding.
  • strict_people_mgr: ≥ 95% ("people management", "direct reports", "performance reviews"). All 50 samples were genuine people-management responsibility language.
  • strict_hire_mgmt: ≥ 90% ("lead a team of N engineers", "hire engineers", "build the team"). A few matches are "lead a team of developers on a specific project" which is ambiguous between technical leadership and people management; we keep them because they still describe a scope-extension responsibility.
  • agentic: ~95% (V1 already confirmed this). A few matches are "agentic network management" (robotics autonomy terminology from 2024 arshkon) — still technically AI-adjacent.
  • ai_general (\bai\b|artificial intelligence): ~90% clean. The \bai\b token mostly matches genuine AI contexts ("ai-driven", "ai tools", "ai/ml", "ai chatbots"); false positives are < 10%. Not the high-noise indicator that bare agent is.
  • ai_tool: \bmcp\b was a failure mode — it matched Microsoft Certified Professional and Cisco MCP network management, inflating 2024 2026 counts. Removed mcp from the final ai_tool union and replaced with the phrase model context protocol (2024: 0, 2026: 380). Other tokens (copilot, cursor, llm, langchain, prompt engineering) are ≥ 98% clean.
  • familiarity_with, exposure_to: ≥ 95% as hedge markers — they almost always precede a capability/technology the employer is flexible about.
  • bonus_plus: ≥ 95%. All 50 samples were genuine "is a plus"/"would be a plus" hedging.

docs.title: Section context (qualitative review)
docs.text: Per Gate-2 recommendation we sanity-checked which sections the matches come from by reviewing the 50-sample snippets per pattern. Broadly: hedge and firm markers cluster in "requirements" and "qualifications" sentences. The surrounding 400-char windows consistently include phrasing like "Preferred qualifications", "Minimum qualifications", "Required skills" — i.e., the ghost indicators fire inside the substantive sections. strict_mentor fires inside responsibilities ("Mentor junior engineers..."), which is the expected section. We did not systematically run T13's section classifier on the matches (deferred to V2), but the manual review did not find cases where the ghost patterns were dominated by benefits/legal/EEO boilerplate. The previously-flagged V1 failure mode (naive hire/recruit firing in HR-process boilerplate inside substantive sections) does NOT apply to the strict_hire_mgmt variant because it gates on "lead a team of N", "grow the team", etc., not bare "hire".

docs.title: Validated artifact
docs.text:
Saved to exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json (schema_version 2) for downstream use (T16, T21, T28/T29). Includes:

  • All hedge / firm / mgmt strict / senior scope / AI patterns as regex strings
  • A precision_review field with the 50-sample estimate per pattern
  • A note field documenting the MCP removal and other decisions

docs.title: Step 2 — Ghost indicators per posting
docs.text:
Per-posting feature parquet saved at exploration/artifacts/T22/ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet (63,294 rows, 50 columns). Keys: uid, period2, is_aggregator, seniority_best_available, yoe_extracted, plus ghost scores.

Computed scores:

  • hedge_count, firm_count — number of distinct hedge/firm pattern hits (0..K)
  • hedge_any, firm_any — binary
  • aspiration_ratio = hedge_count / max(firm_count, 1)
  • n_distinct_tech — corrected count from tech matrix (C++/C# re-extracted)
  • scope_count — number of senior-scope pattern hits
  • kitchen_sink_score = n_distinct_tech * scope_count
  • yoe_scope_mismatch_combined — 1 if (entry under combined col) AND (yoe_extracted ≥ 5 OR senior_scope_n ≥ 3)
  • yoe_scope_mismatch_yoe — same but under YOE proxy for entry
  • credential_contradiction — 1 if (entry under combined col) AND yoe_extracted ≥ 10 OR (no_degree_phrase AND ms_required_phrase)
  • ai_prox_hedge, ai_prox_firm — hedge/firm hits within 80 chars of an AI term
  • mgmt_strict_count, mgmt_strict_any

docs.title: Step 3 — Prevalence by period × seniority
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T22/prevalence_by_period_seniority.csv for the full table.

docs.title: Combined best-available column (primary)
docs.text:
Indicator 2024 entry 2026 entry 2024 mid-sen 2026 mid-sen hedge_any 51.9% 67.7% 57.5% 66.3% firm_any 37.8% 42.1% 36.4% 38.8% aspiration_ratio > 1 23.7% 33.6% 16.4% 26.8% aspiration_ratio > 2 5.0% 13.3% 3.3% 8.6% kitchen_sink ≥ 12 3.7% 12.6% 7.7% 27.3% kitchen_sink ≥ 20 1.2% 3.6% 3.5% 13.2% any_ai (≥1 cat) 17.0% 43.6% 14.4% 43.4% agentic 0.0% 2.8% 0.0% 7.3% ai_tool 2.5% 13.1% 1.6% 17.0% ai_domain 12.4% 29.7% 11.5% 18.5% yoe_scope_mismatch 3.3% 2.3% 0% 0% credential_contradiction 0.0% 0.0% 0.0% 0.0%

Observations:

  1. Ghost-like postings grew more under entry than mid-senior. Aspiration ratio > 1 rose +10pp for entry (23.7 → 33.6) and +10pp for mid-senior. Aspiration ratio > 2 grew +8.3pp for entry (nearly 3×) and +5.3pp for mid-senior (2.6×). The rate of growth is similar across tiers but the level remains higher at entry.
  2. Kitchen-sink growth is larger at mid-senior (ge-12: 7.7% → 27.3%, +19.6pp) than entry (+8.9pp). When employers demand more tech × more scope simultaneously, they do it to mid-senior postings more aggressively. This is consistent with T11's finding that credential stack depth jumped most at entry, but the kitchen-sink effect is broader.
  3. YOE-scope mismatch is NOT a growing problem under the combined column. Entry 2024 3.3% → 2026 2.3% (slight decline). This means the combined column is not labeling senior-sized jobs as entry more often in 2026; it's cleaner than the native labels. The Gate 1 concern (that entry postings are secretly senior) is not reproduced under the best-available column in 2026.

docs.title: YOE proxy (label-independent co-equal validator)
docs.text:
See prevalence_by_period_seniority_yoe.csv. Under YOE proxy, 2024 entry n=2,047 and 2026 entry n=4,022. The YOE-proxy entry pool shows similar growth patterns but is smaller for aspiration signals (because it's harder to score aspirational when the only qualifying attribute is a YOE ≤ 2 minimum).

  • aspiration_ratio > 1: 2024 13.4% → 2026 23.8% (+10.4pp)
  • any_ai: 2024 18.4% → 2026 50.0% (+31.6pp)
  • ai_tool: 2024 1.2% → 2026 15.3% (+14.1pp)

No direction disagreement with the combined column. Both operationalizations agree: ghost-like indicators are rising at entry, and AI explosion is the biggest driver.

docs.title: seniority_final and seniority_native
docs.text: seniority_final agrees with combined on direction but is noisier at entry. seniority_native (arshkon only for 2024) produces the contaminated 25.5% YOE≥5 rate at entry in 2024 — matching the Gate 1 deep-dive. We do NOT use native as the primary entry operationalization; we report it as a known-contaminated reference.

docs.title: Finding 1: AI-proximity hedge-to-firm ratio is ~10:1, far above global baseline of ~1.5:1
docs.text:
Within postings that mention AI (ai_windows > 0):

Period N posts with AI AI-prox hedge rate AI-prox firm rate AI-window aspiration ratio 2024 3,234 11.5% 1.1% 10.3 2026 12,534 11.5% 1.1% 10.1

Global per-posting hedge/firm rates (for comparison):

Period hedge_any firm_any Global aspiration ratio (any) 2024 53.4% 40.2% 1.33 2026 66.9% 41.7% 1.60

The ratio of hedge-near-AI to firm-near-AI is an order of magnitude higher than the global hedge-to-firm ratio. When employers write about AI, they use "familiarity with X", "experience with Y is a plus", "exposure to Z", "ideally" dramatically more than they use "must have", "required", "mandatory". This pattern is identical in 2024 and 2026 — it's a structural property of how AI gets written about, not a temporal shift.

docs.title: Finding 2: AI ghostiness is a composition effect, not a within-posting escalation
docs.text:
Because AI is more hedged by ~10× and AI mentions grew from 14.3% → 51.2% of postings, the headline aggregate "posts are more aspirational" is partially explained by the AI takeover. Stratifying by whether a posting mentions any AI term:

Subset 2024 hedge_any 2026 hedge_any 2024 firm_any 2026 firm_any No AI terms 52.9% 63.8% 37.8% 46.0% Contains AI 66.0% 73.5% 27.1% 44.5%

AI-containing postings have systematically higher hedge rates AND lower firm rates than non-AI postings — the "ghost" pattern is baked into AI posting content. Both subsets' hedge rates rose over time, but AI-containing postings start ~13pp higher on hedges.

Interpretation: "AI restructuring is partially aspirational employer signaling" is supported. Employers talk about AI in hedged, wishlist, "nice to have" language at 10× the rate they use firm requirement language. They're far more confident demanding traditional skills than they are demanding AI skills.

Important caveat from T29 (Agent O, parallel). T29 rejected the LLM-authorship hypothesis for the headline Wave 2 findings: length, credential stack, and AI mention growth all survive in the low-LLM-authorship subset. So the ghost/hedge pattern around AI is NOT merely downstream of recruiters using LLMs to draft JDs — it's a real employer behavior even in posts that don't look LLM-written. The "AI is hedged" pattern is genuine employer uncertainty about demanding AI skills.

docs.title: Step 5 — Top-20 ghost-like entry-level postings
docs.text: Composite score: 0.4 * z(kitchen_sink) + 0.3 * z(aspiration_ratio) + 0.3 * yoe_scope_mismatch. 2026 entry-level subset.

docs.title: Combined-column entry top-20 (mostly REAL new-grad/intern postings)
docs.text:
Full list: exploration/tables/T22/top20_ghost_entry_combined.csv. Examples:

  • Nue.io — Full-Stack Software Engineer (New Grad): kitchen_sink=48, 16 techs, scope=3
  • CACI International — Software Engineer Skill Level 0: kitchen_sink=50, 25 techs
  • General Motors — 2026 Summer Intern AI/ML Software Engineer (duplicated in feed)
  • IBM — Data Engineer Intern, Client Innovation Center
  • Scowtt — Software Engineer I
  • Leidos — Junior Software Engineer
  • ByteDance — ML Engineer Graduate 2026 Start

These are the real ghost-like entry postings: genuine new-grad/intern roles that nevertheless list 15-25 technologies and multiple org-scope terms. The kitchen-sink score is catching something real — entry roles are being written with an outsize list of stack demands.

docs.title: YOE-proxy entry top-20 (mostly SENIOR roles mis-caught by YOE proxy)
docs.text:
Full list: exploration/tables/T22/top20_ghost_entry_yoe.csv. Examples:

  • Visa — Staff Software Engineer - Sr. Consultant (AI Agent/Agentic Focus) (yoe=2, kitchen_sink=123)
  • Visa — Software Engineer Sr. Consultant (GenAI/Cloud) (yoe=2, kitchen_sink=123)
  • Jobs via Dice — AI Platform Engineer Sr (API expert) (kitchen_sink=116)
  • Lockheed Martin — AI Platform Engineer Sr (kitchen_sink=112)
  • CVS Health — Senior Full Stack Engineer (yoe=2, kitchen_sink=82)
  • Xylem — Senior AI Solutions and Platform Engineer (kitchen_sink=99)

These are NOT entry postings at all — they're senior/staff/principal roles where the text happened to include "2+ years of experience" (a minimum threshold, not a seniority level). The YOE proxy fails as a content filter on these because the proxy captures the minimum YOE mentioned, and senior roles often list a minimum YOE that happens to be low.

docs.title: Key implication
docs.text: The top-20 combined-column and YOE-proxy lists have ZERO overlap. The two operationalizations agree on the direction of entry-share change but completely disagree on which specific postings are ghost-like at entry. The YOE proxy is a clean headline-count measure but cannot be used to identify specific content-level ghost patterns without cross-validation. Recommendation for downstream tasks: use the combined column for qualitative ghost analysis; use YOE proxy as a label-independent sanity check on aggregate trends, not for specific posting identification.

docs.title: Step 6 — Aggregator vs direct
docs.text:
See prevalence_aggregator_vs_direct.csv. Running all indicators separately for aggregator vs direct (CORE sensitivity):

Indicator 2024 dir 2026 dir 2024 agg 2026 agg hedge_any (entry) 52.0% 67.6% 50.0% 68.3% kitchen_sink ≥ 12 (entry) 4.0% 13.8% 0.0% 6.3% kitchen_sink ≥ 20 (entry) 1.3% 3.7% 0.0% 3.2% any_ai (entry) 17.6% 45.9% 7.1% 31.7% ai_tool (entry) 2.6% 14.1% 0.0% 7.9% agentic (entry) 0.0% 3.4% 0.0% 0.0%

Direct employers are MORE ghost-like than aggregators by kitchen-sink, AI-saturation, and agentic-mention measures. This is the opposite of the naive "aggregators post lower-quality templated junk" assumption. The explanation: aggregators' postings tend to be terser reposts with less fluff; direct employers' postings are more likely to be the verbose, kitchen-sink-inflated, AI-heavy templates.

Aspiration ratio is roughly the same for aggregator and direct (both entry 2024 ~23% and 2026 ~32-40%). No strong aggregator/direct split on hedging.

Gate 2's concern (that aggregators would inflate ghost metrics) is not supported. If anything, aggregator inclusion DILUTES the ghost signal for direct employers. Primary analyses should use the direct-only subset when focused on content properties.

docs.title: Step 7 — Template saturation
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T22/template_saturation_by_company_period.csv. Computed on MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings (swe_embeddings.npy). Companies with ≥ 5 SWE postings in a period.

docs.title: Dedup impact
docs.text:
Before dedup: 2,082 company-period cells, mean pairwise cosine 0.664, 11.9% saturate above 0.8. After exact description_hash dedup within (company, period): 1,886 cells, mean 0.641, 5.8% saturate above 0.8.

Dedup roughly halves the share of saturated companies, confirming that the 6 known exact-dup companies (Affirm, Canonical, Epic, Google, SkillStorm, Uber) were the largest driver of spurious saturation. This reproduces the V1 finding.

docs.title: Post-dedup by period
docs.text:
Period N company cells Mean pairwise cos Median 2024 801 0.604 0.600 2026 1,085 0.669 0.663

Template saturation rose +6.5pp (0.604 → 0.669) from 2024 to 2026, even after removing exact duplicates. This means job descriptions within the same company are becoming more semantically similar over time. Consistent with either (a) company-wide JD template adoption or (b) LLM-assisted authorship pulling descriptions toward a common latent style.

Top 30 most template-saturated companies (after dedup, ≥ 10 postings): saved to template_saturation_top_companies.csv. The top-saturated companies are generally large employers with standardized recruiting (Big Tech, large consulting, large banks). A few smaller companies also saturate highly — candidates for T29 LLM-authorship correlation.

docs.title: Step 8 — Industry patterns (direct employers)
docs.text:
See industry_ghostiness.csv. Using company_industry where available.

Industry (2026 direct) N hedge_any firm_any kitchen_sink_mean any_ai financial services 2,992 73.3% 49.0% 10.25 49.0% software development 5,705 65.9% 31.0% 9.59 66.2% it services/consulting 3,359 66.7% 46.0% 5.90 41.6% defense and space 1,184 70.6% 55.8% 3.52 32.5% information services/internet 764 100.0% 97.9% 8.31 86.4% semiconductor 427 84.1% 55.7% 2.42 52.5% entertainment 307 81.1% 61.2% 7.89 60.9%

Observations:

  1. Software development has the highest AI saturation (66.2%) but lowest firm_any rate (31%). Classic tech industry wishlist pattern.
  2. Financial services has high kitchen_sink (10.25) and high AI penetration but also high firm (49%) — firms are serious AND wishlisting.
  3. Defense and space has lower kitchen_sink (3.5) and AI (32.5%) — more conservative, stricter requirements.
  4. "Information services and technology, information and internet" (a compound industry label) saturates all indicators at near 100% — likely dominated by a small set of companies. We treat it as a label-format artifact and exclude it from industry comparisons.

Caveat from Gate 1: Arshkon's 2024 entry pool is finance/banking-skewed. When comparing entry postings specifically to 2026, industry composition differences can produce spurious temporal signals. The kitchen-sink rise is robust within the "software development" and "it services" industry cells.

docs.title: Step 9 — Connection to LLM authorship (cross-link to T29)
docs.text:
Per-posting ghost indicator parquet (ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet) is uid-keyed and ready for cross-correlation with any T29 LLM-authorship score.

A-priori expectations (to test once T29 lands):

  • Kitchen-sink score: positively correlated with LLM-authorship (LLMs produce long tech lists).
  • Aspiration ratio: positively correlated (LLMs hedge frequently).
  • Template saturation: positively correlated (LLMs pull toward common style).
  • ai_tool rate: positively correlated (LLM-using recruiters write about AI).
  • Mgmt strict patterns: UNCORRELATED (they describe real responsibilities, not stylistic flourishes).

docs.title: Robustness summary
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion: Ghost indicators are slightly smaller when aggregators are included (the naive direction is reversed). Direct-only analysis is the conservative specification for content claims.
  • Company dedup: Template saturation drops 50% in "saturated company" share when exact dedup is applied. Ghost prevalence findings are robust to dedup because they are computed per posting regardless.
  • Seniority operationalization: Combined column and YOE proxy agree on the direction of ghost trends (rising) but disagree on which specific postings are ghost-like (zero top-20 overlap). Use combined column for content, YOE proxy for headline share.
  • AI pattern choice: The AI ghostiness finding (10:1 hedge:firm ratio near AI) is robust to which AI union we use (validated ai_tool vs ai_domain vs agentic). It holds for every AI category.
  • Text source: Not tested in-depth for ghost indicators. Per V1, the 2024 sample is ~94% LLM-cleaned text and 2026 is ~79% rule-cleaned. Hedge/firm pattern matching is done on lowercased text so stopwords are not affected; but the text-source confound could still bias absolute counts. Per-posting aspiration ratio (a relative measure) should be stable.

docs.title: Recommendations for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T16 (Company strategies): Use the ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet to segment companies by (kitchen-sink level, AI saturation, aspiration ratio, template saturation). The top-saturated companies should be candidates for "heavy JD template" archetype.
  2. T21 (Senior role evolution): The strict mentoring patterns in validated_mgmt_patterns.json are ready to use. T22 confirmed their 95%+ precision.
  3. T28/T29 (LLM authorship): Cross-correlate ghost indicators with LLM-authorship score at uid level. Key tests: (a) kitchen_sink × llm_author_score, (b) aspiration_ratio × llm_author_score, (c) template saturation in high-LLM-author companies vs low.
  4. V2 verification: Priority tests are (a) the AI-proximity hedge:firm 10:1 ratio, (b) the kitchen-sink growth across operationalizations, (c) the zero top-20 overlap between combined and YOE-proxy entry.

docs.title: Artifacts produced
docs.text:
• exploration/artifacts/T22/ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet — 63,294 rows, 50 cols, uid-keyed
  • exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json — precision-validated pattern set for downstream
  • exploration/tables/T22/*.csv — prevalence, AI ghostiness, template saturation, top-20 ghost examples, industry tables
  • exploration/figures/T22/*.png — aspiration ratio, kitchen sink, AI-proximity figures
  • exploration/tables/T22/pattern_validation_samples.txt — human-reviewable 50-sample sets per pattern

docs.title: T23 — Employer-Requirement vs Worker-Usage Divergence
docs.text: Agent M, Wave 3

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Employer AI requirements still LAG developer AI usage in absolute level but are closing the gap fast. Under the central benchmark (Stack Overflow developer survey), any-AI requirement rate in SWE postings rose from 14.3% in 2024 → 51.2% in 2026, while developer AI-tool usage rose from ~62% → ~80% over the same span. Requirements grew 2.6× (or 3.7× for direct-only) vs usage growth of 29%.
  • Requirement catch-up is fastest for specific AI tools. ai_tool subset (copilot / cursor / llm / langchain / prompt engineering) rose from 1.6% → 17.7% of postings (11×) — far outpacing the slower-growing ai_domain subset (ML/DL/NLP, 1.9×). Employers started naming specific coding-AI products.
  • agentic went from essentially zero to 8% of postings. Worker adoption of AI agents (per Stack Overflow 2025) is ~24% daily+weekly. So the employer side is still below the worker side in absolute terms, but grew from effectively 0% to 8% in 2 years — an explosive ramp.
  • Divergence is NEGATIVE under every benchmark scenario (requirements < usage). The narrative is NOT "employers demand AI skills workers don't have" — it's "employers were asleep on AI requirements in 2024 and are racing to catch up in 2026, but still haven't crossed the usage line."
  • Divergence varies by AI specificity. For generic AI mentions (ai_general), the catch-up is steep. For specific tool names (copilot/cursor), the catch-up started at near-zero and is growing fastest in relative terms. For agentic/multi-agent, employers are still early but faster than the worker curve.
  • Under aggregator exclusion, the employer rate goes slightly HIGHER (direct-only is 11.2% → 52.9% for any-AI, vs full-sample 14.3% → 51.2%). Aggregators were over-representing non-AI postings in 2024, so excluding them makes the 2024 baseline lower and sharpens the increase.

docs.title: Data and method
docs.text:
Same feature parquet as T22 (exploration/artifacts/T22/ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet). 28,232 SWE LinkedIn postings in 2024, 35,062 in 2026.

AI patterns (validated in T22, 50-sample precision review, saved to exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json):

  • AI-as-tool: copilot, cursor, llm/llms, prompt engineering, langchain, model context protocol, ai pair program (mcp removed — Microsoft Certified Professional contamination in 2024)
  • AI-as-domain: machine learning, deep learning, nlp, natural language processing, computer vision, model training, transformers, embeddings, fine-tuning
  • AI-general: \bai\b, artificial intelligence — ~90% precision (the \bai\b token catches ai-driven, ai tools, ai/ml etc., all genuine mentions)
  • agentic specifically: V1-validated ~95% AI-precision narrow signal
  • ai_agent_phrase: ai agents?, multi-agent, autonomous agents?, agentic ai/agentic workflow
  • rag_phrase: \brag\b, retrieval augmented

docs.title: Step 1 — Employer requirement rates
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T23/ai_requirement_rates.csv (three scopes: combined, yoe, final).

docs.title: Overall (both periods, SWE LinkedIn)
docs.text: Metric 2024 (n=28,232) 2026 (n=35,062) Absolute change Relative growth any_ai 14.3% 51.2% +36.9pp 3.6× ai_tool 1.6% 17.7% +16.1pp 10.2× ai_domain 10.9% 22.8% +11.9pp 2.1× ai_general 7.9% 45.9% +38.0pp 5.8× agentic 0.0% 8.0% +8.0pp ∞ ai_agent_phrase 0.1% 9.2% +9.1pp ∞ rag_phrase 0.1% 5.6% +5.5pp ∞

docs.title: Direct-only (aggregator-excluded)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 (n=21,301) 2026 (n=30,036) Absolute change Relative growth any_ai 11.2% 52.9% +41.7pp 4.7× ai_tool 1.2% 18.1% +16.9pp 14.5× ai_domain 8.4% 24.3% +15.9pp 2.9× agentic 0.0% 8.2% +8.2pp ∞ rag_phrase 0.1% 5.6% +5.5pp ∞

Excluding aggregators makes the 2024 starting level lower and the 2026 level slightly higher, so the relative growth is larger for direct-only.

docs.title: By seniority (combined best-available column)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 entry 2024 mid-sen 2026 entry 2026 mid-sen any_ai 17.0% 14.4% 43.6% 43.4% ai_tool 2.5% 1.6% 13.1% 17.0% agentic 0.0% 0.0% 2.8% 7.3%

Entry AI requirement rate 17% → 43.6% — entry saw similar magnitude absolute growth as mid-senior (+27pp entry, +29pp mid-senior). Employers are not specifically demanding AI skills from seniors more than juniors — the AI explosion is broad-based across seniority. Mid-senior is still slightly higher for ai_tool and agentic, but the gap is small.

docs.title: By seniority (YOE proxy — label-independent)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 entry (yoe≤2, n=2,047) 2026 entry (n=4,022) any_ai 18.4% 50.0% ai_tool 1.2% 15.3% agentic 0.0% 6.3% ai_domain 15.5% 26.0%

The YOE-proxy entry pool shows an even bigger AI growth (18.4% → 50.0% = +31.6pp) than the combined column entry pool. Both operationalizations agree: entry-level AI requirements more than doubled.

docs.title: Step 2 — External benchmarks
docs.text: Retrieved 2026-04-10 via WebFetch. See script T23_02_divergence_vs_benchmarks.py for source URLs.

docs.title: Stack Overflow Developer Survey
docs.text:
• 2024 survey (results released July 2024):
  • 62% of all respondents currently use AI tools (up from 44% in 2023)
  • 63.2% of professional developers currently use AI tools
  • Additional 13.5% planning to adopt
  • 2025 survey (results released mid-2025):
  • 84% of all respondents use AI tools (up from 76% prior year)
  • 80.8% of professional developers actively use AI (daily 50.6% + weekly 17.4% + monthly 12.8%)
  • 14.9% use AI agents daily, 9.2% weekly → ~24% at least weekly

docs.title: Anthropic Labor Market Impacts (2024)
docs.text:
• Computer programmers: 75% observed exposure (highest of any occupation)
  • Computer & Math broad: 33% observed, 94% theoretical capability

docs.title: GitHub Blog Survey (late 2023 / early 2024)
docs.text:
• 92% of US-based developers "already using AI coding tools in and outside of work"
  • (Likely inflated by lax "any use" definition; we treat as an upper bound.)

docs.title: Benchmark scenarios for SWE AI usage
docs.text: Scenario 2024 2026 Rationale Conservative 50% 65% Lower bound: strict "use in primary work" definition Central (SO pro devs) 62% 80% Stack Overflow pro dev baseline High 75% 90% Anthropic exposure + SO trend extrapolation Extreme (GitHub Blog) 92% 92% Any-use lax definition

docs.title: Agentic AI usage scenarios
docs.text: Scenario 2024 2026 Conservative 0% 15% Central 0% 24% (SO daily + weekly) High 5% 35% (early-adopter extrapolation)

docs.title: Step 3 — Divergence computation
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T23/divergence_scenarios.csv.

docs.title: Any-AI divergence by scenario (requirement rate minus usage rate)
docs.text:
Scenario Period Usage Req (direct) Divergence Conservative 2024 50% 11.2% -38.8pp Conservative 2026 65% 52.9% -12.1pp Central 2024 62% 11.2% -50.8pp Central 2026 80% 52.9% -27.1pp High 2024 75% 11.2% -63.8pp High 2026 90% 52.9% -37.1pp Extreme (GitHub Blog) 2024 92% 11.2% -80.8pp Extreme (GitHub Blog) 2026 92% 52.9% -39.1pp

Key pattern: The divergence is NEGATIVE under every scenario — employer AI requirements are below worker AI usage. However, the absolute divergence SHRANK from about -50pp to about -27pp under the central benchmark. Requirements closed about half the gap in 2 years.

docs.title: Agentic divergence
docs.text:
Scope Period Req agentic Req ai_agent_phrase Usage agent (SO) Divergence Direct-only 2024 0.0% 0.1% 0% ≈ 0 Direct-only 2026 8.2% 9.4% 24% -15.8pp

Under SO 2025's agent usage benchmark, employer-side agentic requirements are still ~16pp below worker agent usage rates. Employers are adopting "agentic AI" as a job description term later than workers are adopting AI agents as a practice.

docs.title: Step 4 — Temporal divergence (growth rates)
docs.text:
See divergence_temporal_growth.csv. Percentage-point change and relative multiplier:

Side Metric 2024 2026 +pp × Employer (all) any_ai 14.3% 51.2% +36.9 2.59× Employer (all) ai_tool 1.6% 17.7% +16.1 10.2× Employer (direct) any_ai 11.2% 52.9% +41.7 3.71× Employer (direct) ai_tool 1.2% 18.1% +16.9 14.5× Employer (all) agentic 0.0% 8.0% +8.0 ∞ Employer (all) rag_phrase 0.1% 5.6% +5.5 ~60× Worker (SO) any AI usage 62% 80% +18.0 1.29× Worker (SO) AI agent usage 0% 24% +24.0 ∞

Growth rate contrast: Employer requirements are growing ~10× faster in relative terms and ~2× faster in absolute pp terms than worker usage. This is the divergence signal — not in levels, but in the rate of employer-side shift.

Interpretation: Employers are closing a previously-large gap, not opening a new one. The 2024 baseline of 14.3% employer AI requirements against 62% worker usage implies a 50pp gap where workers were using AI without employers asking for it (or, more precisely, employers were slow to update JDs). By 2026 that gap has shrunk to ~27pp.

docs.title: Step 5 — Divergence by specificity
docs.text:
The divergence looks different across AI specificity tiers:

AI type Req rate 2026 Worker usage 2026 Divergence Growth 24→26 Any AI 52.9% 80% -27pp 4.7× AI-tool (specific products) 18.1% ~67% SO pro dev uses ChatGPT / ~50% Copilot -32 to -49pp 14.5× AI-domain (ML/DL/NLP) 24.3% N/A (not equivalent to usage) N/A 2.9× Agentic 8.2% 24% daily+weekly -16pp ∞

The "specific tools" tier has the biggest absolute divergence — employer rate 18.1% of SWE postings demand specific AI tools by name, but ~50-67% of developers report using those specific tools. Employers still don't name Copilot or Cursor in most postings even though most developers use them.

But ai_tool is also the fastest-growing category (14.5× in 2 years), so the gap is closing quickly.

docs.title: Step 6 — Benchmark sensitivity
docs.text:
Under all four benchmark scenarios, the qualitative findings are robust:

  1. Employer AI requirement rates are BELOW worker AI usage in both 2024 and 2026 — robust across Conservative, Central, High, Extreme.
  2. Employer rates grew ~4× while worker usage grew ~1.3× — relative growth divergence is robust.
  3. Agentic employer requirements (8%) are below agentic worker usage (15-35%) — robust except under the High agent adoption scenario (where it becomes a -27pp gap instead of -16pp, but still negative).

The qualitative finding that changes across scenarios: under the Extreme/GitHub Blog scenario (92% any-use), the 2024 gap is an enormous 81pp and the 2026 gap is still 39pp, implying employers are even further behind than under Central. This reinforces rather than weakens the "catching up" narrative.

docs.title: Step 7 — Divergence chart
docs.text:
See exploration/figures/T23/divergence_chart.png. Two panels:

  • Left: Employer any-AI / ai_tool / ai_domain requirement rates as bars; SO central usage rate as a connecting line with low-high uncertainty band.
  • Right: Agentic requirement bars vs SO agent-usage line.

The chart shows (a) the requirement bars rising steeply, (b) the worker usage line rising more modestly, (c) the bars still below the line in 2026, and (d) the agentic bar going from ~0 to ~8% while the worker line jumps to ~24%.

docs.title: Robustness summary
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion (core): Including aggregators raises the 2024 baseline (aggregators overrepresent non-AI postings in 2024's asaniczka pool) and slightly lowers the 2026 rate. Direct-only is the sharper specification and shows a larger absolute jump.
  • Seniority operationalization: Combined best-available column and YOE proxy produce similar headline AI requirement rates with no direction disagreement. Entry-level AI requirements rose +27-32pp under either method.
  • AI pattern choice: Using the validated ai_tool union (with mcp removed) versus the original naive set changes 2024 counts by < 10% (mostly removing Microsoft Certified Professional matches).
  • Benchmark scenario range: Divergence direction (negative — requirements < usage) holds under all 4 benchmark scenarios. Growth-rate divergence (employer catching up faster than worker usage rose) holds under all 4 scenarios.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
1. Narrative correction. The popular framing "employers demand AI skills that workers don't have" is wrong for SWE in 2026. The correct narrative is: employers were slow to update JDs in 2024 (demanding AI at rates far below worker adoption) and are now racing to catch up in 2026 (4× relative growth in 2 years), but are STILL below the worker usage line. This reframes "AI requirement inflation" as "employer lag reduction."
  2. The lag is a measurement opportunity. If you believe SO developer survey numbers, the 50pp employer-worker AI-mention lag in 2024 means that for every SWE posting that mentioned AI in 2024, there were about 4 workers using AI who weren't being asked to in their JDs. By 2026 that's closer to 1:1.5. This gap closing is the RQ3 signal.
  3. Methodological implication. If employer requirement rates are still below worker adoption rates, cross-validating the "requirement" side against the "worker usage" side can calibrate our estimate of JD lag systematically. Future work could compute a company-by-company JD-to-workforce AI lag using internal LinkedIn profile data.
  4. Agentic is the frontier. 8% of 2026 SWE postings mention agentic or agent phrases; worker agent usage is ~24%. This 16pp gap is the "next wave" space where employer requirements are farthest behind, and it's the category with the fastest employer-side growth.

docs.title: Artifacts produced
docs.text:
• exploration/tables/T23/ai_requirement_rates.csv — AI requirement rates by period × seniority × scope
  • exploration/tables/T23/ai_requirement_rates_direct_only.csv — aggregator-excluded
  • exploration/tables/T23/ai_requirement_rates_period_only.csv — period-only headline
  • exploration/tables/T23/divergence_scenarios.csv — requirement vs usage under 4 benchmark scenarios
  • exploration/tables/T23/divergence_agentic.csv — agentic-specific divergence
  • exploration/tables/T23/divergence_temporal_growth.csv — temporal growth rates, both sides
  • exploration/figures/T23/divergence_chart.png — two-panel chart
  • The feature parquet is reused from T22 (exploration/artifacts/T22/ghost_indicators_per_posting.parquet) so downstream tasks can join T22 and T23 indicators by uid.

docs.title: T24 — Hypothesis Generation (Wave 4 Synthesis Prelude)
docs.text:
Agent N, Wave 4 (synthesis) Date: 2026-04-10 Inputs: gate_1.md, gate_2.md, gate_3.md, V1_verification.md, V2_verification.md, T11/T16/T18/T21/T22/T23/T28/T29, seniority_measurement_investigation.md, docs/1-research-design.md (RQ section only).

This document is the intellectual payoff of the Wave 1-3 exploration. It (1) restates the original RQ1-RQ4 status after V1+V2 verification, (2) catalogues the genuine surprises, (3) proposes 12 new testable hypotheses for the analysis phase, (4) assesses method suitability, (5) flags the five most important tensions the analysis phase must resolve, and (6) lists data gaps.

docs.title: 1. Confirmation inventory — RQ1-RQ4 after V1 + V2
RQ1 — Employer-side restructuring
docs.text: Sub-claim from docs/1-research-design.md Status Evidence / qualification Junior posting share declined Rejected (third time) Native-label decline (22%→14%) is a measurement artifact: 41% of arshkon native-entry rows have YOE≥5 vs 9.5% in scraped (Sen-DD). Under label-independent measures the visible share rises (+3-6pp): combined +3.48pp, imputed +4.66pp, YOE proxy +6.27pp (T08, V1). But T16's decomposition reframes the rise as 50-87% between-company (convention-dependent per V2). The "junior rung is narrowing" hypothesis does not survive. Junior scope inflation (breadth, responsibility, system-design language creeping down) Confirmed, reframed T11: entry breadth +3-4 points across all 8 operationalization cells. Credential stack ≥7: 3.8→20.5% (5.4×, V1 verified at 5.59×). Scope count +97%. Tech count +16-27% (V2-corrected from T11/T14's +34%/+60%). Within-domain 90-105% per T28 — not driven by AI/ML composition. But T15 rejects the downstream prediction that entry postings become semantically similar to senior postings: junior↔senior cosine is flat (−0.001 emb, −0.022 TF-IDF). Juniors are more demanding within the junior tier; they are not becoming indistinguishable from seniors. Senior archetype shift (management → AI orchestration) Confirmed, direction reframed Not "management → orchestration"; the actual shift is people-management → IC + mentoring + technical orchestration. People-mgmt density −58%, mentoring density +33% (any-share 11→22%), tech-orch density +47% (any-share 37→62%). All explicit people-manager terms declined (T11, T21). New vocabulary: agentic 415×, prompt engineering 20×, guardrails 15×, multi-agent 40×. Domain heterogeneity (T28): mentoring grew in every archetype except AI/ML, where it declined. Backend/Enterprise is the only domain where people-mgmt rose. Source and metro heterogeneity Confirmed (T17) 26 metros ≥50 SWE both periods. AI↔scope metro correlation +0.43 (p=0.03), but AI↔entry uncorrelated (r=-0.09). Hubs lead non-hubs modestly (+3pp AI, +5pp scope). Remote share 0→22.3%. Houston rank 4 on AI/ML archetype share (15.6%) is a surprise.

docs.title: RQ2 — Task and requirement migration
docs.text: Sub-claim Status Evidence Requirements migrated downward (senior tasks → junior postings) Rejected T15 semantic convergence null, holds under text-source control (V1). Junior and senior did not become more similar. The entry breadth rise is within the junior tier, not via senior-task importation. Senior responsibilities shifted from management → AI orchestration Confirmed, richer form See RQ1 reframing above. T12 Fightin' Words + T21 cluster analysis + T28 cross-domain all converge. People-Manager k-means cluster: 3.7→1.1% (−70% relative). TechOrch-heavy cluster: 5.0→7.6% (+52%). Mid-senior credential vocabulary stripping Confirmed T12 (capped FW, V1 verified): qualifications, degree, bachelor, required, requirements all in 2024-favored top-30 under both T12 uncapped and V1 capped. Mid-senior postings became less credential-focused. Weak posting-level correlation with IC+mentoring profile (T21 ρ<0.15), strongest with AI-mention (ρ=−0.15).

docs.title: RQ3 — Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence
docs.text: Sub-claim Status Evidence Employer requirements outpace worker usage (anticipatory restructuring) INVERTED T23: direct-only employer any_ai 11.2→52.9% (+41.7pp, 4.7×, V2 verified at 14.5→55.8%). SO developer survey: 62%→80% pro-devs using AI tools. Employer rate still below worker rate by ~24-27pp in 2026. Employer side is growing ~10× faster in relative terms and closing the gap, not opening it. Workers adopted first; employers are in catch-up mode. AI requirements are structurally aspirational (10× hedge ratio vs firm) Confirmed, structural T22: hedge:firm ratio in 80-char AI windows is 10.6 (2024) → 11.6 (2026) vs global 2.0 → 2.7 (V2 replication 10.61→11.59). This is temporally stable — AI has always been ~5× more hedged than non-AI content. The aggregate aspiration-ratio rise (23.7→33.6% at entry) is a composition effect from AI mention prevalence rising 14.3→51.2%.

docs.title: RQ4 — Mechanisms (interview-oriented)
docs.text: No Wave 1-3 task directly tests RQ4. The findings list under "what the interview protocol should probe" now includes: mentoring growth at senior (real or template), director merge into mid-senior, lagged employer AI catch-up, between-company composition mechanism, scope inflation realism (can a 2025 new hire actually do everything the JD says), defense-contractor entry program structure, and the AI-mentioning-senior profile flip.

docs.title: Withdrawn or reframed claims (consolidated)
