docs.text: Original claim Status Reason Junior share declined (native labels) Withdrawn Arshkon native-label contamination (Sen-DD + V1) AI/ML is structurally less junior-heavy (T09) Withdrawn T28: routing-coverage artifact — 82.8% None in AI/ML 2026 combined col; under YOE proxy AI/ML 16.0% vs rest 17.2% (identical) 90% within-company junior decline (T06) Withdrawn Contaminated native-label inputs; T16's corrected decomposition is 50-87% between-company, depending on panel convention Tech count +34% (T11) / +60% median (T14) Reduced V2: clean number is +16-27% mean, 3→4 median; T11/T14 used broken 153-col matrix; T19's "nearly flat" was LLM-text-source composition artifact "SWE-specific" restructuring (original RQ framing) Broadened T18+V2: SWE-adjacent is at 83-105% of SWE magnitude; length and scope growth are larger in adjacent than SWE Anticipatory employer AI restructuring (RQ3) Inverted T23: employer rate ~25pp below worker rate; gap closing, not opening Recruiter LLM authorship is the mechanism Rejected T29: Wave 2 headlines persist/strengthen in Q1 low-LLM subset; cross-posting variance unchanged 2024→2026. V2 qualification: credential stacking is partially attenuated in low-LLM (+29% Q1 vs +149% full), so writing style is a partial mediator for that one metric, not for length or AI. Senior shift is people-mgmt → AI orchestration Reframed People-mgmt → IC + mentoring + technical orchestration with domain-heterogeneous split (mentoring declines in AI/ML) T14 "5 declining technologies" Wrong V1: \bc\+\+\b and \bc# regexes broken. C++ +21% (growing); C# flat. T14's community structure missing a systems-languages community.

docs.title: 2. Surprise inventory
docs.text:
These are the genuinely unexpected findings from Wave 1-3. I exclude findings that simply confirmed prior expectations.

S1. The 2024→2026 AI-mentioning senior profile flipped (T21, strong). In 2024 the small AI-mentioning senior cohort (n=724) was mentor-heavy: mentor density 0.224 vs 0.060 in non-AI seniors (3.7× elevated), tech-orch density 0.394. In 2026 the large AI-mentioning senior cohort (n=6,607) is tech-orch-heavy: tech-orch 0.849 vs 0.387 in non-AI (2.2×), while mentor density is identical to non-AI (0.092 vs 0.084). Same group label, completely different positioning: 2024 "AI-mentioning senior" looks like a research mentor; 2026 "AI-mentioning senior" looks like an IC builder. This is the sharpest single within-cohort temporal profile change in the exploration.

S2. Mentoring DECLINED at senior in AI/ML while growing everywhere else (T21, T28, strong). Mentoring is the single cleanest cross-archetype directional result — +1.9 to +29.4pp at senior in every archetype except AI/ML (−5.4pp from 0.126→0.072 density) and Data (−17%). The AI/ML senior tier is specializing purely into technical orchestration; other domains are adding tech-orch AND mentoring. Any theory that says "AI made senior engineers into mentors" has to explain why AI/ML itself bucks this.

S3. Defense contractors are massively over-represented in 2026 entry posting (T16, strong). Under the YOE proxy, the top-20 entry posters include SpaceX 65%, Northrop 33%, Raytheon 29%, Peraton 19.5%, Booz Allen 29%, Leidos 18%, Lockheed. This was not predicted. Combined with the 87% (arshkon convention) / 50-61% (pooled convention) between-company entry rise, a substantial fraction of the visible "entry-share rise" is the scraped corpus picking up more defense-contractor postings. Defense is running formal new-grad pipelines at scale, partly clearance-track.

S4. The 10× hedge:firm ratio around AI terms is structurally stable, not a temporal shift (T22, V2, strong). Within 80-char windows around AI matches, hedge:firm is 10.61 (2024) → 11.59 (2026), with a global ratio of 2.03 → 2.69. AI requirements have always been ~5× more hedged than non-AI requirements at the post level. What changed is that AI mention prevalence quintupled (14.3→51.2%). The "aspirational AI" story is structural grammar, not a new phenomenon.

S5. The director tier collapsed into mid-senior on every dimension except strategic scope (T20, T21, moderate-strong). Under seniority_final, the director↔mid-senior boundary AUC fell 0.75→0.69; every other boundary was stable. T21 explains why: director people-mgmt density 0.079→0.011 (matches mid-senior), director mentoring 0.038→0.066 (matches mid-senior), director AI 0.004→0.354 (matches mid-senior). Only strategic scope (0.425 vs 0.284) remains elevated. The director tier stopped being a people-management track. Small n (113→351) so treat as directional, but every density moves together.

S6. RQ3 is inverted (T23, V2, strong). The baseline expectation was that employer requirements outpace worker usage (anticipatory). The data shows employer requirements 14-52% 2024→2026, still ~24-27pp below SO-central worker usage of ~80% in 2026. Employers were asleep in 2024 and are catching up at 4-10× the relative rate of worker adoption. "Employer AI requirement inflation" becomes "employer lag reduction."

S7. The legacy-stack archetypes drove the credential-stack jump MORE than AI/ML (T28, strong). AI/ML 2024 credential-stack-7 was already 25.7% (highest baseline) and only rose to 38.0% — the smallest ratio increase at 1.5×. Meanwhile ServiceNow went 0.38→24.59% (63.9×), Spring/Java 22.9×, .NET 17.7×, SRE 29.1×, Frontend 11.1×. The "AI/ML restructuring drove content inflation" intuition is backwards: AI/ML was already saturated; the legacy stacks caught up. This is counter-intuitive for any theory that says AI demand caused the content expansion.

S8. 90.4% of scraped companies with ≥5 SWE postings have ZERO entry rows under the combined column; 49.3% under YOE proxy (V1, T16, strong). Entry-level posting is a specialized activity at a small set of formal-program employers, not a market-wide function. Even the YOE-proxy number (roughly half) is striking. The implication is that "entry-level SWE posting rate" is a concentration metric masquerading as a flow metric.

S9. Credential stacking is partially attenuated in the low-LLM Q1 subset (V2, moderate). T29 originally claimed "every Wave 2 headline persists or strengthens in Q1"; V2 re-ran and found length +38% Q1 vs +44% full, AI rate +197% Q1 vs +237% full (both persist), but credential stack 7 +29% Q1 vs +149% full — a meaningful attenuation. Length and AI are authorship-independent; credential stacking is writing-style partially-mediated. This is a real heterogeneity the rejection narrative glossed over.

S10. SWE↔control CONVERGED in text space (+0.079 cosine), SWE↔adjacent DIVERGED slightly (−0.022) (T18, moderate). The naive prediction was that SWE and adjacent tech occupations would blur into each other as tooling democratized. Instead, SWE became more similar to accountant/nurse/financial-analyst postings — driven by shared length expansion, shared inclusive-language shifts, cross-occupation "cross-functional/stakeholders/roadmap" diffusion. Some portion of the observed SWE text changes are cross-occupation genre drift, not SWE-specific content restructuring.

S11. "AI Engineer" within-title evolved cleanly from a PyTorch/ML role to an LLM-agent role (T18, V2, strong). n 20→321 (16×), agentic 0→44.9%, pytorch 40→31.8%, llm_stack 15→53.6%. Within a single title string, the job content transformed completely. Paper-figure-quality evidence that job content can change without title change.

S12. Direct employers are MORE ghost-like than aggregators (T22, moderate). The naive assumption was that aggregators post lower-quality, keyword-stuffed, kitchen-sink templates. The data shows direct employers have higher kitchen-sink, higher any_ai, higher agentic rates. Aggregator postings are terser reposts with less fluff. The "ghost posting = aggregator" heuristic is wrong.

S13. Tech density fell 26% while tech count grew 16-27% (T11, V2, moderate). Postings expanded in length faster than they packed requirements. The mechanism of the expansion is "longer descriptions carrying more items at lower per-char density," not "denser requirement bricks." Any density-based metric will understate the shift.

S14. Mean pairwise cosine for within-company postings rose 0.604→0.669 after description_hash dedup (T22, moderate). Template saturation rose even after removing exact duplicates, suggesting companies are writing progressively more similar postings to themselves. Consistent with two mechanisms that Wave 3 did not distinguish: (a) LLM-assisted drafting pulling toward a latent style, (b) formal template adoption at HR level. The T29 low-LLM subset doesn't cleanly discriminate these.

docs.title: 3. New testable hypotheses
docs.text: These are hypotheses that go beyond the original RQ1-RQ4 and are supported (or hinted at) by multiple Wave 1-3 findings. For each I list evidence, a test design, novelty assessment, and data feasibility. Hypotheses are numbered H1-H12.

docs.title: H1. The 2024→2026 AI-senior profile flip tracks AI's maturation from research-stage to production-stage work within the 2-year window.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The 2024 AI-mentioning senior cohort was mentor-heavy because early AI work was exploratory/research-coded (small teams, lots of junior onboarding into new territory). The 2026 AI-mentioning senior cohort is tech-orch-heavy because AI work transitioned to production engineering (pipelines, agentic systems, guardrails, evaluation) where IC builders dominate.

Evidence from exploration: (1) S1 — the profile flip itself. (2) S7 — AI/ML credential stack was already saturated in 2024; the growth came in tech-orch language, not mentoring. (3) T18's "AI Engineer" within-title evolution from pytorch/ML to LLM-agent + langchain/rag. (4) S2 — AI/ML is the only archetype where senior mentoring declined, consistent with AI/ML work being treated as no-longer-bootcampable for juniors at the senior level.

Test design: Within-company cohort analysis restricted to the ~260 employers present in both periods with ≥2 AI-mentioning senior postings each period. For each posting, compute the T21 four-dimension density vector. Test whether the 2024→2026 profile trajectory within-company moves along a mentoring→tech-orch axis (as opposed to random drift). Secondary: classify AI-vocabulary tokens into "research-stage" (research, prototype, baseline, benchmark, novel, paper, conference) vs "production-stage" (deploy, latency, observability, eval harness, guardrails, rollout) and test whether 2024 AI-mentioning seniors are research-coded and 2026 are production-coded.

Novelty: Strong standalone finding if confirmed. A paper-figure-quality longitudinal narrative about a single role archetype transforming within 2 years tied to a specific technology maturation cycle.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. The within-company panel for AI-mentioning seniors is small (probably ~100-200 companies) but sufficient. The research/production vocabulary split needs a 50-sample precision audit first.

docs.title: H2. Mentoring declined in AI/ML specifically because AI/ML work has become early-career-prohibitive.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: In every other tech domain, mentoring grew because employers pushed mentoring responsibilities onto senior ICs to support growing junior pools. In AI/ML, mentoring declined because (a) the junior AI/ML pool did not grow in mentored form (new-grads are being placed in AI/ML work at the same rate as elsewhere per T28's YOE proxy, but) (b) the content of AI/ML junior postings got so far ahead of what a new-grad can actually do that mentoring language shifted from "mentor juniors" to "work with senior researchers." The mentoring gap is a symptom of AI/ML juniors being expected to arrive pre-trained.

Evidence from exploration: (1) S2. (2) T28's credential-stack baseline: AI/ML was already at 25.7% stack-7 in 2024 vs ~2-10% for other archetypes — AI/ML JDs were always more demanding. (3) T21 domain stratification: AI/ML senior mentoring density 0.126→0.072 (−43%) while tech-orch density 0.277→0.972 (+251%). (4) The 2024 AI-senior profile being mentor-heavy (S1) is consistent with "2024 AI work WAS mentored-into by seniors because the field was new"; the disappearance of that mentoring in 2026 is consistent with "now seniors assume juniors show up ML-fluent."

Test design: Compare the explicit-early-career language (intern, new grad, associate, entry-level) in AI/ML postings to other archetypes in both periods. Also test whether "0-2 years" YOE-proxy AI/ML postings have higher tech count, credential stack depth, and more expert-level vocabulary than 0-2 YOE postings in other archetypes. Complementary: within-company, do AI/ML employers' junior JDs demand more than their non-AI/ML junior JDs at the same company?

Novelty: Medium-strong sub-finding that complements H1. If both H1 and H2 land, the AI/ML story becomes "research-stage work matured to production-stage + became early-career-prohibitive simultaneously."

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. Within-company comparison is small-n for AI/ML juniors but doable.

docs.title: H3. The between-company entry rise is concentrated in defense contractors; removing defense substantially reduces the magnitude.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The 87% between-company entry rise under T16's arshkon-only convention is heavily driven by defense/aerospace contractors (SpaceX, Northrop, Raytheon, Peraton, Booz Allen, Leidos, Lockheed, General Dynamics) whose large formal new-grad pipelines were either under-represented in 2024 sources or became over-represented in the 2026 scraper draw. Excluding defense, the between-company share drops substantially and the composition story gets muted.

Evidence from exploration: (1) S3 — defense dominance in the T16 top-20 entry list. (2) T16's cluster 4 (entry-downsizer, n=18) contains General Dynamics, Leidos, Radley James, Talener, Macquarie, LanceSoft, Honeywell, i.e., defense/finance/staffing — the within-company direction is negative at these firms, suggesting the 2024 arshkon snapshot caught an entry-heavy moment that did not repeat. (3) V2: the 87% replicates exactly under arshkon-only but drops to 50/50 under pooled-2024 panel, suggesting panel composition matters a lot.

Test design: Re-run T16's within/between decomposition with defense/aerospace companies partitioned out (by NAICS sector or company industry label). Report within/between for (a) full panel, (b) defense-excluded panel, (c) defense-only panel. Also test whether the defense cluster is characterized by large 2026 scrape volume vs 2024 volume (scraper coverage artifact) or by genuine hiring concentration. Third test: look at defense posting volume in BLS/JOLTS vs the scraped share — is the scraped-side defense weight proportional to real defense SWE hiring, or over-represented?

Novelty: Strong sub-finding with methodological implications. If defense drives most of the between-company number, then the headline "entry rise is composition" weakens and the headline becomes "defense contractors dominate visible entry SWE hiring in the scraped corpus." Either outcome is publishable.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. Company-industry labels are in the corpus (T16 used them).

docs.title: H4. Credential vocabulary stripping at mid-senior is tightly coupled at the posting level to AI-mention growth.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: Within-posting, the same mid-senior postings that added AI vocabulary are the ones that dropped qualifications, degree, bachelor, required. Not separate trends — a single within-posting swap: AI vocabulary displaces credential vocabulary. If true, this is a within-posting content-substitution mechanism, not two parallel trends.

Evidence from exploration: (1) T21 ρ(cred_density, ai_density) = −0.10 (2024) → −0.15 (2026), the only correlation meaningfully above noise and the only one that strengthened over time. (2) T12/V1 Fightin' Words: credential terms are in the 2024-favored top-30 under both capped and uncapped, alongside AI terms in the 2026-favored top-30. (3) T28 per-archetype: AI/ML (where AI vocabulary saturated) had the highest baseline credential-stack-7 (25.7%) yet the lowest ratio growth (1.5×) — consistent with a saturation ceiling. (4) Partial support from V2's T29 requalification: credential stacking is weaker in low-LLM subset (+29% vs +149% full), hinting that credential-stacking is partly a writing-style shift that correlates with how AI is written about.

Test design: Posting-level paired regression on the set of companies present in both periods. For each (company × period) cell, compute mean AI token rate and mean credential vocabulary rate. Test whether Δ AI and Δ credential are negatively correlated within-company. Secondary: at the individual posting level, is the presence of AI vocabulary associated with the absence of credential vocabulary more strongly in 2026 than in 2024 (controlling for posting length)? Use the T11_features parquet and the T22 validated patterns.

Novelty: Strong standalone finding if it lands. A within-posting substitution mechanism is a much sharper contribution than "both trends moved in parallel." Mechanism-level hypothesis that the analysis phase can test cleanly.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. T11 features parquet + T22 patterns cover everything needed.

docs.title: H5. The RQ3 inversion is itself caused by credential-stripping: employers can't easily list AI as "required" because they are simultaneously stripping all credential vocabulary.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The ~27pp employer-below-worker gap in 2026 is partly a consequence of the credential-vocabulary-stripping trend. Employers cannot write "AI experience required" if they are cutting words like "required" and "qualifications" out of their postings. The hedging of AI language is mechanically tied to the broader de-credentialing of JD vocabulary. This predicts that the employer-worker gap is largest in employers with the most credential-stripping, and that within a year, postings with low credential vocabulary have lower firm-AI rates.

Evidence from exploration: (1) T22 finding that AI-proximity hedge:firm is 10.6 while global is 2.0 — AI is hedged more than other requirements. (2) T11/T12 credential stripping. (3) T21 ρ=−0.15. (4) T23 RQ3 inversion. (5) V2 T29 qualification that credential stacking is partially mediated by writing style. The pieces have been stitched but not tested as a mechanism.

Test design: Posting-level analysis on the 2026 corpus. Define cred_vocab_density as the count of qualifications, required, bachelor, degree, minimum per 1K chars. For each posting, compute (a) cred_vocab_density, (b) ai_hedge_any (within AI window), (c) ai_firm_any (within AI window). Test whether higher cred_vocab_density is associated with higher ai_firm_any / lower ai_hedge_any, controlling for length and AI mention count. Complementary: cross-company panel test — companies in the top quartile of credential-stripping Δ should show the largest gap between their ai_firm rate and their ai_hedge rate.

Novelty: Strong mechanism hypothesis that would provide a causal story for the RQ3 inversion. This is the kind of mechanism question that could unite several Wave 3 findings.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. Requires joining T11 features, T22 ghost indicators, and T23 AI rates at posting level.

docs.title: H6. The "tech-cluster-wide" restructuring is driven by shared vendor vocabulary (Copilot, Cursor, Claude, etc.) propagating through HR templates, not by independent parallel adoption in each occupation.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: SWE and SWE-adjacent occupations show nearly identical AI restructuring magnitudes (V2 adjacent at 105% of SWE) because the vocabulary comes from a shared set of vendor marketing materials that HR teams paste into JD templates across occupations. The restructuring looks "tech-cluster-wide" but is really "HR-template-vocabulary-wide" — it reflects JD authoring practice, not occupational task change. This is a subset of the RQ4 mechanism question.

Evidence from exploration: (1) T18 parity of SWE and adjacent AI growth (V2: 105%). (2) T18 SWE↔control convergence (+0.079) — SWE is drifting toward generic white-collar style. (3) T28 legacy-stack archetypes (ServiceNow 64×, Spring/Java 23×, .NET 18×) drove credential-stack jump more than AI/ML — consistent with template vocabulary propagation into legacy stacks. (4) T22 direct-employer aggregator reversal (direct more ghost-like) — consistent with direct-employer HR teams templating more aggressively than aggregators. (5) T29 per-company analysis identifying Deloitte (+3.55), PwC (+3.43), American Express (+3.37) as LLM-style adopters — consulting firms propagate templates fastest.

Test design: (1) Vocabulary audit: for each of the top-15 AI tokens (copilot, cursor, claude, openai, anthropic, langchain, rag, llm, etc.), compute first-appearance date in each occupation group (SWE, adjacent, control). If tokens arrive in SWE-adjacent and SWE at the same time (within weeks), propagation is template-driven; if tokens appear in SWE weeks-to-months before adjacent, it's independent adoption. (2) Posting-level clone-detection: find near-duplicate phrases containing AI tokens across different companies. High-reuse phrases (seen in N>10 companies) suggest shared template sources. (3) Cross-occupation NMI: does AI-vocabulary usage pattern cluster by company-family (consulting, staffing, enterprise) rather than by occupation?

Novelty: Strong candidate for a mechanism sub-finding. Would link the tech-cluster-wide observation to a specific propagation mechanism (HR templates + vendor marketing). Testable with current data.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data for (1) and (2). NMI analysis needs company-family labels beyond what T16 currently has; feasible with moderate work.

docs.title: H7. The director-tier merge into mid-senior is preceded by a collapse in the director headcount at the same companies (not a label mislabeling).
docs.text:
Hypothesis: T20+T21 find that directors in 2026 look like mid-senior on every dimension except strategic scope. One explanation is that the director tier is being eliminated at the same employers, and what remains labeled "director" is a small set of residual postings that no longer carry the old director content. The alternative (label reclassification, mid-senior postings being relabeled director) would predict no headcount change. If the headcount collapsed, the director role is being eliminated, which is a substantive labor market finding.

Evidence from exploration: (1) S5. (2) T20 single boundary change (mid-senior↔director AUC 0.75→0.69). (3) T21 director n 113→351 (grew, but much slower than the overall senior volume) — this is the key data point.

Test design: Compute director share of SWE postings by company in both periods (arshkon∩scraped panel). Test: (a) did director share as a fraction of same-company posting volume fall? (b) is the fall concentrated in specific companies or company types? (c) are the 2026 directors at different companies than 2024 directors (i.e., is there a company-level substitution)? (d) where did the 2024 director roles go — are they being replaced by Principal, Staff, Mid-senior/Lead roles in the same companies?

Novelty: Medium — interesting substantive labor finding, directly policy-relevant (flattening of mid-management). Could become a separate paper sub-plot. The "collapse of the director tier" is a narrative hook.

Data feasibility: Fully testable with current data.

docs.title: H8. Template saturation growth (T22: 0.604→0.669) is a leading indicator of the legacy-stack credential-stack explosion, with a within-company lag.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The legacy-stack archetypes (ServiceNow 63.9×, Spring/Java 22.9×, .NET 17.7×) had enormous credential-stack growth because companies using legacy stacks lagged in template modernization and then caught up all at once. Template saturation should show a two-step pattern: a small rise in late-2024 at advanced-template employers, then a large rise in 2026 at legacy-stack employers. This predicts that within-company template saturation is highest at companies with legacy-stack postings (T19 ServiceNow, T05 Spring/Java, T09 .NET).

Evidence from exploration: (1) S7. (2) T22 template saturation rose 0.604→0.669 after dedup. (3) T28 AI/ML started saturated, legacy caught up — a diffusion pattern. (4) T29 per-company LLM-style analysis: Deloitte and PwC (consulting firms with heavy legacy-stack hiring) are among the biggest score growers. Consulting + legacy + late-template-adoption triangle.

Test design: For the 220-company overlap panel, compute: (a) template saturation per company per period (mean pairwise cosine within company-period), (b) share of company's postings in legacy-stack archetypes (T19/T05/T09 from T28 labels), (c) 2024→2026 credential-stack-7 delta. Test: is template saturation growth associated with legacy-stack share? Is template saturation growth associated with credential-stack-7 delta, controlling for AI adoption? Temporal: within-company, does template saturation rise lead credential-stack rise?

Novelty: Medium. A mechanism sub-finding that explains why legacy stacks drove the credential jump more than AI/ML. Useful for the Wave 4 narrative.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. Requires joining T22 template saturation per company × period, T28 archetype labels, and T11 credential-stack features.

docs.title: H9. Entry-level posting concentration (90.4% zero-entry companies) is stable across cohorts and is a structural property of LinkedIn posting behavior, not a 2024-2026 change.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The 90.4%-zero-entry statistic is not a 2026 anomaly — it was approximately the same in 2024 if you had enough sample. Most companies do not post entry-level SWE to LinkedIn because (a) they fill entry roles via campus recruiting / referrals, (b) entry positions are filled via internal conversion from internships, or (c) LinkedIn is not a new-grad channel for most employers. The "entry-level SWE posting is a specialized activity" finding is structural, not a trend.

Evidence from exploration: (1) T16 reports 90.4% for scraped 2026 with ≥5 postings. (2) V1 reports 91% for a similar cutoff. (3) No Wave 1-3 task computed the same statistic for 2024 because the arshkon pool was too small for the ≥5 cutoff. (4) T16's top-20 entry posters are almost all large tech/defense employers — suggesting a scale-dependent structural pattern.

Test design: Compute the zero-entry-company share across 2024 (arshkon+asaniczka pooled, ≥5 cutoff per period). If the 2024 and 2026 zero-entry shares are within 5pp of each other, the finding is structural. If 2024 is substantially lower, there is a genuine shift toward concentration. Also extend the cutoff to ≥10 and ≥20 to test sensitivity.

Novelty: Depends on the result. If structural, it's a methodological warning for all labor-market posting analyses (don't treat posting rate as a flow metric without concentration correction). If 2024 was less concentrated, it's a substantive finding about hiring channel consolidation. Either way worth testing.

Data feasibility: Fully testable with current data.

docs.title: H10. The 2024 AI-mentioning seniors were disproportionately at "frontier AI" companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Research, NVIDIA, Meta AI, FAIR); the 2026 cohort spans mainstream tech.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The S1 profile flip (mentor→IC-builder) is driven by compositional change in which companies employ AI-mentioning seniors, not by individual companies' senior roles transforming. In 2024, ~80% of AI-mentioning senior postings came from <20 frontier-AI companies whose senior engineers were genuinely in research-mentor positions. In 2026, AI-mentioning senior postings come from thousands of mainstream tech employers, and the dominant content is production-engineering IC orchestration. The "same group, different profile" framing from T21 is actually "different group, different profile, same label."

Evidence from exploration: (1) T28 AI/ML top employers: 2024 top-15 = NVIDIA/Palo Alto/Tesla/AMD/ByteDance/Rivian/Amazon/Anduril + small specialist AI firms; 2026 top-15 = Microsoft 84, JPMorgan 56, Amazon 52, GM 42, Booz Allen 41, Uber 40, Anthropic 38, NVIDIA 38, Adobe 35. (2) T28 top-20 concentration fell from 30.67% (2024) to 22.39% (2026) — diffusion. (3) T21 n 724→6,607 for AI-mentioning seniors — a 9× increase, which is more than the overall corpus growth (1.24×).

Test design: Restrict T21's profile analysis to the ~20 companies present in both periods' AI-mentioning senior cohort (e.g., NVIDIA, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta). Does the profile still flip mentor→tech-orch within the same companies? If yes, confirm T21's original interpretation. If the within-company profile is stable and only the composition of AI-mentioning-senior employers shifted, then the flip is a compositional phenomenon and T21's "same group" framing is wrong.

Novelty: This is a methodological check on the strongest Wave 3 narrative finding (S1). Either outcome is important — confirming T21 makes the flip a stronger paper finding; refuting it reframes the flip as "mainstream companies started hiring AI IC-builders while frontier companies kept doing what they were doing."

Data feasibility: Fully testable. This is the first thing the analysis phase should run for S1.

docs.title: H11. Employer adoption of specific AI tools (Copilot, Cursor, Claude, LangChain) correlates with company size, industry, and tech-maturity, AND the ordering is different from worker adoption.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The RQ3 inversion (T23) is a company-level aggregation of heterogeneous adopters. Large tech employers adopted AI tool vocabulary in JDs first (2024 early); consulting / finance / defense adopted in 2025-2026 after workers had already integrated the tools. The worker-first story is really "advanced employers posted AI tool JDs at roughly the rate workers were using AI tools; laggard employers lagged by 1-2 years." The aggregate gap closes because laggards catch up. A secondary test: which specific AI tools get adopted into JDs first? (Copilot-in-JD presumably lags Copilot-in-use.)

Evidence from exploration: (1) T23: ai_tool rose 1.6→17.7% (10.2×) — fastest-growing category. (2) T16 k=5 clusters — cluster 2 (high-length, AI-forward, +40pp AI) contains ServiceNow, Talent Groups, Verizon, Capgemini — late adopters. (3) T28 T19 ServiceNow AI growth +2789% — the biggest relative adoption. (4) T22 industry breakdown: software-dev industry has the lowest firm_any (31%) despite the highest any_ai (66.2%) — most hedged in the industry closest to the tools. (5) T29 consulting firms (Deloitte, PwC) as top LLM-style adopters.

Test design: Identify the top-30 AI tool tokens (copilot, cursor, claude, openai, anthropic, langchain, rag, llm, etc.). For each token, compute the first-appearance date by company (earliest period with ≥1 mention). Cluster companies by their adoption curve shape. Test whether the "early adopter" cluster is over-represented in big-tech employers vs consulting/finance/defense. Complementary: compute the aggregate ai_tool rate separately for "early adopter" and "late adopter" companies in 2024 and 2026 — does the 2024 employer-worker gap disappear when you restrict to early adopters?

Novelty: Strong sub-finding for the RQ3 inversion story. Reframes the lag as a bimodal adoption curve, which is cleaner than a single-number aggregate gap. Useful for writing up a publishable RQ3 figure.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data.

docs.title: H12. The cross-occupation convergence (SWE↔control +0.079) is driven entirely by the growth of shared "inclusive/cross-functional/stakeholder" boilerplate across white-collar occupations, not by shared technical vocabulary.
docs.text:
Hypothesis: The surprising T18 finding that SWE became more similar to accountant/nurse/financial-analyst postings in text space is caused by a shared HR boilerplate expansion (inclusive-language phrases, cross-functional framing, stakeholder vocabulary, benefits language) that crossed occupational boundaries. If you subtract the boilerplate, the cross-occupation drift disappears. The labor-market content changes in SWE are NOT diffusing into general white-collar occupations.

Evidence from exploration: (1) T18 SWE↔control +0.079 cosine growth. (2) T18 scope-language growth in control 5.23→18.98% (+13.75pp) — substantial catch-up in "stakeholder/cross-functional" vocabulary even in nurses/accountants. (3) T13 boilerplate grew 976% relatively but only 41% of gross delta — the boilerplate category is expanding. (4) T21 "cross-functional" grew +10.9pp at senior SWE. (5) T18 text source confound — 2024 91% LLM-cleaned, 2026 79% rule-cleaned, and LLM-cleaned text strips less boilerplate.

Test design: Define a "boilerplate vocabulary" token set (inclusive language terms, benefits disclaimers, stakeholder/cross-functional phrases, EEO compliance text). Recompute the SWE↔control cosine after stripping boilerplate tokens from each document. Does the +0.079 Δ shrink to zero or even reverse? Secondary test: compute separately for core sections (T13 classifier) only — does the convergence survive in the substantive sections, or only in the boilerplate sections?

Novelty: Strong methodological sub-finding. Would cleanly resolve the T18 surprise and prevent overstating the "SWE is becoming more generic" narrative. Also: the result determines whether to report the cross-occupation drift as a real occupational-blur finding or as a measurement-stratification observation.

Data feasibility: Testable with current data. Uses T13's saved section classifier.

docs.title: For the reframed RQ1 (within/between employer-side restructuring)
docs.text:
• Shift-share decomposition with explicit within/between/interaction terms, under both the arshkon-only panel and the pooled-2024 panel (per V2's sensitivity finding). Report both conventions as co-headlines; do not pick one.
  • Within-company panel regressions for content metrics (AI rate, scope, tech count, credential stack) at the (company × period) cell level, weighted by cell size. This is cleaner than aggregate-corpus comparisons for content change.
  • Company-level clustering (T16 k=5 style) on the change vector rather than the level vector — this is what identifies strategic archetypes.
  • Concentration correction for entry-level flow metrics: when 90%+ of companies post zero entry, any "entry-share" comparison across periods is dominated by the small subset that does post entry. Report alongside the zero-entry share.
  • Ablation set for seniority: combined best-available (primary), seniority_final, YOE proxy, seniority_native (as the known-contaminated reference). Always report all four; do not silently pick.

docs.title: For the reframed RQ2 (within-senior migration + new AI vocabulary)
docs.text:
• Strict pattern validation first (V2 canonical patterns) — use exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json. Growth rates are consistent across pattern variants; absolute levels differ by ~50%.
  • K-means clustering on language-profile vectors (T21 style) to identify archetype share shifts, not just vocabulary prevalence.
  • Within-title content evolution as the key figure (T18's AI Engineer evolution is the template). Pick 5-10 high-volume titles and trace their profile changes.
  • Domain × seniority stratification (T28 style) to surface heterogeneity. Do not aggregate across domains.
  • Fightin' Words with company-level capping (V1's method) to prevent volume-driven artifacts from dominating the vocabulary comparison.

docs.title: For the inverted RQ3 (lagged catch-up + aspirational language)
docs.text:
• Growth-rate comparison (employer vs worker) under multiple benchmark scenarios — the direction of the gap is robust; absolute magnitudes depend on benchmark choice. Report the bimodal-adoption hypothesis (H11) as the primary method of reconciling aggregate vs company-level stories.
  • Hedge:firm ratio analysis in proximity windows around AI terms — T22's 10× ratio is the backbone. Temporal stability of this ratio is itself the finding.
  • Posting-level AI-vocabulary specificity tiers (ai_general vs ai_tool vs ai_domain vs agentic) — the lag magnitude differs by tier (T23).
  • External benchmark sensitivity grid (Conservative/Central/High/Extreme) — never a single-number gap, always a band.
  • First-appearance by company analysis — for H11 testing.

docs.title: For RQ4 (mechanisms, interview-oriented)
docs.text:
• Paired within-company comparison (same company, same role, 2024 vs 2026 posting pairs) as the concrete case study for interviewees.
  • Mechanism-specific vocabulary audits — the new AI-orchestration language (agentic, guardrails, prompt engineering, multi-agent) should be probed directly in interviews.
  • Ghost-posting identification via combined column (T22 showed combined-col surfaces real new-grads with dense stacks, YOE proxy surfaces mislabeled seniors). Use combined col for interview artifact sampling.

docs.title: Methods that should NOT be used
docs.text:
• Native-label-only entry comparisons — inherited contamination; Wave 4 should not produce any table that relies solely on seniority_native or seniority_final for entry trends.
  • Bare agent keyword — V1 showed ~25-45% contamination. Use agentic + multi-agent + ai agent(s) phrases.
  • \bc\+\+\b or \bc# regexes — V1 documented broken. Use c\+\+ and alternate c# patterns or LIKE queries.
  • T11/T14's +34%/+60% tech count headlines — V2 documented broken (inflated by matrix absolute-count artifacts). Use +16-27% mean, 3→4 median.
  • T12 uncapped Fightin' Words top-30 — contains volume-driven Amazon boilerplate per V1. Use T12's capped top-30 + validated AI/credential subsets.
  • Naive hire pattern — V1 showed ~10-28% precision. Use T22's validated strict_hire_mgmt.

docs.title: 5. Key tensions the analysis phase must resolve
Tension 1. Credential stacking under low-LLM: partial mediator or confound?
docs.text: T29 claimed every headline strengthens in Q1 low-LLM; V2 re-ran and found credential stack +29% in Q1 vs +149% full corpus. Length and AI rate persist robustly; credential stacking does not fully persist. Either (a) writing style is a partial mediator for credential stacking but not for length / AI — meaning part of the credential jump is cosmetic and the real content expansion is smaller than T11's headline, or (b) the T29 score is too crude to stratify credential-stacking correctly and the attenuation is a measurement artifact. H4 + H5 + H8 all speak to this tension. The analysis phase must decide which interpretation governs the published number.

docs.title: Tension 2. T16's 87% between-company finding is convention-dependent (0.5% to 50% under alternative panels).
docs.text: V2 showed that the 87% replicates exactly under arshkon-only min≥3 but drops to 0.5% under arshkon-only min≥5, and to 47-61% under pooled-2024 panel. The qualitative claim ("majority is between-company") holds across conventions; the specific number does not. The analysis phase must either (a) pick a single defensible convention with a methodological justification, (b) report a range with the panel dependence explicit, or (c) commit to the arshkon-only convention because native labels can only come from arshkon and the between-company story is about reweighting toward arshkon-compatible employers. H3 (defense-driven composition) offers a third path: decompose the between-company number by sector before reporting a headline.

docs.title: Tension 3. Native-label entry decline vs combined-column/YOE entry rise — the direction flips depending on operationalization.
docs.text: Native / seniority_final: entry share falls 8-11pp, 80-85% within-company. Combined column / YOE proxy: entry share rises 3-6pp, 50-87% between-company. The seniority investigation established that native is contaminated, but the paper cannot avoid reporting both directions because reviewers will ask. The analysis phase needs a defensible convention: the recommended approach is to lead with the YOE-proxy rise, report the native-label decline as a diagnostic of label contamination, and use the contamination diagnostic itself as a methods contribution. The alternative is to have two non-comparable entry trends in the same paper, which is incoherent.

docs.title: Tension 4. What actually causes content expansion if not AI/ML, not LLM authorship, not text source, and not company concentration?
docs.text: Wave 3 ruled out all four candidate mechanisms for the credential-stack 5.4× jump. The finding is robust but causally unexplained. H6 (vendor/template propagation) and H8 (legacy-stack late template adoption) are the remaining mechanism hypotheses. The analysis phase needs to either confirm one of these or explicitly report the mechanism as open. The interview phase (T25) should probe this directly.

docs.title: Tension 5. Mentoring grew everywhere except AI/ML — what is the AI/ML exception's mechanism?
docs.text: S2 is the cleanest domain-heterogeneity finding. AI/ML senior tier specialized into tech-orch, skipping mentoring. H1 + H2 + H10 all propose explanations but they are not mutually exclusive and some are mutually contradictory. The analysis phase must pick a mechanism story: (a) AI work matured so fast that mentoring tasks were displaced by IC build tasks (H1), (b) AI/ML became early-career-prohibitive so there are fewer juniors to mentor at AI/ML companies (H2), or (c) the AI-mentioning senior cohort changed companies, and the company change drives the profile change (H10). These have different implications for labor-market interpretation.

docs.title: Data we need but do not have
docs.text:
1. 2025 snapshot. The entire corpus is two snapshots 2024-01/04 and 2026-03/04. We cannot distinguish a sharp 2024→2025 jump from a gradual 2-year trajectory. T19's within-snapshot variability analysis is the closest we have. A 2025 snapshot would collapse the H1 (AI maturation) and H8 (template adoption lag) hypotheses into testable temporal curves. Priority: highest. Without it, every "when did this happen" question is unanswerable.

  2. JOLTS alignment across all three sources. Arshkon was at a hiring trough (87K); asaniczka was at an uptick (160K); 2026 cycle position is not documented in the exploration. Hiring-cycle confound (Gate 1 gap #7) is not addressed. A public JOLTS overlay for SWE-specific hiring would partially address this.

  3. Worker-side AI usage data with occupational granularity. T23 benchmarks on SO's pro-dev aggregate (~80%). We do not have worker AI usage by seniority, by domain, or by company. The bimodal-adoption hypothesis (H11) would be much stronger with worker-side data that could be matched to the employer-side bimodality. LinkedIn Skills Graph data would be ideal.

  4. Ground-truth LLM authorship labels. T29's score is a proxy, confounded with polished marketing prose. A labeled dataset of "this JD was written by an LLM" vs "this JD was written by a human" would allow a proper test of the authorship-mediation hypothesis. Does not exist publicly; would require an internal crowdsourcing effort.

  5. Defense contractor posting flow via alternative channels. H3 requires knowing whether defense is over-represented in the scraped LinkedIn corpus relative to actual defense SWE hiring. Public JOLTS/OES data have sector-level totals but not LinkedIn-specific shares.

  6. Company-level entry program documentation. For H3 and H7, we need to know which of the 20-50 largest employers run formal new-grad programs, and whether program existence explains the entry-posting concentration. Public press releases and recruiting pages are scrapable but not in our corpus.

  7. Multi-period panel for within-company template saturation. T22 computes template saturation per (company × period). With three or more periods we could compute template-saturation trajectory and identify firms that saturated first — a critical input to H8.

docs.title: Data in the corpus but under-utilized
docs.text:
1. The 2024-01 vs 2024-04 within-2024 split. Gate 1 noted within-source heterogeneity but T19 was the only task to look systematically. The 2024-01 asaniczka and 2024-04 arshkon differences could be used to estimate snapshot-to-snapshot noise floors for every Wave 3 metric.

  2. The yoe_extracted distribution shape. We use it as a binary ≤2 proxy. The full YOE distribution (per company, per period, per archetype) could inform the entry-share-as-a-curve question and resolve the 0-2 vs 2-3 boundary sensitivity.

  3. Posting age / freshness. T19 flagged posting_age_days as 1% coverage (unusable). If the scraper could backfill this, we could address the hiring-cycle confound.

  4. Location-granular seniority. T17 showed AI↔entry geographically decoupled; we have not tested whether the between-company entry rise (H3) is geographically concentrated in defense-heavy metros (Huntsville, DC, Colorado Springs).

  5. Company-industry labels. T16 used them for high-level categorization; H3/H6/H8/H11 all require more granular company attributes (size, age, sector). The existing company_industry field is under-used.

docs.title: Methodological gaps
docs.text:
1. No causal identification strategy. The paper is descriptive. Every headline is "between 2024 and 2026, X changed." A DiD is done only for SWE-vs-control (T18). An instrumental-variable or regression-discontinuity design for the content findings would require variation we don't have. The paper should explicitly disclaim causality and lean into descriptive labor-market restructuring claims.

  2. Limited power for director-tier findings. T21 reports director n 113→351. S5 is a real finding but the confidence intervals are wide. More directors (via expanded scraping) would help.

  3. No pre-2023 baseline. We do not know what the trajectory looked like before 2024. If the 2024 level was already elevated vs 2020, the 2026 numbers could be continuation rather than restructuring. A proxy baseline from a public archive (Archive.org LinkedIn snapshots, or 2021-2022 job posting data from public sources) would anchor the trajectory.

docs.title: 7. Summary
docs.text:
The exploration produced a clear picture of what changed 2024-2026, a clear picture of what did not change, and a good list of what we don't yet know. The consolidated narrative (per gate_3) is stable under V1+V2 verification with specific caveats on magnitudes (tech count +16-27% not +34-60%; entry rise 50-87% between-company depending on panel convention; credential stacking partially writing-style mediated).

The twelve hypotheses in §3 are the substantive follow-ups for the analysis phase, grouped as:

  • Mechanism explanations for the within-senior shift (H1, H2, H10): AI maturation, early-career prohibition, and compositional company shift.
  • Mechanism explanations for the content expansion (H6, H8): HR template propagation and legacy-stack late adoption.
  • Within-posting content mechanism (H4, H5): credential stripping as a cause of RQ3 inversion.
  • Composition unpacking (H3, H11): defense concentration driving the between-company entry story; bimodal AI-tool adoption driving the RQ3 inversion.
  • Methodological robustness checks (H7, H9, H12): director headcount vs label reclassification, zero-entry concentration as structural vs trend, cross-occupation convergence as boilerplate artifact.

H1, H3, H4, H5, H10, and H11 are the highest-priority candidates for the analysis phase — they combine substantive novelty with feasibility on current data and would materially sharpen or extend the paper's narrative.

The five tensions in §5 must be resolved before any Wave 4 table is published — the analysis phase should lead with tension resolution, not with new analyses.

docs.title: T28 — Domain-stratified scope changes by archetype
docs.text: Agent O, Wave 3 (PRIORITY). Completed 2026-04-09.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The 2024→2026 scope-and-content inflation is overwhelmingly WITHIN-domain, not between-domain composition. Under three different seniority operationalizations, the within-domain term is 90–105% of the aggregate entry-share rise; the between-domain (AI/ML composition) term is within noise (±0.2pp). Requirement breadth, tech count, scope_count, AI-mention rate, and credential stack depth ≥7 all rise in every archetype, with magnitudes of +17% to +49% (breadth), +4% to +107% (tech), +41% to +153% (scope), and +1.5 to +64× (credential stack ≥7). AI/ML is the only large grower of market share (+16.5pp within domain), but it is not the source of the scope inflation — scope inflation is a market-wide language shift that affects Spring/Java and .NET stacks just as strongly as AI/ML.

Two surprises: 1. The senior-tier shift toward mentoring is universal. Every archetype shows positive mentor-pp growth for YOE≥5 postings; 14/20 archetypes grow by ≥10pp. Strict people-management language stays near 0% everywhere (no archetype has it above 5% in 2026 except T17). This is the cleanest single-direction result in the task. 2. The "AI/ML is less junior-heavy" finding from T09 is a labeling-column artifact. Under combined, 2026 AI/ML entry share is 0.9% — seemingly confirming T09. But under the label-independent YOE proxy, AI/ML 2026 entry share is 16.0% vs rest-of-market 17.2% — essentially identical. AI/ML is not structurally junior-averse; it just has worse combined-column labeling coverage (82.8% missing vs scraped's baseline). This contradicts T09's headline characterization.

docs.title: 1. Method
docs.text:
Archetype propagation. T09 clustered 8,000 SWE LinkedIn postings with BERTopic; the remaining 55,294 have no label. I propagated by nearest-centroid in the shared embedding space (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, L2-normalized). Centroids were computed per archetype from the 8,000 labeled rows, excluding noise (-1). Held-out 20% cross-validation gave 87.2% nearest-centroid accuracy (range 77%–100% per archetype; the worst-performing is T00 "software/technical/development" at 77%, which is the generalist cluster and expected to absorb ambiguous centroid misses). For all 63,294 rows, the top-cosine similarity distribution is tight (mean 0.77, min 0.10, 25th percentile 0.73); 99.9% exceed 0.35.

Ground-truth labels preserved. Rows that already had a T09 label (including noise = -1) kept it.

Artifact archetypes excluded from decomposition. T09 flagged T14 (Dice reposts), T20 (salary boilerplate), T21 (Expedia travel). These are preprocessing residuals, not tech domains. The "domain" subset = 55,944 rows (excluding the 2,223 noise + 5,127 artifact rows).

Corpus hygiene. Default SWE LinkedIn filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true. Decomposition uses domain subset; scope-inflation analyses additionally exclude aggregators (45,940 rows). Aggregator exclusion is the T28 sensitivity (a); capping within the within-archetype analyses is implicit (no single company dominates any archetype, top-20 AI/ML = 22% in 2026).

Artifacts used. - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet (T09 sample labels) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embeddings.npy + embedding index (propagation) - exploration/tables/T11/T11_features.parquet (63,294 SWE rows with requirement_breadth, tech_count, scope_count, ai_mention, credential_stack_depth, mgmts_mentor, mgmts_* strict people-manager family) - data/unified.parquet (description_hash for dedup)

New artifact: exploration/tables/T28/archetype_assignments.parquet (63,294 × {uid, archetype, archetype_name, cosine_top, is_confident, is_artifact, assign_source}).

docs.title: 2. Archetype distribution by period — AI/ML confirmation
docs.text:
Within the domain subset (aggregators included, 55,944 rows):

Archetype 2024 share 2026 share Δpp T01 AI / learning / machine learning 4.73% 21.18% +16.45 T02 cloud / infrastructure / devops 7.42% 9.95% +2.54 T03 data / analytics / data engineer 7.45% 9.80% +2.35 T00 software / technical / development 19.57% 20.74% +1.17 T12 flight / software / vehicle 2.21% 3.03% +0.82 T15 site reliability / sre 1.17% 1.66% +0.50 T06 android / mobile / ios 2.58% 2.90% +0.31 T11 aws / services / non-internship 3.76% 3.74% -0.02 T08 test / testing / automation 2.51% 2.24% -0.27 T19 servicenow / itsm 1.09% 0.69% -0.40 T22 test / manufacturing / equipment 1.27% 0.62% -0.64 T16 python / python dev / django 1.69% 0.91% -0.77 T05 spring / java / spring boot 4.58% 3.37% -1.21 T10 embedded / firmware 4.22% 2.94% -1.28 T04 frontend / web / react 8.87% 7.14% -1.73 T07 equipment / sales / customer 5.54% 3.28% -2.26 T13 radar / clearance / missile 4.80% 1.96% -2.84 T09 .NET / aspnet / net core 5.35% 1.75% -3.60 T18 systems engineering 4.76% 0.58% -4.18 T17 typescript sql / javascript java 6.44% 1.53% -4.91

AI/ML's +16.45pp is larger than T09's reported +10.96pp because (a) the denominator excludes artifact archetypes which themselves grew in 2026, (b) propagation to asaniczka 2024 spreads 2024 thinly across archetypes but concentrates it in T00/T04/T17/T18, raising the AI/ML delta in 2026.

Two declining archetypes are suspicious: T18 systems engineering (4.76→0.58%) and T17 typescript/javascript/java (6.44→1.53%) look like asaniczka-specific taxonomy rather than real domain decline. T18 noted in T09 as a generic language cluster; T17 is asaniczka's raw-skills heavy cluster. Their decline is partly "asaniczka absent in 2026 scraped pool" — not a technology domain shrinking. T09 T18 had asaniczka 1097 vs arshkon 0; T17 similarly asaniczka-heavy. This doesn't change the AI/ML growth story but softens confidence in the declining-tail taxonomy.

See figure exploration/figures/T28/fig1_archetype_share_shift.png.

docs.title: 3. Within/between/interaction decomposition of entry share
docs.text:
Under three seniority operationalizations × two subsets (domain vs domain+no-aggregator):

Operationalization Subset 2024 2026 Δtotal pp Within pp Between pp Interaction pp combined_best_available domain 0.78% 1.21% +0.43 +0.56 -0.00 -0.13 combined_best_available domain+noagg 0.97% 1.14% +0.17 +0.19 +0.05 -0.07 combined_augmented domain 1.14% 11.15% +10.01 +10.50 -0.02 -0.48 combined_augmented domain+noagg 1.41% 9.81% +8.41 +8.96 +0.03 -0.58 YOE≤2 proxy domain 7.24% 11.71% +4.46 +4.50 +1.50 -1.54 YOE≤2 proxy domain+noagg 7.96% 12.04% +4.08 +4.14 -0.16 +0.10

All three operationalizations agree: the entry-share rise is WITHIN-domain (~90-105% of the aggregate), not between-domain (market shifting toward junior-friendly archetypes).

  • combined_best_available restricts to rows with either seniority_llm or high-confidence rule routing; 83% of 2026 rows lack this and are excluded, yielding small absolute shares.
  • combined_augmented falls back to seniority_final for unrouted rows, reclassifying the bulk of the 2026 corpus. Under this, the aggregate rise is +10pp (large). But it inherits the arshkon native-entry contamination (41% of arshkon entries have YOE≥5; see seniority deep-dive).
  • The YOE proxy is the cleanest result: +4.46pp with within=+4.50, between=+1.50, interaction=-1.54. Between is a meaningful 1/3 of within here, indicating the market DID shift composition toward archetypes with slightly more junior YOE — AI/ML (10.93→16.02% entry-proxy) and T00 (the generalist cluster) are the biggest movers.

Per-archetype entry rate under combined_augmented (key rows):

Archetype 2024 rate 2026 rate Within contrib pp T00 software/technical/development 1.7% 11.4% +1.90 T04 frontend/web/react 1.0% 12.6% +1.03 T17 typescript sql 0.5% 13.2% +0.82 T07 equipment/sales 1.4% 15.4% +0.78 T13 radar/clearance 0.7% 13.7% +0.62 T03 data/analytics 1.3% 9.3% +0.60 T09 .NET 1.0% 12.2% +0.60 T01 AI/ML 0.8% 11.8% +0.52 T02 cloud/devops 0.5% 6.9% +0.48

The entry rate climbs 7–16× in most archetypes. The WITHIN term is dominated by T00 (generalist, largest share) and is large in every archetype because every archetype's entry rate roughly 10×s.

The between-domain term (market composition shift) contributes only +0.13pp from AI/ML and is net negative in several operationalizations. AI/ML growth is NOT the source of the entry-share rise. Whatever is causing the entry rate to climb in every archetype is a uniform phenomenon.

Sensitivities: the aggregator-excluded subset shows a smaller aggregate rise (+8.41 vs +10.01, or +4.08 vs +4.46 under YOE) but the within/between ratio is preserved.

See exploration/figures/T28/fig2_entry_rate_per_archetype.png and fig3_decomposition_bar.png, and table entry_share_decomposition.csv.

docs.title: 4. Scope inflation is universal across archetypes
docs.text:
Aggregator-excluded. All 20 non-artifact archetypes grow on every scope metric — no single domain drives the T11/V1 aggregate.

Requirement breadth Δ% (range): +17.9% (T11 AWS) to +49.6% (T13 radar/clearance). AI/ML is mid-pack at +35.7%. Spring/Java +48.4%, .NET +45.8%, SRE +31.1%. Every archetype grew.

Tech count Δ%: T07 equipment +107%, T12 vehicle +72%, T13 clearance +64%, T01 AI/ML +42%. AI/ML is not the max; classic-stack archetypes (Java, .NET) match or exceed it. Smallest: T11 AWS +4.4%, T06 android +12.7%.

Scope count Δ%: Spring/Java +153%, Python/Django +138%, clearance +116%, embedded +100%. AI/ML +67.7%. All 20 archetypes grew; minimum is T18 systems engineering +44%.

AI mention Δ%: ServiceNow +2789%, .NET +2188%, Spring +1787%. AI/ML itself only +5.3% because it was already saturated (0.92→0.97 mention rate). The biggest relative AI-language adoption is in the classic enterprise stacks — they're bolting AI-assistance language onto otherwise unchanged roles.

Credential stack depth ≥7 (the T11 headline 5.4× finding):

Archetype 2024 2026 Ratio Δpp T19 servicenow 0.38% 24.59% 63.9× +24.2 T15 SRE 0.38% 10.99% 29.1× +10.6 T05 Spring/Java 0.46% 10.54% 22.9× +10.1 T09 .NET 0.47% 8.38% 17.7× +7.9 T06 Android/iOS 0.88% 10.63% 12.1× +9.8 T04 Frontend 1.04% 11.59% 11.1× +10.6 T00 Generalist 4.00% 27.92% 7.0× +23.9 T02 Cloud/devops 2.24% 15.83% 7.1× +13.6 T03 Data eng 9.00% 25.61% 2.9× +16.6 T01 AI/ML 25.72% 38.05% 1.5× +12.3

AI/ML has the highest absolute credential stacking in both years (38% of postings have ≥7 credential categories), but the lowest ratio (1.5×) because it started saturated. AI/ML did not cause the credential-stacking explosion — it was already stacked. The ratio explosion is driven by legacy-stack archetypes catching up, suggestive of T29's LLM-authorship hypothesis: LLMs stack credential categories more uniformly than humans did.

The T11 5.4× aggregate is thus a weighted mixture: AI/ML's 1.5× dragged down by the 11-65× growth in classic-stack archetypes. A compositional rise in AI/ML (which is already at 38%) would push the aggregate up further; but the within-archetype ratio tells the mechanism story.

See fig4_credential_stack_by_archetype.png and scope_inflation_by_archetype.csv, credential_stack_7_by_archetype.csv.

docs.title: 5. Junior vs senior within each archetype — selective convergence
docs.text:
Strict YOE buckets (YOE≤2 = entry-proxy; YOE≥5 = senior-proxy). Gap = senior − entry.

Across 19 archetypes with sufficient sample in both periods, the direction of gap change is mixed:

  • AI mention gap (senior − entry): 9 archetypes widen (seniors use more AI vocab), 10 narrow or flat. Notable: T04 frontend gap went -0.07 → 0.07 (entry-level frontend is using MORE AI vocab); T03 data gap +0.22 (seniors pulling ahead). In aggregate (not shown) the entry-senior AI gap across all archetypes is near flat — entry caught up as much as seniors did, consistent with T11's entry AI-mention 15.8→50.1% finding.
  • Mentorship gap: widens in 13 of 19 archetypes (seniors get the mentoring language, entries don't). Clearest in T12 flight/vehicle (+0.31), T07 equipment (+0.15), and T08 test (+0.09). Shrinks in T03 data (+0.03), T15 SRE (-0.03), T17 (−0.02).
  • Requirement breadth gap: splits evenly — 10 widen, 9 narrow. T09 .NET shows the extreme: gap goes 0.16 → 4.48 (seniors gain 4.3 credential categories more than entries). T19 ServiceNow goes -2.35 → 2.46 (+4.8 swing).
  • Scope count gap: narrows in 9 archetypes (entry catching up on scope language) and widens in 10. No clear direction.

Conclusion for Step 4: Junior-senior content gaps do not converge uniformly. The T15 corpus-level convergence null held because within-archetype changes partially cancel: mentorship gap widens in most archetypes (keeping senior content distinct), while scope and breadth gaps change sign. The most actionable finding: mentorship language is the one consistent marker of senior postings that is holding distinctive in 2026 — every other dimension of senior-ness is leaking into entry postings.

See junior_senior_gap_changes.csv for the full table.

docs.title: 6. Senior-tier mentoring shift by archetype — strongly universal
docs.text:
Senior-tier (YOE≥5) only. Strict mentoring = mgmts_mentor (T11 detector, ≈100% precision per V1); strict people-manager = any of {mgmts_direct_reports, mgmts_performance_review, mgmts_people_manager, mgmts_headcount}.

Archetype n_2024 n_2026 Mentor 2024 Mentor 2026 Δmentor pp Δpmgr pp T19 ServiceNow 115 44 6.96% 36.36% +29.4 0.00 T06 Android/iOS 178 282 25.84% 53.90% +28.1 -1.12 T11 AWS 304 409 30.26% 50.86% +20.6 +0.16 T12 Flight/vehicle 301 355 16.61% 35.77% +19.2 +0.23 T01 AI/ML 263 1989 25.86% 43.39% +17.5 +0.58 T04 Frontend 626 691 30.35% 47.61% +17.3 +0.85 T03 Data eng 691 1073 24.75% 41.94% +17.2 +0.24 T09 .NET 517 167 17.02% 34.13% +17.1 +0.41 T05 Spring/Java 507 304 12.62% 28.62% +16.0 +0.46 T10 Embedded 404 290 17.08% 32.76% +15.7 -1.73 T13 Radar/clearance 529 192 9.64% 20.83% +11.2 -0.19 T22 Test/manufacturing 146 48 14.38% 25.00% +10.6 -1.37 T16 Python/Django 133 76 4.51% 14.47% +10.0 0.00 T17 TS/JS/Java 131 125 39.69% 47.20% +7.5 -10.1 T18 Systems eng 637 58 13.97% 20.69% +6.7 -0.63 T15 SRE 99 194 20.20% 26.80% +6.6 +2.60 T00 Generalist 1971 2324 36.99% 42.13% +5.1 -0.71 T08 Test 264 227 19.70% 23.35% +3.7 +0.06 T02 Cloud/devops 734 996 29.43% 32.03% +2.6 +0.26 T07 Equipment 660 347 22.58% 24.50% +1.9 +2.81

Every archetype's senior-tier mentoring rose. Range +1.9 to +29.4pp. Strict people-manager language stayed near 0% in 17 of 20 archetypes and declined in 6.

The T11 senior-as-IC-mentor story is the single most robust cross-domain finding in T28. It is not concentrated in AI/ML (which is mid-pack at +17.5pp). It is a uniform restructuring of the senior tier away from people-management language toward mentoring/coaching language, across every tech domain.

T17 TypeScript/SQL is the lone outlier with material people-management language decline (22.9% → 12.8%, -10.1pp). T17 is asaniczka-heavy, so this likely reflects a source-composition artifact (asaniczka associate-heavy rows collapsing).

See fig5_senior_mentoring_by_archetype.png and senior_mentor_vs_pmgr_by_archetype.csv.

docs.title: 7. AI/ML archetype deep dive
docs.text: Sample: 6,313 aggregator-excluded AI/ML rows (731 in 2024, 5,582 in 2026) — a 7.6× volume increase in absolute counts (vs 1.24× for the aggregator-excluded domain as a whole).

docs.title: 7.1 Top employers (dedup within company by description_hash)
docs.text:
2024 (top 15): NVIDIA (19), Palo Alto Networks (19), Tesla (12), AMD (10), ByteDance (10), Rivian (9), Amazon (8), Anduril (8), Conch Tech (8), Stripe (7), Aurora (5), Walmart (5), Anyscale (4), Forward (4), RemoteWorker US (5).

2026 (top 15): Microsoft (84), JPMorgan Chase (56), Amazon (52), AWS (49), General Motors (42), Booz Allen Hamilton (41), Uber (40), Anthropic (38), NVIDIA (38), Adobe (35), AMD (34), Walmart (33), OpenAI (32), TikTok (31), ByteDance (29).

The 2026 top-15 is Fortune-500 heavy + AI labs. In 2024, the top slots were specialist GPU/autonomous/ML firms. By 2026, Microsoft, JPMorgan, Amazon, GM, Booz Allen, Uber, Adobe have bolted on AI/ML hiring at scale. This is consistent with "AI/ML growth comes from existing large employers shifting mix" more than "new entrants flooding the market." NVIDIA, AMD, ByteDance stayed; Anthropic and OpenAI joined the top 15 but are not dominant.

Top-20 concentration: 30.67% of 2024 AI/ML rows came from the top 20 employers; only 22.39% in 2026. AI/ML is less concentrated in 2026 than in 2024, consistent with broad diffusion rather than a few big hirers capturing the growth.

docs.title: 7.2 Seniority mix — critical methodological finding
docs.text:
Op Period Entry % Mid-senior % Unknown/missing % combined (strict routed) 2024 1.1% 27.2% 71.1% (incl None 50.1%) combined (strict routed) 2026 0.9% 8.7% 89.9% (incl None 82.8%) combined_augmented (with fallback) 2024 1.4% 69.2% 21.1% combined_augmented (with fallback) 2026 10.3% 72.2% 14.4% YOE proxy (known only) 2024 10.9% (mid: 29.2%) YOE-known n=439 YOE proxy (known only) 2026 16.0% (mid: 31.1%) YOE-known n=3,764

T09 reported AI/ML as "structurally less junior-heavy" based on a 6.0% entry share vs 7-8% sample-wide. Under T28's combined_augmented, 2026 AI/ML entry is 10.3% vs ~7-8% rest — reversed direction. Under YOE proxy, 2026 AI/ML entry is 16.0% vs 17.2% rest — essentially identical, not distinctively junior-averse.

T09's finding was a labeling-column artifact. AI/ML has dramatically worse combined-column routing coverage (82.8% None vs much lower for rest-of-market) because AI/ML postings tend to avoid title-keyword matches that the Stage 5 router relies on — "Machine Learning Engineer", "AI/ML Engineer" titles skip the rule routing and get sent to LLM. When the LLM classification_coverage is missing for those rows, the combined column is NULL and the archetype looks artificially entry-sparse.

Correct characterization: Under label-independent YOE proxy, AI/ML entry share is 10.9% (2024) → 16.0% (2026), rising +5.1pp — more than the market-wide rise of +4.1pp. AI/ML is not junior-averse; it is among the archetypes with higher junior growth.

docs.title: 7.3 AI/ML scope profile (2026)
docs.text:
Metric AI/ML Rest requirement_breadth 19.0 16.3 tech_count 10.2 8.5 scope_count 2.57 1.90 ai_mention 0.97 0.41 credential_stack_depth 6.05 5.43 text_len chars 3,585 3,272 mgmts_mentor rate 29.8% 29.5%

AI/ML postings are longer (+10% chars), broader (+17% breadth), more credential-stacked (+11% depth), and nearly universal on AI mentions (97% vs 41%), but identical on mentorship rate. The "more senior, more demanding" stereotype of AI/ML is confirmed on requirement breadth and tech count but NOT on mentoring — senior-tier restructuring toward mentoring happens uniformly across all archetypes (Section 6).

AI/ML core-growth mechanism: a mix of (a) existing large employers expanding AI hiring (Microsoft 84, JPMC 56, Amazon 52) and (b) new entrants from the AI-lab subset (Anthropic 38, OpenAI 32) contributing ~1.2% of AI/ML volume combined. The bulk of AI/ML growth is existing employers shifting their mix, not market entry. T16 will verify with company-level decomposition.

See aiml_top_employers_{2024,2026}.csv, aiml_seniority_mix.csv.

docs.title: 8. Sensitivities summary
docs.text:
(a) Aggregator exclusion. All decomposition and scope analyses were run with and without aggregators (results in the same table). Within/between/interaction ratios are stable. Aggregate deltas shift ≤2.5pp under aggregator exclusion.

Capping. Within-archetype analyses implicitly cap because top-20 company share inside AI/ML is 22% in 2026 (vs 31% in 2024) — AI/ML is becoming LESS concentrated, not more. For the cross-archetype scope analyses, no capping was applied because the corpus-wide top-20 employers spread across many archetypes. T11 already verified aggregate scope inflation is robust to cap-20.

Seniority operationalization. Every result was run under combined_best_available, combined_augmented, and YOE-proxy where applicable. The combined_best_available column has too few 2026 labeled rows in AI/ML to make a defensible claim (82% None); combined_augmented adds the seniority_final fallback but inherits arshkon contamination; YOE proxy is the co-equal validator and agrees with combined_augmented on direction and within-dominance.

Artifact exclusion. T14, T20, T21 (Dice reposts, salary boilerplate, Expedia travel) were excluded from domain analyses. Noise (-1) was excluded.

Propagation accuracy. 87% holdout; propagated rows (55K of 63K) carry ~13% label noise. This noise is approximately random across the label space and does not systematically bias the domain × period contingency table. However, the 2024 asaniczka rows (23K) were not in T09's training sample and are entirely propagated, so the 2024 T09 AI/ML share (0.73% from sample × 5019 arshkon rows) is higher in T28 (4.7% within domain) because asaniczka propagation added AI/ML rows. The effect is to shrink the AI/ML 2024→2026 delta. The +11pp T09 figure used only arshkon for 2024; T28's +16pp uses the full 2024 pool via propagation. Both are defensible; T28's is consistent with other aggregate metrics.

docs.title: 9. Key takeaways
docs.text:
1. Scope and credential inflation is a universal, within-domain phenomenon. Every archetype grew on requirement breadth (+17-50%), tech count (+4-107%), scope count (+41-153%), and credential stack ≥7 (+2.9-64× ratio). AI/ML is mid-pack, not the driver. This pattern is strongly suggestive of T29's LLM-authorship mechanism — a uniform cross-domain language shift is what we'd expect from recruiters adopting the same authoring tools.

  2. Entry-share rise is 90-105% within-domain. Between-domain composition (AI/ML +16pp) contributes only +0.13pp to the aggregate entry-share rise under the primary operationalization. Whatever is causing more junior postings is happening inside every archetype, not through market re-sorting.

  3. Senior-tier mentoring growth is the single cleanest result. +1.9 to +29.4pp across every archetype; strict people-management near-zero or declining in 17/20. The senior restructuring story holds universally and is not domain-dependent.

  4. T09's "AI/ML is structurally less junior-heavy" should be revised. Under YOE proxy, AI/ML 2026 entry share is 16.0% vs 17.2% rest-of-market — indistinguishable. Under combined_augmented, AI/ML is slightly MORE entry-heavy (10.3% vs ~7.8% rest). The T09 finding was a combined-column routing-coverage artifact.

  5. AI/ML growth is dominated by existing large employers. Top-20 concentration fell 31%→22%, suggesting diffusion. Microsoft, JPMorgan, Amazon, Uber, Adobe all now appear in the AI/ML top-15 — they were not there in 2024. AI labs (Anthropic, OpenAI) join but are not dominant.

  6. Decomposition terminology caveat: when the within-term dominates the aggregate by 90-105%, the between-term is either adding little or subtracting. Here the between term is between -0.2 and +1.5pp depending on operationalization. The between-dominated scenario — "entry-share rose because the market shifted to AI/ML, which is more junior-friendly" — is not supported. The within-dominated scenario — "every archetype's entry-hiring rate climbed ~10×" — IS supported.

docs.title: Outputs
docs.text:
Tables (exploration/tables/T28/): - archetype_assignments.parquet — propagated labels for all 63,294 SWE LinkedIn rows - centroid_holdout_accuracy.csv - archetype_share_by_period_all.csv, archetype_share_by_period_domain.csv - entry_share_decomposition.csv, entry_share_per_archetype_contrib.csv - scope_inflation_by_archetype.csv, credential_stack_7_by_archetype.csv - junior_senior_within_archetype.csv, junior_senior_gap_changes.csv - senior_mentor_vs_pmgr_by_archetype.csv - aiml_top_employers_2024.csv, aiml_top_employers_2026.csv, aiml_seniority_mix.csv

Figures (exploration/figures/T28/): - fig1_archetype_share_shift.png — per-archetype share delta - fig2_entry_rate_per_archetype.png — 2024 vs 2026 entry rate bars - fig3_decomposition_bar.png — within/between/interaction by operationalization - fig4_credential_stack_by_archetype.png - fig5_senior_mentoring_by_archetype.png

Scripts (exploration/scripts/): - T28_01_propagate_archetypes.py - T28_02_decomposition.py - T28_03_figures.py

docs.title: T29 — LLM-authored description detection
docs.text: Agent O, Wave 3 (EXPLORATORY). Completed 2026-04-09.

docs.title: Headline verdict: HYPOTHESIS REJECTED
docs.text:
The low-LLM-score subset of the 2024-2026 corpus exhibits the SAME Wave 2 findings as the full corpus and the high-LLM-score subset, often with larger percentage changes. Credential stack depth ≥7 grows +786% in the low-LLM subset versus +384% in the high-LLM subset. Description length grows +77% in the low-LLM subset versus +79% in the high-LLM subset (identical). AI mention grows +339% low vs +269% high. Tech density falls -22.8% low vs -34.3% high. Requirement breadth grows +42% low vs +35% high.

Not a single Wave 2 headline weakens when the LLM-style subset is removed. If anything, the low-LLM subset shows cleaner, stronger versions of every change.

This rejects the hypothesis that recruiter LLM-adoption mediates the 2024-2026 labor market shifts. Whatever is driving the changes (credential stacking, AI mentions, length growth, mid-senior restructuring) is happening uniformly across postings regardless of whether they read as LLM-style marketing prose or as human-written technical copy. The text-source composition shift (2024 91% LLM-cleaned vs 2026 21% LLM-cleaned, flagged by V1) does contribute to the +72% aggregate length rise, but even under text-source control the length growth persists at +26% (V1) and T29's headline metrics (credential stacking, AI mentions) are well above the text-source baseline.

Caveats: 1. The composite "authorship score" captures polished-marketing-prose style more than it captures LLM-specific tells. Some highest-scoring 2024 companies (Microsoft, Raytheon, Booz Allen, Anduril) are likely producing human-written but marketing-polished copy, not LLM-generated copy. 2. Under text-source-controlled comparison, em-dash density rose 9-11×, LLM-vocab density rose 36-63%, and sentence length roughly doubled. These ARE suggestive of 2026 postings having more LLM-style surface features. But even if we accept those increases are genuine LLM-adoption evidence, the Wave 2 labor-market findings are independent of them. 3. Spot-check validation showed the score discriminates qualitatively well (high = flowing marketing "Do you want to be part of a world-class organization..."; low = terse "seeking .NET/C# Developer, Full Time"). But "LLM-style" and "marketing-style" are intrinsically confounded.

The hypothesis was testable and testable is good — the answer is that recruiter LLM adoption is not a parsimonious unified mechanism for the Wave 2 findings.

docs.title: 1.1 Features (defined in T29_01_features.py)
docs.text:
Computed per posting on the raw description column (not Stage 9 cleaned, to preserve formatting):

Feature Definition Role llm_vocab_density_1k Count of classic LLM tells (leverage, pivotal, cutting-edge, seamless, harness, robust, vibrant, foster, delve, embark, ...) per 1K chars Primary vocabulary signal emdash_density_1k Em-dash (— and --) per 1K chars Strong LLM stylistic tell bullet_density_1k Bullet markers (•, *, -, 1.) per 1K chars Structural; ingestion-sensitive ttr Type-token ratio of lowercased word tokens LLM prose has lower TTR mean_sent_len_chars, std_sent_len_chars Sentence length mean and std LLMs write longer, more uniform sentences mean_para_len, paragraph_count Paragraph stats via \n\n splits Ingestion-sensitive hedge_phrase_count "but not limited to", "such as but", "not limited to" LLM hedge closer_count "in conclusion", "we look forward", "join us" Boilerplate closer comma_density_1k, exclaim_density_1k Punctuation density Style

Pre-flight asserts (in script): regex patterns validated against hand-crafted positive/negative cases before running on corpus.

Composite score (llm_score_robust): z-sum of four ingestion-robust features: llm_vocab_density_1k, hedge_phrase_count, comma_density_1k, and 1 - ttr. Excludes em-dash, bullets, paragraph features because those are too sensitive to ingestion pipeline differences across sources.

docs.title: 1.2 Spot-check validation
docs.text:
Drew 25 high-score (llm_vocab_count≥11) and 25 low-score (llm_vocab_count=0) samples. Qualitative read (see exploration/tables/T29/spotcheck_samples.csv):

High-score examples read like corporate marketing copy: - "Do you want to be part of a growing world-class organization focused on bettering our world? Are you passionate about significantly improving the future of medicine?" (AmplifyBio 2024) - "At Function Health, our mission is to empower individuals to take control of their health. Through our innovative platform..." (Function Health 2024) - "Senior Backend AI Engineer... In this pivotal role, you will be at the forefront..." (Pop-Up Talent 2024)

Low-score examples read as terse, skills-list style: - "If you are a Sr. Software Engineer with experience, please read on! We are seeking a Senior Signal Processing Engineer..." (CyberCoders 2024) - "Syms Strategic Group (SSG) is seeking a talented .Net/C# Developer. Department: Veterans Affairs. Type: Full Time. Min. Experience: Experienced..." (SSG 2024) - "Secure our Nation, Ignite your Future..." (ManTech 2024)

Conclusion: The score discriminates "polished marketing prose" from "terse technical" reliably. But it does NOT directly discriminate "LLM-generated" from "human-written". A 2024 AmplifyBio description with 15 LLM-tell hits is more likely human-written marketing copy than LLM-generated; a 2024 LLM probably would have written even more fluently. This is an important limitation of the approach.

docs.title: 1.3 Score levels
docs.text:
• Raw corpus (n=63,294): 2024 mean z-score -0.48, 2026 mean z-score +0.39 (Δ +0.88 standard deviations)
  • LLM-cleaned subset (n=33,220 — text-source-controlled): 2024 -0.14, 2026 +0.51 (Δ +0.66 SD)

The shift is ~1 SD upward from 2024 to 2026. Under text-source control (LLM-cleaned subset), the shift is still 0.66 SD — substantial.

docs.title: 2. Distribution by period — text-source-controlled
docs.text:
Raw corpus (mixed text sources), no aggregators:

Feature 2024 2026 Ratio llm_vocab_density_1k 0.337 0.468 ×1.39 emdash_density_1k 0.040 0.142 ×3.59 ttr 0.571 0.538 ×0.94 hedge_phrase_count 0.190 0.273 ×1.43 comma_density_1k 11.1 12.2 ×1.10 composite score (z-sum) -0.65 +0.50 Δ +1.16

LLM-cleaned subset (text-source-controlled), no aggregators:

Feature 2024 2026 Ratio llm_vocab_density_1k 0.275 0.448 ×1.63 emdash_density_1k 0.012 0.110 ×9.08 ttr 0.597 0.580 ×0.97 hedge_phrase_count 0.078 0.072 ×0.92 comma_density_1k 10.4 11.8 ×1.14 composite score (z-sum) -0.21 +0.59 Δ +0.80

Under text-source control: - Vocabulary density rises MORE (+63% vs +39%) — text cleaning normalizes away some 2024 marketing flourishes, so post-normalization 2024 looks less LLM-ish, widening the gap. - Em-dash density rises 9× — still huge but slightly smaller than the 3.6× raw. The 2024 asaniczka baseline of 0.012 is very low; 2024 LLM-cleaned text rarely contains em-dashes. 2026 scraped LLM-cleaned text has 0.110, 9× higher. This is the most striking signal that survives text-source control. - Hedge phrases disappear entirely as a signal under text-source control (ratio 0.92). The raw-corpus hedge growth was mostly the 2026 raw text retaining more boilerplate that the LLM cleaner removes. - Comma density grows only +14% — modest. - TTR drops trivially (0.02pp) — not supportive of the "LLM uniformization" hypothesis.

Cross-posting variance (std of composite score within each year):

Subset 2024 std 2026 std raw 2.250 2.192 ctrl (LLM-cleaned) 2.023 2.184
